Feedback Loop to Activity 1] A5 -.Feedback Iteration.-> A1 U1[Umbrella: Software Quality Assurance] -.Overlay.-> A1 U1 -.Overlay.-> A2 U1 -.Overlay.-> A3 U1 -.Overlay.-> A4 U1 -.Overlay.-> A5 U2[Umbrella: Risk Management] -.Overlay.-> A1 U2 -.Overlay.-> A2 U2 -.Overlay.-> A3 U2 -.Overlay.-> A4 U2 -.Overlay.-> A5 U3[Umbrella: Technical Reviews] -.Overlay.-> A1 U3 -.Overlay.-> A2 U3 -.Overlay.-> A3 U3 -.Overlay.-> A4 U3 -.Overlay.-> A5 U4[Umbrella: Configuration Management] -.Overlay.-> A1 U4 -.Overlay.-> A2 U4 -.Overlay.-> A3 U4 -.Overlay.-> A4 U4 -.Overlay.-> A5 style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke:#2px style A4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style U1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 style U2 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 style U3 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 style U4 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide for KTU Exams:** The five **framework activities** form the central linear flow (top to bottom). The **umbrella activities** (yellow nodes on the right) overlay *across* all five. In an exam, if asked to "draw the generic process framework," always draw the 5 central boxes with arrows, and then label at least 3-4 umbrella activities cutting across them. ## 4.2 Layered View: Mapping Development Activities to OOD Deliverables ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L1[Communication Layer] L1a[Use Case Diagram] L1b[Use Case Narrative] L1c[Glossary and SRS] end subgraph L2[Planning Layer] L2a[Iteration Plan] L2b[Risk Register] L2c[Estimation UCP or COCOMO] end subgraph L3[Modeling Layer] L3a[Analysis Class Diagram] L3b[Sequence Diagram] L3c[Statechart Diagram] L3d[Architectural Pattern MVC] end subgraph L4[Construction Layer] L4a[Java Source Code] L4b[JUnit Test Cases] L4c[Build Script Maven or Gradle] end subgraph L5[Deployment Layer] L5a[JAR or WAR Artifact] L5b[User Manual] L5c[Release Notes] end L1a --> L2a L1b --> L2a L1c --> L3a L2a --> L3a L3a --> L4a L3b --> L4a L3c --> L4a L3d --> L4a L4a --> L5a L4b --> L5a L4c --> L5a ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **PEITT411 Module Context:** This layered map is *critical* for understanding how **Module 1 (Software Engineering Development Activities)** feeds directly into **Modules 2-5 of OOD using Java**. For instance, a *Communication Layer Use-Case Diagram* becomes the *Analysis Class Diagram* in Modeling, which becomes the *Java classes* in Construction. ## 4.3 Process Flow Pattern — Linear vs Evolutionary ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Linear[Waterfall Linear Flow] L1[Communication] --> L2[Planning] L2 --> L3[Modeling] L3 --> L4[Construction] L4 --> L5[Deployment] end subgraph Evolu[Spiral Evolutionary Flow] E1[Communication] --> E2[Planning with Risk Analysis] E2 --> E3[Modeling Prototype 1] E3 --> E4[Construction Prototype 1] E4 --> E5[Customer Evaluation] E5 --> E6{Risk Acceptable?} E6 -->|Yes| E7[Deployment of Prototype 1] E6 -->|No| E2 E7 --> E8[Next Spiral Loop] E8 --> E2 end style Linear fill:#E6F3FF style Evolu fill:#FFF0E6 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Define software engineering. List any **four** generic process framework activities.* > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Software engineering is the application of a systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software (IEEE Std 610.12). It applies engineering principles to overcome the inherent complexity of software systems. > > The four generic process framework activities are: > 1. **Communication** — understanding stakeholder requirements. > 2. **Planning** — defining scope, resources, schedule, and risk. > 3. **Modeling** — building analysis and design representations. > 4. **Construction** — code generation and testing. > *(A fifth is Deployment — delivery and support.)* **(1 + 2 = 3 marks)** > **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *Distinguish between **framework activities** and **umbrella activities** with two examples each.* > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Framework Activities:** Applied sequentially or iteratively to produce a working product. *Examples:* Communication, Construction. **(1.5 marks)** > - **Umbrella Activities:** Overlay across the entire project, applied continuously regardless of phase. *Examples:* Software Quality Assurance, Risk Management. **(1.5 marks)** ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, students often *list* points without *explaining their inter-relationship*. KTU examiners specifically look for **causal linkages** (e.g., "Communication *feeds* the SRS into Planning, which *drives* Modeling..."). Marks are lost when students write disconnected points. Always use a **flowing narrative** plus a small **diagram** for full marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Process Activities + Estimation* > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified Model]`** *(a)* Explain the five generic process framework activities of software engineering with their key deliverables. *(7 marks)* > > *(b)* A semi-detached software project is estimated at **5 KLOC**. Using COCOMO basic model with $a_b = 3.0$, $b_b = 1.12$, $c_b = 2.5$, $d_b = 0.35$, calculate the **effort, development time, average team size, and productivity**. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks 1. **Communication (1.4 marks):** Inception, elicitation, elaboration, negotiation, specification (SRS per IEEE 830), validation. *Deliverable:* SRS document, use-case diagrams. 2. **Planning (1.4 marks):** Estimation (LOC/FP/UCP), scheduling, risk identification. *Deliverable:* Project plan, risk register. 3. **Modeling (1.4 marks):** Analysis model (data, functional, behavioral) + design model (architecture, interface, component, database). *Deliverable:* Class diagrams, sequence diagrams. 4. **Construction (1.4 marks):** Code generation, unit testing (JUnit), integration testing. *Deliverable:* Source code, test reports. 5. **Deployment (1.4 marks):** Delivery, support, feedback. *Deliverable:* Installed system, user manual, release notes. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks Given: KLOC = 5, $a_b = 3.0$, $b_b = 1.12$, $c_b = 2.5$, $d_b = 0.35$. $$ \begin{aligned} E &= a_b \times (\text{KLOC})^{b_b} = 3.0 \times (5)^{1.12} \\ &= 3.0 \times 6.07 \\ &= 18.21 \text{ Person-Months} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D &= c_b \times E^{d_b} = 2.5 \times (18.21)^{0.35} \\ &= 2.5 \times 2.69 \\ &= 6.73 \text{ Months} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Average Team Size} &= \dfrac{E}{D} = \dfrac{18.21}{6.73} = 2.71 \approx 3 \text{ persons} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Productivity } P &= \dfrac{\text{KLOC}}{E} = \dfrac{5}{18.21} = 0.275 \text{ KLOC/PM} \\ &= 275 \text{ LOC/PM} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *SDLC Models + Comparison* > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified Model]`** *(a)* Compare the **Waterfall** and **Spiral** models across the five development activities. Which is more suitable for a **high-risk R&D project** and why? *(7 marks)* > > *(b)* For a Java-based **e-commerce web application** with frequent requirement changes, recommend a suitable development process model. Justify with three reasons and mention one limitation. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks *(See the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.2 for the comparison table; examiners award 4 marks for a clean matrix.)* **Comparison Highlights (2 marks):** - **Waterfall:** Linear; each activity (Communication, Planning, ...) is performed once. Customer is involved only at the start and end. Change is *costly*. - **Spiral:** Cyclical; Communication, Planning, Modeling, Construction, Deployment are repeated in *concentric loops* (typically 4 quadrants: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Customer Evaluation). Risk is *explicitly* addressed at every cycle. **Suitability for R&D (1 mark):** The **Spiral Model** is more suitable because: - Risk analysis is a *first-class* activity in each loop. - Prototypes reduce technical uncertainty. - Customer evaluation at each spiral ensures alignment with evolving understanding. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Recommendation: Agile / Scrum (or XP — Extreme Programming)** *(2 marks)* **Three Justifications (3 marks, 1 each):** 1. **Iterative & Incremental Delivery:** Each sprint (2-4 weeks) produces a working, potentially shippable increment — perfect for evolving e-commerce requirements. 2. **Customer Collaboration over Contract Negotiation:** The Product Owner (representing customers) re-prioritizes the backlog before every sprint, naturally absorbing change. 3. **Continuous Integration & Testing:** Java + Maven/Gradle + Jenkins pipelines fit Agile's "always shippable" philosophy; regression bugs are caught early. **Limitation (2 marks):** - **Documentation is minimal**, which becomes a problem during future maintenance or when the project must be handed to another team. For long-term maintainability (e.g., 10-year lifecycle of banking software), Agile alone is insufficient — it must be complemented by a *documentation-heavy* framework like RUP. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-density revision checklist for last-minute KTU exam preparation:** - [ ] **Definition:** Software Engineering = systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to software development (IEEE 610.12). - [ ] **5 Framework Activities:** Communication → Planning → Modeling → Construction → Deployment (acronym: **C-P-M-C-D**). - [ ] **Umbrella Activities** overlay all framework activities. Key ones: **SQA, Risk Management, Technical Reviews, SCM, Measurement**. - [ ] **Communication** produces the **SRS** (IEEE 830), use-case diagrams, and use-case narratives. - [ ] **Modeling** has two sub-models: **Analysis Model** (what) and **Design Model** (how). - [ ] **Construction** includes **Unit Testing (JUnit)** and **Integration Testing**. - [ ] **COCOMO Basic:** $E = a_b \times (\text{KLOC})^{b_b}$, $D = c_b \times E^{d_b}$. - [ ] **COCOMO Modes:** Organic (small team, familiar), Semi-detached (medium), Embedded (tight constraints, real-time). - [ ] **Function Points:** $\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$, with $\text{TCF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i$. - [ ] **Use-Case Points:** $\text{UCP} = \text{UUCP} \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$, where UUCP = weighted sum of actors + weighted sum of use-cases. - [ ] **Defect Density** $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ is a key **Software Quality Metric** falling under the **umbrella activity SQA**. - [ ] **Process Models:** Waterfall (linear), Iterative, Evolutionary (Spiral), Parallel, Agile. - [ ] **Spiral Model** explicitly integrates **risk management** as a first-class activity. - [ ] **Agile Manifesto** priorities: (1) Individuals & interactions over processes & tools, (2) Working software over comprehensive documentation, (3) Customer collaboration over contract negotiation, (4) Responding to change over following a plan. - [ ] **Productivity benchmark for Java:** ~200-400 LOC / Person-Month (including tests and docs). - [ ] **Always draw a small diagram** in 14-mark answers; the KTU valuation key reserves 2-3 marks for neat, labeled schematics. - [ ] **Never confuse** *Verification* (building the product right) with *Validation* (building the right product) — this is a **favourite 3-mark question**.

topics.title: Managing Software Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Managing Software Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Software Project Management (SPM)** is the discipline of planning, organizing, directing, and controlling resources to achieve specific software development goals within defined constraints of **scope, time, cost, and quality**. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus under Module 1, *Managing Software Development* encompasses the systematic orchestration of people, product, process, and project — the four P's of software management — to deliver reliable, maintainable, and customer-satisfying software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — The 4 P's of Software Project Management**
> 1. **People** — Stakeholders, team structure, and communication.
> 2. **Product** — Software objectives, scope, and requirements.
> 3. **Process** — The framework (model/methodology) chosen.
> 4. **Project** — Planning, estimation, scheduling, and tracking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of building a house. Before laying a single brick, you need:
- An **architect** (analyst/designer),
- A **blueprint** (requirements & design),
- A **construction crew** (developers/testers),
- A **timeline** (schedule),
- A **budget** (cost estimation),
- A **supervisor** (project manager),
- A **risk plan** ("What if it rains during foundation work?").

**Software project management is exactly this — but the "house" is an application, and the bricks are lines of Java code.** Without management, even the best developers deliver late, over-budget products that don't meet customer needs. Software is *invisible* and *intangible*, which is why structured management is even more critical than in construction.

## 1.3 Why Software Project Management is Harder

| Challenge | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Invisibility** | You cannot see software progress physically; only documents/working code reflect state. |
| **Complexity** | Modern systems integrate millions of lines, third-party APIs, and distributed components. |
| **Conformity** | Software must conform to human, organizational, and regulatory constraints. |
| **Changeability** | Requirements evolve constantly; software is inherently easy to "modify" mid-build. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The industry standard failure rate of software projects is approximately **30%**, and only **28%** succeed outright (Standish Group CHAOS Report). This underscores the importance of disciplined project management.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4 P's — Detailed Breakdown

### **P-1: People**
- **Stakeholders:** Customers, end-users, developers, managers, auditors.
- **Team Structures:**
  - **Democratic (Chief Programmer Team):** Small, informal, shared leadership.
  - **Centralized Control (Controlled Team):** Manager-driven, hierarchical.
  - **Mixed (Synchronize-and-Stabilize):** Hybrid approach used at Microsoft.
- **Need High Bandwidth Communication:** Effective interpersonal communication is the single most critical success factor.

### **P-2: Product**
- Define the **scope** clearly.
- Decompose the problem into smaller, manageable problem sets.
- Identify **software scope** (functions, features, constraints) and refine it iteratively.

### **P-3: Process**
A *process* is a structured set of activities required to develop software. Common process models are discussed in Section 4.

### **P-4: Project**
- **Project Planning** (what, when, who, how).
- **Estimation** (size, effort, cost, duration).
- **Scheduling** (Gantt Charts, PERT/CPM).
- **Risk Management** (Identification, Analysis, Planning, Monitoring).
- **Tracking & Control** (Earned Value Analysis).

## 2.2 Software Estimation Techniques

Estimation precedes design and coding. The three classical techniques are:

### **A. Lines of Code (LOC) / KLOC**
Counting source lines. Effort is correlated to size.

### **B. Function Point (FP) Analysis**
A measure of *functionality* delivered to the user, independent of programming language.

### **C. COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model) — Barry Boehm (1981)**
The most cited algorithmic model. Three modes:

| Mode | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Basic COCOMO** | Early, rough estimation using only size. |
| **Intermediate COCOMO** | Adds 15 cost drivers (product, hardware, personnel, project attributes). |
| **Detailed COCOMO** | Adds 5 phases (plan, design, implementation, test, maintenance) to intermediate. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for ESE & Internal Exams**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = a_b \times (KLOC)^{b_b}$ | **Basic COCOMO Effort** | Person-Months (PM) |
| 2 | $D = c_b \times (E)^{d_b}$ | **Basic COCOMO Duration** | Months |
| 3 | $P = E / D$ | **Average Team Size / Productivity** | Persons |
| 4 | $FP = UFP \times VAF$ | **Function Point Conversion** | FP |
| 5 | $VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} (F_i)$ | **Value Adjustment Factor** | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $LOC = FP \times LOC\_per\_FP$ | **Code Size from FP** | Lines of Code |
| 7 | $Cost = E \times \$/PM$ | **Total Project Cost** | Currency |
| 8 | $SV = EV - PV$ | **Schedule Variance** | Units of work |
| 9 | $CV = EV - AC$ | **Cost Variance** | Currency |
| 10 | $SPI = EV / PV$ | **Schedule Performance Index** | Ratio $\geq 1$ good |
| 11 | $CPI = EV / AC$ | **Cost Performance Index** | Ratio $\geq 1$ good |
| 12 | $T_{e} = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$ | **PERT Expected Time** | Time units |
| 13 | $\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2$ | **PERT Variance** | (Time units)$^2$ |

**COCOMO Mode Coefficients Table (Boehm 1981):**

| Project Type | $a_b$ | $b_b$ | $c_b$ | $d_b$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Organic** (small, simple team) | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
| **Semi-Detached** (medium, experienced) | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
| **Embedded** (tight constraints, complex) | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |

## 2.4 Project Scheduling Tools

1. **Gantt Chart:** Bar chart showing tasks vs. time. Best for status reporting.
2. **Program Evaluation Review Technique (PERT):** Three-time estimates (Optimistic $O$, Most Likely $M$, Pessimistic $P$) to handle uncertainty.
3. **Critical Path Method (CPM):** Determines longest path of dependent activities — the *critical path*. Any slip here delays the project.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the IT industry, SPM is applied across every project — from a **3-person startup MVP** to a **300-engineer enterprise system**. Tools like **Jira, Microsoft Project, Asana, and Monday.com** are direct implementations of these theories. Certification bodies like **PMP (PMI)** and **PRINCE2 (AXELOS)** standardize these practices globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Basic COCOMO (KTU Exam Classic)

> **Problem:** A semi-detached software project is estimated at **8 KLOC**. Using Basic COCOMO coefficients, calculate: (a) Effort in Person-Months, (b) Development Time, (c) Average Team Size.

**Given Data:**
- Project type: **Semi-Detached** → from table: $a_b = 3.0$, $b_b = 1.12$, $c_b = 2.5$, $d_b = 0.35$
- Size: $KLOC = 8$

### Step 1: Calculate Effort ($E$)

$$ E = a_b \times (KLOC)^{b_b} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ E = 3.0 \times (8)^{1.12} $$

Now evaluate the exponent term. We use logarithms:

$$ \ln(8) = 2.0794 $$

$$ (8)^{1.12} = e^{1.12 \times 2.0794} = e^{2.3289} \approx 10.266 $$

Therefore:

$$ E = 3.0 \times 10.266 \approx 30.8 \text{ Person-Months} $$

### Step 2: Calculate Development Time ($D$)

$$ D = c_b \times (E)^{d_b} $$

$$ D = 2.5 \times (30.8)^{0.35} $$

Compute the exponent:

$$ \ln(30.8) = 3.4275 $$

$$ 0.35 \times 3.4275 = 1.1996 $$

$$ (30.8)^{0.35} = e^{1.1996} \approx 3.319 $$

$$ D = 2.5 \times 3.319 \approx 8.30 \text{ Months} $$

### Step 3: Calculate Average Team Size ($P$)

$$ P = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{30.8}{8.30} \approx 3.71 \approx 4 \text{ Persons} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $E \approx 30.8$ PM, $D \approx 8.3$ Months, $P \approx 4$ team members.

## 3.2 Worked Example — PERT Expected Time

> **Problem:** For a task: $O = 8$ days, $M = 12$ days, $P = 22$ days. Find expected time $T_e$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

### Step 1: Plug into the PERT formula

$$ T_e = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6} $$

$$ T_e = \frac{8 + 4(12) + 22}{6} $$

$$ T_e = \frac{8 + 48 + 22}{6} = \frac{78}{6} = 13 \text{ days} $$

### Step 2: Compute variance

$$ \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2 $$

$$ \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{22 - 8}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{14}{6}\right)^2 = (2.333)^2 \approx 5.44 \text{ days}^2 $$

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{5.44} \approx 2.33 \text{ days} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The activity is expected to take **13 days**, with a standard deviation of **2.33 days**. So a 1-sigma range is **10.67 to 15.33 days**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Function Point (FP) Calculation

> **Problem:** A system has: External Inputs (EI) = 10, External Outputs (EO) = 7, External Inquiries (EQ) = 4, Internal Logical Files (ILF) = 5, External Interface Files (EIF) = 3. Weights: EI=4, EO=5, EQ=4, ILF=10, EIF=7. All 14 complexity factors are rated average (3 each). Calculate FP, and converted LOC if average 53 LOC/FP.

### Step 1: Compute Unadjusted FP (UFP)

$$
\begin{aligned}
UFP &= (10 \times 4) + (7 \times 5) + (4 \times 4) + (5 \times 10) + (3 \times 7) \\
&= 40 + 35 + 16 + 50 + 21 \\
&= 162
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)

$$ \sum F_i = 14 \times 3 = 42 $$

$$ VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 42 = 0.65 + 0.42 = 1.07 $$

### Step 3: Compute Adjusted FP

$$ FP = UFP \times VAF = 162 \times 1.07 = 173.34 \approx 173 \text{ FP} $$

### Step 4: Convert to LOC

$$ LOC = FP \times 53 = 173 \times 53 = 9169 \text{ LOC} \approx 9.2 \text{ KLOC} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** **173 FP**, equivalent to **9.2 KLOC** for a procedural language like C. For Java (object-oriented), the multiplier is higher (≈ 50–100 LOC/FP depending on style).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Models vs. Risk

| Process Model | When to Use | Risk Level | Customer Involvement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Waterfall | Well-defined, stable requirements | High if changes occur | Mostly at start and end |
| Incremental | Partial functionality delivery first | Medium | At each increment |
| Evolutionary (Prototyping) | Unclear requirements, UI-heavy | Medium-High | Continuous |
| Spiral | High-risk, large-scale projects | Low (explicit risk handling) | Continuous |
| Agile (Scrum/XP) | Volatile requirements, small teams | Low-Medium | Daily/continuous |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 4 P's of Software Project Management — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Software Project Management]:::root --> B[People]:::p
    A --> C[Product]:::p
    A --> D[Process]:::p
    A --> E[Project]:::p

    B --> B1[Stakeholders]
    B --> B2[Team Structure]
    B --> B3[Communication Channels]

    C --> C1[Scope Definition]
    C --> C2[Requirements]
    C --> C3[Constraints]

    D --> D1[Waterfall]
    D --> D2[Incremental]
    D --> D3[Spiral]
    D --> D4[Agile Scrum]

    E --> E1[Planning]
    E --> E2[Estimation LOC FP COCOMO]
    E --> E3[Scheduling Gantt PERT CPM]
    E --> E4[Risk Management]
    E --> E5[Tracking and Control]

    E2 --> E2a[Effort PM]
    E2 --> E2b[Duration Months]
    E2 --> E2c[Team Size]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef p fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
```

## 4.2 WBS and Gantt-style Project Decomposition

```mermaid
gantt
    title Software Project Work Breakdown and Schedule
    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD
    section Phase 1 Planning
    Feasibility Study          :a1, 2025-01-01, 14d
    Requirements Gathering    :a2, after a1, 21d
    section Phase 2 Design
    System Design             :b1, after a2, 28d
    Detailed Design           :b2, after b1, 21d
    section Phase 3 Development
    Coding Module A           :c1, after b2, 30d
    Coding Module B           :c2, after b2, 30d
    Integration               :c3, after c1, 14d
    section Phase 4 Testing and Deployment
    Unit and Integration Test :d1, after c3, 21d
    UAT and Deployment        :d2, after d1, 14d
```

## 4.3 Risk Management Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Risk Identification]:::s --> R2[Risk Analysis]:::s
    R2 --> R3[Risk Planning]:::s
    R3 --> R4[Risk Monitoring]:::s
    R4 --> R1
    R3 --> R3a{Triggered?}
    R3a -->|Yes| R3b[Execute Mitigation]
    R3a -->|No| R4
    R3b --> R4
    classDef s fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

## 4.4 COCOMO Estimation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Requirement: KLOC size and project type]:::in --> S2[Choose COCOMO Mode: Basic / Intermediate / Detailed]:::p
    S2 --> S3[Look up a, b, c, d coefficients]:::p
    S3 --> S4[Compute Effort E = a x KLOC^b]:::p
    S4 --> S5[Compute Duration D = c x E^d]:::p
    S5 --> S6[Compute Team Size P = E / D]:::p
    S6 --> S7[Apply Cost Drivers if Intermediate or Detailed]:::p
    S7 --> S8[Output: PM, Months, Persons, Cost]:::out
    classDef in fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef p fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Nodes prefixed with `phase`, `step`, `task` are safe identifiers in Mermaid. Avoid using reserved words like `end` or `subgraph` as standalone node IDs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the 4 P's of software project management.**

**Model Answer:**
The 4 P's of software project management, as defined by Pressman, are the key variables that the project manager must orchestrate:
1. **People** — The stakeholders, software team members, and end-users involved. Proper team structure and communication are essential.
2. **Product** — The software to be built, defined by its scope, requirements, and constraints.
3. **Process** — The chosen software engineering framework (e.g., Waterfall, Spiral, Agile) that defines the activities, tasks, and order of work.
4. **Project** — All the work needed to build and deliver the software: planning, estimation, scheduling, risk handling, and tracking.

These four variables are interdependent; changing one (e.g., process) directly affects the other three.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is COCOMO? Name and explain its three modes.**

**Model Answer:**
COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model) is an algorithmic software cost estimation model proposed by **Barry Boehm (1981)**. It estimates effort, duration, and cost from the size of the software (in KLOC).
The three modes are:
1. **Basic COCOMO** — Uses a single equation $E = a_b (KLOC)^{b_b}$ based on project type (Organic, Semi-Detached, Embedded). Best for early rough estimates.
2. **Intermediate COCOMO** — Extends Basic COCOMO by multiplying the effort with 15 cost drivers (product, hardware, personnel, project attributes) for better accuracy.
3. **Detailed COCOMO** — Combines Intermediate COCOMO with a phase-based multiplier, applying separate multipliers to each of the five project phases (plan, design, code, test, maintain) for the most accurate estimation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **a)** and **b)**, typically 7 marks each. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Basic COCOMO** estimation model in detail. List the three project types and their coefficients.
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model) is one of the most widely used algorithmic software cost estimation models, developed by **Barry W. Boehm in 1981**. It estimates the effort, development time, and average team size directly from the size of the software expressed in **KLOC (Kilo Lines of Code)**.

The Basic COCOMO model uses two key equations:

- **Effort:** $E = a_b \times (KLOC)^{b_b}$ (in Person-Months)
- **Development Time:** $D = c_b \times (E)^{d_b}$ (in Months)

The constants $a_b$, $b_b$, $c_b$, $d_b$ depend on the project type:

| Project Type | Description | $a_b$ | $b_b$ | $c_b$ | $d_b$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Organic** | Small, in-house teams, simple application | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
| **Semi-Detached** | Medium teams, mixed experience | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
| **Embedded** | Complex, tight hardware/software constraints | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |

Once $E$ and $D$ are known, the average team size $P$ is:

$$ P = \frac{E}{D} $$

**Advantages:** Simple, quick, and useful for early-stage estimates. **Limitations:** It does not account for hardware, personnel skill, or reliability requirements.

**(b)** A project is estimated at **10 KLOC** and is of **semi-detached** type. Using Basic COCOMO, compute **Effort**, **Duration**, and **Average Team Size**.
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $KLOC = 10$; Project type = Semi-Detached.
From the coefficient table: $a_b = 3.0$, $b_b = 1.12$, $c_b = 2.5$, $d_b = 0.35$.

**Step 1: Compute Effort**

$$ E = 3.0 \times (10)^{1.12} $$

$$ (10)^{1.12} = e^{1.12 \times \ln 10} = e^{1.12 \times 2.3026} = e^{2.579} \approx 13.18 $$

$$ E = 3.0 \times 13.18 = 39.55 \text{ Person-Months} $$

**Step 2: Compute Duration**

$$ D = 2.5 \times (39.55)^{0.35} $$

$$ (39.55)^{0.35} = e^{0.35 \times \ln 39.55} = e^{0.35 \times 3.678} = e^{1.287} \approx 3.62 $$

$$ D = 2.5 \times 3.62 = 9.06 \text{ Months} $$

**Step 3: Compute Team Size**

$$ P = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{39.55}{9.06} \approx 4.37 \approx 5 \text{ Persons} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $E \approx 39.55$ PM, $D \approx 9.06$ Months, $P \approx 5$ team members.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **PERT** and **CPM** scheduling techniques. List the formula for PERT expected time and variance.
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) | CPM (Critical Path Method) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Origin | U.S. Navy, Polaris Missile (1958) | DuPont & Remington Rand (1957) |
| Time Estimate | **Probabilistic** — 3 estimates (O, M, P) | **Deterministic** — single time estimate |
| Best For | Research, R&D, high-uncertainty projects | Well-known, repetitive construction-like projects |
| Focus | Optimizing time under uncertainty | Optimizing cost-time trade-off |
| Critical Path | Computed statistically | Computed deterministically |
| Time Equation | $T_e = (O + 4M + P)/6$ | Single value per activity |

**PERT Formulas:**

$$ T_e = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2 $$

**Critical Path** is the longest path in the network; any delay on it delays the entire project.

**(b)** For a project activity, $O = 6$ days, $M = 10$ days, $P = 20$ days. Compute expected time, variance, and standard deviation. Also find the **95% confidence range** ($T_e \pm 2\sigma$).
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Expected Time**

$$ T_e = \frac{6 + 4(10) + 20}{6} = \frac{6 + 40 + 20}{6} = \frac{66}{6} = 11 \text{ days} $$

**Step 2: Variance**

$$ \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{20 - 6}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{14}{6}\right)^2 = (2.333)^2 = 5.444 \text{ days}^2 $$

**Step 3: Standard Deviation**

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{5.444} = 2.333 \text{ days} $$

**Step 4: 95% Confidence Range** ($T_e \pm 2\sigma$)

$$ \text{Lower bound} = 11 - 2(2.333) = 11 - 4.666 = 6.33 \text{ days} $$

$$ \text{Upper bound} = 11 + 2(2.333) = 11 + 4.666 = 15.67 \text{ days} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $T_e = 11$ days, $\sigma^2 \approx 5.44$ days$^2$, $\sigma \approx 2.33$ days, and 95% confidence range is **6.33 to 15.67 days**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Coefficient Confusion:** Many students interchange the $b_b$ and $d_b$ values between COCOMO modes. **Tip:** Memorize all three rows of the coefficient table — they are a guaranteed short-answer ask.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Effort is in **Person-Months (PM)**, NOT hours or weeks. Always write the unit explicitly.
> 3. **Logarithm Mistakes:** When computing $x^y$ in COCOMO, use the natural log: $x^y = e^{y \ln x}$. Failing to show the log step costs full marks.
> 4. **PERT Triple Time:** Students often forget the weight "$4M$" in the numerator. The expected time formula is $\frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$, not $\frac{O + M + P}{3}$.
> 5. **VAF Domain Error:** VAF ranges between **0.65 and 1.35** strictly. A VAF outside this range implies a wrong calculation — recheck your $\sum F_i$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **4 P's of SPM:** People, Product, Process, Project — the pillars of every project.
- **Process Models:** Waterfall, Incremental, Evolutionary, Spiral, Agile — choose based on **risk and requirement stability**.
- **Estimation Hierarchy:** Size (LOC/FP) → Effort (PM) → Duration (months) → Team Size → Cost.
- **Basic COCOMO Equations:** $E = a_b \cdot (KLOC)^{b_b}$ and $D = c_b \cdot (E)^{d_b}$; coefficients vary with project mode.
- **Function Point:** $FP = UFP \times VAF$ where $VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i$, and $\sum F_i$ ranges from 0 to 70.
- **COCOMO Modes:** Basic → Intermediate (adds 15 cost drivers) → Detailed (adds phase multipliers).
- **PERT:** Probabilistic; $T_e = (O + 4M + P)/6$, $\sigma^2 = ((P-O)/6)^2$.
- **CPM:** Deterministic; identifies the **critical path** (longest duration path) which determines the project duration.
- **Risk Management:** Cyclic — Identification → Analysis → Planning → Monitoring. Without it, projects fail.
- **Earned Value Metrics:** $SV = EV - PV$, $CV = EV - AC$, $SPI = EV/PV$, $CPI = EV/AC$. Values $\geq 1$ are favorable.
- **Gantt Charts:** Best for status reporting; PERT/CPM networks are best for planning and slack calculation.
- **CMMI Levels (Bonus):** 1-Initial → 2-Managed → 3-Defined → 4-Quantitatively Managed → 5-Optimizing. KTU often asks short questions on maturity levels.
- **Java Project Linkage:** In your course PEITT411, *Managing Software Development* directly feeds into **Agile Scrum practices** when you build Java projects — sprints, burndown charts, and user stories are direct descendants of these theories.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Topper's Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always (i) state the formula first, (ii) substitute values, (iii) compute intermediate logs, (iv) show the final boxed answer with units. Examiners reward clear steps, not just final numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Java: Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Java: Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object (Java Definition — KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Object** is a runtime instance of a class that encapsulates **state** (data/fields), **behavior** (methods), and **identity** (unique memory address). In Java, every object is created on the **heap memory** using the `new` keyword, possesses a unique identity returned by the `Object.hashCode()` and `Object.equals()` contract, and its lifecycle is managed by the **JVM Garbage Collector**.

In the Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) paradigm, an object is the fundamental building block of any Java application. Unlike procedural programming where data and functions are treated as separate entities, an object binds them together into a single, self-contained unit that mirrors a real-world entity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an Object as a "Real-World Thing on Your Desk."**

Imagine a **Pen** lying on your desk.

| Real-World Attribute | Programming Equivalent (Java) |
|---------------------|------------------------------|
| The Pen's color (Blue) | **State / Field** (e.g., `String color = "Blue"`) |
| The Pen's brand (Reynolds) | **State / Field** (e.g., `String brand`) |
| The Pen's price (₹20) | **State / Field** (e.g., `double price`) |
| The Pen's ability to write | **Behavior / Method** (e.g., `write()`) |
| The Pen's ability to refill | **Behavior / Method** (e.g., `refill()`) |
| The physical uniqueness of THIS pen (vs another pen on the same desk) | **Identity** (unique memory address) |

Two pens of the same brand and color are still **two different objects** — they have the same *state values* but different *identities*. This is the essence of how objects work in Java.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Every Java Object

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU High-Yield Fact: Every Java object inherently possesses three properties — these are guaranteed by the `java.lang.Object` superclass.**

1. **State (Fields / Attributes)**: The data values stored inside the object, represented by **instance variables** (non-static fields). Each object maintains its own copy of state.

2. **Behavior (Methods)**: The actions an object can perform, defined as **instance methods**. Methods can read/modify the object's state and interact with other objects via message passing (method invocation).

3. **Identity**: A unique identifier assigned by the JVM at the time of object creation. No two live objects share the same identity, even if their state is identical. Programmatic access is provided via the inherited methods `Object.hashCode()` and `Object.equals(Object obj)`.

## 1.4 Objects vs. Classes — The Blueprint vs. The Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Class** = Blueprint / Template / Recipe (logical definition, no memory).
> **Object** = Physical instance built from that blueprint (occupies heap memory).

A class like `Pen` describes *what a pen looks like and what it can do*. An object is the *actual pen* you hold. A single class can produce **N number of objects**, each with its own identity and state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Object in Memory

When the JVM executes `new MyClass()`, the following sequence occurs in the **heap memory**:

1. **Class Loading Verification**: The JVM verifies the class is loaded, linked, and initialized.
2. **Memory Allocation**: A contiguous block of heap memory is allocated to hold the object's instance fields (including inherited fields from superclasses).
3. **Default Initialization**: All instance fields are set to their **default values** (numeric → 0, boolean → false, references → null).
4. **Constructor Invocation**: The object's constructor runs, typically performing explicit initialization of fields.
5. **Identity Assignment**: The JVM internally tags the object with a unique identity hash code.
6. **Reference Return**: A `reference` (memory address pointer) to the newly created object is returned and stored in the reference variable on the **stack**.

## 2.2 Object Creation — The `new` Keyword

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rule:** In Java, the `new` keyword is the **only** standard way to invoke a user-defined constructor. It performs three operations in a single atomic step: **(1) allocates heap memory, (2) initializes fields, (3) returns a reference**.

```java
// Reference variable (lives on stack)        // Object (lives on heap)
Pen p1 = new Pen("Blue", "Reynolds", 20.0);
```

The variable `p1` does **NOT** hold the object itself. It holds a **reference** (pointer) to the heap location where the object resides.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Description | Memory Location |
|---------|------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Object Creation | `ClassName ref = new ClassName(args);` | Instantiates a new object on the heap | Object → Heap, Reference → Stack |
| Default Field Values | `int → 0`, `double → 0.0`, `boolean → false`, `Object → null` | All instance fields get defaults before constructor body runs | Heap (inside object) |
| Identity Check (`==`) | `obj1 == obj2` | Compares **reference addresses**, NOT object content | Stack (reference comparison) |
| Equality Check (`.equals()`) | `obj1.equals(obj2)` | Compares **logical/structural content** (overridden in classes) | Depends on implementation |
| Hash Code | `obj.hashCode()` | Returns integer identity hash (used in `HashMap`, `HashSet`) | Heap |
| Object to String | `obj.toString()` | Returns textual representation; default = `ClassName@hexHashCode` | String Pool / Heap |
| Garbage Collection Trigger | `System.gc()` *(hint only)* or object becomes unreachable | Reclaims heap memory of unreferenced objects | Heap |
| `instanceof` Check | `obj instanceof ClassName` | Tests if object is an instance of a class/subclass/interface | Stack (boolean evaluation) |

## 2.4 Why Objects Matter — Engineering Utility

- **Modularity**: Each object is a self-contained module, supporting clean software architecture (MVC, Microservices).
- **Reusability**: Objects of a well-designed class can be reused across multiple programs and projects.
- **Modeling Real Systems**: Object-oriented design directly maps to real-world entities (UML diagrams → Java classes).
- **Encapsulation & Security**: Internal state is hidden; access is mediated via methods, reducing coupling.
- **Distributed Systems**: In RMI, EJB, and REST microservices, objects are serialized and transmitted over the network.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: From Source Code to Live Object

Let us trace the lifecycle of the following Java snippet step-by-step, exactly as the JVM executes it.

```java
class Pen {
    String color;        // instance field
    String brand;        // instance field
    double price;        // instance field

    Pen(String c, String b, double p) {
        color = c;
        brand = b;
        price = p;
        System.out.println("Pen object created with identity: " + this.hashCode());
    }

    void write() {
        System.out.println(brand + " pen of color " + color + " is writing.");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Pen p1 = new Pen("Blue", "Reynolds", 20.0);
        Pen p2 = new Pen("Red", "Cello", 15.0);
        System.out.println("p1 hash: " + p1.hashCode());
        System.out.println("p2 hash: " + p2.hashCode());
        System.out.println("p1 == p2 ? " + (p1 == p2));
        p1.write();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 — Class Loading Phase:**
When `Main.main()` is called, the JVM ClassLoader subsystem loads `Main.class` and then resolves the reference to `Pen.class`. The constant pool is populated, and static fields (none here) would be initialized. Since `Pen` has no static initializer, the class metadata is loaded but no object is created yet.

**Step 2 — First `new Pen("Blue", "Reynolds", 20.0)`:**
The JVM encounters the `new` bytecode instruction. It performs the following sub-operations:
- Allocates a contiguous block of heap memory sufficient to hold three fields: one `String` reference (8 bytes on 64-bit JVM with compressed oops), one more `String` reference (8 bytes), and one `double` (8 bytes) — plus object header (~12-16 bytes).
- Zeros the memory region, applying default values: `color = null`, `brand = null`, `price = 0.0`.
- Invokes the instance initializer and constructor `<init>(String, String, double)`.
- Inside the constructor, the `this` reference is implicitly bound to the newly allocated object. Fields are assigned: `color = "Blue"`, `brand = "Reynolds"`, `price = 20.0`.
- The constructor prints the hash code of the freshly minted object.
- A reference to this object is returned and assigned to the local variable `p1` on the stack frame of `main()`.

**Step 3 — Second `new Pen("Red", "Cello", 15.0)`:**
A **completely separate** heap block is allocated. Same initialization sequence applies. A new identity is assigned, and the reference is stored in `p2`.

**Step 4 — Hash Code Comparison:**
`p1.hashCode()` and `p2.hashCode()` invoke `Object.hashCode()` (the default native/JNI implementation). Since the two objects occupy distinct memory regions, their hash codes are **virtually guaranteed to differ** (per the `Object` contract).

**Step 5 — Reference Comparison `p1 == p2`:**
The `==` operator on object references compares the **stack-stored addresses**, not the object contents. Since `p1` and `p2` point to different heap locations, the result is `false`. This is a critical KTU conceptual distinction.

**Step 6 — Method Invocation `p1.write()`:**
The JVM performs dynamic dispatch: it looks up `write()` in `Pen`'s method table (vtable), binds `this` to the object `p1` refers to, and executes the method. The method reads the instance fields of `p1` and prints: `Reynolds pen of color Blue is writing.`

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Identity, State, and Reference

| Comparison Aspect | `p1` | `p2` | Result |
|------------------|------|------|--------|
| Stack Address (Reference) | `0xABC` | `0xDEF` | Different |
| Heap Address (Object) | `0x100` | `0x200` | Different |
| `color` (State) | `"Blue"` | `"Red"` | Different |
| `brand` (State) | `"Reynolds"` | `"Cello"` | Different |
| `price` (State) | `20.0` | `15.0` | Different |
| `hashCode()` (Identity) | e.g., `123456` | e.g., `789012` | Different |
| `p1 == p2` | — | — | `false` |
| `p1.equals(p2)` | — | — | `false` (default `Object.equals` checks `==`) |

### 3.3 Algebraic-Style Memory Mapping Notation

$$
\text{Stack Frame of } main() =
\begin{cases}
p_1 \rightarrow \text{HeapAddr}(0x100) \rightarrow \text{Object}_{p1}\{color="Blue",\ brand="Reynolds",\ price=20.0\} \\
p_2 \rightarrow \text{HeapAddr}(0x200) \rightarrow \text{Object}_{p2}\{color="Red",\ brand="Cello",\ price=15.0\}
\end{cases}
$$

The arrow $\rightarrow$ denotes a **reference**, not a copy. If we were to execute `Pen p3 = p1;`, then `p3` would point to the **same** `0x100` heap object as `p1`, forming an **alias**. Modifications via `p3` would be visible via `p1`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Object Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ClassDefinition : Source code compiled to .class
    ClassDefinition --> Loaded : ClassLoader loads bytecode
    Loaded --> Linked : Verification, Preparation, Resolution
    Linked --> Initialized : Static initializers run
    Initialized --> Instantiated : new ClassName(args) called
    Instantiated --> Reachable : Reference assigned to variable
    Reachable --> InUse : Method invocation in progress
    InUse --> Reachable : Method returns
    Reachable --> Unreachable : All references go out of scope / set to null
    Unreachable --> Finalized : GC calls finalize() (deprecated since Java 9)
    Finalized --> Collected : Memory reclaimed by Garbage Collector
    Collected --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Memory Architecture: Stack vs Heap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stack["STACK MEMORY (per thread)"]
        A["main() stack frame"]
        B["p1 reference = 0x100"]
        C["p2 reference = 0x200"]
    end

    subgraph Heap["HEAP MEMORY (shared, Garbage Collected)"]
        D["Object Instance 1 at 0x100"]
        E["Object Instance 2 at 0x200"]
    end

    subgraph Meta["METASPACE (class metadata)"]
        F["Pen.class bytecode"]
        G["Method table for Pen"]
    end

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E
    F --> G
    G --> D
    G --> E
```

## 4.3 Object Internal Structure (64-bit HotSpot JVM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Object Header (12-16 bytes)"] --> B["Mark Word (8 bytes): hash, GC age, lock state, thread ID"]
    A --> C["Class Pointer (4-8 bytes): pointer to Pen.class metadata"]
    C --> D["Instance Fields (data section)"]
    D --> E["color : String ref (8 bytes)"]
    D --> F["brand : String ref (8 bytes)"]
    D --> G["price : double (8 bytes)"]
    D --> H["Padding to align to 8-byte boundary"]
```

## 4.4 Message Passing Between Objects

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main()
    participant P1 as Pen Object p1
    participant P2 as Pen Object p2

    Main->>P1: new Pen("Blue", "Reynolds", 20.0)
    Note over P1: Constructor runs, identity assigned
    Main->>P2: new Pen("Red", "Cello", 15.0)
    Note over P2: Constructor runs, separate identity
    Main->>P1: p1.write()
    P1->>P1: this.color = "Blue", this.brand = "Reynolds"
    P1-->>Main: prints "Reynolds pen of color Blue is writing."
    Main->>P1: System.out.println(p1.hashCode())
    P1-->>Main: returns e.g. 123456
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> Define an **object** in Java. List its three essential characteristics with a one-line explanation of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An object in Java is a runtime instance of a class that occupies heap memory and is created using the `new` keyword. It has three essential characteristics:
1. **State** — Represented by instance variables (fields) that hold the object's data values.
2. **Behavior** — Represented by instance methods that define what actions the object can perform.
3. **Identity** — A unique identifier assigned by the JVM, accessible via `Object.hashCode()` and `Object.equals()`.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> Differentiate between **`==` operator** and **`.equals()` method** when applied to objects in Java. Give one example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `==` | `.equals()` |
|--------|------|-------------|
| Compares | Reference (memory address) | Logical content (overridable) |
| Default behavior | Compares addresses | Inherited from `Object`, also compares addresses |
| Overridable? | No (operator, not method) | Yes (recommended override in custom classes) |
| Use case | Check if two refs point to same object | Check if two objects are logically equal |

**Example:**
```java
String a = new String("Hello");
String b = new String("Hello");
System.out.println(a == b);       // false (different references)
System.out.println(a.equals(b));  // true  (same content, String overrides equals)
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of an object in Java with a real-world analogy. Discuss the three characteristics of an object: state, behavior, and identity. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Write a Java program to create a class `Student` with fields `rollNo`, `name`, and `marks`. Create three `Student` objects, initialize them via the constructor, and demonstrate how reference variables, `new` keyword, and the `.equals()` method work. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

An **object** in Java is a software entity that models a real-world thing by bundling together **data (state)** and **functions (behavior)** into a single unit living in heap memory.

*Real-world analogy:* Consider a **Mobile Phone**. The phone's model, color, and battery percentage are its **state**; the ability to make calls, send SMS, and play music are its **behavior**; and the unique IMEI number is its **identity**. Two phones of the same model and color are still two different physical phones (different identities).

In Java, this maps as:
- **State** → instance variables declared in a class.
- **Behavior** → instance methods (non-static) declared in a class.
- **Identity** → unique memory address assigned by the JVM when `new` is executed, accessed via `Object.hashCode()`.

Every object is created using the `new` keyword, which triggers: (1) heap allocation, (2) field default initialization, (3) constructor invocation, and (4) return of a reference. The reference is stored in a stack-local variable, not the object itself.

**(b) Java Program (7 marks):**

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;
    double marks;

    Student(int r, String n, double m) {
        rollNo = r;
        name = n;
        marks = m;
    }

    // Overriding equals() for logical comparison
    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        if (this == obj) return true;
        if (!(obj instanceof Student)) return false;
        Student s = (Student) obj;
        return this.rollNo == s.rollNo;
    }

    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Integer.hashCode(rollNo);
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Roll: " + rollNo + ", Name: " + name + ", Marks: " + marks);
    }
}

public class StudentDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Anu",  89.5);
        Student s2 = new Student(102, "Balu", 76.0);
        Student s3 = new Student(101, "Anu Duplicate", 89.5);

        s1.display();
        s2.display();
        s3.display();

        System.out.println("s1 == s2 : " + (s1 == s2));         // false
        System.out.println("s1 == s3 : " + (s1 == s3));         // false
        System.out.println("s1.equals(s3) : " + s1.equals(s3)); // true (same rollNo)
    }
}
```

**Output Trace:**
```
Roll: 101, Name: Anu, Marks: 89.5
Roll: 102, Name: Balu, Marks: 76.0
Roll: 101, Name: Anu Duplicate, Marks: 89.5
s1 == s2 : false
s1 == s3 : false
s1.equals(s3) : true
```

**Key Explanation:** `s1 == s3` is `false` because they are two distinct heap objects. However, `s1.equals(s3)` is `true` because we overrode `equals()` to compare based on `rollNo` — demonstrating **logical** vs **reference** equality.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the lifecycle of a Java object from creation to garbage collection. Include a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate the difference between a **primitive variable** and an **object reference variable**, including memory layout (stack vs heap), aliasing, and the effect of reassignment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Object Lifecycle (7 marks):**

The lifecycle of a Java object consists of the following phases:

1. **Class Loading** — The `.class` file is loaded by the ClassLoader, verified, linked, and initialized.
2. **Instantiation** — The `new` keyword allocates heap memory for the object. Fields are set to default values.
3. **Initialization** — The constructor runs, performing explicit field assignments and any setup logic.
4. **In Use** — The object is referenced by one or more variables (or by other reachable objects). Methods are invoked on it.
5. **Unreachable** — When all references to the object are removed (variables go out of scope, set to `null`, or overwritten), the object becomes eligible for garbage collection.
6. **Finalization** *(deprecated since Java 9)* — The GC may call `finalize()` before reclaiming memory.
7. **Garbage Collection** — The GC reclaims heap memory. The object ceases to exist.

**Diagram:**
```
Source Code → Compilation → .class → Loading → Linking → Initialization
                                                            ↓
                                                       new ClassName()
                                                            ↓
                                              Heap Allocation + Default Init
                                                            ↓
                                                       Constructor
                                                            ↓
                                                       Reachable
                                                            ↓
                                                      Unreachable
                                                            ↓
                                                   Garbage Collection
```

**(b) Primitive vs Reference Variable Program (7 marks):**

```java
public class MemoryDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 10;             // primitive: stored directly on stack
        int b = a;              // primitive copy: b is a separate copy
        b = 20;
        System.out.println("a = " + a + ", b = " + b); // a=10, b=20

        Pen p1 = new Pen("Blue", 20.0);   // reference: stack holds address
        Pen p2 = p1;                      // ALIAS: p2 points to same heap object
        p2.color = "Red";

        System.out.println("p1.color = " + p1.color); // "Red" (affected by p2!)
        System.out.println("p1 == p2 ? " + (p1 == p2)); // true
    }
}
```

**Memory Layout Explanation:**

| Variable | Type | Stored At | Value |
|----------|------|-----------|-------|
| `a` | `int` (primitive) | Stack | `10` (literal) |
| `b` | `int` (primitive) | Stack | `20` (independent copy) |
| `p1` | `Pen` (reference) | Stack | `0x100` (address of heap object) |
| `p2` | `Pen` (reference) | Stack | `0x100` (same address — alias) |
| Object at `0x100` | `Pen` instance | Heap | `color="Red", price=20.0` |

**Key Insight:** Primitives are copied **by value**; references are copied **by address**. Hence, `p2 = p1` creates an **alias** — modifications via `p2` are visible via `p1`. This is a frequent source of bugs in Java and a common KTU exam trap.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()` (–2 marks):** Writing `s1 == s3` returns `true` because they have the same data is **wrong**. `==` compares references, not content.
> 2. **Stating that variables hold objects (–1 mark):** In Java, **variables hold references** to objects, not the objects themselves. The object lives on the heap.
> 3. **Missing `new` keyword analysis (–1 mark):** Failing to mention that `new` triggers heap allocation + default initialization + constructor invocation loses a mark.
> 4. **No mention of Garbage Collector (–1 mark):** When discussing object lifecycle, KTU expects explicit mention of GC as the mechanism for memory reclamation in Java.
> 5. **Forgetting `this` keyword in constructors (–1 mark):** When parameter names shadow field names, students should use `this.fieldName = parameterName;`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU ESE):**

- ✅ An **object** = **state** (fields) + **behavior** (methods) + **identity** (unique memory address).
- ✅ Objects are created on the **heap** using the `new` keyword; references are stored on the **stack**.
- ✅ The `new` operator performs: **allocation → default initialization → constructor call → return reference**.
- ✅ Instance fields are automatically initialized to defaults: `int→0, double→0.0, boolean→false, Object→null`.
- ✅ `==` compares **references (addresses)**; `.equals()` compares **logical content** (overridable).
- ✅ Every class in Java implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`, inheriting `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `getClass()`, `wait()`, `notify()`, etc.
- ✅ Objects become **eligible for GC** when they are no longer reachable from any live thread.
- ✅ `System.gc()` is only a **hint** to the JVM; it does not guarantee immediate collection.
- ✅ `instanceof` operator tests the runtime type of an object (used to safely downcast).
- ✅ Aliasing: assigning one reference to another (`p2 = p1`) makes both point to the **same** object.
- ✅ `this` keyword refers to the **current object instance** — used to disambiguate fields from parameters.
- ✅ **Constructors** must have the same name as the class and have no return type — they are invoked via `new`.
- ✅ Default constructor (no-arg) is **automatically provided** only if no constructor is explicitly defined.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classes # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Class (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A **class** in Java is a **blueprint**, **template**, or **prototype** from which individual **objects** are created. It is a logical construct that defines the **state (attributes/fields)** and **behavior (methods)** that the objects of that type will possess. A class encapsulates data and the methods that operate on that data into a single, cohesive unit, forming the foundational building block of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Outcome-Based Education, NEP 2020), a class is the primary mechanism for achieving the Course Outcome related to **abstraction, encapsulation, and modular design** in Java. ### Grammar of a Java Class ```java [access_modifier] [non_access_modifier] class ClassName [extends ParentClass] [implements Interface1, Interface2] { // 1. Fields / Attributes / State (Variables) // 2. Constructors // 3. Methods / Behaviors // 4. Inner/Nested Classes } ``` --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" 🏛️ Imagine you are an **architect**. Before a single brick is laid for a building, you create a **blueprint**. The blueprint is *not* a building — you cannot live in it, walk through it, or open its doors. However, it describes *everything* a building will have: - **State** → How many floors, how many rooms, the dimensions. - **Behavior** → How doors open, where elevators go, plumbing flow. Now, every actual building constructed from that blueprint (Building A, Building B, Building C) is an **object**, also called an **instance** of the class. | Concept | Real-World Mapping | Java Equivalent | |---|---|---| | Blueprint | A drawing on paper | `class` declaration | | Building | A real constructed entity | `object` (instance) | | Rooms, Doors | Properties of the building | `fields` (instance variables) | | Functions (open door, ring bell) | Actions the building can perform | `methods` | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A class consumes **zero runtime memory** for its members (other than the static constant pool). Only when an object is instantiated using the `new` keyword does the JVM allocate heap memory. --- ## 1.3 Anatomy of a Class — Member Categories A Java class can contain **six** distinct kinds of members: 1. **Fields (Instance Variables)** — Per-object state stored in heap memory. 2. **Static Fields (Class Variables)** — Single shared state across all objects. 3. **Constructors** — Special methods invoked at object creation for initialization. 4. **Instance Methods** — Behaviors that operate on an individual object's state. 5. **Static Methods** — Behaviors that belong to the class as a whole, not to any specific object. 6. **Nested Classes / Inner Classes** — Classes declared within another class for logical grouping. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 specifically tests the distinction between **instance** vs. **class (static)** members, and the role of the **`this`** reference. Master this distinction — it appears in nearly every Part B question on Classes. --- ## 1.4 The `this` Keyword — Implicit Self-Reference Every instance method and constructor receives an **implicit parameter** named `this` — a reference to the *current* object on which the method is being invoked. It is used to: - Disambiguate between **instance variables** and **local/parameter variables** with identical names. - Pass the current object as an argument to another method. - Invoke another constructor in the same class via `this(...)` (constructor chaining). --- ## 1.5 Access Modifiers — Visibility Contract Java provides four access levels to enforce **encapsulation** at the class boundary: | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff package) | World | |---|---|---|---|---| | `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | *default* (package-private) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | | `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | > [!IMPORTANT] > A class itself can only be declared `public` or *default* (package-private). It cannot be `private` or `protected` at the top level (this is a common KTU viva question). # 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Class Lifecycle — A Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown When you write and execute a Java program, the journey from a `.java` source file to a working class in memory follows a strict sequence. Understanding this sequence is essential for KTU questions on memory allocation and object creation. ### Stage 1: Source Compilation The Java compiler (`javac`) parses `MyClass.java` and produces `MyClass.class` — a file containing **bytecode**, not native machine code. The class file contains: - A **constant pool** (literals, symbolic references). - **Field information** (name, type, modifiers). - **Method information** (bytecode, exception tables). - A reference to the **superclass** (`Object.class` if none specified). ### Stage 2: Class Loading When the JVM encounters `MyClass` for the first time during execution, the **ClassLoader Subsystem** loads the `.class` file into the **Method Area** (a logical part of the heap in modern JVMs). A `Class` object is created in the **heap** to represent this loaded type — this is the famous **"class of a class"** concept. ### Stage 3: Linking & Initialization - **Verify**: Bytecode is checked for safety violations. - **Prepare**: Static variables are allocated and initialized to default values (`0`, `false`, `null`). - **Resolve**: Symbolic references are replaced with direct references. - **Initialize**: Static blocks and static variable initializers run in textual order. ### Stage 4: Object Instantiation When `new MyClass()` executes: 1. Memory is allocated in the **heap** for instance fields. 2. Fields are set to default values. 3. The chosen constructor's body executes (after explicit `this()` or `super()` call resolution). 4. A reference to the newly created object is returned to the caller. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula & Syntax Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Since "Classes" is a language-concept topic (not a numerical engineering topic), the "formula sheet" below is a **Syntax & Semantic Reference Matrix** that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce accurately in theory questions. | Concept | General Form | Notes / Constraints | |---|---|---| | Class Declaration | `class Name { ... }` | PascalCase by convention | | Field Declaration | `[mod] type name [= init];` | Default-initialized if no `=` | | Method Declaration | `[mod] retType name(params) { ... }` | `void` allowed for no return | | Constructor | `[mod] Name(params) { ... }` | **No return type**, name = class name | | `this` reference | `this.field` or `this(...)` | Refers to current object | | `super` reference | `super.field` or `super(...)` | Refers to parent class | | Static member | `static type name;` | One per class, not per object | | Final field | `final type NAME = value;` | Must be initialized exactly once | | Access control | `public / private / protected / default` | Applied to members, not local vars | ### Real-World Engineering Utility of Class-Based Design | Domain | Class Usage | |---|---| | **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | `Sensor`, `Actuator`, `MQTTClient` classes encapsulate hardware abstraction. | | **Banking Software** | `Account`, `Transaction`, `Customer` model real entities with state and behavior. | | **Android Development** | Every `Activity`, `Fragment`, `ViewModel` is a class — Kotlin/Java programs are entirely class-based. | | **Compiler Design** | AST nodes are modeled as classes (`BinaryOpNode`, `VariableNode`). | | **Simulation** | `Particle`, `Vehicle`, `Agent` classes model discrete entities in physics/traffic simulations. | --- ## 2.3 Object Identity, State, and Behavior Every object in Java carries three intrinsic properties: 1. **Identity** — Its memory address, accessed only via the reference variable (no pointer arithmetic in Java). 2. **State** — The current values of its instance fields. 3. **Behavior** — The methods it can execute, defined by its class. > [!TIP] > **KTU Viva Favorite:** *"What is the difference between an object and a class?"* > **Model Answer:** A class is a logical definition (a concept). An object is a physical instance occupying real memory in the heap at runtime. One class can produce infinitely many objects; each object has its own copy of instance fields but shares methods (and static members) with other objects of the same class. --- ## 2.4 Static vs. Instance — The Critical Distinction | Aspect | Instance Member | Static Member | |---|---|---| | Belongs to | Each individual object | The class as a whole | | Memory location | Heap (with object) | Method area / Metaspace | | Access via | `objectRef.member` | `ClassName.member` (preferred) | | Lifecycle | Created on `new`, GC'd when unreachable | Loaded once at class initialization | | `this` accessible? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (compile error) | | Can call instance methods? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (compile error) | | Can call static methods? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Static methods cannot use `this` or `super`** because there is no "current object" in a static context. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU Part A. # 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since "Classes" is a programming concept, the "derivations" below are **exhaustive code walkthroughs** — showing exactly how Java compiles, allocates, and executes class-based programs. Every line is justified. ## 3.1 Worked Example: A `Student` Class — Full Lifecycle Trace ### Source Code ```java public class Student { // 1. Static (class-level) field private static int count = 0; // 2. Instance fields (per-object state) private String name; private int rollNo; private double cgpa; // 3. Constructor — initializes instance state public Student(String name, int rollNo, double cgpa) { this.name = name; // disambiguation: this.name = parameter name this.rollNo = rollNo; this.cgpa = cgpa; count++; // increment shared counter System.out.println("Constructor finished for roll " + rollNo); } // 4. Instance method — operates on object state public void display() { System.out.println("Name: " + this.name + ", Roll: " + rollNo + ", CGPA: " + cgpa); } // 5. Static method — belongs to the class public static int getCount() { return count; // can access static, cannot use 'this' } } ``` ### Driver Class ```java public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1 = new Student("Arjun", 1, 9.2); Student s2 = new Student("Meera", 2, 8.7); s1.display(); s2.display(); System.out.println("Total students: " + Student.getCount()); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Execution Trace **Step 1 — Class Loading** The JVM loads `Main.class` first (entry point). It reads `main(String[] args)` and encounters `Student` for the first time, triggering the load of `Student.class`. The class is placed in the **Method Area** with metadata; `count` is prepared and initialized to its default value `0`, then explicitly set to `0` by the initializer (in this case, no change). **Step 2 — `new Student("Arjun", 1, 9.2)` Execution** $$ \text{Heap Allocation} = \text{sizeof(Student object)} = 8 \text{ (object header)} + \text{refs + primitives} $$ - Memory is allocated in the **Young Generation** of the heap. - All instance fields (`name`, `rollNo`, `cgpa`) are set to **default values** (`null`, `0`, `0.0`). - The constructor body begins executing. - `this.name = name` assigns the passed reference — note that Java copies the **reference** for objects (not the object itself). - `count++` increments the **shared** static field from `0` to `1`. - The reference to the newly constructed object is stored in local variable `s1` (which lives on the **stack frame** of `main`). **Step 3 — `new Student("Meera", 2, 8.7)` Execution** - New heap allocation (a different memory address). - `count` increments to `2` (shared across both objects). - Reference stored in `s2`. **Step 4 — `s1.display()` Call** - JVM looks up `display` in `s1`'s class (`Student`). - `s1` is implicitly passed as the `this` argument. - The method reads `this.name`, `rollNo`, `cgpa` from `s1`'s memory. - Prints: `Name: Arjun, Roll: 1, CGPA: 9.2`. **Step 5 — `Student.getCount()` Call** - Static call — no object needed. - Returns the shared `count` value: `2`. - Prints: `Total students: 2`. **Final Output:** ``` Constructor finished for roll 1 Constructor finished for roll 2 Name: Arjun, Roll: 1, CGPA: 9.2 Name: Meera, Roll: 2, CGPA: 8.7 Total students: 2 ``` --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Memory Layout at Runtime | Location | Contents | Lifetime | |---|---|---| | **Method Area / Metaspace** | `Student.class` metadata, bytecode for methods, `count` (static) | Until JVM shutdown | | **Stack (main frame)** | `args`, `s1`, `s2` (references), `temp` locals | Until `main` returns | | **Heap — Young Gen** | `s1` object (name, rollNo, cgpa), `s2` object | Until GC and unreferenced | | **PC Register** | Address of current bytecode instruction | Per-thread, per-instruction | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the memory diagram for a class program, always show **three regions**: Stack, Heap, and Method Area. Mark `this` inside any instance method as a hidden parameter on the stack frame. --- ## 3.3 Constructor Chaining — The `this()` Mechanism When a constructor calls another constructor of the **same class** via `this(...)`, it is called **constructor chaining**. This avoids code duplication. ```java public class Box { int length, width, height; // Primary constructor public Box(int length, int width, int height) { this.length = length; this.width = width; this.height = height; } // Delegating constructor — calls the primary one public Box(int side) { this(side, side, side); // MUST be the first statement } // Another delegating constructor public Box() { this(1); // delegates → Box(1,1,1) } } ``` **Rules enforced by the compiler:** 1. `this(...)` or `super(...)` must be the **first statement** in a constructor. 2. A constructor can use **either** `this(...)` or `super(...)`, never both. 3. Chaining must terminate at a constructor that invokes `super(...)` (implicit `super()` if none written). > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Writing `this(...)` as the second statement causes: *"Call to 'this()' must be first statement in constructor."* This is a 1-mark deduction trap in KTU. --- ## 3.4 The `final` Keyword Applied to Classes and Members | Applied To | Effect | |---|---| | `final` variable | Value cannot be reassigned after initialization (constant) | | `final` method | Cannot be overridden by subclasses | | `final` class | Cannot be extended (e.g., `String`, `Math` are final) | | `final` parameter | Parameter cannot be reassigned inside the method | > [!NOTE] > In KTU theory questions, you may be asked: *"Why is the `String` class declared `final` in Java?"* The expected answer references **security, immutability, and the String Constant Pool** optimization. # 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Class Anatomy — UML-Inspired Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD classDef titleNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fieldNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#1e3a8a classDef methodNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d classDef ctorNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#78350f classDef staticNode fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#a21caf,color:#581c87 title["CLASS: Student
(Blueprint / Template)"]:::titleNode f1["- name : String
PRIVATE INSTANCE FIELD"]:::fieldNode f2["- rollNo : int
PRIVATE INSTANCE FIELD"]:::fieldNode f3["- cgpa : double
PRIVATE INSTANCE FIELD"]:::fieldNode f4["+ MAX_CGPA : double = 10.0
PUBLIC STATIC FINAL FIELD"]:::staticNode f5["- count : int
PRIVATE STATIC FIELD (shared)"]:::staticNode c1["+ Student(name, rollNo, cgpa)
PUBLIC CONSTRUCTOR"]:::ctorNode m1["+ display() : void
PUBLIC INSTANCE METHOD"]:::methodNode m2["+ getCount() : int
PUBLIC STATIC METHOD"]:::staticNode m3["- isValidCGPA(c) : boolean
PRIVATE INSTANCE METHOD"]:::methodNode title --- f1 title --- f2 title --- f3 title --- f4 title --- f5 title --- c1 title --- m1 title --- m2 title --- m3 ``` **Legend:** - `-` = private, `+` = public - `instance` fields/methods belong to each **object** - `static` fields/methods belong to the **class** (one shared copy) - `final` fields are constants set once --- ## 4.2 Object Instantiation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Code executes: new Student(Arjun, 1, 9.2)"]:::stepA B["JVM allocates memory in HEAP for new object"]:::stepB C["All instance fields set to DEFAULT values
null, 0, 0.0"]:::stepC D["Constructor invoked
'this' parameter points to new object"]:::stepD E["Constructor body executes
this.name = name, etc."]:::stepE F["Static field count incremented
(shared across all objects)"]:::stepF G["Reference returned and stored in s1
(on the stack)"]:::stepG H["Object is now LIVE and accessible via s1"]:::stepH A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H classDef stepA fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d classDef stepB fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#ea580c,color:#7c2d12 classDef stepC fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#713f12 classDef stepD fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#65a30d,color:#365314 classDef stepE fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d classDef stepF fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#10b981,color:#064e3b classDef stepG fill:#a5f3fc,stroke:#0891b2,color:#164e63 classDef stepH fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#2563eb,color:#1e3a8a ``` --- ## 4.3 Static vs Instance Memory Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph MA["METHOD AREA / METASPACE"] C1["Student.class
metadata + bytecode"] SF["count = 2
STATIC FIELD — ONE COPY"] end subgraph H["HEAP MEMORY"] O1["s1 object
name=Arjun
rollNo=1
cgpa=9.2"] O2["s2 object
name=Meera
rollNo=2
cgpa=8.7"] end subgraph ST["STACK (main frame)"] R1["s1 (reference)"] R2["s2 (reference)"] end R1 -.points to.-> O1 R2 -.points to.-> O2 O1 -.shared methods.-> C1 O2 -.shared methods.-> C1 O1 -.reads/writes.-> SF O2 -.reads/writes.-> SF classDef methodArea fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#4c1d95 classDef heapArea fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d classDef stackArea fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d class MA,C1,SF methodArea class H,O1,O2 heapArea class ST,R1,R2 stackArea ``` --- ## 4.4 Class Loading and Initialization Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant SRC as Source Code
(.java) participant JAVAC as Java Compiler participant BYTECODE as .class File
