```mermaid
graph TD
    UMLRoot["UML 2.x 14 Official Diagrams"]
    UMLRoot --> SD["Structure Diagrams Static View 7 Diagrams"]
    UMLRoot --> BD["Behavior Diagrams Dynamic View 7 Diagrams"]

    SD --> S1["Class Diagram"]
    SD --> S2["Object Diagram"]
    SD --> S3["Component Diagram"]
    SD --> S4["Deployment Diagram"]
    SD --> S5["Package Diagram"]
    SD --> S6["Composite Structure Diagram"]
    SD --> S7["Profile Diagram"]

    BD --> BGroup1["Primary Behavior 3 Diagrams"]
    BD --> BGroup2["Interaction Diagrams 4 Diagrams"]

    BGroup1 --> B1["Use Case Diagram"]
    BGroup1 --> B2["Activity Diagram"]
    BGroup1 --> B3["State Machine Diagram"]

    BGroup2 --> I1["Sequence Diagram"]
    BGroup2 --> I2["Communication Diagram"]
    BGroup2 --> I3["Timing Diagram"]
    BGroup2 --> I4["Interaction Overview Diagram"]
```

## 4.2 The 4-Layer Meta-Modeling Architecture

This block-level functional architecture flow illustrates how UML itself is defined recursively.

```mermaid
graph TD
    M3["Layer M3 Meta Metamodel The Element Foundation Defines the rules of UML itself"]
    M2["Layer M2 Metamodel Defines Class Attribute Operation Relationship UML Language Elements"]
    M1["Layer M1 Model User Domain Models Specific Classes like Student Course Faculty"]
    M0["Layer M0 User Objects Runtime Instances Live Objects like alice cs201 in memory"]

    M3 -->|Every Element is an instance of| M2
    M2 -->|Every UML Element is an instance of| M1
    M1 -->|Every Domain Class is an instance of| M0

    M3 -.Example.-> M3ex["Class is an instance of Element"]
    M2 -.Example.-> M2ex["Student is an instance of Class"]
    M1 -.Example.-> M1ex["alice is an instance of Student"]
```

## 4.3 UML Modeling Workflow (Step-by-Step Visual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Requirements Gathering SRS Document"] --> B["Step 2 Identify Actors Primary and Secondary"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Identify Use Cases Verb Plus Noun"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Build Use Case Diagram High Level View"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Structural Modeling Class Identification"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Build Class Diagram Static View"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Behavioral Modeling Sequence Activity State"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Architecture Diagrams Component and Deployment"]
    H --> I["Step 9 Generate Java Source Code Forward Engineering"]
```

## 4.4 Real-World Block Diagram: Java Development Pipeline with UML

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ["Requirement Analysis Actor Use Cases"] --> UC["Use Case Diagram"]
    REQ --> CD["Class Diagram"]
    REQ --> SD["Sequence Diagram"]
    REQ --> ACT["Activity Diagram"]

    UC --> FE["Forward Engineering Tool e.g. StarUML"]
    CD --> FE
    SD --> FE
    ACT --> FE

    FE --> JAVA["Java Source Code"]
    JAVA --> COMPILE["javac Compiler"]
    COMPILE --> BYTECODE[".class Bytecode"]
    BYTECODE --> RE["Reverse Engineering Eclipse Plugin"]
    RE --> UML_BACK["Recovered UML Models"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define UML. Mention its full form and governing body. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Unified Modeling Language (UML)** is a standardized, general-purpose visual modeling language used in software engineering to specify, visualize, construct, and document the artifacts of a software-intensive system.

- **Full Form:** Unified Modeling Language
- **Governing Body:** Object Management Group (OMG)
- **Standard Reference:** ISO/IEC 19505
- UML is *not* a programming language; it is a visual blueprinting language.

### Question 2: List any six diagrams of UML 2.x, classifying them as structure or behavior diagrams. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | Diagram | Category |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class Diagram | Structure |
| 2 | Object Diagram | Structure |
| 3 | Component Diagram | Structure |
| 4 | Use-Case Diagram | Behavior |
| 5 | Sequence Diagram | Behavior (Interaction) |
| 6 | Activity Diagram | Behavior |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern Note:** Both questions carry 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A: Overview of UML and its Role in OOAD**

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the four purposes of UML according to Grady Booch. How do these purposes aid the software development lifecycle?**

**Model Answer:**

According to **Grady Booch**, one of the three principal creators of UML, the language serves four explicit purposes:

1. **Visualizing:**
   A UML diagram converts an abstract, text-heavy specification into an intuitive graphical form. Stakeholders such as clients, project managers, and testers can *see* the system.
   *Example:* A `Customer → Places Order → System` use-case diagram communicates the workflow instantly.

2. **Specifying:**
   UML provides a precise, unambiguous notation. Every arrow, every multiplicity number, every constraint has a defined meaning.
   *Example:* An association `1 — 0..*` mathematically means "one to many".

3. **Constructing:**
   UML models can be **forward-engineered** into Java/C++ source code using tools like *Rational Rose*, *Visual Paradigm*, or *StarUML*. Conversely, existing Java code can be **reverse-engineered** back into UML diagrams.

4. **Documenting:**
   The diagrams and supporting notes serve as a permanent record for maintenance, onboarding new developers, and audit purposes.

**Lifecycle Integration:**
In the **Waterfall Model**, UML is heavily used in the Design and Implementation phases. In **Agile**, lightweight UML diagrams (class + sequence) are used in Sprint planning and story elaboration.

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With a neat diagram, explain the 4-layer architecture of UML. State one example for each layer.**

**Model Answer:**

```
M3 (Meta-Metamodel)   →  Element
         ↑ instance of
M2 (Metamodel)         →  Class, Attribute, Operation
         ↑ instance of
M1 (Model)             →  Student, Course, Faculty
         ↑ instance of
M0 (User Objects)      →  alice (an instance of Student)
```

- **Layer M3 (Meta-Metamodel):** Defines the *rules* of UML itself. The foundational element `Element` lives here. *Example:* The very concept of "Class" is defined in M3.
- **Layer M2 (Metamodel):** Defines the *vocabulary* of UML. *Example:* `Class`, `Attribute`, `Operation`, `Association`.
- **Layer M1 (Model):** User-domain model containing actual classes. *Example:* A class named `Student` with attributes `rollNo`, `name`.
- **Layer M0 (User Objects):** Runtime instances. *Example:* An object `alice` with `rollNo = 101` occupying memory.

The **conformity rule** states: every element in layer $L_n$ is an instance of an element in layer $L_{n+1}$.

---

### **Question B: UML Diagrams and Extensibility**

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List and classify all 14 diagrams of UML 2.x. Draw the conceptual categorization diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

UML 2.x defines **14 official diagrams**, split into two super-categories:

**A. Structure Diagrams (7 — Static View):**
1. Class Diagram
2. Object Diagram
3. Component Diagram
4. Deployment Diagram
5. Package Diagram
6. Composite Structure Diagram
7. Profile Diagram

**B. Behavior Diagrams (7 — Dynamic View):**
1. Use-Case Diagram
2. Activity Diagram
3. State Machine Diagram
4. Sequence Diagram (Interaction)
5. Communication Diagram (Interaction)
6. Timing Diagram (Interaction)
7. Interaction Overview Diagram (Interaction)

**Conceptual Categorization Diagram:**

```
                UML 2.x (14 Diagrams)
                       |
        +--------------+--------------+
        |                             |
 STRUCTURE (7)                  BEHAVIOR (7)
        |                             |
  Class, Object,                Use-Case, Activity,
  Component, Deployment,        State Machine,
  Package, Composite            Sequence,
  Structure, Profile            Communication,
                                Timing, Interaction
                                Overview
```

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What are the extensibility mechanisms of UML? Explain each with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

UML provides three extensibility mechanisms to tailor the language to specific domains:

1. **Stereotypes:**
   Allows extension of UML vocabulary. Represented as `<<stereotype-name>>` above a model element.
   *Example:* `<<interface>>` on a class name indicates the class is a Java interface. Other examples: `<<entity>>`, `<<controller>>`, `<<boundary>>`.

2. **Tagged Values:**
   Adds extra metadata as key-value pairs enclosed in `{}` braces.
   *Example:* `{author = "Booch", version = 1.2, status = "draft"}`.

3. **Constraints:**
   Adds invariants or rules that must be satisfied. Enclosed in `{}` braces.
   *Example:* `{ age >= 18 }` placed beside an attribute to enforce a business rule. Constraints can be expressed in natural language or in the **Object Constraint Language (OCL)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing stereotypes with constraints** — Stereotypes extend *vocabulary*, constraints enforce *rules*.
> 2. **Forgetting the curly braces** for tagged values and constraints (`{ }` are mandatory).
> 3. **Wrong diagram count** — Many students write "9 diagrams" (UML 1.x). Always state **14 diagrams** for UML 2.x (post-2005).
> 4. **Missing the Interaction sub-category** — Sequence, Communication, Timing, and Interaction Overview are *behavior* diagrams, not separate from behavior.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **UML** stands for *Unified Modeling Language*; governed by **OMG**; standardized as **ISO/IEC 19505**.
- **Creators:** Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, Ivar Jacobson ("Three Amigos").
- **First version:** UML 1.0 (Jan 1997); **Current:** UML 2.5.1 (Dec 2017).
- **UML is a visual modeling language, NOT a programming language.**
- **Three pillars of UML:** Things, Relationships, Diagrams.
- **Four purposes (Booch):** Visualizing, Specifying, Constructing, Documenting.
- **14 Diagrams total** = 7 Structure + 7 Behavior.
- **Structure (Static):** Class, Object, Component, Deployment, Package, Composite Structure, Profile.
- **Behavior (Dynamic):** Use Case, Activity, State Machine, Sequence, Communication, Timing, Interaction Overview.
- **4-Layer Architecture:** M3 (Meta-Metamodel) → M2 (Metamodel) → M1 (Model) → M0 (User Objects).
- **Extensibility mechanisms:** Stereotypes (`<< >>`), Tagged Values (`{key=value}`), Constraints (`{rule}`).
- **Mnemonic:** *"Some Calm Creatures Depict Packages, Composing Profiles"* (Structure). *"Use Activity States, See Communication Timing In Order"* (Behavior).
- **Exam Tip:** Always cite the **ISO standard number** for bonus marks in theory questions.
- **Java Connection:** UML class diagram → Java class file (forward engineering); `.class` bytecode → UML (reverse engineering).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modeling Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Modeling" in Software Engineering?

In the context of KTU's Object Oriented Design using Java syllabus, **Modeling** is the disciplined process of creating **simplified, abstract representations of a real-world system** before any code is written. A model captures the **essential features** of a system while ignoring irrelevant detail, allowing engineers to *understand, communicate, and design* complex software in a controlled, visual way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rumbaugh's Object Modeling Technique - OMT):**
> *"Modeling is the process of representing a real-world system (problem domain) using a set of graphical and notational symbols so that we can study its structure, behaviour, and functionality before implementation."*

The three classic models that OMT prescribes are:
1. **Object Model** — captures the **static structure** (classes, objects, relationships, attributes, operations).
2. **Dynamic Model** — captures the **time-dependent behaviour** (states, events, transitions).
3. **Functional Model** — captures the **data transformation** (inputs, outputs, processes).

In modern terms (under UML), these map directly to **Structural, Behavioural, and Interaction diagrams**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint Analogy"

Imagine you are constructing a house. You do not start by laying bricks immediately. Instead, an **architect creates a blueprint**:

| Real World (House) | Software World (Program) |
|---|---|
| Architectural blueprint | Object-Oriented model |
| Walls, doors, windows (entities) | Objects |
| Material specifications | Attributes |
| How a door opens/closes | Operations/Methods |
| Electrical wiring plan | Dynamic model (behaviour) |
| Plumbing flow | Functional model (data flow) |
| Building codes & contracts | Constraints & invariants |

> [!NOTE]
> A model is **NOT the system itself**. It is an *abstraction* — a map, not the territory. Like all maps, it must be **simpler than reality but faithful to its essential nature** (Edward Tufte's principle of minimal truthful information).

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Model? — The KTU Pillars

KTU examiners often ask this as a **3-mark direct question**. Memorize the following four pillars:

1. **Communication** — A model is a common language between analyst, designer, and client. A picture (class diagram) speaks 1000 words.
2. **Abstraction** — Hides complexity; we model only what is relevant to the current problem.
3. **Validation & Verification** — Errors caught on paper cost **100x less** than errors caught in code.
4. **Documentation** — Serves as a living contract for future maintenance and team members.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU board exam, if a question says *"Why do we need object-oriented modeling?"* — always structure your answer using the four pillars above: **Communication, Abstraction, Cost-Reduction, Documentation**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Perspectives of OO Modeling (Rumbaugh's OMT)

Any system can be viewed from three orthogonal perspectives:

$$\text{System Understanding} = f(\text{Object Model}, \text{Dynamic Model}, \text{Functional Model})$$

Where each perspective answers a specific question:
- **Object Model →** *"What are the entities and how are they related?"*
- **Dynamic Model →** *"How does the system change over time?"*
- **Functional Model →** *"What transformations occur and what are the inputs/outputs?"*

This **triple-view** ensures that no aspect of the system is overlooked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of the Object Model

The object model is the **cornerstone of OOA&D**. Its primary constructs are:

### A. Objects
An **object** is a concrete, run-time entity with:
- **Identity** — a unique handle (e.g., object reference in Java).
- **State** — the current values of its attributes.
- **Behaviour** — the operations it can perform.

In Java, every object is an instance of some class, allocated on the **heap** at runtime using `new`.

### B. Classes
A **class** is a *blueprint* that describes a set of objects having common:
- **Attributes** (data members, fields)
- **Operations** (methods, member functions)
- **Relationships** with other classes

A class is essentially a **type** in the type-theoretic sense. In Java:

```java
class Student {
    // Attributes (state)
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    
    // Operations (behaviour)
    public void attendClass() { /* ... */ }
    public double calculateGPA() { return 0.0; }
}
```

### C. Attributes and Operations
- An **Attribute** is a named property of a class (e.g., `name`, `rollNo`).
- An **Operation** is a function or transformation that a class can perform (e.g., `attendClass()`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In UML class diagrams, attributes and operations are declared in two separate compartments. The KTU standard notation is:
> 
> **Visibility Symbol:**
> - `+` (public) — accessible everywhere
> - `-` (private) — accessible only within the class
> - `#` (protected) — accessible in subclasses
> - `~` (package/default) — accessible within the package

### D. Links and Associations
- A **Link** is a physical or conceptual connection between *two object instances*.
- An **Association** is the description of a set of links with common structure and semantics (it lives at the **class level**, not the object level).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**A**ssociation is to **C**lass what **L**ink is to **O**bject."
> Association (class-level) : Link (object-level) :: Class : Object

### E. Multiplicity (Cardinality)
Multiplicity specifies **how many objects participate** in an association. Standard multiplicities used in KTU problems:

| Multiplicity | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one | A car has **1** engine |
| `*` | Zero or many (any number) | A library has **\*** books |
| `1..*` | One or many | A mother has **1..*** children |
| `0..1` | Zero or one (optional) | A citizen has **0..1** passport |
| `n..m` | Between n and m | A team has **5..11** players |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: confusing **multiplicity on one end of an association** vs. **total cardinality**. Read the multiplicity from the *opposite* end's perspective.

### F. Inheritance / Generalization
**Generalization** is the relationship between a *superclass* (parent) and *subclass* (child). The subclass **inherits** attributes and operations, and may *override* operations to provide specialized behaviour.

This is the foundation of:
- **Polymorphism** (one interface, many implementations)
- **Code reuse** (no duplication)
- **Extensibility** (open/closed principle)

### G. Aggregation and Composition
Both are *whole-part* relationships, but they differ in **lifecycle coupling**:

| Feature | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|
| Symbol | Hollow diamond | Filled diamond |
| Lifecycle | Part *can* exist without whole | Part *cannot* exist without whole |
| Ownership | Weak / shared | Strong / exclusive |
| Example | Team ◇ Player | House ◆ Room |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In composition, when the whole is destroyed, the parts are **automatically** destroyed. In aggregation, the parts may continue to exist independently (e.g., if a "Team" is dissolved, "Players" still exist and may join other teams).

### H. Encapsulation
**Encapsulation** is the bundling of data and the methods that operate on that data within a single unit (class), and **restricting direct external access** to some of the object's components.

The principle is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Encapsulation} = \text{Data Hiding} + \text{Interface Exposure}$$

In Java, this is implemented via `private` fields and `public` getters/setters.

### I. Modularity and Hierarchy
A good OO model exhibits:
- **Modularity** — decomposition into well-defined, loosely-coupled modules.
- **Hierarchy** — organization into a tree of abstractions (generalization-specialization).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Construct | Notation (UML) | Java Realization | KTU Key Point |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class | `className` | `class ClassName { }` | Blueprint for objects |
| 2 | Object | `objName : ClassName` | `new ClassName()` | Runtime instance |
| 3 | Public attribute/operation | `+name : Type` | `public` | Accessible everywhere |
| 4 | Private attribute | `-name : Type` | `private` | Hides data (encapsulation) |
| 5 | Protected operation | `#op()` | `protected` | Accessible in subclasses |
| 6 | Inheritance | `───▷` (hollow triangle) | `extends` | "is-a" relationship |
| 7 | Association | `────` | reference variable | "has-a" relationship |
| 8 | Aggregation | `◇────` | weak reference | "whole-part" (shared) |
| 9 | Composition | `◆────` | strong reference | "whole-part" (exclusive) |
| 10 | Multiplicity | `1`, `*`, `n..m` | Array/Collection size | Cardinality on association |
| 11 | Polymorphism | override signature | `@Override` method | Late binding, dynamic dispatch |
| 12 | Abstract class | *italic* name | `abstract class` | Cannot be instantiated |
| 13 | Interface | `«interface»` | `interface` | 100% abstract contract |
| 14 | Dependency | `- - -▷` | local variable use | "uses-a" (temporary) |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Modelling flight control systems as objects (Aircraft → Engines → Sensors) for fault-tolerant design.
- **Banking**: Customer, Account, Transaction modelled as objects with strong encapsulation to ensure security.
- **Healthcare**: Patient, Doctor, Appointment, Prescription — composition used for medical records (destroyed with patient privacy).
- **E-Commerce (Flipkart/Amazon)**: Cart ◇ ProductItem (aggregation, items can be saved for later) vs. Order ◆ OrderLine (composition, lines die with the order).

The **aggregation vs. composition** distinction is one of the most heavily tested KTU concepts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Step Object Modeling Methodology (Rumbaugh's OMT)

When the KTU paper asks *"Explain the steps of object modeling"*, use this **canonical 5-step process**:

### Step 1: Identify the Objects and Classes
**Logic:** Brainstorm all candidate nouns from the problem statement. Apply the **retention test** — keep a noun only if it has *independent existence, distinguishable identity, and relevant behaviour*.

> *Example (Library System problem):* Candidate nouns → `Book`, `Member`, `Librarian`, `Library`, `Shelf`, `Loan`, `Fine`, `Author`, `Publisher`.

**Filter using retention criteria:**
- `Book` ✓ (independent existence, identity via ISBN, behaviour `issue()`, `return()`)
- `Librarian` ✓ (role with behaviour)
- `Library` ✓ (the whole system as a top-level container)
- `Shelf` ✗ (typically modelled as an attribute of `Library` or simple container)
- `Publisher` ✓ (separate entity with identity)

### Step 2: Identify the Attributes
**Logic:** For each retained class, identify the descriptive data. Apply the **scope test** (does it apply to the current problem?) and the **default value test** (can we reasonably initialize it?).

For the `Book` class:
- `title : String`
- `ISBN : String`  *(acts as identity)*
- `author : String`
- `price : double`
- `availableCopies : int`

### Step 3: Identify the Operations
**Logic:** For each class, list verbs that act on the class's data. Distinguish:
- **Operations on a single object** (instance methods): `issueBook()`, `returnBook()`.
- **Operations on a class as a whole** (class/static methods in Java): `getTotalBooks()`.

For the `Book` class:
- `void issueBook()` — decrements availableCopies
- `void returnBook()` — increments availableCopies
- `boolean isAvailable()` — checks if copies > 0

### Step 4: Identify the Relationships (Associations)
**Logic:** Look for verbs that imply a *connection* between two nouns: *"has-a"*, *"owns"*, *"is part of"*, *"is a member of"*.

From the Library problem:
- `Member` *borrows* `Book` → **Association** with multiplicity `*` to `*`
- `Library` *contains* `Book` → **Aggregation** (Library ◇ Book) — books exist even if library entry is removed
- `Book` *is written by* `Author` → **Association** with multiplicity `*` to `1..*`

### Step 5: Apply Inheritance (Generalization)
**Logic:** Look for *"is-a"* relationships or common structure. Group similar classes under a superclass.

From the Library example:
- `TextBook`, `ReferenceBook`, `Magazine` are all *kinds of* `LibraryItem` → generalize into `LibraryItem` superclass.

The general formula for the modeling process is:

$$M_{object} = \{C, A, O, R, H\}$$

Where:
- $C$ = set of **C**lasses
- $A$ = set of **A**ttributes
- $O$ = set of **O**perations
- $R$ = set of **R**elationships (associations, aggregations, compositions)
- $H$ = **H**ierarchy of generalization

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: ATM System Modeling

> **Problem Statement:** *"Model the basic structure of an ATM system that allows customers to withdraw, deposit, and check balance using a debit card."*

### Step 1 — Classes Identification

After applying the retention test, we obtain the following candidate class set:

$$C_{ATM} = \{\text{Customer}, \text{Account}, \text{ATM}, \text{Card}, \text{Transaction}\}$$

### Step 2 — Attributes Definition

For each class, we identify the data members. In the **KCU UML notation**:

```
Account
----------------
- accountNumber : String
- balance : double
- accountType : String
----------------
+ deposit(amount : double) : void
+ withdraw(amount : double) : boolean
+ getBalance() : double
```

### Step 3 — Operations Definition

We classify operations as:

$$\text{Operations} = \text{InstanceOps} \cup \text{ClassOps}$$

For `Account`, the set is:

$$O_{Account} = \{\text{deposit}, \text{withdraw}, \text{getBalance}\} \subseteq \text{InstanceOps}$$

### Step 4 — Relationships Derivation

| Source Class | Relationship | Target Class | Multiplicity | Symbol in UML |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Customer | owns | Account | 1 to 1..* | solid line |
| Customer | uses | ATM | 1 to 1 | association |
| ATM | performs | Transaction | 1 to * | composition (◆) |
| Transaction | affects | Account | * to 1 | association |
| Account | accessed via | Card | 1 to 1 | association |

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between `ATM` and `Transaction` is **Composition**, not Aggregation, because a Transaction is meaningless without the ATM context — when the ATM session ends, the Transaction record is finalized and stored, not transferred to another ATM.

### Step 5 — Inheritance

We could generalize:

$$
\text{SavingAccount} \rightarrow \text{Account} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{CurrentAccount} \rightarrow \text{Account}
$$

This is the **"is-a"** relationship expressed mathematically as:

$$x \in \text{SavingAccount} \implies x \in \text{Account}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How KTU Asks Modeling Questions

| Question Pattern | Expected Modeling Element | Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|
| "Identify classes/objects" | List of nouns + retention test | 3-4 |
| "Draw class diagram" | UML with attributes, operations, relationships | 7-10 |
| "Distinguish X and Y" | Comparison table (e.g., aggregation vs composition) | 3-5 |
| "Explain modeling technique" | 5-step OMT process | 5-7 |
| "Identify relationships" | Multiplicities + relationship types | 3-5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 5-Step Object Modeling Workflow (Functional Architecture)

This block-level flow diagram captures the *exact* Rumbaugh OMT process that KTU expects students to write in the 14-mark modeling questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Identify Classes and Objects] --> B[STEP 2: Identify Attributes]
    B --> C[STEP 3: Identify Operations]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Identify Relationships]
    D --> E[STEP 5: Apply Inheritance and Refine]
    E --> F{Final Object Model Valid?}
    F -- No --> A
    F -- Yes --> G[Output: Class Diagram plus Multiplicities]
    
    step1Note[Apply Retention Test, Identity Test] -.-> A
    step2Note[Apply Scope Test, Default Value Test] -.-> B
    step3Note[Look for Verbs acting on Nouns] -.-> C
    step4Note[Multiplicities, Aggregation versus Composition] -.-> D
    step5Note[Refactor Common Structure, Apply Is-A] -.-> E
```

> [!TIP]
> The **"refinement loop"** in this diagram (the dashed line back to Step 1) reflects the *iterative* nature of modeling — you almost never get it right the first time. KTU examiners reward this acknowledgment.

---

## 4.2 UML Class Diagram — Library Management System (KTU Sample)

This is the **exact style** of diagram you should be able to reproduce for 7-10 mark questions.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class LibraryItem {
        -itemID : String
        -title : String
        -price : double
        +getTitle() : String
        +getPrice() : double
    }
    
    class Book {
        -ISBN : String
        -author : String
        -availableCopies : int
        +issueBook() : boolean
        +returnBook() : void
    }
    
    class Magazine {
        -issueNumber : int
        -month : String
    }
    
    class Member {
        -memberID : String
        -name : String
        -phone : String
        +register() : void
    }
    
    class Loan {
        -loanID : String
        -loanDate : Date
        -returnDate : Date
        +calculateFine() : double
    }
    
    LibraryItem <|-- Book : Inheritance
    LibraryItem <|-- Magazine : Inheritance
    Member "1" --> "*" Loan : borrows
    Book "1" --> "*" Loan : is part of
    Library "1" o--> "*" LibraryItem : aggregation contains
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the diagram encodes **all three** modeling concepts simultaneously:
> - **Inheritance** (`LibraryItem <|-- Book`): the "is-a" hierarchy
> - **Aggregation** (`Library o--> LibraryItem`): the "weak whole-part" relationship
> - **Association** (`Member --> Loan`): the "uses-a" relationship with multiplicities

---

## 4.3 Object Model — Three Perspectives (OMT Tri-View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYS[Real World System]
        PROB[Problem Domain]
    end
    
    subgraph MOD[Three Models]
        OM[OBJECT MODEL\nStatic Structure\nClasses, Attributes\nRelationships]
        DM[DYNAMIC MODEL\nTime Behaviour\nStates, Events\nTransitions]
        FM[FUNCTIONAL MODEL\nData Transformation\nInputs, Outputs\nProcesses]
    end
    
    subgraph OUT[Deliverables]
        CD[Class Diagram]
        STD[State Diagram]
        DFD[Data Flow Diagram]
    end
    
    PROB --> OM
    PROB --> DM
    PROB --> FM
    
    OM --> CD
    DM --> STD
    FM --> DFD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **tri-view** is the heart of OMT. KTU board questions on "Explain object modeling methodology" almost always award marks for explicitly mentioning all three models and their corresponding UML diagrams.

---

## 4.4 Encapsulation Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTSIDE[External World]
        CLIENT[Client Code]
    end
    
    subgraph CLASS[Object Instance]
        subgraph PRIV[Private Data Section]
            FIELD1[attribute1]
            FIELD2[attribute2]
        end
        subgraph PUB[Public Interface Section]
            METHOD1[getAttribute1]
            METHOD2[setAttribute1]
            METHOD3[operation]
        end
    end
    
    CLIENT -- Access via Public Methods Only --> METHOD1
    CLIENT -- Cannot Access Directly --> PRIV
    
    METHOD1 -. Reads .-> FIELD1
    METHOD2 -. Writes .-> FIELD1
    METHOD3 -. Uses .-> FIELD2
```

The **dashed boundary** between "External World" and "Class" represents the encapsulation barrier — the only legal path for data flow is *through* the public method interface.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is object modeling? List any four advantages of object modeling."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Object modeling* is the process of representing a real-world system by identifying the classes, objects, attributes, operations, and relationships that constitute it, typically using UML notation.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Improved communication** — visual diagrams provide a shared language among developers, designers, and clients.
2. **Reduced development cost** — errors caught at the modeling stage are 10–100x cheaper to fix than at the coding stage.
3. **Better reusability** — well-modeled classes with clear inheritance hierarchies enable code reuse across projects.
4. **Easier maintenance** — encapsulated, modular models are easier to understand, modify, and extend over time.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**"Distinguish between aggregation and composition with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|
| Symbol | Hollow diamond | Filled diamond |
| Strength | Weak (shared) whole-part | Strong (exclusive) whole-part |
| Lifecycle | Part can outlive the whole | Part dies with the whole |
| Example | Team ◇ Player (Player continues after team dissolves) | House ◆ Room (Room cannot exist without house) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> *"For a University Course Registration System, perform object modeling and draw the class diagram. Identify classes, attributes, operations, and relationships clearly."*

#### (a) Identify the classes, attributes, and operations (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Classes Identified** *(apply retention test)*:
`Student`, `Course`, `Professor`, `Department`, `Registration`, `Grade`, `Schedule`

**Step 2 — Attributes for each class:**

| Class | Attributes |
|---|---|
| Student | studentID, name, email, semester |
| Course | courseCode, courseName, credits, maxCapacity |
| Professor | profID, name, department, specialization |
| Department | deptID, deptName, hod |
| Registration | regID, regDate, status |
| Grade | gradeID, marks, letterGrade |

**Step 3 — Operations for each class:**
- `Student`: `registerCourse()`, `dropCourse()`, `viewGrades()`
- `Course`: `addStudent()`, `removeStudent()`, `isFull()`
- `Professor`: `assignGrade()`, `viewCourses()`
- `Registration`: `confirm()`, `cancel()`

#### (b) Identify relationships and draw the class diagram (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Relationships identified:**

| Source | Relationship | Target | Multiplicity | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Student | registers for | Course | * to * | Association |
| Professor | teaches | Course | 1 to * | Association |
| Department | offers | Course | 1 to * | Aggregation |
| Student | creates | Registration | 1 to * | Composition |
| Course | results in | Grade | 1 to 1..* | Association |
| Registration | belongs to | Student | * to 1 | Association |

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student {
        -studentID : String
        -name : String
        -email : String
        +registerCourse() : void
        +viewGrades() : void
    }
    
    class Course {
        -courseCode : String
        -courseName : String
        -credits : int
        -maxCapacity : int
        +isFull() : boolean
    }
    
    class Professor {
        -profID : String
        -name : String
        +assignGrade() : void
    }
    
    class Department {
        -deptID : String
        -deptName : String
    }
    
    class Registration {
        -regID : String
        -regDate : Date
        -status : String
        +confirm() : void
    }
    
    Department "1" o--> "*" Course : offers
    Professor "1" --> "*" Course : teaches
    Student "*" --> "*" Course : registers
    Student "1" *--> "*" Registration : creates
    Course "1" --> "*" Grade : results in
```

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> *"Explain the five-step methodology of object modeling in detail. Illustrate each step with reference to an 'Online Shopping System'."*

#### (a) Explain the five steps with reference to an Online Shopping System (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Classes and Objects**
A class is a description of a set of objects sharing the same attributes, operations, and relationships. Applying the **retention test** to nouns in the problem statement for an Online Shopping System yields:

$$C_{OS} = \{\text{Customer}, \text{Product}, \text{Cart}, \text{Order}, \text{Payment}, \text{Shipment}, \text{Category}\}$$

**Step 2: Identify the Attributes**
For each class, we identify its data. The **scope test** ensures we keep only relevant attributes:

| Class | Attributes |
|---|---|
| Customer | customerID, name, email, address |
| Product | productID, name, price, stock |
| Cart | cartID, totalAmount |
| Order | orderID, orderDate, status |

**Step 3: Identify the Operations**
Operations are the verbs acting on the classes. For `Cart`: `addItem()`, `removeItem()`, `calculateTotal()`. For `Order`: `placeOrder()`, `cancelOrder()`, `trackStatus()`.

**Step 4: Identify the Relationships**
- `Customer` *owns* a `Cart` — **Composition** (`1` to `1` — when the customer's session ends, the cart is destroyed).
- `Cart` *contains* `Product` — **Aggregation** (`*` to `*` — products remain even after removal from cart).
- `Order` *contains* `Product` — **Composition** (`1` to `*`).

**Step 5: Apply Inheritance (Refinement)**
Group `ElectronicItem`, `ClothingItem`, `BookItem` under a common superclass `Product`. This applies the **"is-a"** test.

#### (b) Explain the three perspectives of object modeling and their inter-relationship (7 marks)

**Solution:**

Rumbaugh's OMT methodology states that any system can be understood from three complementary perspectives:

**1. Object Model** — *What is the static structure?*
- Represents classes, objects, attributes, operations, and relationships.
- Implemented in UML as a **Class Diagram**.
- Answers: *"What are the entities and how are they related?"*

**2. Dynamic Model** — *How does the system change over time?*
- Represents states of objects, events that cause transitions, and the resulting actions.
- Implemented as a **State Diagram**.
- Answers: *"What is the lifecycle of an object?"*

**3. Functional Model** — *What transformations occur?*
- Represents the data flow: inputs → processes → outputs.
- Implemented as a **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)**.
- Answers: *"What does the system do, function-wise?"*

The inter-relationship is captured by:

$$\text{Complete System View} = \text{Object Model} \cap \text{Dynamic Model} \cap \text{Functional Model}$$

That is, **all three views are necessary and complementary**. The Object Model identifies *what* the system is made of, the Dynamic Model shows *when* things happen, and the Functional Model explains *how* data is transformed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** skip the *retention/scope tests* in Step 1 and Step 2. Examiners award **1 mark** specifically for naming these tests.
> - Always mention **multiplicity** on both ends of an association. Forgetting multiplicity loses **1 mark** per association.
> - In composition, the **diamond is placed on the WHOLE side**, not the part side. Drawing it backwards is a common 1-mark deduction.
> - In inheritance arrows, the **triangle points TOWARD the parent class**, not the child. This is a frequently reversed notation in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Modeling Concepts":**

- ☐ **Modeling = Abstraction + Visualization** before any code is written.
- ☐ **OMT's 5 Steps**: (1) Classes/Objects, (2) Attributes, (3) Operations, (4) Relationships, (5) Inheritance refinement.
- ☐ **Three Tests**: Retention (class), Scope (attribute), Default Value (attribute).
- ☐ **Three Models**: Object (static), Dynamic (states), Functional (DFD).
- ☐ **Object = Identity + State + Behaviour** — memorize this triplet.
- ☐ **Class vs. Object**: Class is the *type*; Object is the *instance*.
- ☐ **UML Visibility Symbols**: `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- ☐ **UML Relationship Symbols**:
    - `───▷` Inheritance (hollow triangle, points to parent)
    - `────` Association
    - `◇────` Aggregation (hollow diamond, on whole side)
    - `◆────` Composition (filled diamond, on whole side)
    - `- - -▷` Dependency (dashed arrow)
- ☐ **Aggregation vs. Composition**: lifecycle coupling is the differentiator.
- ☐ **Encapsulation** = Data Hiding + Public Interface (use `private` fields + `public` getters/setters in Java).
- ☐ **Multiplicity notation**: `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`, `n..m`.
- ☐ **Inheritance** uses `extends` in Java; **Interface realization** uses `implements`.
- ☐ **Polymorphism** allows the same interface to invoke different implementations at runtime.
- ☐ **Generalization** organizes classes by *is-a*; **Aggregation/Composition** organize by *has-a/whole-part*.
- ☐ **Real-world examples to remember**: Team ◇ Player (aggregation), House ◆ Room (composition), Car is-a Vehicle (inheritance), Library has-a Book (aggregation).
- ☐ **Common KTU marks-losers**: forgetting multiplicity, drawing inheritance arrows backwards, mixing up aggregation/composition, missing the dynamic/functional model when asked about OMT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Case Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use Case Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Use Case Diagram** is a behavioral diagram in the **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** that belongs to the family of structural-behavioral hybrid diagrams. It captures the *functional requirements* of a system by modeling the external actors interacting with the system and the *use cases* (goals or services) the system provides in response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> *A use case diagram is a UML behavioral diagram that describes the set of actions performed by a system to yield an observable result of value to a particular actor. It depicts the system's functional requirements from an external observer's perspective, identifying who interacts with the system (actors) and what they want to achieve (use cases).*

The diagram is part of the **UML 2.5 specification** governed by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**, the standards body responsible for UML.

| UML Diagram Type | Purpose | Category |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Use Case Diagram | Captures *what* the system does | Behavioral (Requirement Level) |
| Class Diagram | Captures *what* the system is | Structural |
| Sequence Diagram | Captures *how* objects interact over time | Behavioral (Interaction) |
| State Chart Diagram | Captures *how* an object changes state | Behavioral (State-based) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ Restaurant Analogy (Beginner-Friendly Intuition)**
>
> Imagine a **restaurant** as a system you are designing:
> - The **Customers** and **Delivery Person** are *actors* (people outside the system who interact with it).
> - The services offered: *Place Order*, *Pay Bill*, *Reserve Table*, *Cancel Order* are *use cases* (each represents a complete goal of value to an actor).
> - The lines connecting Customers to "Place Order" show that the Customer can request that service.
> - The system boundary (a big rectangle) represents the restaurant itself — everything *inside* is the system, everything *outside* is the actor.
>
> A Use Case Diagram is essentially the **"menu" of services** a system offers, along with **"who can order what"** from that menu.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A Use Case Diagram is *not* about *how* the system works internally. It is a **black-box** view — it shows the boundary between the system and the outside world, and the meaningful interactions at that boundary. Internal details (classes, methods, algorithms) are addressed by *other* UML diagrams (Class, Sequence, Activity).

## 1.3 Why Use Case Diagrams Matter in KTU Examinations

1. They are the **first diagram** drawn during the requirements phase of any OOAD project.
2. They directly map to **Course Outcome 1 (CO1)** in the KTU 2024 scheme: *"Apply the concepts of object-oriented programming and design using UML."*
3. They carry high weightage in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.
4. They test the student's ability to **identify actors, use cases, and relationships** from a given problem statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Four Building Blocks of a Use Case Diagram

Every Use Case Diagram is composed of exactly **four elements** (KTU frequently tests identification of these):

1. **Actors** — Represent roles played by users or external systems.
2. **Use Cases** — Represent goals or services the system provides.
3. **System Boundary** — A rectangle that defines the scope of the system.
4. **Relationships** — Lines and arrows connecting the above elements.

### 2.1.1 Actors

- An **actor** is an external entity (human, hardware, or another software system) that interacts with the system being modeled.
- Represented as a **stick figure** in UML.
- An actor has a **role**, not a specific individual. The same person playing two different roles is treated as two different actors.
- Actors are classified into:

| Actor Type | Symbol | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Actor** | Stick figure | Customer placing an order |
| **Secondary Actor** | Stick figure | Bank verifying payment |
| **Human Actor** | Stick figure | Librarian |
| **Non-Human Actor** | Stick figure with `<<system>>` | Payment Gateway |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Rule:** Actors always lie *outside* the system boundary. They are *not part of* the system being designed.

### 2.1.2 Use Cases

- A **use case** is a set of actions performed by the system to yield an *observable result of value* to a particular actor.
- Represented as a **horizontal ellipse** with the use case name inside.
- Each use case should have a **verb-noun** name (e.g., *Place Order*, *Withdraw Cash*, *Login User*).
- A use case is initiated by an actor to fulfill a goal.

> [!TIP]
> **Naming Convention Rule (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> Use case names must always start with a **strong verb** in the active voice. Avoid vague names like "Order" — prefer "Place Order", "Cancel Order", "Track Order".

### 2.1.3 System Boundary

- A **rectangle** that encloses all the use cases.
- The name of the system is written *inside* or *above* the rectangle.
- It distinguishes the *system* (inside) from the *external environment* (outside, where actors live).

### 2.1.4 Relationships

The four relationship types are the **most tested** aspect of Use Case Diagrams in KTU exams.

| Relationship | UML Notation | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Association** | Plain solid line | Connects an actor to a use case it participates in |
| **Include** | Dashed arrow with `<<include>>` | A use case *mandatorily* uses another use case's behavior |
| **Extend** | Dashed arrow with `<<extend>>` | A use case *optionally* extends another with extra behavior |
| **Generalization** | Solid line with hollow triangle arrowhead | An actor or use case inherits from a parent |

## 2.2 Detailed Semantics of Each Relationship

### 2.2.1 Association

$$
\text{Actor} \xrightarrow{\text{————}} \text{Use Case}
$$

- The simplest relationship. It means the actor *participates* in the use case.
- Direction: not specifically directional; either end can initiate.

### 2.2.2 Include Relationship (Functional Decomposition)

$$
\text{Base Use Case} \xleftarrow{\text{----- <<include>> -----}} \text{Included Use Case}
$$

- **Mandatory reuse:** The base use case *always* invokes the included use case.
- Used to **eliminate duplication** when two or more use cases share common behavior.
- The arrow points **from the base use case to the included use case**.
- Example: *Place Order* includes *Validate Payment* — every order must have payment validation.

### 2.2.3 Extend Relationship (Optional / Conditional Behavior)

$$
\text{Extending Use Case} \xrightarrow{\text{----- <<extend>> -----}} \text{Base Use Case}
$$

- **Optional behavior:** The base use case can stand on its own; the extending use case adds extra behavior *only under certain conditions*.
- The arrow points **from the extending use case to the base use case** (opposite direction to `<<include>>`).
- Example: *Place Order* is extended by *Apply Discount* — the discount is applied only if certain conditions are met.

### 2.2.4 Generalization

$$
\text{Child} \xrightarrow{\text{—————$\triangleright$—————}} \text{Parent}
$$

- An **inheritance-like** relationship.
- The child use case inherits the behavior of the parent and can override it.
- Example: *Online Customer* and *Walk-in Customer* both generalize from *Customer*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall — Include vs. Extend Direction:**
> Many students draw the `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` arrows in the wrong direction. Remember the mnemonic:
> - **INCLUDE** arrow points *toward* the **reused** (factored-out) use case.
> - **EXTEND** arrow points *toward* the **base** (extended) use case.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Element | Notation | Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Actor | Stick figure | Must lie **outside** system boundary |
| Use Case | Horizontal ellipse | **Verb-noun** name inside |
| System Boundary | Rectangle | Encloses all use cases only |
| Association | Solid line | Actor $\leftrightarrow$ Use Case |
| Include | Dashed + `<<include>>` | Base $\rightarrow$ Reused |
| Extend | Dashed + `<<extend>>` | Extending $\rightarrow$ Base |
| Generalization | Solid + hollow triangle | Child $\rightarrow$ Parent |
| Primary Actor | Right-side stick figure | Initiates the use case |
| Secondary Actor | Left-side stick figure | Provides a service to the system |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> Use Case Diagrams are the **first deliverable** in industry-grade Requirements Engineering. They serve as the contract between developers and stakeholders, drive test case generation, and form the basis for estimating project scope. Tools like **Rational Rose, StarUML, Lucidchart, Visual Paradigm, and Enterprise Architect** are used to author them in professional settings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Example

## 3.1 The Six-Step Methodology for Constructing a Use Case Diagram

The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks students to *"Draw the use case diagram for [a given problem]."* Follow this exact six-step methodology for full marks:

**Step 1 — Identify the System Boundary.**
Read the problem statement and determine the *name* and *scope* of the system. Draw the boundary rectangle first and label it.

**Step 2 — Identify the Actors.**
Ask: *"Who or what interacts with the system from outside?"* List every primary actor (initiates a goal) and every secondary actor (provides a service).

**Step 3 — Identify the Use Cases.**
For each actor, ask: *"What goals does this actor want to achieve using the system?"* Each goal becomes a use case.

**Step 4 — Name the Use Cases Properly.**
Use **verb-noun** phrasing. Each name should be short, clear, and represent a complete unit of value.

**Step 5 — Draw the Associations.**
Connect every actor to every use case in which it participates, using solid lines.

**Step 6 — Identify Special Relationships (Include / Extend / Generalization).**
Look for:
- Common sub-behaviors across multiple use cases $\rightarrow$ use `<<include>>`
- Optional or conditional behavior $\rightarrow$ use `<<extend>>`
- Specializations of actors or use cases $\rightarrow$ use **Generalization**

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: ATM System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Model KTU Question Pattern):**
> *"Design a use case diagram for an Automated Teller Machine (ATM) system. The system allows a Customer to withdraw cash, check balance, deposit cash, and transfer funds. The Bank validates every transaction. A Maintenance Officer refills the ATM. The Customer must authenticate before any transaction."*

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1 — System Boundary:**
The system is the **ATM System**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Actors:**

$$
\text{Actors} = \{\text{Customer},\ \text{Bank},\ \text{Maintenance Officer}\}
$$

- **Customer** — Primary actor, initiates transactions.
- **Bank** — Secondary actor, validates transactions.
- **Maintenance Officer** — Primary actor, performs refill operations.

**Step 3 — Identify the Use Cases:**

$$
\text{UseCases} = \{\text{Authenticate User},\ \text{Withdraw Cash},\ \text{Check Balance},\ \text{Deposit Cash},\ \text{Transfer Funds},\ \text{Refill Cash},\ \text{Print Receipt}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Name Standardization (verb-noun check):**

| Tentative Name | Standardized Name | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Balance | Check Balance | Adds verb |
| Withdraw | Withdraw Cash | Adds noun |
| Refill | Refill ATM Cash | Adds full context |

**Step 5 — Association Lines:**

$$
\text{Customer} \longleftrightarrow \text{Withdraw Cash},\ \text{Check Balance},\ \text{Deposit Cash},\ \text{Transfer Funds},\ \text{Authenticate User}
$$

$$
\text{Bank} \longleftrightarrow \text{Validate Transaction}
$$

$$
\text{Maintenance Officer} \longleftrightarrow \text{Refill ATM Cash}
$$

**Step 6 — Special Relationships:**

- **Common sub-behavior:** Every transaction (Withdraw, Deposit, Transfer) must authenticate the user. So:
$$\text{Withdraw Cash} \xleftarrow{\text{<<include>>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$
$$\text{Deposit Cash} \xleftarrow{\text{<<include>>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$
$$\text{Transfer Funds} \xleftarrow{\text{<<include>>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$
$$\text{Check Balance} \xleftarrow{\text{<<include>>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$

- **Optional behavior:** Printing a receipt is *optional* after a withdrawal. So:
$$\text{Print Receipt} \xrightarrow{\text{<<extend>>}} \text{Withdraw Cash}$$

- **Common transaction validation:** All transactions must be validated by the Bank. So:
$$\text{Withdraw Cash} \xleftarrow{\text{<<include>>}} \text{Validate Transaction}$$
$$\text{Transfer Funds} \xleftarrow{\text{<<include>>}} \text{Validate Transaction}$$

**Final Logical Model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{System} &= \{\text{ATM System}\} \\
\text{Actors} &= \{\text{Customer},\ \text{Bank},\ \text{Maintenance Officer}\} \\
\text{UseCases} &= \{\text{Authenticate User},\ \text{Withdraw Cash},\ \text{Check Balance}, \\
&\quad \text{Deposit Cash},\ \text{Transfer Funds},\ \text{Refill ATM Cash}, \\
&\quad \text{Print Receipt},\ \text{Validate Transaction}\} \\
\text{Includes} &= \{(\text{Withdraw}, \text{Authenticate}),\ (\text{Deposit}, \text{Authenticate}), \\
&\quad (\text{Transfer}, \text{Authenticate}),\ (\text{CheckBalance}, \text{Authenticate}), \\
&\quad (\text{Withdraw}, \text{Validate}),\ (\text{Transfer}, \text{Validate})\} \\
\text{Extends} &= \{(\text{Print Receipt}, \text{Withdraw Cash})\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Reasoning:** Notice that the `<<include>>` for *Authenticate User* appears **four times**. This is the textbook signal that *Authenticate User* is a **factored-out common behavior** — the correct UML way to model it. Students who do not use `<<include>>` here will lose **2 to 3 marks** in a 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Use Case Diagram — ATM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CustomerA((Customer))
    BankA((Bank))
    MaintA((Maintenance Officer))
    
    subgraph SYSTEM ["ATM System Boundary"]
        direction TB
        AuthUC(["Authenticate User"])
        WithdrawUC(["Withdraw Cash"])
        CheckUC(["Check Balance"])
        DepositUC(["Deposit Cash"])
        TransferUC(["Transfer Funds"])
        RefillUC(["Refill ATM Cash"])
        PrintUC(["Print Receipt"])
        ValidateUC(["Validate Transaction"])
    end
    
    CustomerA --- AuthUC
    CustomerA --- WithdrawUC
    CustomerA --- CheckUC
    CustomerA --- DepositUC
    CustomerA --- TransferUC
    MaintA --- RefillUC
    BankA --- ValidateUC
    
    WithdrawUC -.->|include| AuthUC
    DepositUC -.->|include| AuthUC
    TransferUC -.->|include| AuthUC
    CheckUC -.->|include| AuthUC
    
    WithdrawUC -.->|include| ValidateUC
    TransferUC -.->|include| ValidateUC
    
    PrintUC -.->|extend| WithdrawUC
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationships at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RELMAP ["UML Use Case Relationship Reference Map"]
        direction TB
        R1["Association - solid line - Actor to UseCase"]
        R2["Include - dashed arrow - base to reused"]
        R3["Extend - dashed arrow - extending to base"]
        R4["Generalization - solid hollow triangle - child to parent"]
    end
    
    A[Actor] -->|initiates| R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of a Use Case Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root["Use Case Diagram"]
    Root --> N1["1 Actors"]
    Root --> N2["2 Use Cases"]
    Root --> N3["3 System Boundary"]
    Root --> N4["4 Relationships"]
    
    N1 --> N1a["Primary Actor"]
    N1 --> N1b["Secondary Actor"]
    N1 --> N1c["Human Actor"]
    N1 --> N1d["System Actor"]
    
    N4 --> N4a["Association"]
    N4 --> N4b["Include"]
    N4 --> N4c["Extend"]
    N4 --> N4d["Generalization"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The above Mermaid block diagram serves as a *functional architecture map* showing how all the building blocks of a use case diagram are interconnected. In professional UML modeling tools like **StarUML** or **Rational Rose**, the same structure is rendered as the standard UML notation (ellipses, stick figures, dashed/solid arrows).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`**

**Q: Define a Use Case Diagram. List any four components of a Use Case Diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Use Case Diagram** is a UML behavioral diagram that depicts the functional requirements of a system by showing the external actors that interact with the system and the various use cases (goals/services) the system provides to those actors. It provides a high-level, black-box view of the system's functionality.

The **four main components** of a Use Case Diagram are:

1. **Actors** — External entities (users or other systems) that interact with the system.
2. **Use Cases** — Ellipses representing the goals or services offered by the system.
3. **System Boundary** — A rectangle that defines the scope of the system.
4. **Relationships** — Lines and arrows (association, include, extend, generalization) that connect actors to use cases and use cases to each other.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`**

**Q: Differentiate between `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships in a Use Case Diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Mandatory reuse of behavior | Optional or conditional addition of behavior |
| **Direction** | From base use case to reused use case | From extending use case to base use case |
| **Trigger** | Always executes when the base use case runs | Executes only when a specified condition is met |
| **Purpose** | Eliminates duplication of common behavior | Adds new or exceptional behavior |
| **Example** | *Place Order* includes *Validate Payment* | *Place Order* is extended by *Apply Coupon* (only if a coupon is provided) |
| **Independence** | The base use case **cannot** complete without the included use case | The base use case is **fully functional** without the extending use case |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]`**

**Q: Draw a detailed Use Case Diagram for a Library Management System. Identify the actors, use cases, and all possible relationships. Justify the use of `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` in your diagram.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Identify and List Actors and Use Cases

**Solution:**

**System:** Library Management System

**Actors Identified:**

| Actor | Type | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Member | Primary | Borrows/returns books, searches catalog |
| Librarian | Primary | Manages books and members |
| System Administrator | Primary | Manages user accounts and configurations |
| Payment Gateway | Secondary | Processes fine payments |

**Use Cases Identified:**

1. **Search Book** — Member searches the catalog.
2. **Issue Book** — Librarian issues a book to a member.
3. **Return Book** — Member returns a book.
4. **Pay Fine** — Member pays overdue fine.
5. **Add Book** — Librarian adds a new book to the catalog.
6. **Register Member** — Librarian registers a new member.
7. **Generate Reports** — Librarian generates usage reports.
8. **Authenticate User** — Common login functionality.
9. **Send Notification** — Sends email/SMS alerts for overdue books.
10. **Validate Payment** — Validates fine payment via gateway.

**Relationship Justification:**

- `<<include>>` for *Authenticate User* — Every use case (Issue Book, Return Book, Add Book, etc.) requires the user to log in first. The behavior is **mandatory** and **shared**, so factoring it out eliminates duplication.
- `<<extend>>` for *Send Notification* — The system sends a notification **only when a book becomes overdue**. The base use case (*Return Book* or *Check Overdue*) can execute without the notification; hence it is an **optional extension**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Draw the Use Case Diagram

The complete diagram should contain:

- A rectangle labelled **"Library Management System"**.
- Four stick figures outside the boundary: **Member, Librarian, System Administrator, Payment Gateway**.
- Ten ellipses inside the boundary, named using verb-noun convention.
- **Association lines** connecting actors to their relevant use cases.
- **Dashed `<<include>>` arrows** from Issue Book, Return Book, Add Book, Register Member, and Generate Reports pointing to *Authenticate User*.
- **Dashed `<<include>>` arrows** from Pay Fine to *Validate Payment*.
- **Dashed `<<extend>>` arrow** from *Send Notification* pointing to *Return Book*.
- Optional **Generalization** arrow from *System Administrator* to *Librarian* (since Admin is a specialized Librarian with extended privileges).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MemberA((Member))
    LibA((Librarian))
    AdminA((System Admin))
    PayA((Payment Gateway))
    
    subgraph LMS ["Library Management System"]
        direction TB
        Auth(["Authenticate User"])
        Search(["Search Book"])
        Issue(["Issue Book"])
        Return(["Return Book"])
        Pay(["Pay Fine"])
        AddB(["Add Book"])
        Reg(["Register Member"])
        Rep(["Generate Reports"])
        Notif(["Send Notification"])
        ValPay(["Validate Payment"])
    end
    
    MemberA --- Search
    MemberA --- Return
    MemberA --- Pay
    LibA --- Issue
    LibA --- AddB
    LibA --- Reg
    LibA --- Rep
    AdminA --- Reg
    AdminA --- Rep
    PayA --- ValPay
    
    Issue -.->|include| Auth
    Return -.->|include| Auth
    AddB -.->|include| Auth
    Reg -.->|include| Auth
    Rep -.->|include| Auth
    Pay -.->|include| ValPay
    Notif -.->|extend| Return
    AdminA -.->|generalization| LibA
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`**

**Q: Consider an Online Shopping System. A Customer can browse products, add items to cart, place an order, make a payment, and track the order. The System validates payment through a Bank. A Delivery Agent delivers the order. The Customer can cancel an order only if it has not been shipped. Draw the Use Case Diagram with proper notation and justify all relationships used.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Components and Relationship Analysis

**Solution:**

**Actors:**

- **Customer** — Primary actor; browses, orders, pays, tracks, cancels.
- **Bank** — Secondary actor; validates payments.
- **Delivery Agent** — Primary actor; delivers and updates delivery status.
- **System Administrator** — Primary actor; manages product catalog.

**Use Cases:**

1. Browse Product
2. Add to Cart
3. Place Order
4. Make Payment
5. Track Order
6. Cancel Order
7. Update Delivery Status
8. Validate Payment
9. Authenticate User
10. Manage Products

**Relationship Justifications:**

- `<<include>>`: *Place Order* includes *Authenticate User* (login is mandatory) and includes *Make Payment* (no order is placed without payment). *Make Payment* includes *Validate Payment* (validation is mandatory).
- `<<extend>>`: *Cancel Order* is **extended** by a *condition* — it is allowed only if order status is "Not Shipped". Alternatively, *Track Order* can be **extended** by *Send Email Notification* (optional email alerts).
- **Generalization**: *Guest Customer* and *Registered Customer* both generalize from *Customer*.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Diagram Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cust((Customer))
    BankA((Bank))
    DelA((Delivery Agent))
    AdmA((System Admin))
    
    subgraph OSS ["Online Shopping System"]
        direction TB
        Auth(["Authenticate User"])
        Browse(["Browse Product"])
        Cart(["Add to Cart"])
        Place(["Place Order"])
        Pay(["Make Payment"])
        Track(["Track Order"])
        Cancel(["Cancel Order"])
        UpdateDel(["Update Delivery Status"])
        ValPay(["Validate Payment"])
        Notify(["Send Email Notification"])
        ManageP(["Manage Products"])
    end
    
    Cust --- Browse
    Cust --- Cart
    Cust --- Place
    Cust --- Pay
    Cust --- Track
    Cust --- Cancel
    DelA --- UpdateDel
    BankA --- ValPay
    AdmA --- ManageP
    
    Place -.->|include| Auth
    Place -.->|include| Pay
    Pay -.->|include| ValPay
    Track -.->|include| Auth
    Cancel -.->|include| Auth
    Notify -.->|extend| Track
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the System Boundary:** Students often place actors *inside* the system rectangle. This is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Wrong arrow direction for `<<include>>`/`<<extend>>`:** Many students reverse the arrows. This costs **1 to 2 marks** per instance.
> 3. **Vague use case names:** Writing "Order" instead of "Place Order" loses **1 mark** per vague name.
> 4. **Confusing `<<include>>` with `<<extend>>`:** Use `<<include>>` only when the behavior is **mandatory and shared**; use `<<extend>>` only when the behavior is **optional or conditional**.
> 5. **Not labeling relationships:** A dashed arrow without the `<<include>>` or `<<extend>>` keyword is incomplete — **0.5 mark deduction per unlabeled arrow**.
> 6. **Drawing actors as concrete people:** Always label actors by **role** (e.g., *Customer*), not by specific names (e.g., *Rahul*).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Use Case Diagrams**

- [x] A **Use Case Diagram** is a **UML behavioral diagram** used during the *requirements phase* of OOAD.
- [x] It provides a **black-box, external view** of the system — it does NOT show internal classes or methods.
- [x] **Four building blocks:** Actors, Use Cases, System Boundary, Relationships.
- [x] **Actors** are drawn as **stick figures** *outside* the system boundary. They represent **roles**, not specific individuals.
- [x] **Use Cases** are drawn as **ellipses** *inside* the system boundary, with **verb-noun names**.
- [x] The **System Boundary** is a **rectangle** that encloses all use cases and is labelled with the system name.
- [x] **Association** = solid line (Actor $\leftrightarrow$ Use Case).
- [x] **`<<include>>`** = dashed arrow, **base $\rightarrow$ reused**; used for **mandatory common behavior**.
- [x] **`<<extend>>`** = dashed arrow, **extending $\rightarrow$ base**; used for **optional or conditional behavior**.
- [x] **Generalization** = solid line with hollow triangle; used for **inheritance** between actors or use cases.
- [x] Use case names must be **short, verb-noun, and represent a complete unit of value** to the actor.
- [x] Use Case Diagrams are authored using tools like **StarUML, Rational Rose, Lucidchart, Visual Paradigm**.
- [x] Always remember: **Primary actors** initiate use cases; **secondary actors** provide services to the system.
- [x] For KTU 14-mark questions, follow the **6-step methodology**: System → Actors → Use Cases → Naming → Associations → Special Relationships.

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** When asked to *"draw"* a use case diagram in the KTU board exam, do not just draw — also **list the actors, list the use cases, and justify every `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` arrow** in 2–3 lines each. This converts a 7-mark drawing answer into a full-14-mark, well-justified response that examiners reward generously.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Class Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Class Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLASS DIAGRAM (UML 2.5 Definition):** A **Class Diagram** is a static structure diagram in the Unified Modeling Language (UML) that describes the **system's structure** by illustrating the system's **classes**, their **attributes**, **operations** (methods), and the **relationships** that exist among the objects of those classes. It represents the blueprint of the system from an object-oriented perspective.

In the context of **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a Class Diagram is the most fundamental and widely used **UML (Unified Modeling Language)** structural diagram. It is generated during the **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** phase to model the static (non-changing over time) view of a software system.

A Class Diagram answers three critical design questions:

1. **What** entities (classes/objects) exist in the system?
2. **What** information (attributes) do they hold?
3. **How** do they interact (relationships) with each other?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (CO1 — KTU 2024):** Class Diagrams fall under *Course Outcome 1: "Apply the concepts of object-oriented programming and design using UML."* Expect direct questions on notation, relationships, and identifying classes from problem statements.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**. Before laying a single brick, you draw a **blueprint** that shows:

- The **rooms** (these are your *classes* — like `Bedroom`, `Kitchen`, `Bathroom`).
- The **dimensions and furniture** inside each room (these are your *attributes* — like `width: 10ft`, `color: white`).
- The **actions** possible in each room (these are your *methods/operations* — like `cook()`, `sleep()`).
- The **doors and hallways** connecting the rooms (these are your *relationships* — like *association* between `Hallway` and `Bedroom`).

A **Class Diagram is the architectural blueprint of software**. It does NOT show time-based behavior (that is the Sequence/Activity diagram's job). Instead, it shows the **static skeleton** of the system — *what the system is made of* — before the dynamic behavior is added.

### Why Class Diagrams Matter in Java OOD

- They map **directly to Java code** — each UML class becomes a `.java` file.
- They help in **identifying classes** from a problem statement (the foundation of OOAD).
- They document **inheritance hierarchies**, **interfaces**, and **associations** clearly.
- They serve as a **communication tool** between developers, architects, and stakeholders.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Components of a Class Diagram (Syllabus Overview)

A class diagram is composed of the following building blocks:

| Component | Purpose | UML Symbol |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **Class** | Template/blueprint for objects | Rectangle with 3 compartments |
| **Attribute** | Data/property of a class | Inside middle compartment |
| **Operation/Method** | Behavior of a class | Inside bottom compartment |
| **Interface** | Contract with no implementation | Lollipop or rectangle with `<<interface>>` |
| **Package** | Logical grouping of related classes | Folder-shaped tab icon |
| **Relationship** | Connection between classes | Lines with various arrowheads |

---

## 1.4 The Class Notation — Three Compartments

A UML class is drawn as a **rectangle divided into three horizontal sections**:

```
+-----------------------+
|      ClassName        |   ← Top: Class Name (bold, centered)
+-----------------------+
| - attribute1: Type    |   ← Middle: Attributes (with visibility)
| + attribute2: Type    |
+-----------------------+
| + method1(): ReturnType|  ← Bottom: Operations/Methods
| - method2(arg: Type)  |
+-----------------------+
```

**Visibility Modifiers (Mandatory for KTU exams):**

| Symbol | Visibility | Java Equivalent | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| `+` | **Public** | `public` | Accessible from any class |
| `-` | **Private** | `private` | Accessible only within the class |
| `#` | **Protected** | `protected` | Accessible within class and subclasses |
| `~` | **Package** | (default / no modifier) | Accessible within the same package |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):** Always remember — *Attribute names start with a lowercase letter* (camelCase), *Class names start with an uppercase letter* (PascalCase), and *abstract operations/methods are written in italics* in UML.

---

## 1.5 Example: A Simple Class Diagram Notation

Consider a `Student` class in a university system:

```
+--------------------------+
|         Student          |
+--------------------------+
| - studentId: int         |
| - name: String           |
| - cgpa: float            |
| - courses: List<Course>  |
+--------------------------+
| + registerCourse(c: Course): void |
| + calculateGPA(): float           |
| + getDetails(): String            |
+--------------------------+
```

This notation, when translated to **Java**, becomes:

```java
public class Student {
    private int studentId;
    private String name;
    private float cgpa;
    private List<Course> courses;

    public void registerCourse(Course c) { /* ... */ }
    public float calculateGPA() { /* ... */ }
    public String getDetails() { /* ... */ }
}
```

---

## 1.6 What is NOT a Class Diagram

| Concept | Where It Belongs |
|---------|------------------|
| Time-based object interaction | **Sequence Diagram** |
| Workflow/process flow | **Activity Diagram** |
| System state changes | **State Chart Diagram** |
| Use case identification | **Use Case Diagram** |
| **Static class structure** | ✅ **Class Diagram (This Topic)** |

---

## 1.7 Summary Snapshot for Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Definition (for 2-mark questions):** A Class Diagram is a static UML structural diagram that visualizes a system's classes, their attributes, operations, and the relationships among them, serving as the primary blueprint in object-oriented analysis and design.

**Key Numbers to Remember:**
- **3** compartments in a class rectangle
- **4** visibility symbols (`+`, `-`, `#`, `~`)
- **6** types of relationships in UML class diagrams
- **UML 2.5** is the current standard version

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6 Types of Relationships in Class Diagrams (Most Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highest Weightage Topic:** Relationship identification and drawing is the **#1 asked question type** in KTU exams for Module 1. Master all 6 with examples.

### 2.1.1 Association

**Definition:** A structural relationship between two or more classes that describes *connections* between their instances (objects). It represents that objects of one class *use* or *are connected to* objects of another class.

- **Notation:** A **solid line** between two classes.
- **Optional labels:** Role names, multiplicity at both ends.
- **Direction:** Can be **directional** (with arrow) or **bidirectional**.

**Example:** A `Teacher` *teaches* a `Student` — a `Student` is *taught by* a `Teacher`.

```
+--------+              +----------+
| Teacher|--------------| Student  |
+--------+              +----------+
    1                       *
```

### 2.1.2 Aggregation (a "weak" *has-a* relationship)

**Definition:** A specialized form of association that represents a **whole-part relationship** where the *part* can exist **independently** of the whole. It is often called a **"weak" containment**.

- **Notation:** Solid line with a **hollow (empty) diamond** at the *whole* side.
- **Key Phrase:** "**has-a**" but can survive without the owner.

**Example:** A `Department` *has* `Faculty` members. If the department is closed, the faculty members still exist (they transfer elsewhere).

```
+-----------+ ◇----------+
| Department|◇------------| Faculty  |
+-----------+              +----------+
```

### 2.1.3 Composition (a "strong" *has-a* relationship)

**Definition:** A stronger form of aggregation where the *part* **cannot exist without** the whole. The part's lifecycle is **tied** to the whole's lifecycle.

- **Notation:** Solid line with a **filled (solid black) diamond** at the *whole* side.
- **Key Phrase:** "**part-of**" — if the whole is destroyed, the parts are destroyed too.

**Example:** A `House` *contains* `Room` objects. If the house is demolished, the rooms are destroyed.

```
+--------+ ◆--------+
|  House |◆----------| Room  |
+--------+           +-------+
    1                   1..*
```

### 2.1.4 Inheritance (Generalization)

**Definition:** A relationship between a **superclass (parent)** and one or more **subclasses (children)** where the subclasses inherit attributes and operations from the superclass. It represents an **"is-a"** relationship.

- **Notation:** Solid line with a **hollow triangle (arrowhead)** pointing to the **superclass**.
- **Java equivalent:** `extends` keyword.

**Example:** A `Car` *is-a* `Vehicle`. A `SavingsAccount` *is-a* `BankAccount`.

```
+-------+            +--------+
|  Car  |─────▷| Vehicle |
+-------+            +--------+
```

### 2.1.5 Realization (Implementation)

**Definition:** A relationship between a **class** and an **interface** where the class commits to implementing all the operations declared in the interface.

- **Notation:** **Dashed line** with a **hollow triangle (arrowhead)** pointing to the interface.
- **Java equivalent:** `implements` keyword.
- **Alternative notation:** The **lollipop notation** (small circle on a stick connected to the class).

**Example:** A `Circle` class *realizes* the `Shape` interface.

```
+--------+ - - -▷ ⟨interface⟩ Shape
| Circle |
+--------+
```

### 2.1.6 Dependency

**Definition:** A **weaker relationship** where one class *uses* another class, but only **temporarily** (e.g., as a method parameter, local variable, or in method body). Changes to the target class may break the source class.

- **Notation:** **Dashed line** with an **open (V-shaped) arrowhead** pointing to the class being depended on.
- **Java equivalent:** A class uses another as a parameter, local variable, or in a static call.

**Example:** A `ShoppingCart` class uses a `CreditCard` object to process payment — but only as a method argument.

```
+---------------+ - - -▷ +-----------+
```
