```
| ShoppingCart  |         | CreditCard|
+---------------+         +-----------+
```

---

## 2.2 Multiplicity Specification (Cardinality)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Special Focus:** Multiplicity in associations and aggregations is a **frequently asked** sub-question (usually for 2-3 marks).

Multiplicity defines **how many objects** of one class are associated with how many objects of the other class. It is written at **both ends** of the relationship line.

### 2.2.1 Common Multiplicity Notations

| Notation | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `1` | Exactly one | A `Person` has **exactly one** `Passport` |
| `0..1` | Zero or one (optional) | A `Person` has **zero or one** `Spouse` |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many | A `Library` has **zero or many** `Books` |
| `1..*` | One or many | A `Department` has **at least one** `Faculty` |
| `n..m` | Range between n and m | A `Team` has **between 5 and 11** `Players` |
| `n` | Exactly n | A `Triangle` has **exactly 3** `Sides` |

### 2.2.2 Reading a Multiplicity Expression

Read multiplicities in the form: **"One [Class A] is associated with [multiplicity] of [Class B]."**

**Example:**
```
+--------+ 1   * +--------+
|  Team  |--------| Player |
+--------+        +--------+
```
**Read as:** "One Team has many (zero or more) Players, and each Player belongs to exactly one Team."

---

## 2.3 Advanced Class Concepts in UML

### 2.3.1 Abstract Classes

- Written in **italics** in UML.
- Cannot be instantiated.
- May have **abstract operations** (also in italics).
- Java equivalent: `abstract class`.

```
«abstract»
+----------------+
|   «abstract»  |
|   Shape        |
+----------------+
| # color: String|
+----------------+
| + draw()       |  ← italic = abstract
| + area(): float|  ← italic = abstract
+----------------+
```

### 2.3.2 Interfaces

- Declared using `<<interface>>` stereotype.
- All operations are implicitly **public abstract**.
- Realized using a dashed line with a hollow triangle.

```
«interface»
+----------------+
| <<interface>>  |
|   Drawable     |
+----------------+
| + draw(): void |
+----------------+
```

### 2.3.3 Static Members

- **Static attributes:** Underlined in the class diagram.
- **Static methods:** Underlined in the operations compartment.
- **Example:** `- `count: int` (the underline is shown beneath the text).

### 2.3.4 Final Classes / Methods

- Cannot be inherited / overridden.
- Usually annotated with a **note** in UML.
- No specific UML symbol, but commonly noted as `{final}` or `leaf` stereotype.

---

## 2.4 The "How to Identify Classes" Technique (CRISP Methodology)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Favorite:** Questions like *"Identify classes, attributes, methods, and relationships from the given problem statement"* (8-14 marks) are extremely common.

### The **CRISP** Heuristic (used by professional OOAD analysts)

1. **C — Categorize nouns:** Underline all nouns/noun phrases in the problem statement. Each is a potential **class**.
2. **R — Reject redundant nouns:** Remove duplicates, synonyms, and vague nouns (e.g., "system", "thing").
3. **I — Identify attributes:** Look for descriptive adjectives/possessives attached to nouns.
4. **S — Specify operations:** Look for verbs (actions) associated with nouns.
5. **P — Pinpoint relationships:** Look for verbs that connect two nouns (e.g., "has", "owns", "is part of", "inherits from").

### Example Application:

**Problem Statement:** *"A university has departments. Each department offers multiple courses. A course is taught by a professor and enrolled in by students. Students submit assignments for courses."*

**Step 1 — Categorize nouns:**
- University, Department, Course, Professor, Student, Assignment.

**Step 2 — Reject redundancies:** "University" → keep as boundary class; the rest are core domain classes.

**Step 3 — Identify attributes:**
- Department: name, code.
- Course: title, code, credits.
- Professor: name, empId, specialization.
- Student: name, rollNo, cgpa.
- Assignment: title, deadline, marks.

**Step 4 — Specify operations:**
- Department: addCourse(), removeCourse().
- Course: enrollStudent(), assignProfessor().
- Student: submitAssignment(), viewGrades().
- Professor: gradeAssignment(), conductClass().

**Step 5 — Pinpoint relationships:**
- University *contains* **Departments** → **Composition** (1 to 1..*).
- Department *offers* **Courses** → **Aggregation** (1 to *).
- Course *taught by* **Professor** → **Association** (* to 1).
- Student *enrolled in* **Course** → **Association** (* to *).
- Student *submits* **Assignment** → **Association** (1 to *).
- Assignment *belongs to* **Course** → **Composition** (1 to *).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Class Diagram Components

| # | Component | UML Notation | Java Equivalent | Example |
|---|-----------|-------------|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | Class | Rectangle, 3 compartments | `class` keyword | `Student` |
| 2 | Abstract Class | Rectangle, name in *italics*, `<<abstract>>` | `abstract class` | `Shape` |
| 3 | Interface | Rectangle, `<<interface>>` stereotype | `interface` | `Drawable` |
| 4 | Public Visibility | `+` symbol | `public` | `+name: String` |
| 5 | Private Visibility | `-` symbol | `private` | `-age: int` |
| 6 | Protected Visibility | `#` symbol | `protected` | `#balance: float` |
| 7 | Package Visibility | `~` symbol | (default) | `~temp: double` |
| 8 | Association | Solid line | Object reference | Teacher—Student |
| 9 | Aggregation | Hollow diamond + line | Weak reference | Department—Faculty |
| 10 | Composition | Filled diamond + line | Strong reference | House—Room |
| 11 | Inheritance | Solid line + hollow triangle | `extends` | Car▷Vehicle |
| 12 | Realization | Dashed line + hollow triangle | `implements` | Circle▷Shape |
| 13 | Dependency | Dashed line + open arrow | Parameter/local var | Cart▷CreditCard |
| 14 | Multiplicity `1` | Label `1` | — | 1-to-1 |
| 15 | Multiplicity `*` | Label `*` or `0..*` | — | 1-to-many |
| 16 | Static Member | Underline | `static` keyword | `- `id: int` |
| 17 | Abstract Operation | Italics | `abstract` method | `+ draw()` |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Use of Class Diagrams |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Banking Software** | Account, Customer, Transaction, Loan classes |
| **E-Commerce** | Product, Cart, Order, Payment, User classes |
| **Healthcare Systems** | Patient, Doctor, Appointment, Prescription |
| **Library Management** | Book, Member, Librarian, IssueRecord |
| **Avionics/Embedded** | Sensor, Actuator, Controller, TelemetryData |
| **AI/ML Systems** | Dataset, Model, Feature, Trainer classes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step: Designing a Class Diagram from a Problem Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-Asked Pattern in KTU 2024:** A 14-mark question typically reads:
> *"Design the Class Diagram for the following system. Identify classes, attributes, methods, and relationships with proper UML notation and multiplicity."*

Let us work through a **complete, exam-ready derivation** using a Library Management System.

---

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (Sample KTU-style)

> *"A library lends books to registered members. The library has a collection of books, each with a unique ISBN, title, and author. Members have a member ID, name, and contact details. A member can borrow up to 3 books at a time. Each loan is recorded with a borrow date and return date. The library is managed by a librarian who can add or remove books and members."*

---

### 3.1.2 Step 1: Identify the Candidate Classes (CRISP - C and R)

We extract all nouns from the problem statement:

**Raw Nouns:** library, book, ISBN, title, author, member, member ID, name, contact details, loan, borrow date, return date, librarian.

**Class Candidates (after eliminating attributes mistakenly read as nouns):**
- `Library` (boundary)
- `Book`
- `Member`
- `Loan` (the borrowing transaction)
- `Librarian`

> We drop `ISBN`, `title`, `author`, `name`, `contact`, `borrow date`, `return date` because these are **attributes**, not classes.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 2: Identify Attributes for Each Class (CRISP - I)

| Class | Attributes (with type & visibility) |
|-------|-------------------------------------|
| `Book` | `- isbn: String`, `- title: String`, `- author: String`, `- available: boolean` |
| `Member` | `- memberId: int`, `- name: String`, `- contact: String`, `- borrowedCount: int` |
| `Loan` | `- loanId: int`, `- borrowDate: Date`, `- returnDate: Date`, `- isReturned: boolean` |
| `Librarian` | `- empId: int`, `- name: String`, `- password: String` |
| `Library` | `- name: String`, `- address: String` |

---

### 3.1.4 Step 3: Identify Methods for Each Class (CRISP - S)

| Class | Methods (with visibility, parameters, return type) |
|-------|---------------------------------------------------|
| `Book` | `+ getDetails(): String`, `+ isAvailable(): boolean` |
| `Member` | `+ borrowBook(b: Book): void`, `+ returnBook(b: Book): void`, `+ getDetails(): String` |
| `Loan` | `+ calculateDueDate(): Date`, `+ closeLoan(): void` |
| `Librarian` | `+ addBook(b: Book): void`, `+ removeBook(isbn: String): void`, `+ registerMember(m: Member): void` |
| `Library` | `+ searchBook(title: String): List<Book>`, `+ issueLoan(m: Member, b: Book): Loan` |

---

### 3.1.5 Step 4: Identify Relationships with Multiplicity (CRISP - P)

We analyze the **verbs** in the problem statement:

| Phrase | Relationship Type | Class A | Class B | Multiplicity |
|--------|------------------|---------|---------|--------------|
| "library has books" | **Aggregation** (weak has-a) | `Library` | `Book` | `1` to `*` |
| "member borrows books" | **Association** (via Loan) | `Member` | `Book` | `*` to `*` (via Loan) |
| "loan is recorded for" | **Association** | `Loan` | `Book` | `1` to `1` |
| "loan is for a member" | **Association** | `Loan` | `Member` | `*` to `1` |
| "library is managed by librarian" | **Association** (or Aggregation) | `Library` | `Librarian` | `1` to `1..*` |
| "librarian registers members" | **Association** | `Librarian` | `Member` | `1` to `*` |

---

### 3.1.6 Step 5: Construct the Class Diagram

```
+---------------------+        +----------------------+
|      Library        |◇--------|        Book          |
+---------------------+  1  *  +----------------------+
| - name: String      |        | - isbn: String       |
| - address: String   |        | - title: String      |
+---------------------+        | - author: String     |
| + searchBook(...):  |        | - available: boolean |
|   List<Book>        |        +----------------------+
| + issueLoan(...):   |        | + getDetails():String|
|   Loan              |        | + isAvailable(): bool|
+---------------------+        +----------------------+
                                       △
                                       | (uses)
                                       |
                                +-------------------+
                                |       Loan        |
                                +-------------------+
                                | - loanId: int     |
                                | - borrowDate: Date|
                                | - returnDate: Date|
                                +-------------------+
                                | + calculateDueDate|
                                | + closeLoan(): void|
                                +-------------------+
                                       |1
                                       |
                                       |*
                                +-------------------+
                                |      Member       |
                                +-------------------+
                                | - memberId: int   |
                                | - name: String    |
                                | - contact: String |
                                | - borrowedCount:  |
                                |     int           |
                                +-------------------+
                                | + borrowBook():void|
                                | + returnBook():void|
                                | + getDetails():...|
                                +-------------------+
```

(Plus, the `Librarian` → `Library` and `Librarian` → `Member` association lines as discussed.)

**Class `Book.java`:**
```java
public class Book {
    private String isbn;
    private String title;

    private boolean available;

    public String getDetails() { /* ... */ }
    public boolean isAvailable() { /* ... */ }
}
```

**Class `Library.java`:**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class Library {
    private String name;

    private List<Book> books;       // Aggregation

    public List<Book> searchBook(String title) { /* ... */ }
    public Loan issueLoan(Member m, Book b) { /* ... */ }
}
```

**Class `Loan.java`:**
```java
import java.util.Date;

public class Loan {
    private int loanId;
    private Date borrowDate;
    private Date returnDate;
    private Book book;             // Association
    private Member member;         // Association

    public Date calculateDueDate() { /* ... */ }
    public void closeLoan() { /* ... */ }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark question often asks you to **also write the equivalent Java class signatures** for the diagram you drew. This step proves the diagram is *implementable* and earns you **bonus marks** if shown.

## 3.2 Decision Matrix: How to Choose the Right Relationship Type

This is the most common confusion point for students. Use this **decision flowchart matrix**:

| Condition | Relationship to Use | Notation |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| Class A is the *parent type* of class B (B is a specialized A) | **Generalization / Inheritance** | Solid line + hollow triangle to A |
| Class A *implements the contract* of an interface | **Realization** | Dashed line + hollow triangle to interface |
| Class A *uses* Class B as method parameter / local var (temporary) | **Dependency** | Dashed line + open arrow to B |
| Class A *has-a* Class B, but B can exist without A | **Aggregation** | Hollow diamond at A's end |
| Class A *contains* Class B, and B dies with A | **Composition** | Filled diamond at A's end |
| Classes are just *connected* but neither owns the other | **Association** | Plain solid line |

### 3.2.1 Quick Memory Trick for the 6 Relationships

**Memory Phrase:** *"**A**ssociation **A**ggregation **C**omposition **G**eneralization **R**ealization **D**ependency"* → **AACGRD** → "**A** **C**ompany **G**rows **R**apidly **D**aily"

Or visualize: **"Has-a, Has-a (strong), Is-a, Implements, Uses"**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Multiplicity Resolution Examples

### 3.3.1 Example 1: One-to-One (1 : 1)

*"Each person has exactly one passport, and each passport belongs to exactly one person."*

```
+----------+ 1   1 +------------+
|  Person  |--------| Passport   |
+----------+        +------------+
```

**Java Hint:** `Person` has a field `Passport passport;` and `Passport` has `Person owner;` (bidirectional).

### 3.3.2 Example 2: One-to-Many (1 : *)

*"A department has many employees, but each employee belongs to exactly one department."*

```
+-----------+ 1   * +-----------+
| Department|--------| Employee  |
+-----------+        +-----------+
```

**Java Hint:** `Department` has `List<Employee> employees;`. `Employee` has `Department dept;`.

### 3.3.3 Example 3: Many-to-Many (* : *)

*"A student can enroll in many courses, and a course can have many students."*

```
+--------+ *   * +---------+
| Student|--------| Course  |
+--------+        +---------+
```

**Java Hint:** Often implemented via an **associative class** (e.g., `Enrollment`) to capture additional data like `grade`, `enrollmentDate`.

```
+--------+              +-----------+              +---------+
| Student|1      *  * 1|Enrollment |*      1  *   | Course  |
+--------+ -------------+-----------+--------------+---------+
```

### 3.3.4 Example 4: Self-Association (Reflexive)

*"An Employee may manage other Employees."*

```
+----------+
| Employee |
+----------+
     |
     |◇ (manages)
     |0..*
     ◇─────►
     |
   (self)
```

In Java, this is modeled by having a reference to the same class within itself.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: From Use Case → Class Diagram (Top-Down OOAD Approach)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to know the **traceability** between requirements and class diagrams. Here is the derivation chain:

$$\text{Requirements} \rightarrow \text{Use Cases} \rightarrow \text{Conceptual Classes} \rightarrow \text{Class Diagram} \rightarrow \text{Java Code}$$

**Step A:** Identify actors and use cases (Use Case Diagram).
**Step B:** From each use case, extract nouns → candidate classes.
**Step C:** Apply **Abstraction** to merge similar classes (e.g., `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` both are `BankAccount`).
**Step D:** Apply **Generalization** if there is an "is-a" relationship.
**Step E:** Apply **Aggregation/Composition** for "has-a" or "part-of" relationships.
**Step F:** Apply **Association** for "uses" or "knows" relationships.
**Step G:** Add **multiplicity** based on business rules.
**Step H:** Add **attributes** and **operations** based on the use case descriptions.
**Step I:** Validate by translating to **Java** and ensuring the design is implementable.

---

## 3.5 Common Pitfalls in Class Diagram Design (Avoid These in KTU Exams)

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequent mistakes students make in KTU exams:**

1. **Confusing Aggregation with Composition.** Ask: *"If the whole is destroyed, do the parts still make sense?"* If NO → **Composition**. If YES → **Aggregation**.
2. **Forgetting to put attributes/methods in separate compartments.** Attributes go in the *middle* section, methods in the *bottom*.
3. **Not writing visibility modifiers** (`+`, `-`, `#`). KTU examiners **deduct 1-2 marks** for missing visibility.
4. **Using wrong arrow for inheritance.** The hollow triangle must point to the **PARENT**, not the child.
5. **Forgetting multiplicity** on both ends of the relationship.
6. **Drawing bidirectional arrows incorrectly.** A bidirectional association is just a line; directional is a line with an arrow on one end.
7. **Including implementation details** (like getters/setters) in the class diagram. These are optional and not required for design-level diagrams.

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out Example: E-Commerce Cart System

**Problem:** *"A Customer can add Products to a ShoppingCart. The cart can have multiple items. Each item references one product and a quantity. The cart is owned by exactly one customer. Customers can place Orders, which contain a copy of the cart contents at the time of order."*

### Solution Derivation:

**Classes:** `Customer`, `ShoppingCart`, `CartItem`, `Product`, `Order`, `OrderItem`.

**Relationships:**
- `Customer` **owns** `ShoppingCart` → **Composition** (1 to 1).
- `ShoppingCart` **contains** `CartItem` → **Composition** (1 to *).
- `CartItem` **references** `Product` → **Association** (* to 1).
- `Order` **contains** `OrderItem` → **Composition** (1 to *).
- `OrderItem` **references** `Product` → **Association** (* to 1).
- `Customer` **places** `Order` → **Association** (1 to *).

This structured derivation would earn full marks in a KTU exam because every choice is justified.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram: A Complete Library System

Below is a fully-rendered Mermaid class diagram representing the Library Management example derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Library {
        -name: String
        -address: String
        +searchBook(title: String) List~Book~
        +issueLoan(m: Member, b: Book) Loan
    }

    class Book {
        -isbn: String
        -title: String
        -author: String
        -available: boolean
        +getDetails() String
        +isAvailable() boolean
    }

    class Member {
        -memberId: int
        -name: String
        -contact: String
        -borrowedCount: int
        +borrowBook(b: Book) void
        +returnBook(b: Book) void
        +getDetails() String
    }

    class Loan {
        -loanId: int
        -borrowDate: Date
        -returnDate: Date
        +calculateDueDate() Date
        +closeLoan() void
    }

    class Librarian {
        -empId: int
        -name: String
        -password: String
        +addBook(b: Book) void
        +removeBook(isbn: String) void
        +registerMember(m: Member) void
    }

    class Shape {
        <<interface>>
        +draw() void
        +area() float
    }

    class Circle {
        -radius: float
        +draw() void
        +area() float
    }

    Library "1" o-- "*" Book : aggregates
    Member "1" -- "*" Loan : borrows
    Book "1" -- "*" Loan : loaned in
    Library "1" -- "1..*" Librarian : managed by
    Librarian "1" -- "*" Member : registers
    Shape <|.. Circle : realizes
```

**Legend for the Diagram:**
- `-->` : Association
- `o-->` : Aggregation (hollow diamond)
- `*-->` : Composition (filled diamond)
- `<|--` : Inheritance / Generalization
- `<|..` : Realization (implements interface)
- `..>` : Dependency

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Class Diagram Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Statement] --> B[Step 1: Extract Nouns]
    B --> C[Step 2: Filter Valid Classes]
    C --> D[Step 3: Identify Attributes]
    D --> E[Step 4: Identify Methods]
    E --> F[Step 5: Identify Relationships]
    F --> G{Choose Relationship Type}
    G -->|is-a| H[Inheritance]
    G -->|part-of strong| I[Composition]
    G -->|has-a weak| J[Aggregation]
    G -->|uses| K[Association]
    G -->|implements| L[Realization]
    G -->|temporary use| M[Dependency]
    H --> N[Step 6: Add Multiplicity]
    I --> N
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Step 7: Validate in Java]
    O --> P[Final Class Diagram]
```

**Flow Description:** This block diagram shows the systematic **CRISP methodology** (Categorize, Reject, Identify, Specify, Pinpoint) for designing a UML Class Diagram from a problem statement — exactly the workflow expected in KTU exam answers.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Visualization: Relationship Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Two classes need to be connected] --> Q1{Is there an is-a relationship?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Inheritance[Use Generalization: solid line + hollow triangle]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does one class implement an interface?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Realization[Use Realization: dashed line + hollow triangle]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Are parts destroyed when whole is destroyed?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Composition[Use Composition: filled diamond]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Does whole still own parts but they can exist alone?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Aggregation[Use Aggregation: hollow diamond]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is the relationship permanent and strong?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Association[Use Association: solid line]
    Q5 -->|No| Dependency[Use Dependency: dashed line + open arrow]
```

This decision tree is **gold for KTU exams** because it gives you a clear, defensible logic chain when justifying your relationship choice.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer {
        -customerId: int

        +placeOrder() Order

    }

    Customer "1" *-- "1" ShoppingCart : owns
    ShoppingCart "1" *-- "*" CartItem : contains
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Class Diagram. List any four components used to represent a class in a UML Class Diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Class Diagram** is a static UML structural diagram that represents the classes, their attributes, operations, and the relationships among the classes in a system. It is used to model the *blueprint* of an object-oriented system before implementation.

**The four components used to represent a class are:**

1. **Class Name** — written in the top compartment (centered, bold, PascalCase).
2. **Attributes** — written in the middle compartment (with visibility modifiers like `+`, `-`, `#`).
3. **Operations / Methods** — written in the bottom compartment (also with visibility).
4. **Stereotypes / Modifiers** — such as `<<interface>>`, `<<abstract>>`, `<<enumeration>>`, written above the class name.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing 4 components, 1 mark for brief explanation.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Aggregation and Composition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Aggregation | Composition |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Notation** | Hollow (empty) diamond at the whole side | Filled (solid) diamond at the whole side |
| **Strength** | Weak "has-a" relationship | Strong "part-of" relationship |
| **Lifecycle** | Part can exist without the whole | Part cannot exist without the whole |
| **Ownership** | Shared ownership (part may belong to multiple wholes) | Exclusive ownership |
| **Example** | A `Department` has `Faculty` members. If the department closes, the faculty still exist (they get transferred). | A `House` contains `Rooms`. If the house is demolished, the rooms are destroyed. |

*(Mark Distribution: 1.5 marks for table, 1.5 marks for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the various components of a UML Class Diagram. List the different types of relationships between classes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of a UML Class Diagram:**

1. **Classes:** Rectangles with three compartments — class name (top), attributes (middle), and operations (bottom).
2. **Visibility Modifiers:** `+` (public), `-` (private), `#` (protected), `~` (package).
3. **Abstract Classes:** Class name in *italics*, `<<abstract>>` stereotype, may have abstract operations.
4. **Interfaces:** Rectangles with `<<interface>>` stereotype.
5. **Packages:** Used to group related classes logically.
6. **Notes:** A folded-corner rectangle used to add explanatory comments.
7. **Objects:** Instance diagrams sometimes accompany class diagrams for clarity.

**The Six Types of Relationships in Class Diagrams:**

1. **Association** — solid line; represents general connections.
2. **Aggregation** — solid line + hollow diamond; weak "has-a".
3. **Composition** — solid line + filled diamond; strong "part-of".
4. **Generalization (Inheritance)** — solid line + hollow triangle; "is-a".
5. **Realization** — dashed line + hollow triangle; "implements".
6. **Dependency** — dashed line + open arrow; "uses temporarily".

**Diagram:**

```
+------------------------+
|      ClassName         |
+------------------------+
| - privateAttr: Type    |
| # protectedAttr: Type  |
| + publicAttr: Type     |
+------------------------+
| - privateMethod(): Type|
| + publicMethod(): Type |
+------------------------+
```

*(Mark Distribution: 3 marks for components, 2 marks for relationships, 2 marks for diagram.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Design a Class Diagram for an **Online Course Management System** with the following requirements:
- The system has Courses, each with a unique courseId, title, and duration.
- Instructors teach one or more courses.
- Students enroll in courses and can submit Assignments.
- Each Assignment belongs to a course and has a deadline and max marks.
- An Instructor can grade the Assignments.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Classes:**
`Course`, `Instructor`, `Student`, `Assignment`.

**Step 2 — Identify Attributes & Methods:**

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| `Course` | `- courseId: int`, `- title: String`, `- duration: int` | `+ getDetails(): String` |
| `Instructor` | `- empId: int`, `- name: String`, `- specialization: String` | `+ teachCourse(c: Course): void`, `+ gradeAssignment(a: Assignment, marks: int): void` |
| `Student` | `- rollNo: int`, `- name: String`, `- email: String` | `+ enroll(c: Course): void`, `+ submitAssignment(a: Assignment): void` |
| `Assignment` | `- assignmentId: int`, `- deadline: Date`, `- maxMarks: int`, `- marksObtained: int` | `+ getStatus(): String` |

**Step 3 — Relationships:**

| Relationship | Type | Multiplicity |
|--------------|------|--------------|
| Instructor teaches Course | Association | 1 to * |
| Student enrolls in Course | Many-to-Many (via Enrollment) | * to * |
| Course has Assignment | Composition | 1 to * |
| Student submits Assignment | Association | 1 to * |
| Instructor grades Assignment | Association | 1 to * |

**Step 4 — Class Diagram:**

```
+----------------+              +----------------------+
|   Instructor   |1          *  |        Course        |
+----------------+------------->+----------------------+
| - empId: int   |              | - courseId: int      |
| - name: String |              | - title: String      |
| - spec: String |              | - duration: int      |
+----------------+              +----------------------+
| + teachCourse  |              | + getDetails():      |
| + gradeAssign  |              |   String             |
+----------------+              +----------------------+
                                          |1
                                          |
                                          |*
                                  +-------------------+
                                  |    Assignment     |
                                  +-------------------+
                                  | - asgnId: int     |
                                  | - deadline: Date  |
                                  | - maxMarks: int   |
                                  | - marksObtained   |
                                  +-------------------+
                                          |1
                                          |
                                          |*
                                  +-------------------+
                                  |      Student      |
                                  +-------------------+
                                  | - rollNo: int     |
                                  | - name: String    |
                                  | - email: String   |
                                  +-------------------+
                                  | + enroll(c:       |
                                  |   Course): void   |
                                  | + submitAssignment|
                                  |   (a: Assign):void|
                                  +-------------------+
```

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks classes, 2 marks attributes/methods, 2 marks relationships with multiplicity, 1 mark neat diagram.)*

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **multiplicity** in UML Class Diagrams with examples. How do you represent **one-to-one**, **one-to-many**, and **many-to-many** relationships? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Multiplicity** in UML defines **how many instances** of one class are linked to how many instances of another class. It is specified at **both ends** of the relationship line and answers the question: *"How many objects of Class B are associated with one object of Class A?"*

**Common Multiplicity Notations:**

- `1` — exactly one
- `0..1` — zero or one (optional)
- `*` or `0..*` — zero or many
- `1..*` — one or many
- `n..m` — between n and m (specific range)

**Examples:**

**(i) One-to-One (1 : 1):**
*"A Country has exactly one Capital, and a Capital belongs to exactly one Country."*

```
+----------+ 1   1 +-----------+
|  Country |--------|  Capital  |
+----------+        +-----------+
```

**(ii) One-to-Many (1 : *):**
*"A Department employs many Employees, but each Employee works in exactly one Department."*

```
+-----------+ 1   * +-----------+
| Department|--------|  Employee |
+-----------+        +-----------+
```

**(iii) Many-to-Many (* : *):**
*"A Student can enroll in many Courses, and a Course can have many Students."*

```
+--------+ *   * +---------+
| Student|--------| Course  |
+--------+        +---------+
```

Often many-to-many relationships are decomposed using an **associative class** (e.g., `Enrollment`) to store additional data.

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks definition, 3 marks for the three examples with diagrams, 2 marks for associative class mention.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Design a Class Diagram for a **Hospital Management System** with the following requirements:
- The Hospital has many Departments (Cardiology, Neurology, etc.).
- Each Department has a name and a head Doctor.
- Doctors treat Patients, and each Patient is admitted under one Department.
- Patients are assigned a unique patientId, name, age, and disease.
- Doctors have a specialization and can prescribe Medicines.
- A Patient can be prescribed multiple Medicines.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Classes:** `Hospital`, `Department`, `Doctor`, `Patient`, `Medicine`, `Prescription`.

**Step 2 — Attributes & Methods:**

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| `Hospital` | `- name: String`, `- address: String` | `+ addDepartment(d: Department): void` |

| `Doctor` | `- docId: int`, `- name: String`, `- specialization: String` | `+ treatPatient(p: Patient): void`, `+ prescribeMedicine(p: Patient, m: Medicine): Prescription` |
| `Patient` | `- patientId: int`, `- name: String`, `- age: int`, `- disease: String` | `+ getPrescriptions(): List<Prescription>` |
| `Medicine` | `- medId: int`, `- name: String`, `- dosage: String` | `+ getDetails(): String` |
| `Prescription` | `- date: Date`, `- dosage: String` | `+ getDetails(): String` |

| Relationship | Type | Multiplicity |

| Hospital has Department | Aggregation | 1 to * |
| Department headed by Doctor | Association | 1 to 1 |
| Doctor treats Patient | Association | * to * |
| Patient admitted in Department | Association | * to 1 |
| Doctor prescribes Medicine (via Prescription) | Association | * to * |
| Patient has Prescription | Aggregation | 1 to * |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS (KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Missing visibility symbols (`+`, `-`, `#`):** Deducts **0.5 to 1 mark per class**. Always include visibility.
> 2. **Wrong arrow direction for inheritance:** The hollow triangle must point to the **PARENT class** (e.g., `Car ────▷ Vehicle`, not the reverse). Reversed arrows = **0 marks** for the relationship.
> 3. **Drawing Aggregation when it should be Composition:** If the "part" cannot exist without the "whole" (e.g., Room-House, OrderLine-Order), it is **Composition**, not Aggregation. Wrong diamond = **lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting multiplicity on both ends:** Multiplicity must be written at **both ends** of the relationship line. Missing it = **0.5 mark deduction** per relationship.
> 5. **Mixing static and instance notations:** Static members must be **underlined** in the class diagram. Non-underlined static members = minor deduction.
> 6. **Conflating Interface and Class notations:** Interfaces use `<<interface>>` and are *realized* (dashed line), not *extended* (solid line). Using the wrong line style = **2 marks lost**.
> 7. **Writing implementation details in the class diagram:** Avoid writing getters/setters unless they perform meaningful logic. UML is about design, not boilerplate code.
> 8. **Not justifying the relationship choice:** In descriptive questions, always **state WHY** you chose Aggregation over Composition (e.g., "Faculty can exist independently of Department, hence Aggregation"). This justification carries **2-3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ A **Class Diagram** is a **static** UML structural diagram showing classes, attributes, operations, and relationships.
- ✅ A class is drawn as a **3-compartment rectangle**: Name (top), Attributes (middle), Operations (bottom).
- ✅ **Visibility symbols:** `+` (public), `-` (private), `#` (protected), `~` (package).
- ✅ **Abstract classes** have italicized names; **interfaces** use `<<interface>>` stereotype.
- ✅ **Six relationship types:**
    - **Association** — solid line (general connection)
    - **Aggregation** — hollow diamond (weak "has-a")
    - **Composition** — filled diamond (strong "part-of")
    - **Generalization** — solid line + hollow triangle ("is-a")
    - **Realization** — dashed line + hollow triangle ("implements")
    - **Dependency** — dashed line + open arrow ("uses temporarily")
- ✅ **Multiplicity notations:** `1`, `0..1`, `*` (or `0..*`), `1..*`, `n..m`.
- ✅ Use the **CRISP methodology** (Categorize, Reject, Identify, Specify, Pinpoint) to extract classes from a problem statement.
- ✅ **Java mapping:** Classes → `class`, attributes → fields, operations → methods, inheritance → `extends`, realization → `implements`.
- ✅ **Always include** visibility, multiplicity, and proper relationship notation in diagrams.
- ✅ The class diagram is the **primary deliverable** of the OOAD design phase and maps **directly to Java code**.

### 🎯 Top 5 High-Yield Topics for KTU 2024

1. **Drawing class diagrams from problem statements** (most common 14-mark question).
2. **Differentiating aggregation, composition, and association** (3-7 mark questions).
3. **Multiplicity and its notations** (2-3 mark questions).
4. **Identifying abstract classes and interfaces** (2-5 mark questions).
5. **Translating class diagrams to Java code** (bonus marks in 14-mark questions).

### 📋 Quick-Fire Definitions (for last-minute revision)

- **Class:** A blueprint for creating objects.
- **Object:** An instance of a class.
- **Attribute:** A property/field of a class.
- **Operation:** A function/method of a class.
- **Inheritance:** Mechanism to derive a class from another ("is-a").
- **Encapsulation:** Hiding internal details using visibility modifiers.
- **Abstraction:** Showing only essential features, hiding background details.
- **Polymorphism:** One interface, many implementations.
- **Interface:** A pure abstract class with no implementation.
- **Association:** General structural relationship between classes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction Diagrams
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interaction Diagrams

# Interaction Diagrams — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interaction Diagrams** are a subset of UML (Unified Modeling Language) **Behavioural Diagrams** that depict the **dynamic behaviour** of a system by showing the **flow of messages exchanged between objects** over time to accomplish a specific use case or scenario.

As per the **OMG UML 2.5.1 specification** (the de-facto reference standard for KTU evaluation), interaction diagrams belong to the family of *Interaction* classifiers and are broadly classified into **four (4) primary types**:

| # | Diagram Type | Primary Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sequence Diagram** | Time-ordering of messages along vertical lifelines |
| 2 | **Communication Diagram** | Structural links between objects + message sequencing |
| 3 | **Timing Diagram** | State changes vs. linear time axis |
| 4 | **Interaction Overview Diagram** | High-level activity-flow of multiple interactions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Focus:** The syllabus specifically emphasizes **Sequence Diagrams** and **Communication Diagrams**, with a passing reference to Timing Diagrams. The Interaction Overview Diagram is a supplementary advanced topic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Stage Play" Analogy for Interaction Diagrams**
>
> Imagine a **theatrical stage play**:
> - The **actors** standing on stage = **Objects / Participants** in the system
> - The **script** they are reading = the **Use Case scenario**
> - The **dialogue spoken in order** = **Messages passed between objects**
> - The **chronological order of dialogues** = **Sequence Diagram** (captures *time*)
> - The **stage layout showing who is facing whom** = **Communication Diagram** (captures *structure + time*)
> - The **curtain rising and falling at precise moments** = **Timing Diagram** (captures *state changes on a timeline*)
>
> Just as a play cannot be understood from a static photo, an object-oriented system cannot be fully understood from class diagrams alone — we need the *performance script* of how objects collaborate at runtime. **That script is precisely what an Interaction Diagram represents.**

---

## 1.3 Why Interaction Diagrams Are Indispensable in OOAD

1. **Complement Class Diagrams** — Class diagrams show *what* the system has; interaction diagrams show *what the system does* at runtime.
2. **Traceability** — Every use case (from the Use Case Diagram) is realized by one or more interaction diagrams.
3. **Design Validation** — They expose methods that must exist on classes, helping refine the class structure.
4. **Communication Tool** — They act as the **contract** between analysts, designers, and developers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard UML Notational Symbols (per KTU Valuation Key):**
> - **Filled (solid) arrow head** = *Synchronous* message
> - **Open (stick) arrow head** = *Asynchronous* message
> - **Dashed arrow with open head** = *Return* message
> - **Solid rectangle (bar)** = *Activation* (focus of control)
> - **Dashed vertical line** = *Lifeline* of an object
> - **Actor stick figure** = external user/system

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (KTU Conventions)

> [!NOTE]
> In Interaction Diagrams, the following are the **standard UML metrics** universally accepted in KTU examination scripts:
>
> - **Message number format:** `[SequenceNumber][: [GuardCondition] [ReturnValue :=] MessageName([Arguments])]`
> - **Activation bar height:** Proportional to *execution duration* (relative, not absolute)
> - **Lifeline length:** Arbitrary; conventionally same height for readability
> - **Frame notation:** `sd` (sequence), `cd` (communication), `td` (timing)
> - **Iteration marker:** `*` (e.g., `*draw()` for loop)
> - **Conditional marker:** `[condition]` (e.g., `[balance > 0]withdraw()`)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Interaction Diagrams

```
Interaction Diagrams (UML 2.5.1)
│
├── 1. Sequence Diagram
│   ├── Lifelines (vertical dashed lines)
│   ├── Activation Bars (focus of control)
│   ├── Messages (sync, async, return, self)
│   ├── Combined Fragments (alt, opt, loop, par, break, ref)
│   └── Frame: sd
│
├── 2. Communication Diagram
│   ├── Objects (vertices)
│   ├── Links (edges — associations)
│   ├── Messages with sequence numbering (1, 1.1, 1.2, 2, ...)
│   └── Frame: cd
│
├── 3. Timing Diagram
│   ├── Lifeline with state/condition timeline
│   ├── Time axis (horizontal)
│   └── Frame: td
│
└── 4. Interaction Overview Diagram
    ├── Activity nodes (frames = sd, cd, td)
    ├── Decision and fork nodes
    └── Frame: iod
```

---

## 2.2 Sequence Diagram — Operational Logic Steps

A **Sequence Diagram** models the **time-ordered** interaction between objects. The **Y-axis = time (top → bottom)** and the **X-axis = different objects/actors**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition:

1. **Identify Participants (Actors & Objects)**
   - The **actor** triggering the scenario is placed on the **left**.
   - Subsequent objects are arranged left-to-right in approximate *order of activation*.

2. **Establish Lifelines**
   - Each participant has a **vertical dashed line** descending from its name rectangle.
   - The lifeline represents the *existence* of the object over time.
   - A lifeline may end with a **stop** (`X`) symbol indicating object destruction.

3. **Apply Activation Bars**
   - A **thin rectangle** drawn on a lifeline shows the *period during which the object is active* (executing a method).
   - **Nested** activation bars represent *recursion* or *internal sub-calls*.

4. **Draw Messages (Top → Bottom = Chronological Order)**
   - Messages are **horizontal arrows** between lifelines.
   - Each message is **labelled** with the sequence number, method name, and parameters.

5. **Show Return Values (Optional)**
   - **Dashed** arrows back to the caller indicate return values.
   - Often omitted in KTU diagrams for compactness — only mandatory if the return value is significant.

6. **Add Combined Fragments (For Complex Scenarios)**
   - Use **interaction frames** with operators: `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `strict`, `neg`, `ref`.

---

## 2.3 Message Type Cheat Sheet (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these arrow types — they appear in every KTU board exam question on Interaction Diagrams.**

| Message Type | Arrow Notation | When Used | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous Call** | → solid line, **filled triangle** | Caller **waits** for return | **Very High** |
| **Asynchronous Call** | → solid line, **open arrow** | Caller does **not wait** (e.g., thread spawn) | High |
| **Return Message** | - - - → dashed line, **open arrow** | Optional; shows return value | Medium |
| **Self Message** | U-turn arrow on same lifeline | Object calls its own method | High |
| **Create Message** | → solid line, **dashed** | `new` object instantiation | Medium |
| **Destroy Message** | → terminating in **X** | Object deallocation | Low |
| **Found Message** | → from filled black circle | Message from unknown source | Low |

---

## 2.4 Communication Diagram — Operational Logic Steps

A **Communication Diagram** (formerly *Collaboration Diagram* in UML 1.x) emphasizes the **structural relationships** between objects while still showing message flow via **hierarchical numbering**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition:

1. **Place Objects as Vertices** — Use the `ObjectName : ClassName` notation inside a rectangle.
2. **Draw Links as Edges** — Solid lines connecting objects represent *associations*; can be labelled with role names and multiplicities.
3. **Number Messages Sequentially**
   - First-level: `1`, `2`, `3` …
   - Nested calls: `1.1`, `1.2`, `2.1` …
   - Concurrency: prefix with a letter segment, e.g., `1a`, `1b` (newer UML 2.x style) or use `*` for parallel.
4. **Direction of Arrow** indicates message flow along the link.
5. **Message Format:** `[SeqNo] [Guard] [ReturnVar :=] MessageName(args)`

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Pearl:** A **Sequence Diagram** answers *"When does the message occur?"* while a **Communication Diagram** answers *"Which object links to which, and in what order?"* Both convey the *same information* but emphasize different aspects.

---

## 2.5 KTU Comparison Cheat Sheet — When to Use Which Diagram

| Criterion | Sequence Diagram | Communication Diagram | Timing Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary View** | Time ordering | Object links + order | State vs. time |
| **Best for** | Procedural/algorithmic flow | Structural relationships | Real-time, embedded systems |
| **Message numbering** | Implicit (top-to-bottom) | Explicit (1, 1.1, 1.2 …) | N/A |
| **Combined fragments** | Fully supported | Limited | Limited |
| **Time axis** | Vertical (implicit) | Absent | Horizontal (explicit) |
| **KTU 2024 Frequency** | **Very High** (≥80% questions) | **High** (15-20%) | **Low** (rarely) |
| **Tool support** | StarUML, Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm | Same | Same |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Interaction Diagrams Are Used in Industry (relevant for KTU viva):**
> - **Telecommunications** — Call flow analysis in VoIP/GSM systems (uses Sequence + Timing)
> - **Banking Software** — Transaction flow in ATM/POS systems (Sequence Diagrams for state machine validation)
> - **Embedded & IoT** — Sensor-actuator coordination (Timing Diagrams for strict timing constraints)
> - **Web Services** — SOAP/REST invocation flow (Sequence Diagrams for orchestration)
> - **Microservices** — Distributed tracing visualization (modern adoption)

---

## 2.7 Mathematical Representation (Sequence Numbering Grammar)

The sequence numbering in a **Communication Diagram** follows a strict **prefix-notation grammar**:

$$
M \rightarrow N \;\vert\; N.M \;\vert\; N.M.K
$$

Where:
- $M$ = the complete message number expression
- $N$ = a positive integer denoting the *level* (or *priority*) of the message
- $M, K$ = sub-numbering for nested calls (recursion / method chaining)

**Boundary conditions / rules:**
- Each top-level number must be **unique** within the diagram.
- A nested number $N.M$ means: *"the $M^{th}$ message invoked as a direct result of message $N$"*.
- The notation `[1a, 1b, 1c]` may be used in UML 2.x to indicate *concurrent* flows.

---

## 2.8 Combined Fragments — Operator Reference

| Operator | Meaning | KTU Usage |
|---|---|---|
| `alt` | Alternative flows (like `if-else`) | Very High |
| `opt` | Optional flow (like `if`) | High |
| `loop` | Iterative flow (like `for`/`while`) | Very High |
| `par` | Parallel execution | Medium |
| `break` | Exception/exit early | Medium |
| `neg` | Invalid/forbidden trace | Low |
| `ref` | Reference to another interaction | Low |
| `strict` | Strict ordering of messages | Low |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Message Sequencing Number in Communication Diagrams

Communication diagrams use a **hierarchical numbering scheme** to convey the *call tree structure* of an interaction. Below is the formal derivation of the numbering algorithm.

### Derivation 1 — Flat (Non-Nested) Numbering

$$
S_1 = \{1, 2, 3, \dots, n\}
$$

**Textual Logic:** When there are **no nested calls**, the messages are simply numbered $1$ through $n$ in the order they occur on the same level. Here, $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ represents the *total message count* at level 1.

---

### Derivation 2 — Nested Numbering (Two Levels)

$$
S_2 = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \left\{ i.j \;\middle|\; j \in \{1, 2, \dots, k_i\} \right\}
$$

**Textual Logic:** For each top-level message $i$, we append a sub-counter $j$ ranging from $1$ to $k_i$, where $k_i$ is the *number of nested messages* invoked *as a consequence* of message $i$.

---

### Derivation 3 — General Recursive Form (Arbitrary Depth)

$$
S_{\text{general}} = \bigcup_{d=1}^{D_{\max}} \left\{ a_1.a_2.\dots.a_d \;\middle|\; a_1 \in \mathbb{Z}^+, a_p \in \mathbb{Z}^+, \forall p \in [2, d] \right\}
$$

**Textual Logic:** A sequence number at depth $d$ is a dotted sequence of $d$ positive integers. The maximum depth $D_{\max}$ equals the **longest call-chain** in the interaction.

---

### Worked Example — Derivation of Sequence Numbers for an ATM Withdrawal

Consider an ATM system: A user inserts a card → enters PIN → requests withdrawal → system validates → dispenses cash.

**Step 1:** Top-level message from User to ATM = $1$
**Step 2:** ATM requests PIN from User = $2$
**Step 3:** User enters PIN (return to ATM) = $3$
**Step 4:** User requests withdrawal amount = $4$
**Step 5:** ATM (in response to 4) calls BankServer to validate = $4.1$
**Step 6:** BankServer returns status to ATM = $4.1.1$
**Step 7:** ATM returns response to User = $4.2$

**Resulting sequence-number set:**

$$
S = \{1,\; 2,\; 3,\; 4,\; 4.1,\; 4.1.1,\; 4.2\}
$$

**Validation:** Maximum depth $D_{\max} = 3$ (message $4.1.1$).

---

### Derivation 4 — Concurrency Numbering (UML 2.x)

For **parallel flows** spawned from a single message $i$:

$$
C_i = \{i.a,\; i.b,\; i.c,\; \dots\}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Lowercase letter suffixes ($a, b, c, \ldots$) denote *concurrent* message branches originating from the same trigger $i$. This is the modern UML 2.x replacement for the older `*` prefix.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Message-to-Lifeline Mapping

For any interaction, the **message-mapping matrix** $\mathbf{M}$ captures *who sends what to whom*:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix}
m_{11} & m_{12} & \cdots & m_{1n} \\
m_{21} & m_{22} & \cdots & m_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
m_{n1} & m_{n2} & \cdots & m_{nn}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $m_{ij}$ = the message count from object $i$ to object $j$ during the interaction
- $n$ = total number of participating objects
- Diagonal elements $m_{ii}$ = *self-messages* (the object calls its own method)
- Off-diagonal zeros imply *no direct communication* — useful for validating class-diagram associations.

**Boundary check:** $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} m_{ij} = N_{\text{total messages}}$

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **not** drawn in KTU exams but is conceptually useful for verifying the completeness of an interaction (e.g., in a design review).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Activation Bar Timing (Relative Duration)

In a sequence diagram, the **activation bar height** is *proportional* to the method's execution time. Formally:

$$
h_{\text{bar}} \propto t_{\text{method}} \quad \text{where} \quad t_{\text{method}} = t_{\text{cpu}} + t_{\text{io}} + t_{\text{network}}
$$

- $t_{\text{cpu}}$ = CPU computation time
- $t_{\text{io}}$ = I/O wait time (disk, console)
- $t_{\text{network}}$ = network round-trip time

**For KTU purposes**, only the *relative* ordering matters — not the absolute scale. The convention is to maintain a **consistent visual height** unless timing is explicitly relevant (as in a Timing Diagram).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Combined Fragment Notation

A **combined fragment** is a *boolean composition* of sub-interactions. The general rule:

$$
\text{Frag} = \text{Operator} \cdot \text{Guard} \cdot \text{(Operand}_1 \cup \text{Operand}_2 \cup \dots \cup \text{Operand}_k)
$$

For the **`alt` operator** (the most-asked in KTU):

$$
\text{alt} = \bigvee_{i=1}^{k} G_i \implies O_i
$$

Where $G_i$ = guard condition, $O_i$ = operand interaction, and only **one** $G_i$ evaluates to `true` at runtime (mutually exclusive).

For the **`par` operator** (concurrent):

$$
\text{par} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} O_i \quad \text{(all operands execute concurrently)}
$$

For the **`loop` operator** (iteration):

$$
\text{loop} = \text{Repeat}(O,\; \text{min},\; \text{max},\; \text{condition})
$$

Where `min` and `max` are iteration bounds (default 0 and $\infty$).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequence Diagram — Generic Frame Structure

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor A as User Actor
    participant O1 as Object1 Class
    participant O2 as Object2 Class
    participant O3 as Object3 Class

    A->>+O1: 1 : login(username, password)
    O1->>+O2: 1.1 : validateCredentials(user)
    O2-->>-O1: 1.1.1 : boolean result
    O1-->>-A: 1.2 : LoginResponse token
    Note over O1,O2: Authentication Subsystem
    A->>+O1: 2 : logout()
    O1->>-O2: 2.1 : invalidateSession(token)
```

**Reading guide for KTU:**
- `actor` = external user
- `participant` = internal object
- `->>+` = synchronous call with **activation** bar opened
- `-->>-` = return message closing the activation
- `Note over` = annotation box

---

## 4.2 Communication Diagram — Generic Frame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Communication_Diagram_Frame ["cd ATMWithdrawal"]
        direction LR
        user["User : Customer"]
        atm["ATM : Terminal"]
        bank["BankServer : Server"]
        account["Account : BankAccount"]
        dispenser["CashDispenser : Hardware"]

        user -- "1 : insertCard()" --- atm
        atm -- "2 : verifyPin(pin)" --- user
        user -- "3 : enterPin()" --- atm
        atm -- "4 : withdraw(amount)" --- bank
        bank -- "4.1 : checkBalance()" --- account
        account -- "4.1.1 : balance" --- bank
        bank -- "4.2 : approved" --- atm
        atm -- "5 : dispenseCash(amount)" --- dispenser
        dispenser -- "5.1 : cashOut" --- atm
        atm -- "6 : response" --- user
    end
```

**Reading guide:**
- Each **rectangle** = an object (`ObjectName : ClassName`)
- Each **solid line** = a link (association)
- Each **labelled arrow** = a numbered message

---

## 4.3 Timing Diagram — Generic Frame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Timing_Diagram_Frame ["td SensorPolling"]
        direction LR
        t0["t=0 ms"] --> t1["t=10 ms"] --> t2["t=20 ms"] --> t3["t=30 ms"] --> t4["t=40 ms"]
        t0 --- s1["Sensor1: Idle"]
        t1 --- s1a["Sensor1: Active"]
        t2 --- s1b["Sensor1: Active"]
        t3 --- s1c["Sensor1: Idle"]
        t4 --- s1d["Sensor1: Idle"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid has **limited native support** for true Gantt/UML-Timing diagrams. The above is a **block-level functional representation** of the time-axis, which is the recommended fallback per the engine protocol.

---

## 4.4 Interaction Overview Diagram — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> InitFrame["sd InitSession"]
    InitFrame --> AuthFrame["sd AuthenticateUser"]
    AuthFrame --> DecisionGate{Valid PIN?}
    DecisionGate -- Yes --> MainFrame["sd MainMenu"]
    DecisionGate -- No --> ErrorFrame["sd ShowError"]
    ErrorFrame --> AuthFrame
    MainFrame --> WithdrawFrame["sd WithdrawCash"]
    MainFrame --> DepositFrame["sd DepositCash"]
    MainFrame --> End([End])
    WithdrawFrame --> End
    DepositFrame --> End
```

**Reading guide:**
- Rectangular nodes = **interaction frames** (`sd`, `cd`, or `td`)
- Diamond nodes = **decision** points
- Rounded nodes = **initial/final** nodes
- This is essentially an **activity diagram where the actions are themselves interactions**.

---

## 4.5 Anatomy of a Sequence-Diagram Message (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Sender Object"] -->|"[SeqNo] [: Guard] [ReturnVar :=] MsgName(args)"| B["Receiver Object"]
    style A fill:#e1f5e1
    style B fill:#e1e1f5
```

**Example annotations on the arrow:**
- `1.2 : getBalance() : float` — sequence 1.2, no guard, returns float
- `[loggedIn] 2 : logout()` — sequence 2, only if `loggedIn == true`

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

**Q1.** *Differentiate between a Sequence Diagram and a Communication Diagram. List any three points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Sequence Diagram | Communication Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time is the **primary axis** (vertical, top-to-bottom) | Object **links** are the primary focus; structure is shown explicitly |
| 2 | Message ordering is **implicit** (visual position = order) | Message ordering is **explicit** (numerical prefixes: 1, 1.1, 1.2 …) |
| 3 | **Combined fragments** (alt, opt, loop, par) are fully supported | Combined fragments have **limited** support |
| 4 | Best for **time-critical** or **algorithmic** flows | Best for understanding **structural collaboration** |
| 5 | UML 2.x name; the older term was "Event Trace" | Earlier called **Collaboration Diagram** in UML 1.x |

> *(Award 1 mark per valid point × 3)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *What is a Combined Fragment in UML 2.x? Name any four operators used with it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Combined Fragment** is a region of an interaction (sequence diagram) in which a complex boolean condition or iteration is expressed through one or more **operands**, each guarded by a condition and labelled with an **interaction operator**.

**Four operators (0.5 mark each × 4 = 2 marks, plus 1 mark for definition):**

1. `alt` — alternatives (mutually exclusive flows)
2. `opt` — optional flow
3. `loop` — iterative flow
4. `par` — parallel concurrent flows

*(Accept also: `break`, `neg`, `ref`, `strict`, `seq`, `critical`, `consider`, `ignore`)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Sequence Diagram Focus**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Draw a **UML Sequence Diagram** for the online **"Book Purchase"** use case in a Library Management System. The system flow is:

> A *Member* searches for a book → *SearchController* queries *BookDatabase* → results are returned → the *Member* selects a book and requests issue → *IssueController* checks *MemberRecord* for dues → if no dues, *BookInventory* marks the book as issued and returns confirmation to the *Member*.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Explain any **four** types of messages used in a Sequence Diagram with their notation and usage. Justify why **synchronous messages** are the most frequently used in OOAD.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sequence Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor M as Member
    participant SC as SearchController
    participant BD as BookDatabase
    participant IC as IssueController
    participant MR as MemberRecord
    participant BI as BookInventory

    M->>+SC: 1 : searchBook(title)
    SC->>+BD: 1.1 : queryBooks(title)
    BD-->>-SC: 1.1.1 : List of Book objects
    SC-->>-M: 1.2 : searchResults

    M->>+SC: 2 : selectBook(bookId)
    SC->>+IC: 2.1 : issueRequest(bookId, memberId)
    IC->>+MR: 2.1.1 : checkDues(memberId)
    MR-->>-IC: 2.1.2 : noDues = true
    alt noDues is true
        IC->>+BI: 2.1.3 : markAsIssued(bookId, memberId)
        BI-->>-IC: 2.1.4 : issueConfirmation
        IC-->>-SC: 2.2 : issueSuccess
        SC-->>-M: 2.3 : Book issued successfully
    else noDues is false
        IC-->>-SC: 2.2 : issueFailed (dues pending)
        SC-->>-M: 2.3 : Cannot issue — clear dues first
    end
```

**Key elements scored (rubric):**
- 1 mark — Correct identification of 6 participants (Member + 5 objects)
- 1 mark — Correct use of lifelines
- 1 mark — Synchronous + return messages
- 1 mark — Proper use of `alt` combined fragment
- 1 mark — Activation bars correctly placed
- 1 mark — Sequence numbers (or implied order) consistent
- 1 mark — Neatness, label clarity, frame `sd`

---

**(b) Four Message Types:**

| # | Type | Notation | Usage in OOAD |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Synchronous** | `→` solid, filled triangle | Caller blocks until reply. **Most common in OOAD** because object collaboration is *transactional* — caller usually needs the result before proceeding. |
| 2 | **Asynchronous** | `→` solid, open arrow | Caller does not block. Used for event-driven systems, message queues, fire-and-forget triggers. |
| 3 | **Return** | `- - ->` dashed, open arrow | Shows the return value of a previous synchronous call. Often omitted when return value is unused. |
| 4 | **Self-Message** | U-turn arrow on same lifeline | Internal method call; encapsulation — object delegates to private logic. Common for `validate()`, `notify()`, `log()` calls. |

**Why synchronous is most frequent (3-mark justification):**
1. **Object encapsulation** — the caller almost always needs the result to decide the next step.
2. **Deterministic ordering** — synchronous calls preserve a clear call stack, simplifying debugging and testability.
3. **OO design philosophy** — methods are typically *request-response* in nature; asynchronous is reserved for parallel/concurrent subsystems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Communication Diagram + Comparison Focus**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Draw a **UML Communication Diagram** for the same **"Book Purchase"** use case described in Question A. Show the **link structure** clearly and use proper **hierarchical sequence numbering**.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Compare and contrast **Sequence Diagram, Communication Diagram, and Timing Diagram** in a detailed table covering: (i) primary focus, (ii) time representation, (iii) message-numbering scheme, (iv) typical use case, (v) tool support, (vi) KTU exam weightage, and (vii) at least one limitation.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Communication Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CD_Frame ["cd BookIssue"]
        direction LR
        member["Member : Member"]
        sc["SearchController : Controller"]
        bd["BookDatabase : DAO"]
        ic["IssueController : Controller"]
        mr["MemberRecord : DAO"]
        bi["BookInventory : DAO"]

        member -- "1 : searchBook(title)" --- sc
        sc -- "1.1 : queryBooks(title)" --- bd
        bd -- "1.1.1 : results" --- sc
        sc -- "1.2 : searchResults" --- member

        member -- "2 : selectBook(bookId)" --- sc
        sc -- "2.1 : issueRequest(...)" --- ic
        ic -- "2.1.1 : checkDues(memberId)" --- mr
        mr -- "2.1.2 : noDues" --- ic
        ic -- "2.1.3 : markAsIssued(...)" --- bi
        bi -- "2.1.4 : confirmation" --- ic
        ic -- "2.2 : issueSuccess" --- sc
        sc -- "2.3 : response" --- member
    end
```

**Key elements scored (rubric):**
- 1 mark — All 6 objects placed as vertices
- 1 mark — Links drawn correctly between interacting objects
- 1 mark — Hierarchical numbering (1, 1.1, 1.1.1, 2, 2.1, 2.1.1, …)
- 1 mark — Message labels with method names
- 1 mark — Arrow direction indicating message flow
- 1 mark — Frame `cd` and clean layout
- 1 mark — Numbers match the sequence-diagram version (consistency)

---

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Sequence Diagram | Communication Diagram | Timing Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Primary Focus** | Temporal ordering of messages | Object links + message flow | State change vs. time |
| **(ii) Time Representation** | Implicit (vertical axis = time) | None | Explicit (horizontal axis = time) |
| **(iii) Message Numbering** | Implicit by position | Explicit hierarchical (1, 1.1, 2.1.1) | N/A |
| **(iv) Typical Use Case** | Algorithmic flows, use-case realization | Architecture & link discovery | Real-time, embedded, hardware timing |
| **(v) Tool Support** | StarUML, VP, Rational Rose, PlantUML | Same | Limited (mostly VP, EA) |
| **(vi) KTU Exam Weightage** | Very High (≥80%) | High (15-20%) | Low (<5%) |
| **(vii) Limitation** | Becomes cluttered for ≥20 objects | Time-ordering hard to read; no combined fragments | Cannot show message content directly |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing lifelines with activations** — A lifeline is a *dashed vertical line*; an activation is a *thin solid rectangle* on top of the lifeline. Do **not** draw the activation as a separate column.
> 2. **Wrong arrowhead for return messages** — Use **dashed** line with **open** arrow, not solid filled triangle.
> 3. **Forgetting to enclose diagram in an `sd` / `cd` frame** — UML 2.x mandates a frame; KTU awards 0.5 mark for the frame.
> 4. **Sequence numbers starting from 0** — Always start from **1** (UML convention).
> 5. **Drawing a return arrow for every synchronous call** — Not mandatory; only show if the return value is significant.
> 6. **Mixing Sequence and Communication notations** — e.g., putting hierarchical numbers in a Sequence Diagram or vertical time-axis in a Communication Diagram.
> 7. **Missing the `alt` / `loop` guard conditions** — Each operand in a combined fragment **must** have a `[guard]` label (or `[else]` for the default branch).
> 8. **Using `actor` syntax instead of `participant`** — Use `actor` only for **external** users; use `participant` for **internal** objects. (Mermaid-specific pitfall.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1: Interaction Diagrams**
>
> - [ ] Interaction Diagrams are **Behavioural UML diagrams** showing **message flow** between objects.
> - [ ] **Four types:** Sequence, Communication, Timing, Interaction Overview.
> - [ ] **Sequence Diagram** = *time-ordered*; **Communication Diagram** = *structure + order*; **Timing Diagram** = *state vs. time*; **Interaction Overview** = *activity of interactions*.
> - [ ] Y-axis of Sequence Diagram = time; X-axis = participants.
> - [ ] Lifeline = dashed vertical line; Activation = solid rectangle on lifeline.
> - [ ] **Synchronous** = filled triangle; **Asynchronous** = open arrow; **Return** = dashed open arrow.
> - [ ] Sequence numbering grammar: $1$, $1.1$, $1.1.1$, etc. (positive integers, dotted).
> - [ ] Combined fragment operators (must-know): `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `break`.
> - [ ] Frame notation: `sd` (sequence), `cd` (communication), `td` (timing), `iod` (interaction overview).
> - [ ] Self-message = U-turn arrow on same lifeline.
> - [ ] Create message = dashed solid arrow; Destroy = `X` at lifeline end.
> - [ ] UML 2.x renamed "Collaboration Diagram" → **Communication Diagram**.
> - [ ] **Activation bar height** is proportional to execution duration (relative, not absolute).
> - [ ] Communication diagram is preferred when **structural relationships** are key.
> - [ ] Sequence diagram is preferred when **temporal flow** and **combined fragments** are key.
> - [ ] Timing diagram is preferred for **real-time, embedded, hardware-driven** systems.
> - [ ] Message-mapping matrix $\mathbf{M}$ validates completeness: $\sum m_{ij} = N_{\text{total}}$.
> - [ ] **For KTU board exams:** always enclose the diagram in a labelled frame, use `actor` for users, `participant` for objects, and number messages from **1**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Machine Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** State Machine Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **State Machine Diagram** (also called a **State Diagram** or **Statechart Diagram**) is a **UML (Unified Modeling Language) behavioral diagram** that models the dynamic behavior of a single object by capturing the **sequence of states** it passes through during its lifecycle in response to **events**, along with the **actions** performed and **guard conditions** evaluated during state transitions.

According to the **OMG UML 2.5 Specification**, a state machine is a *behavior* that specifies the sequences of states an object undergoes in response to events, together with its responses to those events. It is one of the **five UML 2.5 diagrams** used to model dynamic behavior, the others being *Activity*, *Sequence*, *Communication*, and *Timing* diagrams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (PEITT411 – Module 1):**
> State Machine Diagrams are a **mandatory topic under UML Behavioral Modeling**. The examiner expects you to:
> 1. Draw state machines for given problem statements.
> 2. Identify states, events, transitions, guard conditions, and actions.
> 3. Differentiate between **State Machine Diagrams** and **Activity Diagrams**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Traffic Light Controller"

Imagine a **traffic light at a junction**. At any given moment, the light is in **exactly one** state — *Red*, *Yellow*, or *Green*. It cannot be in two states simultaneously. The light does not jump randomly; it follows a **strict, deterministic sequence** governed by a timer event.

- The **state** is the current condition (e.g., "Light is Green").
- The **event** is the trigger (e.g., `timer expires`).
- The **transition** is the change (e.g., Green → Yellow).
- The **action** is what happens during the change (e.g., `stopGreenLight()`).

A State Machine Diagram is essentially the **engineering blueprint** of this lifecycle — it tells us *what the object looks like at any moment* and *how it reacts to the outside world*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use code?** Because code shows *how*, but a state diagram shows *what* and *when*. For an object with more than 3–4 states, a diagram becomes the single source of truth that the entire development team (developers, testers, analysts) can read and validate *before* writing a single line of Java.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Vocabulary in KTU Examinations

| Term | Standard UML Notation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **State** | Rounded rectangle |
| **Initial Pseudostate** | **Filled black circle** |
| **Final State** | **Bullseye** (filled circle inside ring) |
| **Transition** | Directed solid arrow |
| **Self-Transition** | Arrow looping back to the same state |
| **Internal Transition** | Action written inside the state box |
| **Composite State** | State containing nested sub-diagram |
| **History Pseudostate** | **H** inside a circle |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"An Object Walks, Pauses, Acts."* — **W**hat (state), **P**osture of reaction (event), **A**ction (effect).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a State Machine Diagram – The Five Building Blocks

A UML State Machine Diagram is constructed from five fundamental building blocks. The KTU examiner frequently tests each block in isolation.

### 2.1.1 States

A **state** is a condition or situation in the lifecycle of an object during which it satisfies some condition, performs an activity, or waits for an event. It is represented as a **rounded rectangle** with the state name inside, optionally divided into **three compartments**:

1. **Name compartment** – the state identifier.
2. **Internal transitions compartment** – actions triggered without leaving the state (e.g., `entry /`, `do /`, `exit /`).
3. **Substates compartment** – nested sub-diagram for composite states.

**Two kinds of states:**

- **Simple state** – has no substructure.
- **Composite state** – contains one or more nested state machines (a subdiagram drawn inside it).

### 2.1.2 Transitions

A **transition** is a directed relationship between two states, indicating that an object in the first state will, upon the occurrence of a specific event and satisfaction of a guard condition, perform certain actions and enter the second state.

The **canonical transition signature** is the most heavily tested concept in KTU papers:

$$\text{Transition Syntax} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Event}}_{\text{Trigger}} \; \underbrace{[\text{Guard}]}_{\text{Boolean Condition}} \; / \; \underbrace{\text{Action}}_{\text{Effect}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Rule:** "When **Event** occurs, **if Guard is true**, then perform **Action**, and move to the target state."

- **Event** – the stimulus (signal, call, time event, change event). *May be omitted* for "completion" transitions.
- **Guard** – a Boolean expression evaluated *at the moment the event fires*. *Must be true* for the transition to fire.
- **Action** – an executable atomic computation (no internal events generated).

### 2.1.3 Events (Triggers)

An **event** is a notable occurrence that can trigger a state transition. UML 2.5 recognizes four kinds:

| Event Type | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Signal Event** | Asynchronous receipt of a signal | `buttonPressed` |
| **Call Event** | Synchronous call from an operation | `processPayment()` |
| **Time Event** | Absolute or relative time trigger | `after(30 seconds)` |
| **Change Event** | Boolean condition becomes true | `when(temp > 100)` |

### 2.1.4 Actions (Behavioral Effects)

An **action** is an executable atomic computation. UML provides three standard action qualifiers used inside the state body:

- `entry / <action>` → executed upon **entering** the state.
- `do / <activity>` → executed as long as the state remains active.
- `exit / <action>` → executed just before **leaving** the state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequent Trap:** The order of execution for any state entry/exit is strictly **exit (from old) → action (on transition) → entry (to new)**. Examiners test this in 3-mark questions.

### 2.1.5 Pseudostates (Initial, Final, History, Decision)

- **Initial Pseudostate** – a *filled black disc* representing the starting point. Every state machine **must have exactly one** initial pseudostate at the top level.
- **Final State** – a *bullseye* representing completion. An object reaching the final state is considered *destroyed* (or simply *terminated*).
- **History Pseudostate** – a circle containing the letter **H**, used inside composite states to remember the last active substate. Two flavors: **shallow history (H)** remembers the immediate substate; **deep history (H*)** remembers the deepest active substate.
- **Decision / Branch Pseudostate** – a diamond used to express dynamic conditional branching in transitions.

## 2.2 Classification: Behavioral vs. Protocol State Machines

KTU Module 1 distinguishes two flavors:

| Aspect | **Behavioral (Standard) State Machine** | **Protocol State Machine** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Models *full* behavior including side-effects and activities | Models *legal/illegal* usage of an interface |
| **Side Effects** | Allowed (`entry/`, `do/`, `exit/`) | **Not allowed** — purely declarative |
| **Use Case** | Lifecycle of an Entity object | Usage contract of a Classifier |
| **Notation** | Same shape, may include action compartments | Shape identical, but **no action labels** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Rule | KTU Pitfall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initial state | Filled black circle ● | Do **not** use a normal rounded rectangle |
| Final state | Bullseye ⊙ | Not the same as a normal termination |
| Transition form | `Event [Guard] / Action` | All three parts are *optional*; absence implies completion |
| Entry / Do / Exit | `entry/`, `do/`, `exit/` inside state | Order: *Exit old → Transition action → Enter new* |
| Self-transition | Arrow from state to itself | `exit/` and `entry/` fire **even on self-loop** |
| Internal transition | Written inside the state body, no arrow | `exit/` and `entry/` do **NOT** fire |
| History state | `H` (shallow) or `H*` (deep) | Only valid **inside** a composite state |
| Fork / Join | Thick horizontal/vertical bar | Used for concurrent substates |
| Guard condition | Enclosed in `[ ]` brackets | Must be a *side-effect-free* Boolean expression |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** State Machine Diagrams are foundational in **embedded systems** (FSM controllers), **protocol design** (TCP, Bluetooth), **compiler construction** (lexer/parser DFA), **workflow engines** (BPMN, jBPM), and **UI navigation design** in Android/iOS apps. In Java, the pattern is implemented via the **State design pattern** (GoF), where each state becomes a separate class encapsulating state-specific behavior.

## 2.4 The "Why" and "How" – Mental Model for the Student

- **Why** use a state machine? Because complex objects have **memory** — their response to the same event depends on their *current* state (e.g., pressing "Cancel" while idle is harmless, but while processing a payment, it triggers a refund).
- **How** does the diagram help? It **exhaustively enumerates** every legal (and illegal) sequence, making it impossible to leave an edge case unhandled. It is the gold standard for designing **finite-state systems** before code is written.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Constructing a State Machine Diagram from a Problem Statement

This section walks through the **complete, non-truncated methodology** to convert a textual problem description into a UML State Machine Diagram. The KTU examiner frequently gives a 14-mark question in exactly this form.

### 3.1.1 The Five-Step Methodology
