**Use `<>` for mandatory shared sub-flows, `<>` for optional conditional sub-flows.** - **Multiplicity notations:** `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`, `n..m`. - **Object Diagram notation:** `objectName: ClassName` (both underlined), with `attribute = value` (no type). - **Class Diagram notation:** `attributeName: Type`, `methodName(): ReturnType` (with visibility symbols `+`, `-`, `#`, `~`). - **Sequence Diagram control fragments to know:** `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `neg`, `ref`. - **Memorize the standard practice metrics:** 1 Use-Case + 1 Package + 2–4 Class + 1–2 Sequence + 1 State + 1 Deployment for a typical KTU-grade model. - **Traceability is mandatory:** every Class Diagram must trace back to a Use-Case; every Deployment Diagram must host a Component Diagram. - **Examiners reward cleanliness and correct notation** — do not hand-draw sloppy boxes; use UML tool output (StarUML, Lucidchart, PlantUML) where possible. - **Always include a system boundary rectangle** in Use-Case Diagrams unless explicitly told otherwise. --- **END OF NOTES — DIAGRAM ORGANIZATION (MODULE 1)** **PEITT411 — Object Oriented Design Using Java | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Diagram Extensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diagram Extensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Extensions** in Unified Modeling Language (UML) refer to the **lightweight extension mechanisms** used to enrich, refine, and customize standard UML diagrams (Use Case, Class, Sequence, Activity, etc.) without altering the underlying UML metamodel. They allow modelers to add domain-specific semantics, constraints, and visual conventions on top of standard UML notations.

The four primary extension mechanisms defined by the **Object Management Group (OMG) UML specification** are:

1. **Stereotypes (`<<name>>`)** — Extend the UML vocabulary by allowing creation of new model element kinds derived from existing ones.
2. **Constraints (`{constraint}`)** — Extend the semantics of UML elements by adding well-formedness rules.
3. **Tagged Values** — Extend the properties of UML elements with name-value pairs (used in older UML 1.x and basic attribute decoration).
4. **Notes (Comments)** — Free-form textual annotations attached to any model element.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEITT411**, *Diagram Extensions* also popularly covers **Use-Case Diagram Extensions** — the `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships — and the three standard Jacobson class stereotypes: `<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, and `<<control>>`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are working on a **blueprint of a house** (this is your UML diagram). The blueprint already has standard symbols: rectangles for rooms, circles for switches, etc.

- **Stereotype** = You add a custom label like `<<smart>>` on a switch to say "this is a smart switch" — you are *reusing the switch symbol but adding meaning*.
- **Constraint** = You write `{max_weight = 200kg}` next to a staircase — you are *adding a rule* the design must obey.
- **Tagged Value** = You stick a sticky note `{author = "Arjun", version = 1.2}` — you are *attaching extra metadata*.
- **Note** = You draw a callout box with plain English explanation — you are *adding a comment* for the reader.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** *Stereotype = type label*, *Constraint = rule*, *Tagged Value = metadata*, *Note = comment*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

- The **four standard stereotypes** used in KTU board answers for OOD are: `<<include>>`, `<<extend>>`, `<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<control>>`.
- Stereotype names are **case-insensitive but conventionally written in lowercase**, e.g., `<<entity>>` not `<<Entity>>`.
- Constraints are enclosed in **curly braces `{}`** and written in **OCL (Object Constraint Language)** or natural language.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four UML Extension Mechanisms — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Stereotypes
- Represented as a **name inside guillemets** (double angle brackets): `<<stereotype-name>>`
- Placed **above or beside** the name of a model element.
- Three categories per OMG:
  - **Class stereotypes** (e.g., `<<interface>>`, `<<enumeration>>`, `<<datatype>>`)
  - **Behavior stereotypes** (e.g., `<<focus>>` for primary class in a diagram)
  - **Standard elements** (`<<document>>`, `<<executable>>`, `<<library>>`, `<<process>>`, `<<thread>>`)

#### 2.1.2 Constraints
- Represented in **curly braces `{}`** and may use **OCL syntax**.
- Used to specify **invariants**, **preconditions**, **postconditions**.
- Example: `{ordered}, {unique}, {subset}, {xor}, {readOnly}, {frozen}`

#### 2.1.3 Tagged Values
- Format: `{tag = value}`
- Example: `{author = "Anu", version = 2.1, status = "Reviewed"}`
- Used in older UML versions to attach **properties** to elements.

#### 2.1.4 Notes
- A **dog-eared rectangle** (folding corner) containing free-form text.
- Connected to other elements with a **dashed line**.

### 2.2 Use-Case Diagram Extensions (`<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`)

This is a **very high-yield KTU topic** — appears almost every year.

| Feature | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | From base to required | From extension to base |
| Mandatory? | **Yes** — the included use case **always executes** | **No** — executes only when condition is met |
| Purpose | Reuse **common behaviour** across multiple use cases | Add **optional/conditional behaviour** |
| Arrow | Dashed arrow with `<<include>>` pointing to the **included** UC | Dashed arrow with `<<extend>>` pointing to the **base** UC |
| Triggering | Base UC triggers included UC | Extension point of base UC triggers extension UC |
| KTU Mnemonic | **"I always need this"** | **"Sometimes I need that"** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often reverse the arrow direction. The arrow **points TO the use case that is being USED**, not the one using it.

### 2.3 The Three Jacobson Stereotypes (Boundary, Entity, Control)

Used during **Use-Case Realization** to design class diagrams from use cases:

| Stereotype | Role | Example (ATM System) |
|---|---|---|
| `<<boundary>>` | Interface between system and actors | `ATMForm`, `ReceiptPrinter`, `Keypad` |
| `<<entity>>` | Stores information / business data | `Account`, `Transaction`, `Customer` |
| `<<control>>` | Coordinates flow between boundary and entity | `ATMController`, `TransactionController` |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Notation | Meaning | Visual Form | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| `<<include>>` | Mandatory reuse of another UC | Dashed arrow → included UC | Use-Case Diagram |
| `<<extend>>` | Optional/conditional addition | Dashed arrow → base UC | Use-Case Diagram |
| `<<boundary>>` | User/system interface class | `«boundary» ClassName` | Class Diagram |
| `<<entity>>` | Data/storage class | `«entity» ClassName` | Class Diagram |
| `<<control>>` | Coordination/mediator class | `«control» ClassName` | Class Diagram |
| `{ordered}` | Collection maintains order | Note/label on association | Class Diagram |
| `{unique}` | No duplicates allowed | Note/label on attribute | Class Diagram |
| `{frozen}` | Immutable after creation | Label on attribute | Class Diagram |
| `{readOnly}` | Cannot be modified | Label on attribute | Class Diagram |
| Note | Free-text comment | Dog-eared rectangle | Any diagram |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stereotypes** are foundational in **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)** and allow domain-specific languages (DSLs) to be built atop UML.
- **Constraints in OCL** are used in safety-critical systems (aerospace, medical) to formally verify designs.
- **Boundary-Entity-Control pattern** is the basis of **MVC architecture** (Model-View-Controller) in Java Spring, Java Swing, and Android development.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Use-Case to Class Diagram Using Jacobson's Stereotypes

This is the **most repeated KTU derivation** for 14-mark questions.

> **Problem Statement:** Given the use case *"Withdraw Cash"* in an ATM system, derive the class diagram with the three Jacobson stereotypes.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Actors
The user (Customer) interacts with the system. We have **one external actor**: `Customer`.

#### Step 2 — Identify the Boundary Class
A boundary class handles **all interaction between actors and the system**.

$$
\text{Boundary} = f(\text{Actor}, \text{System Interface})
$$

For ATM cash withdrawal, the user types on a screen and reads output, so:

$$
\text{Boundary Class} \Rightarrow \texttt{ATMScreen}
$$

#### Step 3 — Identify the Entity Class
An entity class **persists information** needed by the use case. Cash withdrawal requires account information:

$$
\text{Entity Class} \Rightarrow \texttt{Account}, \texttt{Transaction}
$$

#### Step 4 — Identify the Control Class
A control class **manages the workflow**, validates the PIN, dispatches to entity, returns result:

$$
\text{Control Class} \Rightarrow \texttt{WithdrawalController}
$$

#### Step 5 — Establish the Interaction Sequence
The complete chain of events:

$$
\texttt{Customer} \rightarrow \texttt{ATMScreen} \rightarrow \texttt{WithdrawalController} \rightarrow \texttt{Account}
$$

The controller mediates:

$$
\texttt{ATMScreen} \dashrightarrow \texttt{WithdrawalController} \dashrightarrow \texttt{Account}
$$

#### Step 6 — Apply Stereotypes to Class Diagram

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{ATMScreen} &\;\mapsto\; \texttt{<<boundary>>} \\
\texttt{Account}   &\;\mapsto\; \texttt{<<entity>>} \\
\texttt{WithdrawalController} &\;\mapsto\; \texttt{<<control>>}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing `<<include>>` vs `<<extend>>`

Apply this decision matrix step-by-step:

| Step | Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Does the use case **always** need this behaviour? | Use `<<include>>` | Go to step 2 |
| 2 | Does the behaviour run only on a **specific condition**? | Use `<<extend>>` with extension point | Consider duplication |
| 3 | Is the behaviour shared by **3 or more** use cases? | Strongly use `<<include>>` | Use `<<extend>>` |
| 4 | Can the use case **still succeed** without the optional behaviour? | `<<extend>>` | `<<include>>` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** If a use case has the words *"always"*, *"must"*, *"required"*, use `<<include>>`. If it has *"sometimes"*, *"if"*, *"optionally"*, use `<<extend>>`.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Library Management System

**Use Cases identified:**
- `IssueBook`
- `ReturnBook`
- `PayFine`
- `Login` *(common functionality)*

**Step 1 — Detect commonality:** All three (Issue, Return, PayFine) require `Login`.

$$
\forall UC \in \{\texttt{IssueBook, ReturnBook, PayFine}\},\; \text{Login} \subseteq UC
$$

Therefore, use `<<include>>`:

$$
\texttt{IssueBook} \dashrightarrow_{<<include>>} \texttt{Login}
$$

$$
\texttt{ReturnBook} \dashrightarrow_{<<include>>} \texttt{Login}
$$

$$
\texttt{PayFine} \dashrightarrow_{<<include>>} \texttt{Login}
$$

**Step 2 — Detect optional behaviour:** During `IssueBook`, if the book is overdue, then `PayFine` is invoked.

This is an **optional, conditional** flow → use `<<extend>>`:

$$
\texttt{PayFine} \dashrightarrow_{<<extend>>} \texttt{IssueBook}
$$

with extension point defined on `IssueBook`:

$$
\text{ExtensionPoint} = \texttt{"if\_book\_overdue"}
$$

### 3.4 Constraints Worked Example

A class `Student` has attribute `rollNo` that **cannot be modified after creation**.

$$
\texttt{rollNo} : \text{int} = \{ \texttt{frozen} \}
$$

A class `ExamResult` has attribute `marks` that is **read-only after publish**:

$$
\texttt{marks} : \text{float} = \{ \texttt{readOnly} \}
$$

A list `subjects` in `Student` must be **ordered and unique**:

$$
\texttt{subjects} : \text{List} \langle \texttt{String} \rangle = \{ \texttt{ordered, unique} \}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Use-Case Diagram with `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` (Library System)

```mermaid
graph LR
    actor1((Student))
    actor2((Librarian))

    UC1[Issue Book]
    UC2[Return Book]
    UC3[Pay Fine]
    UC4[Login]
    UC5[Send Overdue Notice]
    UC6[Search Catalog]

    actor1 --> UC1
    actor1 --> UC2
    actor1 --> UC3
    actor1 --> UC6
    actor2 --> UC1
    actor2 --> UC2

    UC1 -.->|<<include>>| UC4
    UC2 -.->|<<include>>| UC4
    UC3 -.->|<<include>>| UC4
    UC6 -.->|<<include>>| UC4

    UC3 -.->|<<extend>>| UC2
    UC5 -.->|<<extend>>| UC2

    note1[/Note: Pay Fine is invoked\nonly if overdue\nat Return Book/]
    UC2 --- note1
```

### 4.2 Class Diagram with Jacobson Stereotypes (ATM Cash Withdrawal)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ATMScreen {
        <<boundary>>
        +displayMenu()
        +readPIN()
        +showBalance()
    }

    class WithdrawalController {
        <<control>>
        +validatePIN()
        +executeWithdrawal()
        -amount : double
    }

    class Account {
        <<entity>>
        -accountNo : String
        -balance : double
        +debit(amount)
        +credit(amount)
    }

    class Transaction {
        <<entity>>
        -txnId : String
        -timestamp : Date
        -amount : double
    }

    ATMScreen "1" --> "1" WithdrawalController : sends request
    WithdrawalController "1" --> "1..*" Account : manages
    WithdrawalController "1" --> "1..*" Transaction : creates

    note for Account "{readOnly} accountNo\n{unique} accountNo"
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of UML Extension Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[UML Diagram] --> B[Core Notation]
    A --> C[Extension Mechanisms]

    B --> B1[Use Case Symbol - Ellipse]
    B --> B2[Class Symbol - Rectangle]
    B --> B3[Actor Symbol - Stickman]
    B --> B4[Association - Line]

    C --> C1[Stereotype]
    C --> C2[Constraint]
    C --> C3[Tagged Value]
    C --> C4[Note]

    C1 --> D1[include - Mandatory Reuse]
    C1 --> D2[extend - Optional Addition]
    C1 --> D3[boundary - UI Class]
    C1 --> D4[entity - Data Class]
    C1 --> D5[control - Logic Class]

    C2 --> E1[OCL Invariants]
    C2 --> E2[Pre/Post Conditions]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships in a use-case diagram with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`<<include>>`:** A relationship where one use case **mandatorily** invokes the behaviour of another use case. The included use case is **never optional** and is required for the base use case to complete its goal. Drawn as a **dashed arrow** with the `<<include>>` label pointing **from the base to the included use case**.

  *Example:* The use case *"Place Order"* always includes *"Validate Payment"* because no order can be placed without payment validation.

- **`<<extend>>`:** A relationship where one use case **optionally** adds behaviour to another use case at a defined **extension point**, and only when a specific **condition** is satisfied. Drawn as a **dashed arrow** with the `<<extend>>` label pointing **from the extending use case to the base use case**.

  *Example:* The use case *"Apply Coupon"* extends *"Place Order"* only at the extension point *"before_payment"* if the user has a valid coupon.

| Aspect | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|---|---|---|
| Mandatory | Yes | No |
| Direction of arrow | Base → Included | Extending → Base |
| Use case being referred to | Required sub-flow | Optional addition |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List and briefly explain the three Jacobson stereotypes used in use-case realization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Jacobson proposed three stereotypes to identify the role of classes in a use-case realization:

1. **`<<boundary>>` class (1 mark):** Represents the **interface between the system and external actors**. It captures all user inputs and system outputs. *Example:* Login form, ATM keypad, GUI window.

2. **`<<entity>>` class (1 mark):** Represents **persistent information / business data** that the system stores and manipulates. Usually mapped to database tables. *Example:* Student, Account, Order.

3. **`<<control>>` class (1 mark):** Acts as a **mediator/coordinator** between boundary and entity classes. It encapsulates the control logic of a use case. *Example:* LoginController, OrderController.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the four UML diagram extension mechanisms with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four UML extension mechanisms are used to enrich standard UML diagrams without changing the core metamodel:

1. **Stereotypes (2 marks):** Represented using guillemets `<<name>>`. They extend the UML vocabulary by introducing new element kinds derived from existing ones. *Example:* `<<interface>>`, `<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<control>>`, `<<include>>`, `<<extend>>`.

2. **Constraints (2 marks):** Enclosed in curly braces `{}`. They extend the semantics of UML elements by adding well-formedness rules. Often written in OCL. *Example:* `{ordered, unique}` on a list, `{frozen}` on an immutable attribute, `{readOnly}` on a published value.

3. **Tagged Values (1.5 marks):** Enclosed in curly braces in the form `{tag = value}`. They attach metadata to model elements. *Example:* `{author = "Anu", version = 2.0, status = "Verified"}`.

4. **Notes (1.5 marks):** Drawn as a **dog-eared rectangle** with free-form text, connected to any element with a **dashed line**. Used for plain-English comments. *Example:* A note explaining the business rule "Account cannot be closed if balance is non-zero".

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget to mention the visual shape (dog-eared rectangle) for notes** and lose 1 mark. They also confuse **constraints vs tagged values** — constraints are *rules*, tagged values are *key-value metadata*.

---

**Q. (b)** Consider an **Online Shopping System**. Draw a use-case diagram showing `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships among the following use cases: `BrowseProducts`, `AddToCart`, `Checkout`, `Login`, `ApplyCoupon`, `ProcessPayment`. State clearly any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Every product-related activity (`BrowseProducts`, `AddToCart`, `Checkout`) requires the user to be logged in.
- During `Checkout`, the user *may* apply a coupon.
- The system always processes payment before order confirmation.

**Use-Case Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    C((Customer))
    S((System))

    UC1[Browse Products]
    UC2[Add to Cart]
    UC3[Checkout]
    UC4[Login]
    UC5[Apply Coupon]
    UC6[Process Payment]

    C --> UC1
    C --> UC2
    C --> UC3

    UC1 -.->|<<include>>| UC4
    UC2 -.->|<<include>>| UC4
    UC3 -.->|<<include>>| UC4

    UC5 -.->|<<extend>>| UC3
    UC3 -.->|<<include>>| UC6
```

**Explanation of Extensions (2 marks):**

- **Three `<<include>>` relationships:** All shopping activities (`BrowseProducts`, `AddToCart`, `Checkout`) *mandatorily* require user authentication, so they all include `Login`.
- **Checkout includes `ProcessPayment`:** Payment must be processed for the order to complete.
- **One `<<extend>>` relationship:** `ApplyCoupon` *optionally* extends `Checkout` at the extension point *"before_payment"* — coupon is applied only if the user enters a valid code.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently **draw solid arrows instead of dashed arrows** for `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`. Loss of 1 mark. Also, drawing arrows in the **wrong direction** costs 1.5 marks. Remember: include arrow goes from **base to included**, extend arrow goes from **extending to base**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** What are stereotypes in UML? List **any six** pre-defined UML stereotypes with their meanings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A stereotype is a UML extension mechanism that allows the modeler to create a new kind of model element derived from an existing one. It is represented as a name inside double angle brackets `<<stereotype-name>>`, placed above or beside the name of the model element. Stereotypes extend the **UML vocabulary**.

**Six Pre-Defined Stereotypes (5 marks — 1 mark each approx):**

1. **`<<actor>>`** — Used to denote a system or external entity playing a role.
2. **`<<interface>>`** — Specifies a contract of operations without implementation.
3. **`<<boundary>>`** — Marks a class that interfaces with an external actor.
4. **`<<entity>>`** — Marks a class that holds persistent data.
5. **`<<control>>`** — Marks a class coordinating use-case behaviour.
6. **`<<utility>>`** — A class with only static attributes and operations (no instances).
7. **`<<exception>>`** — Signals an abnormal condition.
8. **`<<focus>>`** — Identifies the primary class of focus in a class diagram.
9. **`<<datatype>>`** — Identifies a class whose instances are values, not objects.
10. **`<<enumeration>>`** — A fixed set of named values.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Avoid giving the **same stereotype multiple times** with different words. Each example must be a *unique* pre-defined stereotype from the OMG UML specification.

---

**Q. (b)** For the use case *"Register Student"* in a *University Admission System*, identify the **`<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, and `<<control>>` classes**. Draw the corresponding class diagram with proper stereotypes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Use Case Description (1 mark):**
A prospective student fills a registration form online, the system validates the form, creates a student record, and sends a confirmation email.

**Identification of Classes (3 marks):**

| Stereotype | Class | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `<<boundary>>` | `RegistrationForm` | Handles all input from the student actor |
| `<<entity>>` | `Student`, `AdmissionRecord` | Stores persistent data of students |
| `<<control>>` | `RegistrationController` | Mediates between form and entity, performs validation |

**Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class RegistrationForm {
        <<boundary>>
        +acceptInput()
        +displayErrors()
        +showConfirmation()
    }

    class RegistrationController {
        <<control>>
        +validateForm()
        +checkEligibility()
        +createStudent()
    }

    class Student {
        <<entity>>
        -studentId : String
        -name : String
        -email : String
    }

    class AdmissionRecord {
        <<entity>>
        -recordId : String
        -date : Date
        -status : String
    }

    RegistrationForm "1" --> "1" RegistrationController : submits
    RegistrationController "1" --> "1" Student : creates
    RegistrationController "1" --> "1" AdmissionRecord : generates
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students sometimes mark the **Student class as `<<boundary>>`** because it has fields like email. Remember: **fields do not make a class a boundary**. A class is `<<boundary>>` only if it directly **interacts with an actor**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Diagram Extensions**

- ✅ **Stereotypes** are written in `<<double angle brackets>>` — they extend UML **vocabulary**.
- ✅ **Constraints** are written in `{curly braces}` — they extend UML **semantics** (rules).
- ✅ **Tagged values** are in `{tag = value}` format — they extend UML **properties**.
- ✅ **Notes** are drawn as a **dog-eared rectangle** connected via a **dashed line**.
- ✅ **`<<include>>` = MANDATORY** reuse (base → included, dashed arrow).
- ✅ **`<<extend>>` = OPTIONAL** addition (extending → base, dashed arrow, with extension point).
- ✅ The **three Jacobson stereotypes** are `<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<control>>` — they map directly to the **MVC pattern** in Java.
- ✅ Boundary classes interact with **actors**; Entity classes store **data**; Control classes **coordinate** flow.
- ✅ Standard OCL constraints to remember: `{ordered}`, `{unique}`, `{frozen}`, `{readOnly}`.
- ✅ KTU standard mark split for 14-mark diagram questions: **4 marks diagram + 3 marks explanation** (varies).
- ✅ Always write **assumptions** before drawing a use-case diagram (1 mark reserved).
- ✅ Arrows for `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` are **DASHED**, not solid. Wrong line style = −1 mark.
- ✅ `<<include>>` direction: **Base UC → Included UC**; `<<extend>>` direction: **Extending UC → Base UC**.
- ✅ The `<<entity>>` class typically corresponds to **database tables** in Java persistence (e.g., JPA `@Entity`).
- ✅ The `<<control>>` class corresponds to **Service/Controller** in Java Spring Boot architecture.
- ✅ In the **MVC pattern**: Boundary = View, Entity = Model, Control = Controller.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Object Oriented Design using Java**, the Introduction to Module 2 formally establishes the foundational pillars of the **Java Class–Object Model**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, this module introduces the transition from procedural programming (covered in earlier programming modules) to the **Object Oriented Programming (OOP) paradigm** using the Java programming language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Class:**
> A *class* in Java is a **blueprint, template, or prototype** from which individual **objects** are created. It is a logical construct that defines the **state (attributes/fields)** and **behavior (methods)** that the objects of that type will possess.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Object:**
> An *object* is a **runtime instance of a class** that occupies memory, holds unique state values, and can invoke the methods defined by its class. Objects are the fundamental building blocks of any Java application.

The Java language treats everything (except primitives) as an object. The four core OOP principles — **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism** — are introduced conceptually in this module and form the bedrock of all subsequent OOP design in the syllabus.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Let us understand Classes and Objects using a real-world analogy that a first-time learner can instantly grasp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint vs. The House**
>
> Imagine an **architect's blueprint** for a house. The blueprint specifies:
> - How many rooms the house will have (attributes/fields)
> - The design of doors, windows, and layout (structure/blueprint)
> - The functional capabilities (you can open a door, close a window) (methods)
>
> Now, using **one single blueprint**, an architect can construct **many different houses** in different locations. Each house is a *real, physical entity* with its own wall color, family name on the door, number of occupants, and interior design. Each house can independently open its door or switch on its lights.
>
> - The **blueprint = Java Class** (a logical, memory-free template)
> - Each **constructed house = Java Object** (a physical, memory-occupying instance)
> - Each house's own colors and family = **unique state** of the object
> - The ability to open a door = **method/behavior** defined in the class but invoked by the object

This blueprint-to-house mapping is the central intuition of Object Oriented Design.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced in Module 2

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 explicitly introduces the following vocabulary that students must internalize:

| Term | Standard Meaning (KTU Terminology) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Class** | User-defined reference type / blueprint for objects. |
| **Object** | Runtime instance of a class residing in the heap memory. |
| **Instance Variable** | A non-static field belonging to each individual object. |
| **Instance Method** | A non-static method invoked using an object reference. |
| **Reference Variable** | A variable that holds the memory address of an object (stored in stack). |
| **Constructor** | Special member function used to initialize objects at creation time. |
| **`new` Operator** | Keyword that allocates heap memory and invokes a constructor. |
| **Access Modifiers** | `public`, `private`, `protected`, and default — used to enforce **encapsulation**. |
| **Encapsulation** | Binding of data and methods together while hiding internal state. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2 Learning Outcome):**
> *"On completion of this module, the student should be able to design and implement Java classes, instantiate objects, apply access control mechanisms, and demonstrate the principle of encapsulation using constructors and accessor/mutator methods."* — Verified alignment with the **CO2** of the PEITT411 course.

---

## 1.4 The Procedural-to-OOP Paradigm Shift

To fully appreciate the introduction of classes and objects, the student must understand *why* the shift is necessary.

**Procedural Approach (C-style):**
- Data and functions are **separate entities**.
- Functions operate on data passed as parameters.
- As programs grow, managing and protecting data becomes difficult.

**Object Oriented Approach (Java style):**
- Data and the functions that operate on that data are **bundled into a single unit** — the class/object.
- Internal data is protected using access modifiers.
- Programs become collections of cooperating objects.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for First-Time Learners:**
> **C**lass = **C**oncept / **C**ontract / **C**ategory
> **O**bject = **O**ccurrence / **O**perational unit / **O**wnership of state

This is the foundation upon which every other OOP concept in this course will be built.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Class — Structural Breakdown

According to KTU 2024 expectations, a complete Java class declaration contains the following ordered components:

1. **Package Declaration** *(optional but recommended)* — `package com.ktu.student;`
2. **Import Statements** *(optional)* — `import java.util.Scanner;`
3. **Class Modifiers** — `public`, `final`, `abstract`, etc.
4. **Class Declaration Header** — `class ClassName`
5. **Class Body** — Contains fields, constructors, methods, and nested types.

The general syntactic template prescribed by the KTU Board is:

```java
[access_modifier] class ClassName {
    // 1. Fields (Instance Variables / State)
    [access_modifier] datatype fieldName;
    
    // 2. Constructors (Object Initialization)
    [access_modifier] ClassName(parameters) {
        // initialization logic
    }
    
    // 3. Methods (Behavior)
    [access_modifier] returnType methodName(parameters) {
        // method body
        return value;
    }
}
```

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Component

### 2.2.1 Fields (Instance Variables)
- **Why?** To represent the **state** of each object. Every object gets its own copy of the fields.
- **How?** Declared inside a class but **outside** any method, constructor, or block.

### 2.2.2 Constructors
- **Why?** To ensure that every object is brought to a **valid initial state** the moment it is created (i.e., before any other method is called).
- **How?** A special method having:
  - The **exact same name** as the class.
  - **No return type** (not even `void`).
  - Invoked automatically by the `new` operator.

### 2.2.3 Methods
- **Why?** To define the **behavior** — the actions an object can perform.
- **How?** A method has a *signature* (name + parameter list) and a *body* (logic block).

### 2.2.4 Access Modifiers
- **Why?** To implement **Encapsulation** — controlling visibility of class members.
- **How?** Java provides four levels of access:
  - `private` — accessible only within the same class.
  - *default (package-private)* — accessible within the same package.
  - `protected` — accessible within the same package and by subclasses.
  - `public` — accessible from anywhere.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following compact reference table encapsulates every high-yield syntax rule the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to reproduce in the examination hall.

| KTU Concept | Java Syntax Template | Memory Location | Lifetime |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class Declaration** | `public class Name { }` | Method Area (Metaspace) | Until classloader unloads it |
| **Object Creation** | `Name obj = new Name();` | Heap Memory | Until Garbage Collected |
| **Reference Variable** | `Name ref;` (holds address) | Stack Memory | Until the method returns |
| **Instance Field Access** | `obj.fieldName = value;` | Resolves to the Heap | Bound to object lifetime |
| **Method Invocation** | `obj.methodName(args);` | Stack frame created | Until method returns |
| **Constructor Call** | Implicitly via `new` | Stack then Heap | Single execution per object |
| **Encapsulation Rule** | `private` fields + `public` getters/setters | Heap (data) + Stack (calls) | Program duration |
| **`this` Keyword** | Refers to *current* invoking object | Implicit reference | Per method invocation |
| **Static Field** | `static int count;` | Method Area | Class lifetime (single copy) |
| **Static Method** | `static void display(){ }` | Method Area | Class lifetime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict KTU Rule:**
> In Java, a `.java` file may contain **at most one `public` class**, and the file name **must exactly match** the public class name. This is a frequently tested 2-mark question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Class–Object Model

The Class–Object model is not merely an academic exercise. It directly maps to the engineering of large-scale software systems used in industry:

- **Banking Software:** A `BankAccount` class is defined once; millions of account objects are instantiated, each holding unique customer data.
- **Embedded / IoT Systems (Java ME):** A `Sensor` class is instantiated for every physical sensor, each object holding real-time readings.
- **Android Development:** Every `Activity`, `Fragment`, and `View` in Android is a class; the running app is a set of cooperating objects.
- **Web Backends (Spring Boot):** REST controllers, services, and repositories are all classes wired together via *Dependency Injection* — an advanced OOP pattern.

Understanding the class–object model at this introductory level prepares the student for the advanced Design Patterns, SOLID principles, and framework-based development covered later in the KTU curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: From Blueprint (Class) to Reality (Object)

Below is a **complete, KTU-board-acceptable Java program** that demonstrates the introduction to classes and objects. Each transition is explained step-by-step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Program Logic — Build a `Student` class that stores a student's name and roll number, and displays the details.**

### Step 1 — Define the Class Blueprint

```java
// File name MUST be Student.java
public class Student {
    // Step 1a: Private instance fields (Encapsulation)
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    
    // Step 1b: Parameterized constructor to initialize the object
    public Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        this.name = name;       // 'this.name' = current object's field
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
    }
    
    // Step 1c: Public getter methods (controlled read access)
    public String getName() {
        return name;
    }
    
    public int getRollNo() {
        return rollNo;
    }
    
    // Step 1d: Public setter method (controlled write access)
    public void setName(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }
    
    // Step 1e: Public method to display the state
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Name   : " + this.name);
        System.out.println("RollNo : " + this.rollNo);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Logic Explanation:**

- **Line 1:** The `public class Student` declaration creates the *blueprint*. The file must be saved as `Student.java` per Java's compilation rule.
- **Lines 4–5:** The fields `name` and `rollNo` are declared `private` to enforce **encapsulation** — no external class can directly read or modify them.
- **Lines 8–11:** The constructor accepts two arguments. The keyword `this` is used to **disambiguate** between the parameter `name` and the instance field `this.name`. Without `this`, both names would resolve to the local parameter, leaving the field uninitialized.
- **Lines 14–20:** Public getters expose read-only access. This is the *read* part of encapsulation.
- **Lines 23–25:** The setter provides a *controlled write* — it can later be extended to include validation logic (e.g., reject empty strings), which is the real power of encapsulation.
- **Lines 28–31:** The `display()` method prints the state. It is an *instance method* — it can only be called through an object reference.

---

### Step 2 — Instantiate Objects from the Blueprint

```java
// File name: StudentDemo.java
public class StudentDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 2a: Create the first object
        Student s1 = new Student("Rahul", 101);
        
        // Step 2b: Create the second object from the SAME class
        Student s2 = new Student("Priya", 102);
        
        // Step 2c: Invoke instance methods on each object
        s1.display();
        s2.display();
        
        // Step 2d: Mutate state via setter (encapsulated write)
        s1.setName("Rahul Krishnan");
        s1.display();
    }
}
```

**Memory Layout During Execution (KTU high-yield concept):**

```
+---------------------- STACK MEMORY ----------------------+
|                                                          |
|  main() frame                                            |
|  +-----------+         +-----------+                     |
|  | s1  (ref) | ---->   | s2  (ref) | ---->               |
|  +-----------+         +-----------+                     |
+----------------------------|-----------------------------+
                             |
                             v
+---------------------- HEAP MEMORY ------------------------+
|                                                            |
|  +-------------------------+    +-------------------------+|
|  | Object: s1              |    | Object: s2              ||
|  |----------------------   |    |----------------------   ||
|  | name   = "Rahul"        |    | name   = "Priya"        ||
|  | rollNo = 101            |    | rollNo = 102            ||
|  |----------------------   |    |----------------------   ||
|  | display()  (method ptr) |    | display()  (method ptr) ||
|  +-------------------------+    +-------------------------+|
|                                                            |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Logic Explanation:**

- The `new` operator:
  1. Allocates memory in the **heap** for the new object.
  2. Initializes all instance fields to default values (`null` for objects, `0` for numeric types, `false` for boolean).
  3. Invokes the matching constructor to perform custom initialization.
  4. Returns the **memory address** of the newly created object.
- The reference variable `s1` (in the stack) **stores the address**, not the object itself.
- Each object has its **own independent copy** of the fields. Modifying `s1.name` has **no effect** on `s2.name`.

---

### Step 3 — Analytical Matrix: Conceptual Comparison

The following table crystallizes the relationships the KTU examiner expects you to articulate:

| Property | Class | Object |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | A logical template | A physical runtime instance |
| **Memory Allocation** | None at runtime (loaded once into Metaspace) | Allocated in the heap via `new` |
| **Keyword to Create** | N/A (declared with `class`) | `new ClassName(args)` |
| **Number of Copies** | Exactly one per classloader | Zero or many |
| **Field Access** | Static fields only | All instance fields + static fields |
| **Lifetime** | Until JVM unloads the class | Until unreferenced and Garbage Collected |
| **Declaration Syntax** | `class Student { }` | `Student s = new Student();` |
| **Storage of State** | No state of its own | Holds state in instance fields |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: The `this` Keyword in Action

The `this` keyword is one of the most frequently tested KTU topics in Module 2. Here is a derivation of when and why it is used.

> **Rule:** Within an instance method or constructor, `this` refers to the **current object** — the object on which the method was invoked.

```java
public class Box {
    private double length, width, height;
    
    // Constructor using 'this' to resolve naming conflict
    public Box(double length, double width, double height) {
        this.length = length;   // LHS = field, RHS = parameter
        this.width  = width;
        this.height = height;
    }
    
    // Method to compute and return volume
    public double volume() {
        return this.length * this.width * this.height;
    }
    
    // Method to return the current object reference
    public Box getThisObject() {
        return this;
    }
}
```

**Logical Derivation:**

$$
\text{Volume} = \text{length} \times \text{width} \times \text{height}
$$

$$
V = l \cdot w \cdot h
$$

For a `Box` object with $l = 5.0$, $w = 3.0$, and $h = 2.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 5.0 \times 3.0 \times 2.0 \\
  &= 15.0 \times 2.0 \\
  &= 30.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **When can `this` be omitted?**
> Only when there is **no naming conflict** between a local variable/parameter and the instance field. However, the KTU Board recommends the *best practice* of using `this` consistently for clarity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Constructor Overloading (Preview of Module Depth)

Although deeper treatment of overloading appears in later KTU modules, the *introduction* to classes and objects must include a basic demonstration of constructor overloading — the ability to define **multiple constructors** in a class with different parameter lists.

```java
public class Employee {
    private String name;
    private int id;
    private double salary;
    
    // Constructor 1: No-argument constructor
    public Employee() {
        this.name = "Unknown";
        this.id = 0;
        this.salary = 0.0;
    }
    
    // Constructor 2: Two-parameter constructor
    public Employee(String name, int id) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
        this.salary = 25000.0; // default salary
    }
    
    // Constructor 3: Three-parameter constructor
    public Employee(String name, int id, double salary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
        this.salary = salary;
    }
}
```

**Usage in `main()`:**

```java
Employee e1 = new Employee();                    // calls Constructor 1
Employee e2 = new Employee("Anu", 201);          // calls Constructor 2
Employee e3 = new Employee("Anu", 201, 55000.0); // calls Constructor 3
```

**Compilation Rule (KTU high-yield):** The compiler matches the constructor by examining the **number, type, and order of arguments** at the call site. This is called *compile-time polymorphism* or *static binding* — a concept the KTU board frequently tests for 3–4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Class–Object Relationship Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the architectural relationship between a Java **class template** and the **objects** instantiated from it, including their interactions with the **stack** and **heap** memory regions of the JVM.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef classStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef objectStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef memoryStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef processStyle fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    
    source[Source File Student.java]:::processStyle
    compiler[Javac Compiler]:::processStyle
    bytecode[Student.class Bytecode]:::processStyle
    jvm[JVM ClassLoader]:::processStyle
    metaspace[Metaspace Method Area]:::memoryStyle
    
    classTemplate[CLASS TEMPLATE Student Fields name rollNo Methods display getName setName]:::classStyle
    
    obj1[OBJECT 1 s1 name Rahul rollNo 101]:::objectStyle
    obj2[OBJECT 2 s2 name Priya rollNo 102]:::objectStyle
    obj3[OBJECT 3 s3 name Anu rollNo 103]:::objectStyle
    
    heap[(HEAP MEMORY)]:::memoryStyle
    stack[(STACK MEMORY)]:::memoryStyle
    
    source --> compiler
    compiler --> bytecode
    bytecode --> jvm
    jvm --> metaspace
    metaspace --> classTemplate
    
    classTemplate -.new Student args.-> obj1
    classTemplate -.new Student args.-> obj2
    classTemplate -.new Student args.-> obj3
    
    obj1 --> heap
    obj2 --> heap
    obj3 --> heap
    
    ref1[Reference s1 in stack] --> obj1
    ref2[Reference s2 in stack] --> obj2
    ref3[Reference s3 in stack] --> obj3
    stack --> ref1
    stack --> ref2
    stack --> ref3
```

**Reading the Diagram (KTU board-style narration):**

- A Java source file is compiled by `javac` into platform-neutral **bytecode** stored in a `.class` file.
- The **JVM ClassLoader** loads this bytecode into the **Method Area (Metaspace)** — this is where the class template resides.
- Each time the `new` operator is used at runtime, the JVM allocates space in the **Heap** for a new object, and the address is stored in a **Stack** reference variable.
- **One class → many objects**, each with its own state but sharing the same method definitions.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of OOP Concepts in Module 2

The following Mermaid block diagram presents the **functional architecture** of the OOP concepts introduced in Module 2, showing how each concept interlocks to form the complete module learning map.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef coreStyle fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef principleStyle fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef featureStyle fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    
    intro[Module 2 Introduction to OOP using Java]:::coreStyle
    
    intro --> a1[Class Definition]:::featureStyle
    intro --> a2[Object Instantiation]:::featureStyle
    intro --> a3[Encapsulation]:::principleStyle
    intro --> a4[Access Modifiers]:::featureStyle
    intro --> a5[Constructors]:::featureStyle
    intro --> a6[this Keyword]:::featureStyle
    intro --> a7[Static Members]:::featureStyle
    
    a1 --> a1a[Fields State]
    a1 --> a1b[Methods Behavior]
    a1 --> a1c[Nested Classes]
    
    a2 --> a2a[new Operator]
    a2 --> a2b[Reference Variables]
    a2 --> a2c[Heap vs Stack]
    
    a3 --> a3a[Data Hiding]
    a3 --> a3b[Public Getters]
    a3 --> a3c[Public Setters]
    
    a4 --> a4a[private]
    a4 --> a4b[default]
    a4 --> a4c[protected]
    a4 --> a4d[public]
    
    a5 --> a5a[Default Constructor]
    a5 --> a5b[Parameterized Constructor]
    a5 --> a5c[Constructor Overloading]
    
    a6 --> a6a[Field Disambiguation]
    a6 --> a6b[Passing Current Object]
    a6 --> a6c[Constructor Chaining this args]
    
    a7 --> a7a[static Fields Class Variables]
    a7 --> a7b[static Methods Class Methods]
    a7 --> a7c[static Blocks]
```

**Architectural Reading:**

- The **core entry point** is the *Introduction* topic itself, which fans out into **seven major OOP concepts** in Module 2.
- The **four pillars of OOP** (encapsulation is highlighted in *purple* as it is the primary focus of the introduction).
- Each concept branches into its **specific sub-features** that the student must master by the end of the module.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Object Lifecycle State Machine

The KTU 2024 board also expects students to understand the **lifecycle of an object** — from creation to destruction.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Declared: Reference variable declared
    Declared --> Instantiated: new ClassName args executes
    Instantiated --> InUse: Methods invoked on object
    InUse --> InUse: Methods continue to be invoked
    InUse --> Unreachable: All references set to null or go out of scope
    Unreachable --> GarbageCollected: JVM Garbage Collector runs finalize
    GarbageCollected --> [*]: Memory reclaimed
```

**Lifecycle Explanation:**

1. **Declared:** A reference variable is created in the stack. It currently holds `null`.
2. **Instantiated:** The `new` operator allocates heap memory, calls the constructor, and assigns the address to the reference.
3. **InUse:** The object is actively used — fields read/written, methods called.
4. **Unreachable:** No live reference points to the object. It is *eligible* for garbage collection, but not yet collected.
5. **GarbageCollected:** The JVM's Garbage Collector reclaims the memory. The `finalize()` method (deprecated since Java 9) is optionally called.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** In modern Java, programmers **cannot force** garbage collection. The call `System.gc()` is merely a *request* to the JVM. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
*Define a class and an object in Java. How are they related?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

A **class** in Java is a user-defined blueprint or template that encapsulates data (fields) and behavior (methods) for a specific type of entity. It is a logical construct that does not occupy runtime memory on its own. An **object** is a runtime instance of a class created using the `new` operator. Each object occupies heap memory, holds its own unique state, and can invoke the methods defined by its class.

The relationship is best described as: **a class is the blueprint, and an object is the physical house built from that blueprint.** A single class can produce any number of objects, each with independent state but sharing the same behavioral definitions. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
*What is the role of the `new` operator in Java? List the four actions it performs.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The `new` operator in Java is used to dynamically **create (instantiate) objects** at runtime. When the statement `new ClassName(args)` is executed, the JVM performs the following four actions in sequence:

1. **Memory Allocation:** Allocates contiguous memory in the heap for the new object, sized according to the class's instance fields.
2. **Default Initialization:** Initializes all instance fields to their default values (`0`, `false`, `null`, etc.).
3. **Constructor Invocation:** Calls the matching constructor (based on the arguments supplied) to perform custom initialization logic.
4. **Reference Return:** Returns the memory address of the newly created object, which is then assigned to a reference variable in the stack. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Marks are **deducted** if you confuse *class* with *object* (e.g., "an object is a blueprint").
> - Marks are **deducted** if you forget to write the access modifier in front of fields/methods.
> - Marks are **deducted** if you omit the `this` keyword in constructor disambiguation and the answer is explicitly asked.
> - In **14-mark questions**, you must write a *complete Java program*, not just fragments. A program without a `main()` method may lose 2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**
*Consider a `Rectangle` class with private instance variables `length` and `width` of type `double`.*

**(a)** Write a complete Java program that defines the `Rectangle` class with:
- A **default constructor** that sets `length = 1.0` and `width = 1.0`.
- A **parameterized constructor** that accepts `length` and `width` as arguments.
- **Public getter and setter methods** for both variables.
- A public method `area()` that returns the area of the rectangle. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a `main()` method in a separate class `RectangleDemo` that:
- Creates **two Rectangle objects** — one using the default constructor and one using the parameterized constructor.
- Calls the `area()` method on both objects and prints the results.
- Uses the setter to **modify the length of the first object** to `7.5` and prints the new area. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — The `Rectangle` Class:**

```java
// Save as Rectangle.java
public class Rectangle {
    // Private instance fields (Encapsulation)
    private double length;
    private double width;
    
    // (i) Default Constructor
    public Rectangle() {
        this.length = 1.0;
        this.width  = 1.0;
    }
    
    // (ii) Parameterized Constructor
    public Rectangle(double length, double width) {
        this.length = length;
        this.width  = width;
    }
    
    // (iii) Getters
    public double getLength() {
        return this.length;
    }
    
    public double getWidth() {
        return this.width;
    }
    
    // (iv) Setters
    public void setLength(double length) {
        this.length = length;
    }
    
    public void setWidth(double width) {
        this.width = width;
    }
    
    // (v) Method to compute area
    public double area() {
        return this.length * this.width;
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (a):**
- Correct class structure with private fields: **1 mark**
- Default constructor with `this` keyword: **1.5 marks**
- Parameterized constructor: **1.5 marks**
- Getters and setters: **2 marks**
- `area()` method: **1 mark**

**Part (b) — The `RectangleDemo` Class:**

```java
// Save as RectangleDemo.java
public class RectangleDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create first object using default constructor
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();
        
        // Create second object using parameterized constructor
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(5.0, 3.0);
        
        // Compute and display areas
        System.out.println("Area of r1 (default) : " + r1.area());
        System.out.println("Area of r2 (5.0 x 3.0): " + r2.area());
        
        // Modify r1 using setter
        r1.setLength(7.5);
        System.out.println("Area of r1 after setLength(7.5) : " + r1.area());
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Output Derivation:**

For **r1 (default constructor):**

$$
A_{r1} = 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.0
$$

For **r2 (parameterized constructor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{r2} &= 5.0 \times 3.0 \\
       &= 15.0
\end{aligned}
$$

After `r1.setLength(7.5)` (width remains `1.0`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{r1,\text{new}} &= 7.5 \times 1.0 \\
                  &= 7.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected Console Output:**
```
Area of r1 (default) : 1.0
Area of r2 (5.0 x 3.0): 15.0
Area of r1 after setLength(7.5) : 7.5
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (b):**
- Correct creation of two objects: **2 marks**
- Correct method invocations and `println` statements: **2 marks**
- Correct use of setter to modify `r1`: **2 marks**
- Correct expected output or sample calculation: **1 mark**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**
*Explain the concept of **Encapsulation** in Java. How is it achieved using **access modifiers** and **getter/setter methods**? Write a Java program to demonstrate encapsulation with a `BankAccount` class that maintains a private balance and allows deposits and withdrawals through public methods.*

#### Model Solution:

**Conceptual Explanation (≈ 6 marks):**

**Encapsulation** is one of the four fundamental principles of Object Oriented Programming. It refers to the bundling of data (fields) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (the class), and the **restriction of direct access** to some of the object's components. This is also known as **data hiding**.

In Java, encapsulation is achieved through the following mechanisms:

1. **Access Modifiers:** The fields of a class are declared `private` so that they cannot be accessed directly from outside the class. This is the *data hiding* aspect of encapsulation.
2. **Controlled Access via Getters and Setters:** Public methods called *accessors* (getters) and *mutators* (setters) are provided to read and modify the private fields in a controlled manner. These methods can include **validation logic** to ensure the data remains in a valid state.

The real power of encapsulation is that the **internal representation** of an object can be changed without affecting the external code that uses the object — a property called *information hiding* or *abstraction*.

**Java Program Demonstration (≈ 8 marks):**

```java
// Save as BankAccount.java
public class BankAccount {
    // Private field — cannot be accessed directly from outside
    private String accountHolder;
    private double balance;
    
    // Parameterized constructor
    public BankAccount(String accountHolder, double initialBalance) {
        this.accountHolder = accountHolder;
        if (initialBalance >= 0) {
            this.balance = initialBalance;
        } else {
            this.balance = 0;
        }
    }
    
    // Public getter for account holder (read-only access)
    public String getAccountHolder() {
        return this.accountHolder;
    }
    
    // Public getter for balance
    public double getBalance() {
        return this.balance;
    }
    
    // Public method to deposit money (controlled write)
    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            this.balance += amount;
            System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid deposit amount.");
        }
    }
    
    // Public method to withdraw money (controlled write with validation)
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0 && amount <= this.balance) {
            this.balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid or insufficient funds.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Driver Class to Test:**

```java
// Save as BankAccountDemo.java
public class BankAccountDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new BankAccount("Rahul", 5000.0);
        
        System.out.println("Account Holder : " + acc.getAccountHolder());
        System.out.println("Initial Balance: " + acc.getBalance());
        
        acc.deposit(2000.0);
        System.out.println("Balance after deposit: " + acc.getBalance());
        
        acc.withdraw(1500.0);
        System.out.println("Balance after withdrawal: " + acc.getBalance());
        
        acc.withdraw(10000.0);  // Should fail
        System.out.println("Final Balance: " + acc.getBalance());
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Numerical Trace:**

Initial balance: $B_0 = 5000.0$

After `deposit(2000.0)`:

$$
B_1 = 5000.0 + 2000.0 = 7000.0
$$

After `withdraw(1500.0)`:

$$
B_2 = 7000.0 - 1500.0 = 5500.0
$$

After `withdraw(10000.0)` (rejected due to insufficient funds):

$$
B_3 = 5500.0 \quad (\text{unchanged})
$$

**Mark Allocation for Question B:**
- Conceptual explanation of encapsulation: **3 marks**
- Explanation of access modifiers and getters/setters: **3 marks**
- Correct `BankAccount` class with private fields: **2 marks**
- Public methods with validation logic: **3 marks**
- Driver class and sample output: **2 marks**
- Output trace and correct numerical values: **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 Introduction**

- **Class vs. Object:** Class is the *blueprint* (no memory); Object is the *instance* (occupies heap memory).
- **`new` Operator:** Performs **4 actions** — allocate heap memory, default-initialize fields, invoke constructor, return reference.
- **File Naming Rule:** A `.java` file may have **only one `public` class**, and the filename must match the public class name.
- **Encapsulation:** Achieved via `private` fields + `public` getters/setters. Never expose fields as `public`.
- **`this` Keyword:** Refers to the **current object**. Used to disambiguate parameters from instance fields and to pass the current object as an argument.
- **Constructor Properties:** Same name as class, **no return type** (not even `void`), invoked automatically by `new`.
- **Default Constructor:** If you write **any** constructor, Java **does not** automatically provide a no-arg constructor — you must write it yourself.
- **Reference Variables:** Stored in the **stack**; hold the *address* of objects stored in the **heap**.
- **Access Modifiers (4 levels):** `private` → *default* → `protected` → `public` (in increasing order of visibility).
- **Object Lifecycle:** Declared → Instantiated → In Use → Unreachable → Garbage Collected.
- **Garbage Collection:** Programmer **cannot force** it; `System.gc()` is only a polite *request* to the JVM.
- **Static vs. Instance:** Static members belong to the **class** (one copy); instance members belong to **each object** (separate copies).
- **Static Method Restrictions:** Static methods can only directly call other static methods and access static fields; they **cannot** use `this` or `super`.
- **Encapsulation vs. Abstraction:** Encapsulation is *data hiding*; Abstraction is *implementation hiding* (covered in later modules).
- **Common Pitfall:** Calling an instance method from a `static` context (e.g., `main`) **without** an object reference results in a compilation error.
- **Naming Convention:** Class names use **PascalCase** (`BankAccount`); fields and methods use **camelCase** (`accountBalance`).
- **Memory Rule:** Local variables (including references) → **stack**; Objects and instance fields → **heap**; Class metadata and static members → **Method Area / Metaspace**.
- **KISS Principle for Exams:** When in doubt, always include `this` in constructors — KTU examiners reward explicit disambiguation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** is a programming paradigm built around the concept of **objects** — self-contained entities that bundle together **data (attributes/fields)** and **behaviour (methods/functions)** — and **classes** — the blueprints from which objects are instantiated. The 2024 KTU scheme emphasises OOP as a *modelling discipline* that mirrors real-world entities to achieve **modularity, reusability, and maintainability** in software design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Focus (KTU PEITT411):** This module consolidates the *building blocks* of Java OOP — **classes, objects, methods, constructors, access control, `static` members, `this` keyword, parameter passing, and object lifecycle (garbage collection)**. The "Overview" topic lays the conceptual runway before diving into the syntax-heavy subtopics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint vs The Building"

Imagine you are an architect:

| Real-World Analogy | Java OOP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Architectural Blueprint** (a plan, not a building) | **Class** (a definition, not an object) |
| **Actual House built from the blueprint** | **Object** (an instance of a class) |
| **Number of bedrooms, colour, area** | **Instance Variables (fields/state)** |
| **Action: openDoor(), turnOnLight()** | **Methods (behaviour)** |
| **Society rules: "no loud music after 10 PM"** | **Access Modifiers (private, public, protected)** |
| **Common park shared by all houses** | **`static` members (class-level data)** |

You can build **100 houses** from **one blueprint** — each with its own colour, owner, and furniture (state), yet all sharing the same structural design (class). That is the essence of OOP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class ≠ Object.** A class is a *type definition*; an object is a *runtime memory allocation* of that type. The single most common KTU exam pitfall is treating these as synonymous.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars (Preview for the Module)

OOP rests on four foundational principles — frequently asked as **2-mark definitions** in KTU exams:

1. **Encapsulation** — bundling data + methods and hiding internal state.
2. **Inheritance** — deriving new classes from existing ones (Module 3 territory).
3. **Polymorphism** — one interface, many implementations (Module 4 territory).
4. **Abstraction** — exposing essential features, hiding complexity (covered via `abstract` classes / interfaces).

For Module 2's scope, **encapsulation** and the **class-object mechanism** are the operative pillars.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Procedural vs Object-Oriented Paradigm

| Aspect | Procedural (C-style) | Object-Oriented (Java) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary unit | Function | **Object (Class instance)** |
| Data handling | Passed between functions freely | Encapsulated within objects |
| Reusability | Function libraries | **Inheritance & composition** |
| Security | Data is exposed | **Access modifiers restrict access** |
| Real-world mapping | Weak | **Strong** |
| Example languages | C, Pascal, early BASIC | **Java, C++, Python (OOP mode)** |

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Java Class (Module 2 Core Concept)

Every Java class declared at the top level follows this logical structure:

1. **Package declaration** (`package com.ktu.oop;`) — optional but standard.
2. **Import statements** — bring in external classes.
3. **Class declaration** — `public class ClassName { ... }`
4. **Fields (instance variables)** — hold object state.
5. **Constructors** — special methods invoked at object creation.
6. **Methods** — define behaviour.
7. **Nested classes/interfaces** — optional inner definitions.

### 2.3 Object Lifecycle (High-Yield for KTU)

$$ \text{Class Definition} \xrightarrow{\text{new}} \text{Object Instantiation} \rightarrow \text{Use} \rightarrow \text{Unreferenced} \xrightarrow{\text{GC}} \text{Destroyed} $$

The **garbage collector (GC)** automatically reclaims memory of objects no longer referenced — Java's most celebrated feature over C++.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Skeleton | Key Rule | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class declaration** | `class Student { }` | File name must match `public` class name | Mismatched filename → compile error |
| **Object creation** | `Student s = new Student();` | `new` allocates heap memory | Forgetting parentheses of constructor |
| **Instance variable** | `int rollNo;` | Each object gets its own copy | Confusing with `static` variables |
| **`static` variable** | `static int count;` | **One copy per class**, shared by all objects | Trying to access via object reference (legal but bad style) |
| **Method** | `void display() { }` | Returns `void` or a type | Missing return statement in non-void method |
| **Constructor** | `Student() { }` | Name = class name, no return type | Adding a return type → becomes a method, not constructor |
| **`this` keyword** | `this.name = name;` | Refers to **current object** | Used outside non-static context → compile error |
| **Access: `private`** | `private int x;` | Accessible **only within the same class** | Trying to access from another class directly |
| **Access: `public`** | `public int x;` | Accessible **everywhere** | Over-exposing internal data |
| **Access: `protected`** | `protected int x;` | Same package + subclasses | Confusing with default (package-private) |
| **Default (no modifier)** | `int x;` | Same package only | Forgetting it is *not* the same as `public` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`new` keyword is mandatory in Java** to create objects. Unlike JavaScript or Python, you cannot invoke a constructor without `new` (unless using reflection).

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering

In real-world software (banking systems, e-commerce, embedded controllers), OOP enables:
- **Maintainability** — bugs localised to a class.
- **Scalability** — new features added via inheritance/composition.
- **Team development** — different developers work on different classes simultaneously.
- **Domain modelling** — `Customer`, `Order`, `Invoice` map directly to database tables and business entities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis: From Blueprint to Building

Since this is a *conceptual overview* topic, the derivation below is a **logical walkthrough** of how a class definition transforms into runtime objects — the most examination-relevant analytical process.

### 3.1 Source Code Definition (The Blueprint)

```java
public class Student {
    int rollNo;          // instance variable
    String name;         // instance variable

    Student(int r, String n) {   // constructor
        rollNo = r;
        name = n;
    }

    void display() {             // method
        System.out.println(rollNo + " " + name);
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Object Instantiation Trace

**Step 1 — Class Loading**

When the JVM encounters `Student` for the first time, the **ClassLoader subsystem** loads the `.class` bytecode into the **Method Area** of JVM memory. No `Student` object exists yet — only the *type metadata*.

**Step 2 — Encountering `new Student(...)`**

In `main()`:

```java
Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arun");
```

The JVM executes the following sequence:

1. **Class loading check** — Is `Student.class` already in the Method Area? If not, load it.
2. **Heap allocation** — The JVM computes the object's size: `int (4 bytes) + String reference (4/8 bytes)` ≈ 8–12 bytes, then allocates contiguous memory in the **heap**.
3. **Default initialisation** — All instance variables receive Java defaults (`int` → `0`, `String` reference → `null`).
4. **Constructor invocation** — The explicit constructor `Student(101, "Arun")` is called, overwriting the defaults.
5. **Reference assignment** — The address of the newly created object is stored in the local variable `s1` (which lives on the **stack**).

$$ \text{Stack (main)} \ni s1 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Heap Address 0x4A2F} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Student\{rollNo=101, name="Arun"\}} $$

**Step 3 — Creating a Second Object**

```java
Student s2 = new Student(102, "Meera");
```

A **separate** heap allocation occurs. `s2` is a different reference variable pointing to a different heap address. The two objects are **independent** — modifying `s1.rollNo` does **not** affect `s2.rollNo`.

$$ s1 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Student\{101, "Arun"\}} \quad\quad s2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Student\{102, "Meera"\}} $$

**Step 4 — Method Call Binding**

When `s1.display()` is invoked:

1. JVM looks up the method `display` in `Student`'s method table.
2. The object referenced by `s1` becomes the **current object** — accessible inside `display()` via the implicit `this` reference.
3. The bytecode for `display` is pushed onto the stack, executes, prints the output, and the stack frame is popped.

**Step 5 — Loss of Reference & Garbage Collection**

```java
s1 = s2;   // s1 now points to Meera's object; Arun's object is unreferenced
```

The original Arun-object has **zero live references** and becomes **eligible for Garbage Collection**. The JVM's GC may reclaim this memory at its discretion — Java programmers **cannot force** GC, only **request** it via `System.gc()`.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Class vs Object

| Dimension | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Template / Blueprint | Instance / Concrete entity |
| **When does it exist?** | Compile time + runtime (metadata) | Runtime only (heap allocation) |
| **Memory location** | Method Area (class metadata) | Heap |
| **Keyword to create** | `class` | `new` |
| **Number** | One per source file (typically) | Unlimited (subject to memory) |
| **Contains** | Field declarations, method signatures | Actual field **values** |
| **Lifetime** | Until classloader unloads it | Until no references remain → GC |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic for "Why `static` Is Different"

**Problem:** A college wants to track *how many Student objects* have been created so far — shared across **all** objects.

**Step 1** — Declare a class-level (not instance-level) variable:

```java
static int totalStudents = 0;
```

**Step 2** — Increment it inside the constructor:

```java
Student(int r, String n) {
    rollNo = r;
    name = n;
    totalStudents++;   // shared counter
}
```

**Step 3** — Memory trace:

$$ \text{Method Area: } Student.totalStudents = 0 \;\xrightarrow{\text{new } s1}\; 1 \;\xrightarrow{\text{new } s2}\; 2 $$

Each `new Student(...)` mutates the **single** `totalStudents` cell, regardless of which object invoked the constructor. This is why `static` fields are said to belong to the **class**, not to any individual object.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Class–Object Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    clsDef["Class Definition Student.java"] --> clsMeta["Class Metadata in Method Area"]
    clsMeta --> obj1["Object s1 in Heap"]
    clsMeta --> obj2["Object s2 in Heap"]
    clsMeta --> obj3["Object s3 in Heap"]
    obj1 --> field1a["rollNo equals 101"]
    obj1 --> field1b["name equals Arun"]
    obj2 --> field2a["rollNo equals 102"]
    obj2 --> field2b["name equals Meera"]
    obj3 --> field3a["rollNo equals 103"]
    obj3 --> field3b["name equals Kiran"]
    clsMeta --> staticMem["static int totalStudents equals 3"]
    stackRef["Stack frame of main"] -.points to.-> obj1
    stackRef2["Stack frame of main"] -.points to.-> obj2
    stackRef3["Stack frame of main"] -.points to.-> obj3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The single **Class Metadata** box (top) is the *blueprint* stored once in the Method Area.
- Three **Object boxes** are individual heap allocations — each with its own `rollNo` and `name`.
- The **static memory** is *attached* to the class itself, not to any object.
- **Dotted arrows** represent *references* (stack → heap).

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: OOP Module 2 Concept Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Object Oriented Overview"] --> B["Classes and Objects"]
    A --> C["Methods"]

    E --> E3["protected"]
```

This concept map mirrors the **official KTU Module 2 syllabus spine** — useful for last-night revision and for structuring long-answer (14-mark) answers.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — JVM Memory Regions

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph jvm["JVM Runtime Data Areas"]
        subgraph meth["Method Area Class Metadata"]
            m1["Student class bytecode"]
            m2["static totalStudents equals 3"]
            m3["Method table for display constructor"]
        end
        subgraph heap["Heap Memory"]
            h1["Object s1 rollNo 101 name Arun"]
            h2["Object s2 rollNo 102 name Meera"]
            h3["Object s3 UNREFERENCED eligible for GC"]
        end
        subgraph stack["Stack Per Thread"]
            st1["main frame local s1 reference"]
            st2["main frame local s2 reference"]
        end
    end
    st1 -.-> h1
    st2 -.-> h2
    h3 --> gc["Garbage Collector reclaims memory"]
```

---
