```java
explicitIntent.putExtra("age", 21);
startActivity(explicitIntent);
```

**Implicit Intent — Theory [2 marks]:**

An Implicit Intent declares a **general action** (e.g., `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_DIAL`) along with optional data (e.g., a URI). The Android system inspects all installed apps' manifest files to find a component whose `<intent-filter>` matches the declared action and data type. If multiple components match, the user is presented with a chooser dialog.

**Code Example:**
```java
// Opening a web page
Intent webIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://www.ktu.edu.in"));
startActivity(webIntent);

// Dialing a phone number
Intent dialIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_DIAL, Uri.parse("tel:+911234567890"));
startActivity(dialIntent);

// Sending an email
Intent emailIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_SEND);
emailIntent.setType("text/plain");
emailIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_EMAIL, new String[]{"admissions@ktu.edu.in"});
emailIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_SUBJECT, "Inquiry");
startActivity(Intent.createChooser(emailIntent, "Send Email via:"));
```

**Comparison Table [3 marks]:**

| Parameter | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Target | Specific component (class name) | Abstract action resolved by OS |
| Resolution | Direct call | Via Intent Resolution mechanism |
| Cross-app communication | No (same app) | Yes (across apps) |
| Security risk | Low | Potential (intent redirection) |
| Use case | Internal navigation | Common actions (web, dial, email) |
| Manifest declaration | Not required in target | Required (intent-filter) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Question:** Write a complete Java program that demonstrates launching a `SecondActivity` from `MainActivity` using an Explicit Intent and passing a user's name and age. The `SecondActivity` should display the received data. Show both the XML layout and Java code. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Layout for `MainActivity` (`activity_main.xml`)**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="24dp"
    android:gravity="center">

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Main Activity"
        android:textSize="28sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/nameInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter your name"
        android:inputType="textPersonName"
        android:layout_marginTop="24dp" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/ageInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter your age"
        android:inputType="number"
        android:layout_marginTop="16dp" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/submitBtn"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Send to Second Activity"
        android:layout_marginTop="24dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 2: Layout for `SecondActivity` (`activity_second.xml`)**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="24dp"
    android:gravity="center">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/displayText"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Received data will appear here"
        android:textSize="22sp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 3: `MainActivity.java`**

```java
package com.example.intentdemo;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.Button;
import android.widget.EditText;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private EditText nameInput, ageInput;
    private Button submitBtn;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        nameInput = findViewById(R.id.nameInput);
        ageInput = findViewById(R.id.ageInput);
        submitBtn = findViewById(R.id.submitBtn);

        submitBtn.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            String name = nameInput.getText().toString().trim();
            String ageStr = ageInput.getText().toString().trim();

            if (name.isEmpty() || ageStr.isEmpty()) {
                nameInput.setError("Required");
                ageInput.setError("Required");
                return;
            }

            int age = Integer.parseInt(ageStr);

            Intent intent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, SecondActivity.class);
            intent.putExtra("USER_NAME", name);
            intent.putExtra("USER_AGE", age);
            startActivity(intent);
        });
    }
}
```

**Step 4: `SecondActivity.java`**

```java
package com.example.intentdemo;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.TextView;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class SecondActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private TextView displayText;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_second);

        displayText = findViewById(R.id.displayText);

        Intent incomingIntent = getIntent();
        String name = incomingIntent.getStringExtra("USER_NAME");
        int age = incomingIntent.getIntExtra("USER_AGE", 0);

        String displayMessage = "Hello, " + name + "!\nYou are " + age + " years old.";
        displayText.setText(displayMessage);
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Manifest Declaration**

```xml
<activity
    android:name=".SecondActivity"
    android:exported="false"
    android:parentActivityName=".MainActivity">
    <meta-data
        android:name="android.support.PARENT_ACTIVITY"
        android:value=".MainActivity" />
</activity>
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Intent-Based Questions:**
> 1. **Key Mismatch** — Using different keys in `putExtra()` and `getStringExtra()` (e.g., `"USERNAME"` vs `"USER_NAME"`). This will result in a `null` value at runtime. Always double-check the key strings — **1 mark deduction** per mismatch.
> 2. **Type Mismatch** — Using `getStringExtra()` for an integer value or vice versa will throw a `ClassCastException`. Use the corresponding typed getter method.
> 3. **Missing `setContentView()`** — `findViewById()` will return `null` if the layout is not set first.
> 4. **Forgetting `parentActivityName`** — This prevents the Up button from working in the ActionBar.
> 5. **Not handling empty input** — Real-world applications must validate user input before passing it as extras.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Preparation:**

- **Definition [Critical]:** An Activity is a single-screen UI component extending `AppCompatActivity` (or `Activity`). It is the entry point for user interaction in an Android app.

- **Lifecycle Methods [Critical — 14-mark favorite]:** Memorize the seven callbacks in their exact invocation order:
  - `onCreate()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()` → [running] → `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onDestroy()`
  - Conditional: `onRestart()` is called between `onStop()` and `onStart()` when returning from background.

- **State vs Method Distinction [Critical]:**
  - `onCreate()` = **Created** state (not visible).
  - `onStart()` = **Started** state (visible but not focused).
  - `onResume()` = **Resumed** state (visible AND focused — interactive).
  - `onPause()` = **Paused** state (partially obscured).
  - `onStop()` = **Stopped** state (completely hidden).
  - `onDestroy()` = **Destroyed** state (instance finalized).

- **Back Stack [Critical]:** A LIFO data structure maintained by the Android `ActivityManager`. `startActivity()` pushes; Back button or `finish()` pops.

- **Intents [High Yield]:**
  - **Explicit** → specifies target class: `new Intent(this, TargetActivity.class)`
  - **Implicit** → declares action: `new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, uri)`
  - Data passing: `intent.putExtra("KEY", value)` and `getIntent().getXxxExtra("KEY")`

- **Manifest Declaration [Required]:** Every activity must be declared in `AndroidManifest.xml`. The launcher activity must include the `<intent-filter>` with `MAIN` action and `LAUNCHER` category.

- **Best Practices [Coding Exam]:**
  1. Always call `super.onXxx()` as the **first statement** in overridden lifecycle methods.
  2. Use `findViewById()` only after `setContentView()`.
  3. Release heavy resources in `onStop()`, not `onDestroy()` (because `onDestroy()` may not be called under memory pressure).
  4. Use `Log.d(TAG, message)` for lifecycle debugging.
  5. Validate input data before passing as intent extras.

- **Common Configuration Changes:** Screen rotation triggers full activity recreation. To handle this without recreation, declare `android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize"` in the manifest (rarely recommended in modern apps).

- **Modern Android Note:** `startActivityForResult()` is **deprecated** since AndroidX Activity 1.2.0 (2020). Use `registerForActivityResult()` with `ActivityResultContracts` instead. This is a **probable follow-up question** in advanced KTU papers.

- **Memory Rule of Thumb:** Activities at the bottom of the back stack are the **first to be killed** under memory pressure. Do not store critical state only in memory — use `onSaveInstanceState()` for transient UI state and persistent storage (SharedPreferences, Room DB) for permanent data.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Activities Notes**
> **Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Pattern**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Requirements Elicitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Managing Requirements Elicitation ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Requirements Elicitation** is the systematic process of discovering, gathering, identifying, and defining the precise needs, constraints, and business objectives of a software system from all relevant stakeholders (customers, end-users, domain experts, and regulatory bodies) to produce a structured, validated, and prioritized set of requirements that serve as the foundation for subsequent analysis, design, and implementation phases. In the context of **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** using Java, requirements elicitation precedes the construction of **Use Case diagrams**, **Class diagrams**, and **Sequence diagrams** — it is the *upstream* activity feeding the entire **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** modeling pipeline. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes requirements elicitation as a **critical Software Engineering Process (SEP)** activity, directly mapped to: - **CO2**: Apply object-oriented concepts to model real-world systems. - **CO3**: Analyze requirements using UML notations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Managing requirements elicitation encompasses **four pillars** — (1) *Identifying Stakeholders*, (2) *Elicitation Techniques*, (3) *Requirements Negotiation*, and (4) *Requirements Documentation & Validation*. Mastering these is mandatory for Module 2 university examinations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" Mental Model Imagine you want to build a house. Before laying a single brick, you must talk to: - The **family** (end-users) who will live in it — *How many bedrooms? Garden?* - The **neighbors** (regulatory bodies) — *What are the local building codes?* - The **engineer** (domain expert) — *What is the soil type? Climate constraints?* - The **carpenter** (developer) — *What materials are practically available?* If you skip these conversations and just start constructing, the house will collapse. **Requirements Elicitation is the conversation phase** — it is the *listening* stage of software construction, ensuring that what is built matches what is *needed*. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic — "S.T.A.R." for Elicitation Success:** > **S** — Stakeholder identification > **T** — Techniques applied > **A** — Analysis of constraints > **R** — Review and validation ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Definitions) | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Stakeholder** | Any individual, group, or organization affected by or having influence over the system. | | **Functional Requirements (FR)** | Statements describing *what the system must do* — inputs, processing, outputs. | | **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** | Quality attributes — *performance, security, usability, scalability*. | | **User Story** | A short, informal description of a feature from an end-user perspective (e.g., "As a *user*, I want to *login* so that *I can access my profile*"). | | **Use Case** | A structured narrative describing actor-system interactions for a specific goal. | | **Domain Expert** | A person with deep knowledge of the business area where the system will operate. | | **Elicitation** | The act of drawing out hidden, tacit, or unstated needs from stakeholders. | | **Conflict Resolution** | Negotiating contradictory requirements among stakeholders. | --- ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Requirements Elicitation Process (Stepwise Decomposition) Requirements elicitation is not a single event — it is a **multi-stage iterative process** governed by the IEEE 830-1998 standard and aligned with the **Rational Unified Process (RUP)**. **Step 1 — Stakeholder Identification** - **Why:** You cannot elicit from unknown parties. The *Who* must be defined before the *What*. - **How:** Use the **ONION MODEL** (a layered stakeholder classification). - **Core:** Identify primary users, secondary users, tertiary users, and external regulators. **Step 2 — Selecting Elicitation Techniques** - **Why:** Different stakeholders communicate differently. A CEO prefers interviews; a developer prefers workshops; an end-user prefers observation. - **How:** Map stakeholder personality to technique using a **Technique-Stakeholder Matrix**. **Step 3 — Conducting Elicitation Sessions** - **Why:** This is the *data collection* phase where tacit knowledge is converted into explicit statements. - **How:** Apply techniques such as interviews, focus groups, observation, and prototyping. **Step 4 — Documenting Requirements** - **Why:** Verbal information must be crystallized into a **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** document. - **How:** Use templates aligned with **IEEE 830-1998** or **Volere Standard**. **Step 5 — Validation and Negotiation** - **Why:** Requirements conflict, are ambiguous, or are infeasible. - **How:** Conduct **requirements review meetings**, apply **win-win negotiation** (Boehm's model), and prioritize via **MoSCoW** rules. **Step 6 — Requirements Management (Traceability)** - **Why:** A requirement at the start must map to a design element and a test case at the end. - **How:** Maintain a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**. ### 2.2 The Stakeholder Onion Model (Layered View) The Onion Model classifies stakeholders based on their **closeness to the system core**: - **Layer 1 — Core System:** Direct users, system administrators. - **Layer 2 — Immediate Environment:** Help desk staff, supervisors. - **Layer 3 — External Influencers:** Vendors, partners, third-party API providers. - **Layer 4 — Macro Environment:** Government regulators, market conditions, legal frameworks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always cite the **Onion Model** when answering stakeholder identification questions. Examiners award bonus marks for using named frameworks. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Elicitation Techniques — Comparative Matrix | Technique | Best For | Strength | Weakness | Stakeholder Type | |-----------|----------|----------|----------|------------------| | **Interviews** | One-on-one detailed needs | Deep insight, privacy | Time-consuming, single perspective | Domain experts, executives | | **Workshops / JAD Sessions** | Group consensus building | Rapid conflict resolution | Dominant personalities may hijack | Cross-functional teams | | **Observation (Ethnography)** | Understanding actual workflows | Reveals tacit workarounds | Hawthorne effect — workers change behavior | End-users in real environment | | **Questionnaires / Surveys** | Large distributed user base | Scalable, quantifiable | Low response rate, surface-level | Geographically dispersed users | | **Prototyping** | Unclear UI/UX requirements | Visual clarity, early feedback | Time investment, scope creep | End-users, customers | | **Document Analysis** | Legacy systems, regulatory domains | Historical accuracy | Outdated, missing rationale | Domain experts, compliance teams | | **Brainstorming** | Creative feature generation | Volume of ideas | Idea quality varies | Cross-functional teams | | **Focus Groups** | Sentiment analysis, perception gathering | Group dynamics spark ideas | Groupthink risk | Marketing, customer segments | | **Use Case Workshops** | OOAD context — building UML Use Cases | Direct bridge to UML diagrams | Requires UML expertise | Analysts, developers | | **Reuse of Existing Requirements** | Product-line engineering | Cost-effective | Domain mismatch | Senior analysts | ### 2.4 Boehm's Win-Win Negotiation Model — The Formula Boehm's model frames requirements negotiation as a **multi-variable optimization problem** where the goal is to maximize *stakeholder satisfaction* under *technical and economic constraints*. The **Win Condition** for a requirement is mathematically expressed as: $$ W_{req} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} (S_i \geq S_{i,min}) $$ where: - $W_{req}$ = Win condition satisfied for the requirement. - $S_i$ = Satisfaction level of stakeholder $i$. - $S_{i,min}$ = Minimum acceptable satisfaction threshold for stakeholder $i$. - $n$ = Total number of stakeholders. - $\bigwedge$ = Logical AND (all stakeholder conditions must be met). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Boehm's Win-Win model is widely used in **agile enterprise software**, **aerospace systems (NASA)**, and **medical device software (FDA-regulated)**. It is the de-facto negotiation framework when stakeholder satisfaction is mission-critical. ### 2.5 MoSCoW Prioritization — Classification Rules | Priority | Meaning | Action | |----------|---------|--------| | **M — Must Have** | System fails without this | Non-negotiable, delivered in release 1 | | **S — Should Have** | Important but not vital | Delivered if time permits | | **C — Could Have** | Desirable, smaller impact | Delivered in later releases | | **W — Won't Have (this time)** | Explicitly out of scope | Deferred to future versions | --- ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Requirements Elicitation Workflow — Detailed Derivation The elicitation workflow can be modeled as a **state machine** with six discrete states. The transition from state $S_n$ to $S_{n+1}$ is governed by a *gating condition* $G_n$. **State Definition:** $$ E = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5, S_6\} $$ where: - $S_1$ = Stakeholder Identification - $S_2$ = Technique Selection - $S_3$ = Data Collection - $S_4$ = Documentation - $S_5$ = Validation & Negotiation - $S_6$ = Traceability & Management **Transition Equation:** $$ T: S_n \xrightarrow{G_n} S_{n+1} $$ **Step-by-Step Derivation of the Transition Path:** **Step 1 — Initial State (Pre-Elicitation):** The project begins with the *problem statement* $\mathcal{P}$ provided by the client. This is ambiguous and high-level. $$ S_0 = \mathcal{P} = \text{``Build an online library management system.''} $$ **Step 2 — Transition to Stakeholder Identification ($S_0 \rightarrow S_1$):** The trigger is project kickoff. Stakeholders are enumerated: $$ S_1 = \{ \text{Librarian, Member, Admin, Payment Gateway, Govt. Auditor} \} $$ **Step 3 — Transition to Technique Selection ($S_1 \rightarrow S_2$):** The gating condition $G_1$ is *"stakeholder count $\geq 1$ AND stakeholder personas defined"*. Techniques selected: $$ S_2 = \{ \text{Interview (Librarian), JAD (Admin), Survey (Members), Doc Analysis (Govt. compliance)} \} $$ **Step 4 — Transition to Data Collection ($S_2 \rightarrow S_3$):** Elicitation sessions yield raw requirements: $$ S_3 = \{R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_k\} $$ For example, $R_1 =$ *"The system shall allow members to search books by title, author, ISBN."* **Step 5 — Transition to Documentation ($S_3 \rightarrow S_4$):** Raw requirements are written into SRS using the IEEE 830 template. $$ S_4 = \text{SRS Document} = \langle \text{ID, Description, Priority, Source, Acceptance Criteria} \rangle $$ **Step 6 — Transition to Validation ($S_4 \rightarrow S_5$):** Review meetings identify conflicts. For example: - Librarian wants *"Maximum 3 books per member"*. - Admin wants *"Unlimited books for premium members"*. These are **conflicting requirements**. Negotiation yields: $$ R_{final} = \arg\max_{R} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i(R) \right) \quad \text{subject to} \quad C(R) \leq B $$ where $C(R)$ is cost, $B$ is budget, and $\sum S_i$ is total stakeholder satisfaction. **Step 7 — Transition to Traceability ($S_5 \rightarrow S_6$):** Each requirement $R_j$ is mapped to design elements (classes) and test cases. $$ \text{RTM}[R_j] = \langle \text{Class}_j, \text{UseCase}_j, \text{TestCase}_j, \text{Status}_j \rangle $$ ### 3.2 The Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Formal Construction The RTM is a **bi-directional mapping** between requirements and downstream artifacts. **Forward Traceability:** $$ R_j \rightarrow D_k \rightarrow T_m $$ (Requirement $\rightarrow$ Design element $\rightarrow$ Test case) **Backward Traceability:** $$ T_m \rightarrow D_k \rightarrow R_j $$ (Test case $\rightarrow$ Design element $\rightarrow$ Requirement) **Complete Traceability Condition:** $$ \forall R_j \in \text{ReqSet}, \exists (D_k, T_m) : \text{RTM}[R_j] \neq \emptyset $$ That is, every requirement must be traceable to at least one design element and one test case. ### 3.3 Conflict Resolution — Mathematical Formulation Given two conflicting requirements $R_a$ and $R_b$ from stakeholders $A$ and $B$: $$ C_{conflict} = R_a \oplus R_b = \text{Logical XOR (mutually exclusive)} $$ **Resolution Strategies:** 1. **Compromise:** $R_{res} = R_a \cap R_b$ (intersection of both) 2. **Escalation:** $R_{res} = \arg\max (\text{Business Value})$ 3. **Substitution:** $R_{res} = R_c$ where $R_c$ is a third alternative proposed by a mediator 4. **Deferral:** $R_{res} = \text{OutOfScope}$ (using MoSCoW "Won't Have") **Composite Negotiation Score:** $$ N_{score} = w_1 \cdot S_A + w_2 \cdot S_B + w_3 \cdot S_C $$ where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ are weights assigned to stakeholders based on priority, and $S_X$ is satisfaction of stakeholder $X$ on a scale of 0 to 1. The optimal requirement is: $$ R_{opt} = \arg\max_{R} (N_{score}(R)) $$ subject to feasibility constraints $F(R) = 1$ and budget $B(R) \leq B_{max}$. --- ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 The Requirements Elicitation Lifecycle — Mermaid Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Problem Statement S0] --> B[Identify Stakeholders S1] B --> C[Select Elicitation Techniques S2] C --> D[Conduct Elicitation Sessions S3] D --> E[Document Requirements SRS S4] E --> F{Conflicts Detected?} F -->|Yes| G[Negotiation and Win-Win Resolution] G --> E F -->|No| H[Requirements Validation Review] H --> I{Approved?} I -->|No| C I -->|Yes| J[Build Requirements Traceability Matrix RTM S6] J --> K[Output: Validated SRS Document] K --> L[Hand-off to OO Analysis Phase] ``` ### 4.2 Stakeholder Onion Model — Mermaid Layered View ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Layer 1: Core System Users
Direct Operators, Administrators] L2[Layer 2: Immediate Environment
Help Desk, Supervisors, Trainers] L3[Layer 3: External Influencers
Vendors, Partners, Third Party APIs] L4[Layer 4: Macro Environment
Government, Regulators, Market Forces] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 ``` ### 4.3 Technique-Stakeholder Mapping — Mermaid Decision Graph ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stakeholder Type] --> B{Is it an End User?} B -->|Yes| C[Observation or Prototyping] B -->|No| D{Is it an Executive?} D -->|Yes| E[Structured Interview] D -->|No| F{Is it a Cross Functional Team?} F -->|Yes| G[JAD Workshop or Brainstorming] F -->|No| H{Is it a Distributed Large Group?} H -->|Yes| I[Survey or Questionnaire] H -->|No| J[Document Analysis or Reuse] ``` ### 4.4 Use Case Elicitation — Bridge to UML Modeling ```mermaid flowchart TD REQ[Raw Requirement from Elicitation] --> UC[Identify Actor] UC --> GOAL[Define Actor Goal] GOAL --> UC2[Draft Use Case Title] UC2 --> SCEN[Write Main Success Scenario] SCEN --> ALT[Identify Alternative and Exception Flows] ALT --> UC3[Add Pre and Post Conditions] UC3 --> UML[Render as UML Use Case Diagram] UML --> CLASS[Map to Class Diagram in OOD Phase] ``` ### 4.5 RTM Structure — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR REQ[Requirement ID] --> DES[Design Element Class] REQ --> TEST[Test Case ID] DES --> CODE[Java Source File] TEST --> RES[Test Result Pass or Fail] RES --> SIG[Signoff and Closure] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define requirements elicitation. List any four elicitation techniques.* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Requirements elicitation is the process of gathering, discovering, and documenting the needs, constraints, and business objectives of a software system from all relevant stakeholders through systematic techniques such as interviews, workshops, observation, and prototyping. **Four Elicitation Techniques:** 1. **Interviews** — One-on-one structured or unstructured sessions with stakeholders. 2. **Workshops (JAD Sessions)** — Joint Application Development sessions with cross-functional groups. 3. **Observation** — Watching end-users perform their actual workflows to uncover tacit requirements. 4. **Prototyping** — Building a working mockup to elicit user feedback on UI/UX. **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** - Definition — 1.5 marks - Four techniques — 1.5 marks (0.375 each) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *What is a stakeholder? Explain the Onion Model of stakeholder identification.* **Model Answer:** **Stakeholder Definition:** A stakeholder is any individual, group, or organization that has a direct or indirect interest in the system being developed, including users, customers, developers, managers, regulators, and competitors. **Onion Model Explanation:** The Onion Model, proposed by McManus (2004), classifies stakeholders into four concentric layers based on their proximity to the system core: 1. **Layer 1 — Core System:** Direct users and operators. 2. **Layer 2 — Immediate Environment:** Help desk, support staff, supervisors. 3. **Layer 3 — External Influencers:** Suppliers, partners, third-party service providers. 4. **Layer 4 — Macro Environment:** Government bodies, market forces, legal frameworks. Each layer has different influence and elicitation strategies. **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** - Stakeholder definition — 1 mark - Onion Model layered explanation — 2 marks --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern --- ## ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Set Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **requirements elicitation process** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the challenges encountered during requirements elicitation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **Use Case** elicitation? Explain the steps involved in transforming elicited requirements into UML Use Case diagrams with an example. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer for Q.A(a) — Requirements Elicitation Process (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Requirements elicitation is a structured, multi-stage process involving stakeholder identification, technique selection, data collection, documentation, validation, and management. It is the most critical phase in software development, as errors here propagate downstream at 50x to 200x the cost of post-deployment fixes (Boehm, 1981). **Process Steps (4 marks):** **Step 1 — Stakeholder Identification:** Use the **Onion Model** to classify all interested parties. Create a **Stakeholder Register** capturing their roles, influence, and communication preferences. **Step 2 — Technique Selection:** Apply the **Technique-Stakeholder Matrix** to map techniques to stakeholder types. For example, executives → interviews; end-users → observation; cross-functional groups → JAD workshops. **Step 3 — Conducting Elicitation Sessions:** Execute selected techniques. Capture raw data using audio recordings, meeting minutes, and field notes. Use the **5W1H framework** (Who, What, When, Where, Why, How) to ensure comprehensive coverage. **Step 4 — Documentation:** Convert raw data into structured requirement statements using the format: *"The system shall [action] [object] [constraint]."* Example: *"The system shall send an email notification to the user within 5 minutes of order placement."* **Step 5 — Validation and Negotiation:** Apply **Boehm's Win-Win Model** to resolve conflicts. Use **MoSCoW** rules for prioritization. **Step 6 — Traceability:** Build a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** linking each requirement to design, code, and test cases. **Challenges (2 marks):** 1. **Stakeholder Unavailability** — Domain experts are often busy; scheduling is difficult. 2. **Tacit Knowledge** — Users often do operations automatically without articulating them. 3. **Conflicting Requirements** — Multiple stakeholders with competing goals. 4. **Ambiguity** — Natural language requirements can be interpreted differently. 5. **Scope Creep** — New requirements added without proper change control. 6. **Volatility** — Requirements change as business conditions evolve. **Diagram Reference:** Section 4.1 of these notes provides the complete flow diagram. --- ### Model Answer for Q.A(b) — Use Case Elicitation (7 marks) **Use Case Definition (1 mark):** A **Use Case** is a UML construct that captures a discrete unit of interaction between an actor (external entity) and the system to achieve a specific goal. It is the primary bridge between requirements and object-oriented design. **Transformation Steps (5 marks):** **Step 1 — Identify Actors:** From elicited requirements, identify all external entities (human users, external systems). For an **Online Banking System**: Actor = Customer, Bank Staff, ATM Machine, External Payment Gateway. **Step 2 — Identify Use Cases:** For each actor, identify goals: - Customer: Login, Check Balance, Transfer Funds, View Statement. - Bank Staff: Approve Loan, Block Account, Generate Report. - ATM Machine: Dispense Cash, Print Receipt. **Step 3 — Write Use Case Narratives:** Use the template: | Section | Content | |---------|---------| | Use Case Name | Transfer Funds | | Actor | Customer | | Precondition | Customer is logged in | | Main Flow | 1. Customer selects "Transfer". 2. System displays form. 3. Customer enters details. 4. System validates. 5. System debits and credits accounts. 6. System displays success. | | Postcondition | Funds transferred; transaction logged | | Alternate Flow | Insufficient balance: System displays error message | **Step 4 — Draw UML Use Case Diagram:** Render actors as stick figures and use cases as ellipses. Connect with solid lines for primary actor associations and dashed lines for <> and <> relationships. **Step 5 — Refine for OOD:** Each use case becomes a candidate for class identification. The noun-extraction technique (Abbott's method) is applied to identify classes, attributes, and methods. **Example (1 mark):** For *"Transfer Funds"* use case, extracted classes include `Account`, `Transaction`, `Customer`, `AuditLog`. Methods include `debit()`, `credit()`, `validateBalance()`. --- ## ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Set Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 2024 Alternative]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the various **requirements elicitation techniques** with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **requirements negotiation**? Explain **Boehm's Win-Win negotiation model** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** ### Model Answer for Q.B(a) — Elicitation Techniques (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Requirements elicitation techniques are structured methods used to extract needs from stakeholders. Each technique has strengths and limitations depending on the context, stakeholder type, and project phase. **Detailed Techniques (5 marks):** **1. Interviews (1 mark):** - **Merits:** Deep insight, privacy for sensitive topics, flexible (structured/unstructured). - **Demerits:** Time-consuming, single perspective, interviewer bias. - **Best for:** Domain experts, executives, compliance officers. **2. Workshops / JAD Sessions (0.75 marks):** - **Merits:** Rapid consensus, cross-functional alignment, real-time conflict resolution. - **Demerits:** Dominant personality hijack, scheduling difficulty. - **Best for:** Cross-functional teams during early project phases. **3. Observation (0.75 marks):** - **Merits:** Reveals tacit knowledge and actual workflows. - **Demerits:** Hawthorne effect (workers alter behavior), time-intensive. - **Best for:** End-users in operational environments. **4. Questionnaires / Surveys (0.75 marks):** - **Merits:** Scalable, quantifiable, geographically independent. - **Demerits:** Low response rates, surface-level data, no follow-up clarification. - **Best for:** Large distributed user bases. **5. Prototyping (0.75 marks):** - **Merits:** Visual clarity, early feedback, reduces ambiguity. - **Demerits:** Risk of scope creep, user may mistake prototype for final system. - **Best for:** UI/UX-heavy applications. **6. Document Analysis (0.5 marks):** - **Merits:** Historical accuracy, regulatory compliance validation. - **Demerits:** Outdated information, missing rationale. - **Best for:** Legacy systems, heavily regulated domains. **7. Brainstorming (0.5 marks):** - **Merits:** High volume of creative ideas. - **Demerits:** Quality variance, requires strong facilitation. - **Best for:** Innovation workshops, feature ideation. **Conclusion (1 mark):** No single technique is universally optimal. Experienced business analysts combine multiple techniques — a strategy called **triangulation** — to validate requirements from multiple sources, ensuring completeness and correctness. --- ### Model Answer for Q.B(b) — Requirements Negotiation & Win-Win Model (7 marks) **Requirements Negotiation Definition (1 mark):** Requirements negotiation is the structured process of resolving conflicts, balancing trade-offs, and reaching mutual agreement among stakeholders on requirement priorities, scope, and constraints. It transforms competing "wants" into agreed "wins." **Boehm's Win-Win Model Explanation (5 marks):** Barry Boehm's **Win-Win Model** (1989) treats requirements as a *negotiation process* where the goal is to achieve a **win condition** for *all* key stakeholders, not just the customer. **Key Concepts:** 1. **Win Conditions:** Desired outcomes for each stakeholder. Example: Customer wants low cost; Developer wants technical feasibility; Manager wants on-time delivery. 2. **Stakeholder Satisfaction Function $S_i$:** A score (0 to 1) representing how well requirement $R$ satisfies stakeholder $i$. 3. **Negotiation as Multi-Objective Optimization:** - The goal is to find $R$ such that all $S_i \geq S_{i,min}$. 4. **The Win Condition Equation:** $$ W_{req} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} (S_i \geq S_{i,min}) $$ 5. **Iteration through Negotiation Rounds:** - Round 1: Initial proposal. - Round 2: Stakeholders identify losses. - Round 3: Propose alternative "win-win" options. - Round 4: Re-evaluate satisfaction functions. - Repeat until $W_{req} = \text{TRUE}$. **Example (1 mark):** **Scenario:** A hospital information system has three stakeholders: - **Doctors** want: Real-time patient data access. - **IT Department** wants: Strong security and encryption. - **Management** wants: Low infrastructure cost. **Initial Requirement $R_1$:** *"System shall provide real-time patient data."* - Doctor satisfaction: $S_D = 0.9$ ✓ - IT satisfaction: $S_{IT} = 0.3$ ✗ (security too weak) - Management satisfaction: $S_M = 0.4$ ✗ (real-time = expensive) **Win condition fails** for $R_1$. **Negotiated Requirement $R_2$:** *"System shall provide near-real-time patient data with 5-minute latency, end-to-end AES-256 encryption, on a cloud-hosted server with pay-per-use pricing."* - Doctor satisfaction: $S_D = 0.7$ ✓ (acceptable) - IT satisfaction: $S_{IT} = 0.85$ ✓ (encryption added) - Management satisfaction: $S_M = 0.8$ ✓ (pay-per-use saves capex) **Win condition satisfied:** $W_{R_2} = \text{TRUE}$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:** > 1. **Confusing "Elicitation" with "Analysis"** — Elicitation is the *gathering*; Analysis is the *modeling*. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for interchangeable use. > 2. **Listing techniques without merits/demerits** — KTU evaluators expect a comparative table or matrix. A bare list yields only 50% marks. > 3. **Skipping the Onion Model** — Whenever stakeholder identification appears, mentioning the **Onion Model by name** is mandatory. Skipping it costs 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting traceability** — Elicitation is incomplete without mentioning the **RTM**. This is a frequent 2-mark loss. > 5. **Using absolute value `|x|` in tables** — Always use `\vert x \vert` notation. Broken markdown tables cost presentation marks. > 6. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers** — Each 14-mark question *must* include at least one neat block diagram or table. Missing diagrams lead to 1-2 mark deductions. > 7. **Skipping the "Why" in Win-Win** — Merely defining the equation is insufficient. You must *apply* it to a real-world example to earn full marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** > > ✅ **Definition:** Requirements elicitation = discovering, gathering, and documenting stakeholder needs through systematic techniques. > > ✅ **Six-Step Process:** Stakeholder ID → Technique Selection → Data Collection → Documentation → Validation/Negotiation → Traceability. > > ✅ **Onion Model:** Four layers — Core System, Immediate Environment, External Influencers, Macro Environment. > > ✅ **Ten Key Techniques:** Interviews, JAD Workshops, Observation, Surveys, Prototyping, Document Analysis, Brainstorming, Focus Groups, Use Case Workshops, Reuse. > > ✅ **Boehm's Win-Win Equation:** $W_{req} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} (S_i \geq S_{i,min})$ — all stakeholders must achieve minimum satisfaction. > > ✅ **MoSCoW Prioritization:** Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, Won't Have (this time). > > ✅ **RTM:** Forward and backward traceability between requirements, design, code, and tests. > > ✅ **UML Bridge:** Elicited requirements → Use Case diagrams → Class diagrams → Sequence diagrams (the OOD pipeline). > > ✅ **IEEE 830-1998:** The standard SRS template for documenting elicited requirements. > > ✅ **Triangulation:** Use multiple techniques to cross-validate requirements and reduce single-source bias. > > ✅ **Change Control:** All new requirements must go through a formal change request and impact analysis before approval. > > ✅ **Agile Note:** In Scrum, elicitation is embedded in *Sprint Planning* and *Backlog Grooming* sessions, not as a separate phase. ---

topics.title: Analysis: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA)** is the **first technical activity** in the software development lifecycle of an object-oriented system. It is a *requirements-discovery* phase in which the **problem domain is investigated**, the **requirements of the system are understood**, and a **set of graphical models** is built to capture the **essential behavior and structure** of the system under construction — *without committing to any implementation technology or programming language*.

According to standard Object-Oriented (OO) terminology accepted in the KTU 2024 syllabus, OOA answers the question:
> *"What must the system do, and what are the objects that exist in the problem domain?"*

It is an **investigation phase** (not a coding phase). The artefacts produced during OOA include:
- **Use-Case Diagrams**
- **Class Diagrams (Analysis-level, not Design-level)**
- **Activity Diagrams**
- **Sequence / Collaboration Diagrams (Analysis-level)**
- **CRC Cards (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Architect's Blueprint Analogy**
>
> Imagine a family that wants a new house. Before the engineer starts pouring concrete or laying bricks, an **architect** visits the family to understand their needs:
> - How many rooms? (Requirements)
> - How big should the kitchen be? (Domain Knowledge)
> - Where should the doors and windows face? (Objects and their behavior)
>
> The architect then produces a **set of blueprints** that describe *what the house should look like and how it should behave* — **without yet choosing the type of bricks, the brand of cement, or the colour of paint**.
>
> The **Analysis phase** in Object-Oriented Design is exactly this architect's visit. The **analyst** is the architect of the software. The **diagrams produced** are the blueprints. The **Design phase** (next module) is what follows, where we *choose the technology, the database, the framework, and the Java classes*.

### 1.3 Why Analysis Comes *Before* Design — A Critical Distinction

| Phase | Question Answered | Focus | Output |
|------|------------------|------|--------|
| **Analysis (OOA)** | **WHAT** should the system do? | Problem domain, requirements | Conceptual models |
| **Design (OOD)** | **HOW** should the system do it? | Solution domain, technology | Detailed class diagrams, architecture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 introduces **Analysis** as a **language-independent** activity. The Java-specific coding decisions belong to Module 3 and beyond. **Do not confuse OOA with Java coding** in exam answers.

### 1.4 Key Vocabulary (High-Frequency KTU Terms)

- **Problem Domain** — The real-world subject area for which the software is being built (e.g., Banking, Hospital, Library).
- **Stakeholder** — Any person who has an interest in the system (user, manager, customer, regulator).
- **Requirement** — A specific need that the system must fulfil. Classified as *Functional* or *Non-Functional*.
- **Use Case** — A description of a sequence of actions the system performs to yield a valuable result to a particular actor.
- **Actor** — An external entity (person, system, or device) that interacts with the system.
- **Object** — An entity with well-defined boundaries, identity, state, and behavior.
- **Class (Analysis-level)** — A description of a group of objects with common properties, behavior, and relationships.
- **CRC Card** — A brainstorming tool that lists a class's **C**lass name, **R**esponsibilities, and **C**ollaborators.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Activities of Object-Oriented Analysis

OOA is not a single step — it is a **structured investigation** consisting of the following five logical activities. Examiners frequently ask for these in either short-answer or descriptive form.

**Activity 1 — Requirements Gathering (Elicitation)**
- Meet with stakeholders (end-users, managers, domain experts).
- Capture **functional requirements** (what the system does) and **non-functional requirements** (performance, security, usability, scalability).
- Techniques: interviews, questionnaires, observation, document analysis, workshops.

**Activity 2 — Domain Analysis**
- Study the *problem domain* to build a *common vocabulary* between developers and users.
- Identify the **key concepts** (candidate objects and classes).
- This produces a *domain dictionary / glossary* used throughout OOA and OOD.

**Activity 3 — Use-Case Modelling**
- Identify the **actors** and the **use cases** (scenarios of interaction).
- Build the **Use-Case Diagram** showing actors outside the system boundary and use cases as ovals inside.
- Write textual **Use-Case Descriptions** (preconditions, main flow, alternate flows, postconditions).

**Activity 4 — Identifying Objects and Classes (Object Identification)**
- Apply techniques such as:
  - **Grammatical Approach** (use nouns from the use case description as candidate classes).
  - **Common-Class Approach** (look for classes that are commonly needed in any system — e.g., `Transaction`, `Report`).
  - **CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) Cards** — write index cards for each class.
  - **Use-Case Driven Approach** — extract objects from the actors and scenarios of the use cases.
- Filter the candidate classes using the *Class Selection Criteria*: retain the class only if it has **retained information**, **needed services**, and **multiple instances** (Coad & Yourdon rule).

**Activity 5 — Establishing Class Relationships and Behaviour**
- Identify **associations** (semantic connections), **inheritance** (generalization/specialization), **aggregation** and **composition** (whole-part relationships).
- Build the **Analysis Class Diagram**.
- Use **Sequence Diagrams** and **Collaboration Diagrams** to capture how objects collaborate over time to fulfil each use case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Analysis phase is critical in *all large-scale Java enterprise applications* (banking, healthcare, e-commerce). The models produced are **language-agnostic** and can later be implemented in Java, Python, C#, or any OO language. A solid analysis phase **prevents costly re-engineering** in the design and implementation phases.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| **OOA Term** | **Definition / Meaning** | **Typical KTU Marks** |
|--------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **OOA** | Object-Oriented Analysis — *what* the system must do | Conceptual / 3-mark definition |
| **Problem Domain** | Real-world area the software addresses | 3-mark |
| **Use Case** | A sequence of actions yielding observable value to an actor | 3-mark / 7-mark |
| **Actor** | External entity interacting with the system | 3-mark |
| **Functional Requirement** | A specific behaviour the system must exhibit | 3-mark |
| **Non-Functional Requirement** | Quality attribute (speed, security, usability) | 3-mark |
| **CRC Card** | Class–Responsibility–Collaborator brainstorming card | 7-mark |
| **Domain Analysis** | Study of the problem domain to build shared vocabulary | 7-mark |
| **Grammatical Approach** | Identify candidate classes from nouns in the requirement | 7-mark |
| **Class Selection Criteria** | Retain a class if it has *retained info*, *needed services*, *multiple instances* | 3-mark |
| **Use-Case Diagram** | UML diagram showing actors, use cases, and system boundary | 14-mark full question |
| **Analysis Class Diagram** | UML diagram showing classes, attributes, operations, relationships | 14-mark full question |

### 2.3 Coad & Yourdon's Class Selection Criteria — A Deeper Look

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU question**. A candidate class is *kept* in the analysis model only if **ALL THREE** of the following conditions are satisfied:

1. **Retained Information** — Does the class need to remember data across multiple operations? *(If yes, the class must store attributes.)*
2. **Needed Services** — Does the class provide a clear set of operations that other classes will require? *(If yes, it has well-defined behavior.)*
3. **Multiple Instances** — Will there ever be more than one instance of this class during execution? *(If no, it is a degenerate object and may be dropped.)*

If **any one** condition fails, the candidate is *rejected* as a full class and may be demoted to an *attribute* of another class.

### 2.4 The Output Models of OOA — A Summary

| **Model** | **Purpose** | **Primary Use** |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Use-Case Diagram | Show who interacts with the system and what they want | Capturing requirements |
| Use-Case Description | Textual narrative of a scenario | Detailed behaviour |
| Class Diagram (Analysis) | Show domain classes, attributes, operations, relationships | Static structure |
| Sequence Diagram | Show object interactions in time order | Dynamic behaviour |
| Collaboration Diagram | Show objects and the messages they exchange | Dynamic behaviour (focus on links) |
| Activity Diagram | Show workflow / business process | Process flow |
| CRC Card | Brainstorm classes, responsibilities, and collaborators | Object identification |
| State-Chart Diagram | Show how an object changes state in response to events | Lifecycle behaviour |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Performing OOA

> [!NOTE]
> Analysis is a **process**, not a calculation. The "derivation" equivalent for this topic is the **ordered sequence of steps** an analyst follows. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step procedure a KTU examiner expects when answering "Explain how OOA is performed" or "Describe the steps involved in Object-Oriented Analysis".

---

### **Step 1 — Define the Problem Statement**
Write a single, clear paragraph that captures the **purpose**, **scope**, and **major players** of the system.

> *Example:* "Develop an **ATM (Automated Teller Machine) system** that allows bank customers to **withdraw cash, check balance, and transfer funds** using a card and PIN, while the **bank** monitors and records all transactions."

---

### **Step 2 — Gather Requirements**
Conduct stakeholder interviews and capture both:

- **Functional Requirements (FRs):**
  - *FR-1:* The system shall authenticate the customer using a PIN.
  - *FR-2:* The system shall dispense cash on a valid withdrawal request.
  - *FR-3:* The system shall print a receipt for every transaction.
- **Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs):**
  - *NFR-1:* Response time for any transaction must be **≤ 5 seconds**.
  - *NFR-2:* All financial data must be encrypted (security).
  - *NFR-3:* The system shall be available **24 × 7** (availability).

---

### **Step 3 — Build the Domain Model (Domain Analysis)**
Extract **domain concepts** (nouns) and **domain operations** (verbs) from the requirements document.

| **Noun (Candidate Class)** | **Verb (Candidate Responsibility)** |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Customer | authenticate, checkBalance |
| ATM | dispenseCash, readCard |
| BankAccount | debit, credit, getBalance |
| Transaction | record, generateReceipt |
| Bank | verifyPIN, monitor |

This becomes the *Domain Dictionary* — a common vocabulary shared by analysts, users, and developers.

---

### **Step 4 — Identify Actors and Use Cases**
Actors are **external** to the system; they represent roles.

| **Actor** | **Type** | **Use Cases Triggered** |
|-----------|----------|--------------------------|
| Customer | Primary | Withdraw Cash, Check Balance, Transfer Funds |
| Bank Server | Secondary | Verify PIN, Update Account |
| Maintenance Staff | Secondary | Refill Cash, Diagnose Fault |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** If an entity is *inside* the system, it is **not** an actor. The **Bank Server** is an actor only if it is external to the ATM system under construction; otherwise it is an internal collaborating object.

---

### **Step 5 — Create the Use-Case Diagram**
Draw the system boundary, place the actor icons outside, the use cases as ovals inside, and connect with simple lines (no arrows needed between actor and use case — KTU convention).

---

### **Step 6 — Write Use-Case Descriptions**
For the use case **"Withdraw Cash"**:

| **Field** | **Description** |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Use-Case Name | Withdraw Cash |
| Actor | Customer |
| Precondition | Customer is authenticated, has a valid account with sufficient balance |
| Main Flow | 1. Customer inserts card → 2. System asks for PIN → 3. Customer enters PIN → 4. System validates → 5. Customer selects "Withdraw" → 6. Customer enters amount → 7. System checks balance → 8. System dispenses cash → 9. System prints receipt → 10. System ejects card |
| Alternate Flow | If balance is insufficient, system displays an "Insufficient Funds" message and ends the use case. |
| Postcondition | Customer's account is debited by the withdrawal amount, and cash is dispensed. |

---

### **Step 7 — Identify Classes via the Grammatical / CRC Approach**
Apply the **Grammatical Approach** — every **noun** in the use-case description is a candidate class. Then apply **Coad & Yourdon's Class Selection Criteria**.

| **Candidate** | Retained Info? | Needed Services? | Multiple Instances? | Decision |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|----------|
| Customer | YES (name, account) | YES (auth, transact) | YES | **Keep** |
| Card | YES (cardNo, expiry) | YES (validate) | YES | **Keep** |
| PIN | NO (transient value) | NO | NO | **Drop → attribute of Customer/Card** |
| Cash | YES (denomination, count) | YES (dispense) | YES | **Keep** |
| Receipt | YES (txnNo, time) | YES (print) | YES | **Keep** |

---

### **Step 8 — Establish Class Relationships**
For the ATM domain, the relationships that emerge are:

- **Customer *uses* ATM** → Association
- **ATM *has-a* CashDispenser, ReceiptPrinter, CardReader** → Aggregation (whole-part)
- **Transaction *is-a* Withdrawal / Deposit / Transfer** → Generalization (inheritance)
- **BankAccount *is owned by* Customer** → Association with multiplicity `1 to 1..*`

---

### **Step 9 — Build the Analysis Class Diagram**
Produce the **UML Class Diagram** showing each class with:
- **Class name** (top compartment)
- **Attributes** (middle compartment) — only the *essential* ones for analysis
- **Operations / Responsibilities** (bottom compartment)
- **Relationships** with proper multiplicity (`1`, `*`, `1..*`)

> **Example (Analysis-level — not Java):**
> ```
> +----------------------+
> |     BankAccount      |
> +----------------------+
> | - accountNumber      |
> | - balance            |
> +----------------------+
> | + debit(amount)      |
> | + credit(amount)     |
> | + getBalance()       |
> +----------------------+
> ```

---

### **Step 10 — Build Dynamic Models (Sequence / Collaboration / Activity)**
For each major use case, draw a **Sequence Diagram** showing:
- The **lifelines** of the actor and key objects (vertical dashed lines)
- **Messages** exchanged in chronological order (horizontal arrows)
- **Activation bars** showing when an object is busy executing a method

> This dynamic model answers: *"When the Customer withdraws cash, which object talks to which object, and in what order?"*

---

### **Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for KTU Answers**

| **OOA Step** | **Input Artefact** | **Output Artefact** | **KTU-Exam Importance** |
|--------------|--------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 1. Problem statement | Stakeholder need | Problem description | Low (setup) |
| 2. Requirement gathering | Interviews, docs | FR & NFR list | **High** (3-mark / 7-mark) |
| 3. Domain analysis | Requirements | Domain dictionary | **High** (7-mark) |
| 4. Use-case modelling | Domain model | Use-case diagram + descriptions | **Very High** (14-mark) |
| 5. Object identification | Use cases | Candidate classes + CRC | **Very High** (7-mark / 14-mark) |
| 6. Class relationships | Candidate classes | Class diagram (analysis) | **Very High** (14-mark) |
| 7. Dynamic modelling | Class diagram | Sequence / Collaboration diagrams | High (7-mark) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure A — The OOA Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete flow of Object-Oriented Analysis** as expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Inception] --> B[Step 1: Define Problem Statement]
    B --> C[Step 2: Gather Requirements]
    C --> C1[Functional Requirements]
    C --> C2[Non-Functional Requirements]
    C1 --> D[Step 3: Domain Analysis]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Step 4: Identify Actors and Use Cases]
    E --> F[Step 5: Build Use-Case Diagram]
    E --> F2[Step 6: Write Use-Case Descriptions]
    F --> G[Step 7: Identify Candidate Classes]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Step 8: Apply Class Selection Criteria]
    H --> I{Candidate Passes All Three Tests?}
    I -->|No| J[Reject or Demote to Attribute]
    I -->|Yes| K[Step 9: Establish Class Relationships]
    J --> G
    K --> L[Step 10: Build Analysis Class Diagram]
    L --> M[Step 11: Build Dynamic Models]
    M --> M1[Sequence Diagram]
    M --> M2[Collaboration Diagram]
    M --> M3[State-Chart Diagram]
    M1 --> N[End: OOA Artefacts Complete]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show the **logical sequence**. The `I` (diamond) is a **decision point** — candidates that fail Coad & Yourdon's three tests are sent back to the candidate list. The final output `N` represents the **OOA artefact set** which is then handed over to the **Design phase** (Module 3).

---

### 4.2 Figure B — Block-Level Functional Architecture of OOA Artefacts

This diagram shows **how the various OOA models relate to each other** within the analysis phase.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Requirements Document] --> B[Use-Case Diagram]
    A --> C[Activity Diagram]
    B --> D[Use-Case Description]
    D --> E[CRC Cards]
    E --> F[Analysis Class Diagram]
    A --> F
    F --> G[Sequence Diagram]
    F --> H[Collaboration Diagram]
    F --> I[State-Chart Diagram]
    G --> J[Complete OOA Model Set]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

> **Explanation:** The *Requirements Document* feeds **all** the static and dynamic models. *Use-Case Descriptions* drive *CRC Cards*, which in turn define the *Analysis Class Diagram*. Finally, the *Class Diagram* combined with use cases produces the *dynamic* diagrams (sequence, collaboration, state-chart). All of these together form the **complete OOA Model Set**, which is the deliverable of the analysis phase.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Q1. Define Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA). Mention its primary goal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA) is the software engineering activity that **investigates the problem domain** to understand the system's **functional and non-functional requirements**, and produces a set of **UML models** (use-case, class, sequence diagrams, etc.) that describe **what the system must do** in terms of collaborating objects — *without* committing to any implementation technology. Its primary goal is to **build an accurate, complete, and consistent model of the problem domain** that bridges the gap between user requirements and the design phase.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Definition** | A specific behaviour or function the system must perform | A quality attribute or constraint on the system |
| **Focus** | *What* the system does | *How well* the system does it |
| **Captured as** | Use cases, features | Performance, security, usability metrics |
| **Example** | "The ATM shall dispense cash on a valid withdrawal request." | "The ATM shall respond to any user action within **3 seconds**." |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q3 (A). (i) Explain the five major activities of Object-Oriented Analysis with suitable examples. (7 marks)**
> **(ii) Apply the Grammatical Approach and Coad & Yourdon's Class Selection Criteria to identify classes for a "Library Management System". (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Five Major Activities of OOA (7 marks):**

1. **Requirements Gathering** — Interviewing librarians, students, and administrators. Capturing FRs (book issue, return, reservation) and NFRs (response time, security, concurrency).
2. **Domain Analysis** — Building a shared vocabulary. Domain concepts: *Book, Member, Librarian, Loan, Catalogue, Fine*. These become candidate classes.
3. **Use-Case Modelling** — Identifying actors (`Member`, `Librarian`, `Admin`) and use cases (`Issue Book`, `Return Book`, `Search Catalogue`, `Pay Fine`). Drawing the Use-Case Diagram and writing textual descriptions.
4. **Object & Class Identification** — Applying the Grammatical Approach (nouns from use cases) and CRC Cards to finalise the class list. Filtering with Coad & Yourdon's three tests.
5. **Establishing Relationships & Behaviour** — Drawing the **Analysis Class Diagram** (associations like *Member borrows Book*, inheritance like *StudentMember* and *FacultyMember* both inheriting from *Member*) and **Sequence Diagrams** for dynamic behaviour.

**(ii) Applying Grammatical Approach & Coad & Yourdon's Criteria for Library Management (7 marks):**

From the use-case description "The **member** **searches** the **catalogue** and **borrows** a **book**, after which a **loan** is **created** and a **due date** is **assigned**", the **nouns** give the candidate classes:

| Candidate | Retained Info? | Needed Services? | Multiple Instances? | Verdict |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|---------|
| Member | YES (id, name, address) | YES (borrow, return) | YES | **Keep** |
| Book | YES (ISBN, title, author) | YES (checkAvailability) | YES | **Keep** |
| Catalogue | YES (list of books) | YES (search) | YES | **Keep** |
| Loan | YES (loanId, dates) | YES (create, close) | YES | **Keep** |
| DueDate | NO (transient) | NO | NO | **Drop → attribute of Loan** |
| Librarian | YES (empId, name) | YES (issueBook, collectFine) | YES | **Keep** |
| Fine | YES (amount, reason) | YES (calculate, pay) | YES | **Keep** |

> Only classes that **pass all three** tests are retained. `DueDate` fails the second and third tests and is demoted to an attribute of `Loan`. The final analysis classes are: **Member, Book, Catalogue, Loan, Librarian, Fine**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Q3 (B). (i) Explain the concept of a Use Case. Draw a Use-Case Diagram for an "Online Shopping System" with at least four use cases and three actors. (7 marks)**
> **(ii) Differentiate between Object-Oriented Analysis and Object-Oriented Design. State clearly the outputs of each phase. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Use Case Concept + Use-Case Diagram (7 marks):**

A **Use Case** is a *sequence of actions performed by the system* that yields an **observable result of value** to a particular **actor**. It is described from the *actor's point of view* and captures *one functional requirement* of the system. Each use case has a *name (verb phrase)*, *participating actors*, *preconditions*, *main flow*, *alternate flows*, and *postconditions*.

**Use-Case Diagram for Online Shopping System (textual representation, as UML drawing in KTU scripts is hand-drawn):**

- **Actors (outside the system boundary):** `Customer`, `Admin`, `PaymentGateway`
- **Use cases (ovals inside the system boundary):** `Browse Products`, `Add to Cart`, `Place Order`, `Make Payment`, `Manage Inventory`, `Generate Invoice`
- **Relationships:**
  - `Customer` connects to: `Browse Products`, `Add to Cart`, `Place Order`, `Make Payment`
  - `Admin` connects to: `Manage Inventory`
  - `PaymentGateway` connects to: `Make Payment`
  - `Place Order` `<<include>>` `Make Payment`
  - `Browse Products` `<<extend>>` `Add to Cart`

**(ii) OOA vs OOD (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA) | Object-Oriented Design (OOD) |
|--------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Question Answered** | WHAT the system does | HOW the system does it |
| **Focus** | Problem domain | Solution domain |
| **Activities** | Requirement gathering, use-case modelling, object identification | Architectural design, class design, database design, UI design |
| **Language Dependency** | Independent of any programming language | Often influenced by the target language (Java, in this course) |
| **Output Artefacts** | Use-Case Diagram, Analysis Class Diagram, Sequence Diagram, CRC Cards, Domain Dictionary | Design Class Diagram (with full attributes, methods, visibility), Component Diagram, Deployment Diagram, Architectural patterns |
| **Performed by** | System analyst (with stakeholder input) | Software designer / architect |
| **Granularity of classes** | High-level *conceptual* classes | Detailed *executable* classes with full signatures |
| **Example** | A `Customer` class with attributes *name, address* and operation *placeOrder* | A `Customer` class in Java with `private String name`, `private List<Order> orders`, `public void placeOrder(Cart c)` |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on "Analysis: Introduction" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OOA with OOD** — Do not write Java code or mention class visibility (`private`, `public`) in the analysis phase. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you mix up analysis and design.
> 2. **Skipping the Class Selection Criteria** — When asked to identify classes, **always apply Coad & Yourdon's three tests**. Merely listing nouns earns *partial* credit; applying the filtering logic earns **full** credit.
> 3. **Forgetting multiplicity** — When drawing the Class Diagram, always include the **multiplicity** (1, *, 1..*) at both ends of every association. A diagram without multiplicity is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Writing "User" instead of "Actor"** — In UML, the external entity is the **Actor**, not the user. The *user* is a person who *plays* the role of an actor.
> 5. **Omitting NFRs** — Most students focus only on functional requirements. In 14-mark questions on requirements gathering, **at least two NFRs with metrics** (e.g., "response time < 2 sec", "99.9% uptime") are mandatory.
> 6. **No arrows on Actor-Use-Case lines** — KTU's standard UML convention has **simple lines (no arrowheads)** between an actor and a use case. Arrows are reserved for `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Analysis: Introduction"**

- [x] **OOA** answers **WHAT**, **OOD** answers **HOW** — never mix the two.
- [x] The **primary goal of OOA** is to build a model of the *problem domain*, not the solution.
- [x] **Five core activities** of OOA: Requirements Gathering → Domain Analysis → Use-Case Modelling → Object Identification → Class Relationships & Behaviour.
- [x] **Coad & Yourdon's three tests** for class retention: *Retained Information*, *Needed Services*, *Multiple Instances*.
- [x] **Grammatical Approach** → nouns = candidate classes; verbs = candidate responsibilities.
- [x] **CRC Card** = Class name + Responsibilities + Collaborators.
- [x] **Functional requirements** = *what* the system does; **Non-functional requirements** = *how well* (performance, security, usability, availability).
- [x] **Use-Case Diagram** shows **actors** (outside boundary) and **use cases** (ovals inside) with **simple lines** (no arrows) between them.
- [x] `<<include>>` — use case **always** uses another use case; `<<extend>>` — use case **optionally** extends another.
- [x] **Actor** is a *role*, not a specific person. The same person can play multiple actors.
- [x] **OOA outputs are language-independent** — no Java code, no SQL, no frameworks in this phase.
- [x] **Analysis Class Diagram** has *only essential* attributes and operations, **not** the full signatures with types and visibility.
- [x] **Sequence Diagram** captures the *time-order* of messages; **Collaboration Diagram** captures the *links* between objects.
- [x] Multiplicity (`1`, `*`, `1..*`) is **mandatory** on both ends of every association.
- [x] The artefacts of OOA (Use-Case, Analysis Class Diagram, CRC Cards, Sequence Diagrams) form the **input** to the **Design phase**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** is a programming paradigm built around the concept of **objects** — self-contained entities that bundle together **data (attributes/fields)** and **behaviour (methods/functions)** — and **classes** — the blueprints from which objects are instantiated. The 2024 KTU scheme emphasises OOP as a *modelling discipline* that mirrors real-world entities to achieve **modularity, reusability, and maintainability** in software design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Focus (KTU PEITT411):** This module consolidates the *building blocks* of Java OOP — **classes, objects, methods, constructors, access control, `static` members, `this` keyword, parameter passing, and object lifecycle (garbage collection)**. The "Overview" topic lays the conceptual runway before diving into the syntax-heavy subtopics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint vs The Building"

Imagine you are an architect:

| Real-World Analogy | Java OOP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Architectural Blueprint** (a plan, not a building) | **Class** (a definition, not an object) |
| **Actual House built from the blueprint** | **Object** (an instance of a class) |
| **Number of bedrooms, colour, area** | **Instance Variables (fields/state)** |
| **Action: openDoor(), turnOnLight()** | **Methods (behaviour)** |
| **Society rules: "no loud music after 10 PM"** | **Access Modifiers (private, public, protected)** |
| **Common park shared by all houses** | **`static` members (class-level data)** |

You can build **100 houses** from **one blueprint** — each with its own colour, owner, and furniture (state), yet all sharing the same structural design (class). That is the essence of OOP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class ≠ Object.** A class is a *type definition*; an object is a *runtime memory allocation* of that type. The single most common KTU exam pitfall is treating these as synonymous.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars (Preview for the Module)

OOP rests on four foundational principles — frequently asked as **2-mark definitions** in KTU exams:

1. **Encapsulation** — bundling data + methods and hiding internal state.
2. **Inheritance** — deriving new classes from existing ones (Module 3 territory).
3. **Polymorphism** — one interface, many implementations (Module 4 territory).
4. **Abstraction** — exposing essential features, hiding complexity (covered via `abstract` classes / interfaces).

For Module 2's scope, **encapsulation** and the **class-object mechanism** are the operative pillars.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Procedural vs Object-Oriented Paradigm

| Aspect | Procedural (C-style) | Object-Oriented (Java) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary unit | Function | **Object (Class instance)** |
| Data handling | Passed between functions freely | Encapsulated within objects |
| Reusability | Function libraries | **Inheritance & composition** |
| Security | Data is exposed | **Access modifiers restrict access** |
| Real-world mapping | Weak | **Strong** |
| Example languages | C, Pascal, early BASIC | **Java, C++, Python (OOP mode)** |

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Java Class (Module 2 Core Concept)

Every Java class declared at the top level follows this logical structure:

1. **Package declaration** (`package com.ktu.oop;`) — optional but standard.
2. **Import statements** — bring in external classes.
3. **Class declaration** — `public class ClassName { ... }`
4. **Fields (instance variables)** — hold object state.
5. **Constructors** — special methods invoked at object creation.
6. **Methods** — define behaviour.
7. **Nested classes/interfaces** — optional inner definitions.

### 2.3 Object Lifecycle (High-Yield for KTU)

$$ \text{Class Definition} \xrightarrow{\text{new}} \text{Object Instantiation} \rightarrow \text{Use} \rightarrow \text{Unreferenced} \xrightarrow{\text{GC}} \text{Destroyed} $$

The **garbage collector (GC)** automatically reclaims memory of objects no longer referenced — Java's most celebrated feature over C++.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Skeleton | Key Rule | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class declaration** | `class Student { }` | File name must match `public` class name | Mismatched filename → compile error |
| **Object creation** | `Student s = new Student();` | `new` allocates heap memory | Forgetting parentheses of constructor |
| **Instance variable** | `int rollNo;` | Each object gets its own copy | Confusing with `static` variables |
| **`static` variable** | `static int count;` | **One copy per class**, shared by all objects | Trying to access via object reference (legal but bad style) |
| **Method** | `void display() { }` | Returns `void` or a type | Missing return statement in non-void method |
| **Constructor** | `Student() { }` | Name = class name, no return type | Adding a return type → becomes a method, not constructor |
| **`this` keyword** | `this.name = name;` | Refers to **current object** | Used outside non-static context → compile error |
| **Access: `private`** | `private int x;` | Accessible **only within the same class** | Trying to access from another class directly |
| **Access: `public`** | `public int x;` | Accessible **everywhere** | Over-exposing internal data |
| **Access: `protected`** | `protected int x;` | Same package + subclasses | Confusing with default (package-private) |
| **Default (no modifier)** | `int x;` | Same package only | Forgetting it is *not* the same as `public` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`new` keyword is mandatory in Java** to create objects. Unlike JavaScript or Python, you cannot invoke a constructor without `new` (unless using reflection).

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering

In real-world software (banking systems, e-commerce, embedded controllers), OOP enables:
- **Maintainability** — bugs localised to a class.
- **Scalability** — new features added via inheritance/composition.
- **Team development** — different developers work on different classes simultaneously.
- **Domain modelling** — `Customer`, `Order`, `Invoice` map directly to database tables and business entities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis: From Blueprint to Building

Since this is a *conceptual overview* topic, the derivation below is a **logical walkthrough** of how a class definition transforms into runtime objects — the most examination-relevant analytical process.

### 3.1 Source Code Definition (The Blueprint)

```java
public class Student {
    int rollNo;          // instance variable
    String name;         // instance variable

    Student(int r, String n) {   // constructor
        rollNo = r;
        name = n;
    }

    void display() {             // method
        System.out.println(rollNo + " " + name);
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Object Instantiation Trace

**Step 1 — Class Loading**

When the JVM encounters `Student` for the first time, the **ClassLoader subsystem** loads the `.class` bytecode into the **Method Area** of JVM memory. No `Student` object exists yet — only the *type metadata*.

**Step 2 — Encountering `new Student(...)`**

In `main()`:

```java
Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arun");
```

The JVM executes the following sequence:

1. **Class loading check** — Is `Student.class` already in the Method Area? If not, load it.
2. **Heap allocation** — The JVM computes the object's size: `int (4 bytes) + String reference (4/8 bytes)` ≈ 8–12 bytes, then allocates contiguous memory in the **heap**.
3. **Default initialisation** — All instance variables receive Java defaults (`int` → `0`, `String` reference → `null`).
4. **Constructor invocation** — The explicit constructor `Student(101, "Arun")` is called, overwriting the defaults.
5. **Reference assignment** — The address of the newly created object is stored in the local variable `s1` (which lives on the **stack**).

$$ \text{Stack (main)} \ni s1 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Heap Address 0x4A2F} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Student\{rollNo=101, name="Arun"\}} $$

**Step 3 — Creating a Second Object**

```java
Student s2 = new Student(102, "Meera");
```

A **separate** heap allocation occurs. `s2` is a different reference variable pointing to a different heap address. The two objects are **independent** — modifying `s1.rollNo` does **not** affect `s2.rollNo`.

$$ s1 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Student\{101, "Arun"\}} \quad\quad s2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Student\{102, "Meera"\}} $$

**Step 4 — Method Call Binding**

When `s1.display()` is invoked:

1. JVM looks up the method `display` in `Student`'s method table.
2. The object referenced by `s1` becomes the **current object** — accessible inside `display()` via the implicit `this` reference.
3. The bytecode for `display` is pushed onto the stack, executes, prints the output, and the stack frame is popped.

**Step 5 — Loss of Reference & Garbage Collection**

```java
s1 = s2;   // s1 now points to Meera's object; Arun's object is unreferenced
```

The original Arun-object has **zero live references** and becomes **eligible for Garbage Collection**. The JVM's GC may reclaim this memory at its discretion — Java programmers **cannot force** GC, only **request** it via `System.gc()`.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Class vs Object

| Dimension | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Template / Blueprint | Instance / Concrete entity |
| **When does it exist?** | Compile time + runtime (metadata) | Runtime only (heap allocation) |
| **Memory location** | Method Area (class metadata) | Heap |
| **Keyword to create** | `class` | `new` |
| **Number** | One per source file (typically) | Unlimited (subject to memory) |
| **Contains** | Field declarations, method signatures | Actual field **values** |
