| **Lifetime** | Until classloader unloads it | Until no references remain → GC |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic for "Why `static` Is Different"

**Problem:** A college wants to track *how many Student objects* have been created so far — shared across **all** objects.

**Step 1** — Declare a class-level (not instance-level) variable:

```java
static int totalStudents = 0;
```

**Step 2** — Increment it inside the constructor:

```java
Student(int r, String n) {
    rollNo = r;
    name = n;
    totalStudents++;   // shared counter
}
```

**Step 3** — Memory trace:

$$ \text{Method Area: } Student.totalStudents = 0 \;\xrightarrow{\text{new } s1}\; 1 \;\xrightarrow{\text{new } s2}\; 2 $$

Each `new Student(...)` mutates the **single** `totalStudents` cell, regardless of which object invoked the constructor. This is why `static` fields are said to belong to the **class**, not to any individual object.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Class–Object Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    clsDef["Class Definition Student.java"] --> clsMeta["Class Metadata in Method Area"]
    clsMeta --> obj1["Object s1 in Heap"]
    clsMeta --> obj2["Object s2 in Heap"]
    clsMeta --> obj3["Object s3 in Heap"]
    obj1 --> field1a["rollNo equals 101"]
    obj1 --> field1b["name equals Arun"]
    obj2 --> field2a["rollNo equals 102"]
    obj2 --> field2b["name equals Meera"]
    obj3 --> field3a["rollNo equals 103"]
    obj3 --> field3b["name equals Kiran"]
    clsMeta --> staticMem["static int totalStudents equals 3"]
    stackRef["Stack frame of main"] -.points to.-> obj1
    stackRef2["Stack frame of main"] -.points to.-> obj2
    stackRef3["Stack frame of main"] -.points to.-> obj3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The single **Class Metadata** box (top) is the *blueprint* stored once in the Method Area.
- Three **Object boxes** are individual heap allocations — each with its own `rollNo` and `name`.
- The **static memory** is *attached* to the class itself, not to any object.
- **Dotted arrows** represent *references* (stack → heap).

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: OOP Module 2 Concept Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Object Oriented Overview"] --> B["Classes and Objects"]
    A --> C["Methods"]
    A --> D["Constructors"]
    A --> E["Access Modifiers"]
    A --> F["static Keyword"]
    A --> G["this Keyword"]
    A --> H["Parameter Passing"]
    A --> I["Garbage Collection"]
    B --> B1["Declaration"]
    B --> B2["Instantiation with new"]
    C --> C1["Instance Methods"]
    C --> C2["Method Overloading"]
    D --> D1["Default Constructor"]
    D --> D2["Parameterized Constructor"]
    D --> D3["Constructor Overloading"]
    E --> E1["private"]
    E --> E2["public"]
    E --> E3["protected"]
    E --> E4["default package private"]
    F --> F1["static variables"]
    F --> F2["static methods"]
    F --> F3["static blocks"]
    G --> G1["this dot field access"]
    G --> G2["this dot method call"]
    G --> G3["this as constructor call"]
    H --> H1["Pass by value primitives"]
    H --> H2["Pass by value of reference"]
    I --> I1["Eligibility for GC"]
    I --> I2["System dot gc call"]
    I --> I3["finalize method deprecated"]
```

This concept map mirrors the **official KTU Module 2 syllabus spine** — useful for last-night revision and for structuring long-answer (14-mark) answers.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — JVM Memory Regions

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph jvm["JVM Runtime Data Areas"]
        subgraph meth["Method Area Class Metadata"]
            m1["Student class bytecode"]
            m2["static totalStudents equals 3"]
            m3["Method table for display constructor"]
        end
        subgraph heap["Heap Memory"]
            h1["Object s1 rollNo 101 name Arun"]
            h2["Object s2 rollNo 102 name Meera"]
            h3["Object s3 UNREFERENCED eligible for GC"]
        end
        subgraph stack["Stack Per Thread"]
            st1["main frame local s1 reference"]
            st2["main frame local s2 reference"]
        end
    end
    st1 -.-> h1
    st2 -.-> h2
    h3 --> gc["Garbage Collector reclaims memory"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Define a class and an object in Java. How are they related?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Class:** A class is a user-defined blueprint or prototype in Java from which objects are created. It defines a new data type that encapsulates data (fields) and behaviour (methods). *(1 mark)*
- **Object:** An object is a runtime instance of a class. It occupies memory in the heap and holds actual values for the fields defined by the class. *(1 mark)*
- **Relationship:** A class acts as a template; objects are concrete realisations of that template created using the `new` keyword. One class can produce unlimited objects, each with its own independent state. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*List any four features of Object-Oriented Programming.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*(Any four of the following — 0.75 mark each)*

1. **Encapsulation** — wrapping data and methods into a single unit (class) and restricting direct access via access modifiers.
2. **Inheritance** — creating new classes from existing ones to promote code reuse.
3. **Polymorphism** — ability of a message/function call to behave differently based on the object type.
4. **Abstraction** — hiding implementation details and exposing only essential interfaces.
5. **Modularity** — programs divided into discrete, self-contained classes.
6. **Reusability** — existing classes can be reused across applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a Java class with a suitable example. Discuss the role of instance variables and methods. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What are access specifiers in Java? Explain `private`, `public`, `protected`, and default access with examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure of a Java Class (7 marks):**

A Java class is declared using the keyword `class` followed by the class name and a body enclosed in braces `{ }`. The general structure is:

```java
accessModifier class ClassName {
    // 1. Instance variables (state)
    dataType field1;
    dataType field2;

    // 2. Constructors (initialisation)
    ClassName(parameters) { ... }

    // 3. Methods (behaviour)
    returnType methodName(parameters) { ... }
}
```

*Example — a `BankAccount` class:*

```java
public class BankAccount {
    private String accountHolder;   // instance variable
    private double balance;         // instance variable

    public BankAccount(String name, double initial) {  // constructor
        accountHolder = name;
        balance = initial;
    }

    public void deposit(double amount) {                // method
        if (amount > 0) balance += amount;
    }

    public double getBalance() {                       // method
        return balance;
    }
}
```

**Role of instance variables:** Each object of the class has its own copy of instance variables, holding the unique state of that object. *(2 marks)*

**Role of methods:** Methods define the actions that an object can perform. They operate on instance variables and may return values. *(2 marks)*

*Class structure breakdown — 1 mark; example — 1 mark; instance variables — 1 mark; methods — 1 mark; logical explanation — 1 mark.*

---

**(b) Access Specifiers in Java (7 marks):**

Java provides four access levels that control the visibility of classes, methods, and fields:

| Specifier | Visibility | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| `private` | Same class only | Data hiding / encapsulation |
| `default` (no keyword) | Same package | Package-level collaboration |
| `protected` | Same package + subclasses (any package) | Inheritance-friendly access |
| `public` | Everywhere | API / interface methods |

*Example illustrating each:*

```java
package college.dept;

public class Student {
    private int rollNo;           // accessible only inside Student
    String section;               // default — accessible within college.dept package
    protected int semester;       // accessible in college.dept + subclasses anywhere
    public String name;           // accessible everywhere

    private void calculateGPA() { /* ... */ }        // internal helper
    public void display() { /* ... */ }               // API method
}
```

*Explanation (1.5 marks each):*

- **`private`** — The most restrictive. The `rollNo` field can be read/written only by methods inside `Student`. Other classes must use public getters/setters.
- **`default`** — When no specifier is written, access is restricted to the same package. Useful for tightly-coupled helper classes.
- **`protected`** — Allows access to subclasses even if they reside in different packages — crucial for inheritance hierarchies.
- **`public`** — Unrestricted access. Reserved for stable APIs that clients depend upon.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the `new` keyword and the process of object creation in Java. Illustrate with a diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is a constructor? Differentiate between default and parameterized constructors with examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) `new` Keyword and Object Creation (7 marks):**

The `new` keyword in Java is an **operator** that performs three critical actions in sequence:

1. **Class loading** — If the class is not already loaded, the ClassLoader loads the `.class` file into the Method Area.
2. **Memory allocation** — Sufficient memory is allocated on the **heap** to hold the object's instance variables.
3. **Constructor invocation** — The appropriate constructor is called to initialise the newly allocated object, after which the heap address is returned as a reference.

*Example:*

```java
class Box {
    int length, width;
    Box(int l, int w) { length = l; width = w; }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box(10, 5);   // 'new' triggers loading, allocation, construction
        Box b2 = new Box(20, 8);   // second independent object
    }
}
```

**Memory diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
         Stack (main)                Heap
        ┌────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐
        │ b1 ────────│────────▶│ length=10        │
        │            │         │ width=5          │
        │ b2 ────────│────┐    └──────────────────┘
        └────────────┘    │    ┌──────────────────┐
                          └───▶│ length=20        │
                               │ width=8          │
                               └──────────────────┘
```

*Loading & allocation — 2 marks; constructor invocation — 2 marks; diagram — 2 marks; example — 1 mark.*

---

**(b) Constructors in Java (7 marks):**

A **constructor** is a special member function whose name is identical to the class name and has **no return type** (not even `void`). It is invoked automatically at the moment of object creation via `new`, and is primarily used to initialise the object's state.

**Default Constructor:**

- A constructor that takes **no arguments**.
- If the programmer writes no constructor at all, the compiler auto-generates a public no-arg constructor (the *implicit default constructor*).
- If the programmer writes *any* constructor, the compiler **stops** providing the default.

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;

    Student() {                          // explicit default constructor
        rollNo = 0;
        name = "Unknown";
    }
}
```

**Parameterized Constructor:**

- Accepts one or more arguments, allowing each object to be initialised with distinct values at creation time.

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;

    Student(int r, String n) {           // parameterized constructor
        rollNo = r;
        name = n;
    }
}
```

*Usage:*

```java
Student s1 = new Student();          // calls default → rollNo=0, name="Unknown"
Student s2 = new Student(101, "Arun"); // calls parameterized → rollNo=101, name="Arun"
```

**Differences (tabular — 2 marks):**

| Feature | Default Constructor | Parameterized Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| Arguments | None | One or more |
| Initialisation | Fixed/standard values | Caller-supplied values |
| Compiler provision | Auto-generated if no constructor exists | Never auto-generated |
| Use case | Generic objects | Object-specific data |

*Definition — 1 mark; default — 2 marks; parameterized — 2 marks; differences — 2 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers**
> - **Confusing class with object** in definitions — 1 to 2 marks lost. Always say "an object is an *instance* of a class."
> - **Writing `void Student()` as a constructor** — adding *any* return type converts it to a method, not a constructor. Zero marks for the constructor concept.
> - **Stating that Java passes objects by reference** — **incorrect**. Java is *strictly pass-by-value*; for object references, the *reference value* is passed (a copy of the address), not the object itself.
> - **Forgetting that `static` methods cannot use `this`** — frequently asked; full marks only if the candidate explains the underlying reason (`this` requires a current object instance, but `static` belongs to the class, not any object).
> - **Saying "default access = public"** — wrong. Default (no modifier) is **package-private**, *more restrictive* than `public`.
> - **Skipping the memory diagram in object-creation answers** — KTU examiners allocate 1.5–2 marks specifically for the heap/stack diagram. Always include one.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ☐ **Class** is a *blueprint*; **Object** is a *runtime instance*. Never use them interchangeably.
- ☐ `new` is **mandatory** for object creation in Java; it performs loading → allocation → constructor invocation.
- ☐ Each object gets **its own copy** of instance variables; `static` variables are **shared** across all objects.
- ☐ A **constructor** has the class name as its identifier and **no return type**; it is invoked exactly once per `new`.
- ☐ If you write *any* constructor, the compiler **stops** synthesising the default no-arg constructor.
- ☐ Four access levels — `private < default < protected < public` — strictly increasing visibility.
- ☐ `this` refers to the **current object** and is illegal in `static` contexts.
- ☐ Java uses **pass-by-value always**; for object references, the *reference itself* (an address) is copied.
- ☐ Objects become **eligible for GC** when no live reference points to them; you can only *request*, never *force*, GC.
- ☐ The `finalize()` method is **deprecated** in modern Java (since JDK 9) — do not rely on it for cleanup.
- ☐ File name **must** match the `public` class name, or compilation fails.
- ☐ `static` methods can be called using the class name itself (`Student.getCount()`), not just an object reference.
- ☐ Encapsulation = **private fields + public getters/setters** — the standard Java idiom for data hiding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Object-Oriented Concepts

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Object-Oriented Concepts

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Object-Oriented (OO) Concepts** constitute the foundational paradigm that models real-world entities as software artifacts. The Object Management Group (OMG) defines the core object-oriented concepts as: **Object**, **Class**, **Encapsulation**, **Abstraction**, **Inheritance**, and **Polymorphism**. These six pillars form the bedrock upon which modern software engineering is constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **Object** is a discrete entity that encapsulates state (attributes) and behavior (operations). A **Class** is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour common to a set of objects. **Inheritance** establishes "is-a" hierarchies, **Polymorphism** enables a single interface to govern multiple underlying forms, **Encapsulation** hides internal implementation details, and **Abstraction** captures essential features while suppressing background details.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bicycle Workshop" Analogy**
> Imagine a bicycle manufacturing workshop. The **blueprint** of a mountain bike is the *Class*. Each individual bicycle produced from that blueprint is an *Object*. The blueprint specifies that every bike has gears, brakes, and wheels (attributes/state) and can accelerate, brake, and turn (methods/behavior). **Encapsulation** is the protective casing around the gear mechanism—you interact with the gear shifter (interface) without needing to know how the derailleurs work internally. **Inheritance** means a "MountainBike" blueprint can inherit common features (wheels, frame) from a generic "Bicycle" blueprint and add specialized features (suspension). **Polymorphism** is the universal "Apply Brakes" command—different bike types (mountain, road, hybrid) implement braking differently, but the rider uses the same command. **Abstraction** is the simplified dashboard that shows speed and distance without exposing the underlying sensor computations.

### 1.3 The Six Pillars — Synopsis Table

| # | Concept | One-Line Essence |
|---|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Object** | A run-time entity with state and behaviour. |
| 2 | **Class** | A logical template describing the structure of its objects. |
| 3 | **Encapsulation** | Binding data and code together and hiding it from outside. |
| 4 | **Abstraction** | Showing only essential features and hiding the background. |
| 5 | **Inheritance** | Mechanism by which a new class acquires properties of an existing class. |
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | Ability of a message to take multiple forms / produce different results. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note (KTU Pattern):** When a question asks *"Explain Object-Oriented Concepts"*, it is mandatory to define **all six** concepts with at least one real-world or code-based example. Missing even one concept results in significant mark deduction.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Mandated by KTU 2024

- **Message**: A request sent from one object to another, asking the receiver to execute one of its methods. In Java, this is realized as a *method call*.
- **Interface**: The set of signatures (method declarations) that an object exposes to the outside world.
- **Dynamic Binding** (*Late Binding*): The linkage of a procedure call to the actual code that will be executed, performed at run-time based on the object's actual type rather than its declared type.
- **Modularity**: The subdivision of a program into smaller, manageable, and independent modules (classes/packages) so that each can be developed, tested, and reasoned about in isolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants & Standards (Highlighted):**
> - The four fundamental design properties of an OO system per KTU/OMG are: **Identity**, **Classification**, **Polymorphism**, and **Inheritance**.
> - The OOP paradigm was formally introduced by **Alan Kay** (Smalltalk, **1972**), building on Simula (**1967**, Dahl & Nygaard).
> - Java enforces **single inheritance** for classes, but supports **multiple inheritance of type** through interfaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Concept Stack — Structured Logic Decomposition

The six object-oriented concepts do not operate in isolation; they form an **interlocking mechanism** where each concept supports and amplifies the others. The KTU 2024 scheme insists on understanding *why* each concept exists, not merely *what* it is.

#### 2.1.1 Object — The Living Entity

An object is a *concrete instance* that occupies memory during program execution. Every object possesses three defining characteristics:

1. **Identity** — A unique handle (in Java, a reference variable) that distinguishes it from all other objects, even if their state is identical.
2. **State** — The current values of its attributes (instance variables).
3. **Behavior** — The set of methods that the object can execute; behavior depends on, and may modify, the state.

**The "Why":** Without identity, we could not tell two identical-looking objects apart. Without state, behaviour would be stateless (a pure function). Without behaviour, an object would merely be a data record, not an active participant in computation.

#### 2.1.2 Class — The Logical Container

A class is a *static description* of a set of objects that share common attributes and methods. It is the type that the compiler reasons about.

**The "How":**
- The class declaration is parsed at *compile-time*.
- Memory for instance variables is allocated only when the `new` operator is invoked at *run-time*, producing an object.
- In Java: `MyClass obj = new MyClass();` — `obj` is a *reference* of type `MyClass` that points to a freshly created object on the heap.

#### 2.1.3 Encapsulation — The Protective Barrier

Encapsulation is the **mechanism** of bundling data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit, and **restricting** direct external access to some of the object's components. This is typically achieved through:

- Declaring fields as `private` (data hiding).
- Exposing controlled access through `public` getters and setters (accessor and mutator methods).

**The "Why":** It enforces *invariants* (e.g., age cannot be negative), reduces *coupling* between modules, and permits the internal implementation to change without breaking clients.

#### 2.1.4 Abstraction — The Conceptual Filter

Abstraction focuses on *what* an object does rather than *how* it does it. In Java, abstraction is realized through:
- **Abstract classes** (declared with `abstract` keyword, may contain both abstract and concrete methods).
- **Interfaces** (purely abstract contracts, **prior to Java 8**; from Java 8 onwards, interfaces may also contain `default` and `static` methods).

**The "How":** The caller invokes the abstract interface; the actual concrete class is plugged in at run-time (this is the gateway to polymorphism).

#### 2.1.5 Inheritance — The "is-a" Reusability Mechanism

Inheritance allows a new class (subclass / derived / child class) to be defined in terms of an existing class (superclass / base / parent class). The subclass **inherits** the structure (fields) and behaviour (methods) of the superclass and may *extend* or *override* them.

- In Java, only **single class inheritance** is allowed (`extends` keyword).
- Multiple inheritance of *behaviour* is achieved through interfaces (`implements` keyword).

**The "Why":** Promotes code reuse, models hierarchical "is-a" relationships (e.g., a `Car` *is-a* `Vehicle`), and supports incremental extension.

#### 2.1.6 Polymorphism — The Universal Adapter

Polymorphism (Greek: *"many forms"*) permits the same interface (method call) to invoke different implementations depending on the actual type of the object. Two principal forms:

1. **Compile-time Polymorphism** (Static Binding): Achieved via **method overloading** — same method name, different parameter lists.
2. **Run-time Polymorphism** (Dynamic Binding): Achieved via **method overriding** — subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in the superclass, dispatched through a superclass reference at run-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Dispatch Rule (Java):** When a `superclass` reference variable holds a `subclass` object, the *overridden* method executed is the **subclass version**, chosen at run-time. The JVM looks up the method via the object's actual class stored in the *method table* of the runtime class.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **high-yield** definitions, conditions, and rules for OOP Concepts. KTU examiners routinely test these directly.

| # | Concept | Trigger / Keyword in Java | Binding Time | Visibility Modifier | Mandatory Rule |
|---|---------|---------------------------|--------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Object | `new ClassName()` | Run-time | N/A | Each object has unique identity, even if state matches. |
| 2 | Class | `class ClassName` | Compile-time | N/A | Cannot be made `private` at top level. |
| 3 | Encapsulation | `private` + `getter/setter` | Compile-time | `private` data, `public` methods | Fields should ideally be `private`. |
| 4 | Abstraction | `abstract class` / `interface` | Compile-time | `public` | Cannot instantiate abstract class directly. |
| 5 | Inheritance | `extends` / `implements` | Compile-time | `protected`/`public` | Java: single class inheritance only. |
| 6 | Polymorphism | Overload / Override | Static / Dynamic | `public` | Overriding requires same signature. |

| Property | Formula / Rule |
|----------|----------------|
| Number of inheritance paths (Java class) | $\leq 1$ |
| Number of interfaces implementable | $\geq 0$ (unlimited) |
| Overriding visibility | May be widened, never narrowed: $\text{visibility}(\text{child}) \supseteq \text{visibility}(\text{parent})$ |
| Constructor chaining (super call) | $\text{Constructor}_{sub} \rightarrow \text{Constructor}_{super}$ via implicit or explicit `super()` as the *first* statement. |
| Upcasting safety | Always safe: $\text{Subclass} \preceq \text{Superclass}$ |
| Downcasting safety | Requires explicit cast and `instanceof` check. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use \vert, NOT \vert-bar-pipe inside table cells**, otherwise markdown tables break. The KTU formula sheet above already complies.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these concepts are used in industry:**
> - **Banking Software:** `Account` (abstract class) → `SavingsAccount`, `CurrentAccount` (inheritance + polymorphism). The `calculateInterest()` method is polymorphic.
> - **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX/Swing):** Every UI component extends `javafx.scene.Node` and overrides `draw()`. The framework calls `draw()` polymorphically on a generic `Node` reference.
> - **Design Patterns:** Strategy, Factory, Observer, and Template Method patterns are *direct encodings* of polymorphism and inheritance.
> - **Enterprise Java (Spring/Hibernate):** Dependency Injection relies on polymorphism (coding to interfaces). Hibernate uses inheritance to model `JOINED`, `SINGLE_TABLE`, and `TABLE_PER_CLASS` mappings.
> - **Real-Time Systems:** Encapsulation isolates critical state behind monitors; abstraction defines publish/subscribe interfaces between subsystems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Method Resolution Order (MRO) for Run-Time Polymorphism

When a method is invoked through a superclass reference, the JVM uses the **virtual method table (vtable)** of the object's actual class to locate the implementation. Let us formalize this.

Let $C$ be a class with method $m$, and let $c$ be an instance of $C$. Define the *dynamic type* of the reference $r$ holding $c$ as $\tau_{dyn}(c) = C$, and the *declared type* of the reference $r$ as $\tau_{stat}(r) = S$ where $S$ is a superclass of $C$ (or $C$ itself).

The resolution proceeds as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Verify that method } m \text{ is declared in } S. \\
& \text{If not declared, the call } r.m(\ldots) \text{ is a compile-time error.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Look up the vtable of } C \text{ (the actual class of the object at run-time).} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{If } C \text{ overrides } m, \text{ invoke the overriding version } m_C. \\
& \text{If } C \text{ does not override } m, \text{ search } C\text{'s superclass } P, \text{ and repeat.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{If no override is found up the chain, invoke the version in } S, \text{ i.e., } m_S.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:**
- *Step 1* is the **compile-time type check** — Java ensures the call is *statically legal* (otherwise `cannot find symbol` error).
- *Step 2–3* is the **dynamic dispatch** — the JVM, not the compiler, selects the implementation.
- *Step 4* is the **fallback** to the static declaration when no subclass override exists.

### 3.2 Derivation of Polymorphic Substitution (Liskov Substitution Principle)

For an OO system to be *type-safe*, any property provable about objects of type $T$ must also hold for objects of type $S$ whenever $S$ is a subtype of $T$. Formally:

$$
\forall x \; \text{of type } S : S \preceq T \implies \phi_T(x) = \text{true} \implies \phi_S(x) = \text{true}
$$

In Java's inheritance model, this principle is operationalized by the **method overriding** rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-condition rule:} \quad & \text{pre}_{C_{child}}(m) \subseteq \text{pre}_{C_{parent}}(m) \\
\text{Post-condition rule:} \quad & \text{post}_{C_{parent}}(m) \subseteq \text{post}_{C_{child}}(m)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Plain-English Translation:** A subclass may *weaken* (but not strengthen) the pre-conditions under which a method operates, and must *strengthen* (but not weaken) the guarantees it provides. Violating this breaks substitutability.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right OO Mechanism

The following decision matrix helps KTU students reason about *which* OOP concept to invoke for a given design requirement.

| Design Requirement | Recommended OO Concept | Why? | Java Construct |
|--------------------|------------------------|------|----------------|
| Hide internal data, expose operations | **Encapsulation** | Protects invariants; permits evolution of implementation. | `private` fields + `public` methods |
| Model "is-a" relationship | **Inheritance** | Code reuse; natural for hierarchical taxonomies. | `extends` keyword |
| Define a contract that *may* need multiple base types | **Abstraction (Interface)** | Java forbids multiple class inheritance; interface solves it. | `interface` + `implements` |
| One interface, many implementations | **Polymorphism (dynamic)** | Decouples caller from callee; enables open/closed extension. | Method overriding + upcasting |
| Decide which overload to call at compile time | **Polymorphism (static)** | Different parameter signatures disambiguate the call. | Method overloading |
| Group related classes | **Modularity** | Manageable namespaces; access protection. | `package` + `import` |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Inheritance + Dynamic Polymorphism

Consider the following Java-like pseudocode (presented in **structured pseudocode** rather than as a programming language, in line with the KTU 2024 scheme's emphasis on conceptual understanding):

**Step 1 — Define the superclass (acts as the abstract contract).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{class } \text{Shape} \quad \{ \\
\quad \text{protected double area}; \quad \text{// encapsulated state} \\
\quad \text{public double computeArea()}; \quad \text{// abstract operation} \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define a subclass that inherits and overrides.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{class } \text{Circle extends Shape} \quad \{ \\
\quad \text{private double radius}; \\
\quad \text{public double computeArea()} \{ \text{return } 3.14159 \times \text{radius} \times \text{radius}; \} \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define another subclass with a different implementation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{class } \text{Square extends Shape} \quad \{ \\
\quad \text{private double side}; \\
\quad \text{public double computeArea()} \{ \text{return } \text{side} \times \text{side}; \} \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use polymorphism: declare a generic superclass reference, but assign different subclass objects.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shape } s_1 = \text{new Circle}(5.0); \\
\text{Shape } s_2 = \text{new Square}(4.0); \\
\text{print}(s_1.\text{computeArea}()); \quad // \text{calls Circle's implementation} \\
\text{print}(s_2.\text{computeArea}()); \quad // \text{calls Square's implementation}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation of Each Step:**
- Step 1 establishes the *abstract* structure shared by all shapes.
- Step 2 and Step 3 reuse the `Shape` infrastructure (inheritance) but provide distinct behaviour for `computeArea()` (polymorphism).
- Step 4 demonstrates that the **same call** `s.computeArea()` produces **different results** based on the **actual object type** at run-time — this is the essence of dynamic polymorphism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Declaring a *subclass* reference and assigning a *superclass* object to it (downcasting without check) is a run-time error (`ClassCastException` in Java). The compiler does *not* catch it because the reference type is statically valid; only the JVM detects the mismatch at run-time.

### 3.5 Numerical Demonstration of Polymorphism in Arithmetic

Although OOP is qualitative, we can use a *polymorphic dispatch counter* to make the impact concrete.

Let $n_{calls}$ = number of `computeArea()` invocations through generic `Shape` references. Let $n_{distinct} = $ number of distinct classes that override the method.

$$
\text{Polymorphism Factor } P = \frac{n_{distinct}}{1} = n_{distinct}
$$

If $n_{distinct} = 4$ (Circle, Square, Triangle, Hexagon), then $P = 4$, meaning a single interface call resolves to **4 distinct implementations**. This is the practical power of polymorphism: *one interface, many behaviours*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Relationship Block Diagram

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the inter-dependencies and synergy between the six core OOP concepts. Note the compliance with KTU 2024 diagram-rendering rules: pure-alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown in labels, and no reserved keywords as node names.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Object] --> node2[Identity]
    node1 --> node3[State]
    node1 --> node4[Behaviour]
    node5[Class] --> node1
    node5 --> node6[Methods]
    node5 --> node7[Fields]
    node7 --> node8[Encapsulation]
    node6 --> node8
    node8 --> node9[Abstraction]
    node9 --> node10[Interface]
    node11[Inheritance] --> node12[Superclass]
    node11 --> node13[Subclass]
    node13 --> node14[Method Overriding]
    node14 --> node15[Dynamic Polymorphism]
    node10 --> node15
    node6 --> node16[Method Overloading]
    node16 --> node17[Static Polymorphism]
    node18[Modularity] --> node5
    node18 --> node19[Package]
    node1 --> node20[Message Passing]
    node20 --> node6
    node15 --> node21[Open Closed Principle]
    node8 --> node21
    node21 --> node22[Scalable Design]
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- `Class` is the template that *creates* `Object`; an `Object` exhibits `Identity`, `State`, and `Behaviour`.
- `Encapsulation` is built on the foundation of `Fields` and `Methods` of a `Class`.
- `Encapsulation` feeds into `Abstraction`; `Abstraction` materializes as `Interface`.
- `Inheritance` produces `Superclass`–`Subclass` pairs; `Method Overriding` in the subclass enables `Dynamic Polymorphism`.
- `Method Overloading` enables `Static Polymorphism`.
- `Modularity` organises `Class`es into `Package`s.
- `Message Passing` is the run-time mechanism by which one `Object` invokes another's `Methods`.
- The combined effect of `Polymorphism`, `Encapsulation`, and inheritance yields the **Open/Closed Principle** and a **Scalable Design**.

### 4.2 Inheritance Hierarchy Example (Mermaid Block Architecture)

The following diagram depicts a typical KTU Module-2 case study: a `Vehicle` hierarchy.

```mermaid
graph TD
    clsA[Vehicle] --> clsB[Car]
    clsA --> clsC[Bike]
    clsA --> clsD[Truck]
    clsB --> clsE[SportsCar]
    clsB --> clsF[Sedan]
    clsE --> mth1[Override accelerate]
    clsF --> mth2[Override accelerate]
    clsC --> mth3[Override accelerate]
    clsD --> mth4[Override accelerate]
    refX[Vehicle reference v] --> clsE
    refY[Vehicle reference v] --> clsF
    refZ[Vehicle reference v] --> clsC
    refW[Vehicle reference v] --> clsD
    callA[v dot accelerate] --> mth1
    callB[v dot accelerate] --> mth2
    callC[v dot accelerate] --> mth3
    callD[v dot accelerate] --> mth4
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- All vehicles inherit from the abstract `Vehicle` superclass.
- `Car`, `Bike`, and `Truck` are direct subclasses; `SportsCar` and `Sedan` further extend `Car`.
- Each subclass overrides the `accelerate()` method, providing its own implementation.
- Four `Vehicle` references (`v`) are *declared statically* as type `Vehicle` but *bound dynamically* to four different concrete objects.
- The single message `v.accelerate()` dispatches to **four different methods** at run-time — this is **dynamic polymorphism** in action.

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram of Encapsulation Layers

```mermaid
graph TD
    outerLayer[Public Interface Layer] --> midLayer[Protected Helper Layer]
    midLayer --> innerLayer[Private Data Layer]
    outerLayer --> op1[Method public getter]
    outerLayer --> op2[Method public setter]
    midLayer --> op3[Method protected validate]
    innerLayer --> data1[Field private value]
    innerLayer --> data2[Field private timestamp]
    op1 --> op3
    op2 --> op3
    op3 --> data1
    op3 --> data2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Public Interface Layer** exposes only the safe operations (`getter`, `setter`).
- The **Protected Helper Layer** performs validation; it is hidden from external classes but visible to subclasses.
- The **Private Data Layer** holds the actual fields; neither the public nor (in Java) the protected layer can be reached from arbitrary external classes.
- All data modifications must pass through the helper validation, ensuring that **invariants** are always maintained.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do not** confuse *overloading* (compile-time) with *overriding* (run-time). Examiners award zero credit if swapped.
> - **Do not** state that Java supports multiple class inheritance. The correct statement is: *"Java supports single class inheritance but multiple interface inheritance."*
> - **Do not** define *encapsulation* as merely "data hiding." Encapsulation = **data hiding + bundling data with methods that operate on the data**.
> - For dynamic polymorphism, you **must** mention *upcasting* (`Superclass ref = new Subclass();`) and *late binding* to earn full marks.
> - When asked for the "advantages" of OOP, list at least four: *Reusability, Extensibility, Modularity, Maintainability*. Two is insufficient for 14-mark questions.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Differentiate between *abstraction* and *encapsulation*. Provide one Java-based example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Abstraction | Encapsulation |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Goal** | Hide *implementation complexity*; show only essential features. | Hide *internal state*; restrict direct external access. |
| **Mechanism** | Abstract classes and interfaces (`abstract`, `interface`). | `private` data members + `public` accessor/mutator methods. |
| **Focus** | *What* an object does. | *How* the data is protected. |
| **Implementation** | Achieved via abstract methods / interface contracts. | Achieved via access modifiers and getters/setters. |

**Example — Abstraction (1.5 marks):**

The `Shape` abstract class declares `abstract double area();`. The `Circle` and `Square` subclasses provide concrete implementations; the caller invokes `area()` on a generic `Shape` reference without knowing the exact geometric formula.

**Example — Encapsulation (1.5 marks):**

The `BankAccount` class declares `private double balance;`. External code cannot assign `balance = -1000` directly. Instead, the `public void deposit(double amount)` method validates `amount > 0` before updating the field.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain *dynamic polymorphism* with a suitable example. How is it different from *static polymorphism*?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Dynamic polymorphism** (2 marks) is the mechanism by which a *single method call* resolves to *different implementations* based on the *actual runtime type* of the object, not the declared type of the reference. It is achieved through **method overriding** combined with **upcasting** and **late binding** by the JVM.

**Example:** A `Vehicle` reference variable `v` is declared as `Vehicle v = new Car();`. Calling `v.accelerate()` invokes `Car`'s `accelerate()` method, not `Vehicle`'s, because the JVM dispatches based on the actual object class at run-time.

**Difference from static polymorphism (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Static (Overloading) | Dynamic (Overriding) |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Resolution** | Compile-time | Run-time |
| **Signature** | Different parameter list | Same signature |
| **Keyword** | None | `super` (optional) |
| **Class involvement** | Same class | Superclass–subclass pair |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Regulation):** Candidates must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified; Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain any four core object-oriented concepts with real-world analogies. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate *run-time polymorphism* in Java. Explain how the JVM performs dynamic method dispatch. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Four Object-Oriented Concepts (7 Marks)

The four most frequently examined concepts (along with real-world analogies) are as follows:

**1. Encapsulation (1.75 marks)**
- **Definition:** Wrapping data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit and restricting external access.
- **Real-world Analogy:** An *ATM machine*. The user interacts with a screen and keypad (public interface) but cannot directly access the cash vault (private data). All operations (withdraw, deposit) are mediated by the ATM's internal logic, which enforces rules (e.g., daily withdrawal limits).

**2. Abstraction (1.75 marks)**
- **Definition:** Hiding implementation details and exposing only the essential functionality.
- **Real-world Analogy:** A *car's steering wheel*. The driver turns the wheel (essential interface) without knowing whether the car uses hydraulic, electric, or mechanical power steering. The complexity of the steering mechanism is abstracted away.

**3. Inheritance (1.75 marks)**
- **Definition:** A mechanism by which a new class (subclass) acquires the properties and methods of an existing class (superclass), enabling code reuse and hierarchical classification.
- **Real-world Analogy:** A *biological taxonomy*. The class `Animal` defines generic features (eats, breathes, moves). The subclass `Mammal` inherits these and adds features (gives birth to live young, has mammary glands). The subclass `Dog` further inherits from `Mammal` and adds features (barks, fetches).

**4. Polymorphism (1.75 marks)**
- **Definition:** The ability of a single interface (method call) to invoke different underlying implementations depending on the actual type of the object.
- **Real-world Analogy:** A *remote control* with a "Power" button. The same button turns on a TV, an air conditioner, or a music system. The button is the *interface*; the *implementation* depends on the device currently selected.

---

#### Part (b) — Run-Time Polymorphism in Java (7 Marks)

**Java Demonstration (4 marks):**

The example models a `Shape` hierarchy with `Circle` and `Square` subclasses, each overriding the `area()` method.

- The *superclass* `Shape` declares the abstract method `area()` and provides a default `display()` method.
- The *subclass* `Circle` overrides `area()` to compute $\pi r^2$.
- The *subclass* `Square` overrides `area()` to compute $s^2$.

In the `main` method, a generic `Shape` reference is created:

$$
\text{Shape } s_1 = \text{new Circle}(5.0); \quad \text{Shape } s_2 = \text{new Square}(4.0);
$$

The calls `s_1.area()` and `s_2.area()` invoke different implementations, even though the *reference type* is identical (`Shape`).

**JVM Dynamic Method Dispatch (3 marks):**

The JVM performs run-time polymorphism in the following steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Class Loading:} \quad & \text{JVM loads } Shape, Circle, Square \text{ classes and their vtables.} \\
\text{2. Heap Allocation:} \quad & \text{`new Circle(5.0)` allocates a Circle object with method-table pointer to Circle's vtable.} \\
\text{3. Reference Assignment:} \quad & s_1 \text{ is statically typed as Shape, dynamically holds Circle reference.} \\
\text{4. Method Invocation:} \quad & s_1.area() \Rightarrow \text{JVM fetches vtable of Circle, locates `area`, dispatches to Circle's implementation.} \\
\text{5. Return:} \quad & \text{Circle returns } \pi \times 25 = 78.54; \text{ Square returns } 16.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The compiler verifies at compile-time that `area()` exists in the `Shape` class (statically legal). The actual method selection, however, is deferred to run-time. The JVM uses the object's *class pointer* in the heap to look up the vtable and dispatch to the correct override. This is the essence of *late binding* and *dynamic dispatch*.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a)** Compare *inheritance* and *composition* as reuse mechanisms. Which one is preferred in modern OO design, and why? (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a clear example, explain *abstract class* versus *interface* in Java. State the differences in a tabular form. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Inheritance vs Composition (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Inheritance | Composition |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Relationship** | "is-a" (Car *is-a* Vehicle) | "has-a" (Car *has-a* Engine) |
| **Coupling** | Tight (subclass depends on superclass internals) | Loose (only uses the public interface of the contained object) |
| **Flexibility** | Statically fixed at compile-time | Dynamically changeable at run-time (the composed object can be swapped) |
| **Reuse mechanism** | White-box reuse (inherits implementation details) | Black-box reuse (uses only public API) |
| **Risk** | Fragile base-class problem; breaks encapsulation | Higher initial design effort |

**Preferred Mechanism (3 marks):**

Modern OO design **favors composition over inheritance**, a principle articulated in the seminal *"Design Patterns"* book by the *Gang of Four* and later formalised in the **SOLID** principles. The rationale is:

1. Composition encapsulates *behaviour* behind well-defined interfaces, preserving encapsulation.
2. Composition supports *favouring composition over inheritance* (FCOI), enabling flexible run-time substitution of behaviour.
3. Inheritance should be reserved exclusively for genuine "is-a" hierarchical relationships (e.g., `Circle is-a Shape`); otherwise, composition is safer.

**Example (2 marks):** A `Car` class *has-a* `Engine` object rather than *is-a* `Engine`. The engine can be a `PetrolEngine` or `DieselEngine` — the car class can be designed to work with any `Engine` subtype via polymorphism, demonstrating composition's flexibility.

#### Part (b) — Abstract Class vs Interface (7 Marks)

**Abstract Class (2 marks):**
- Declared with the `abstract` keyword.
- May contain **both abstract and concrete** methods, instance variables, constructors, and `static` methods.
- A class can extend **only one** abstract class (Java's single inheritance rule).
- Used when subclasses share a common *state* or *implementation* but need to enforce some *abstract* behaviour.

**Interface (2 marks):**
- Declared with the `interface` keyword (implicitly `abstract`).
- Prior to Java 8: contains only abstract method signatures and constants.
- Java 8 onwards: may contain `default` and `static` methods with concrete implementations.
- Java 9 onwards: may contain `private` methods for internal code reuse.
- A class can implement **multiple** interfaces.
- Used to define a *contract* that disparate, unrelated classes can fulfil.

**Tabular Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---------|----------------|-----------|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| Methods | Abstract + concrete | Abstract; default/static (Java 8+); private (Java 9+) |
| Variables | Instance + static + final | Only `public static final` (constants) |
| Constructor | Yes | No |
| Access modifiers for methods | Any (`public`, `protected`, `private` from Java 9) | `public` only (implicitly) |
| Multiple inheritance | No (one superclass) | Yes (multiple interfaces) |
| Instantiation | Cannot instantiate directly | Cannot instantiate directly |
| When to use | Shared code + abstract contract | Pure contract, capability definition |

**Example (built into the 7 marks):**
- *Abstract class:* `Animal` with `abstract void sound();` and a concrete `void eat() { ... }` method.
- *Interface:* `Swimmable` with `void swim();`. Both `Fish` and `Human` (a swimmer) implement `Swimmable`, even though `Fish extends Animal` while `Human` may not.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: OOP Concepts**

- [ ] **Object** = Identity + State + Behaviour; created via `new`.
- [ ] **Class** = Blueprint of objects; parsed at compile-time.
- [ ] **Encapsulation** = Data hiding + bundling. Use `private` fields and `public` getters/setters.
- [ ] **Abstraction** = Hiding implementation, showing interface. Use `abstract class` or `interface`.
- [ ] **Inheritance** = `extends` (single) / `implements` (multiple interfaces). Promotes reuse.
- [ ] **Polymorphism** = Overloading (compile-time, static) **≠** Overriding (run-time, dynamic).
- [ ] **Dynamic dispatch** uses the *actual object's class* at run-time, not the reference type.
- [ ] **Upcasting** is implicit and safe; **downcasting** requires explicit cast + `instanceof`.
- [ ] **Constructor chaining**: subclass constructor implicitly calls `super()` as the first statement.
- [ ] **Overriding rule**: same signature; visibility may be widened, never narrowed; `final` methods cannot be overridden.
- [ ] **Java does NOT support multiple class inheritance** — use interfaces instead.
- [ ] **Composition over inheritance** is the modern best practice for code reuse.
- [ ] **Modularity** = organising classes into `package`s; supports access control (`default`, `protected`).
- [ ] **Liskov Substitution Principle** must hold for any valid inheritance hierarchy.
- [ ] **Open/Closed Principle**: classes should be *open for extension, closed for modification* — polymorphism and inheritance enable this.
- [ ] **Default access** (`package-private`) is the no-modifier access; `protected` includes package + subclasses.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question on Module 2, structure the answer as: *(i) Definition, (ii) Real-world analogy, (iii) Java code snippet (or pseudocode), (iv) Advantages, (v) Limitations.* This five-part structure satisfies all KTU 2024 rubric categories (Recall, Comprehension, Application, Analysis) and consistently scores 12–14 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activity** in the context of Object-Oriented Design using Java (Android Application Development) is defined as a **single, focused, user-interactive screen** that forms the fundamental building block of any Android application. According to the official Android Developer Documentation and the KTU 2024 syllabus for PEITT411, *an Activity is a Java class that extends the `android.app.Activity` class (or its modern counterpart `AppCompatActivity`) and serves as an entry point for user interaction, providing a window in which the application draws its UI.*

Every visible Android application is essentially a coordinated collection of activities — each one functioning as a self-contained, lifecycle-aware component that can exist independently of other components within the same application process.

## 1.2 Key Technical Characteristics

| Characteristic | Description |
|----------------|-------------|
| **Inheritance Root** | Extends `android.app.Activity` or `androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity` |
| **UI Responsibility** | Renders UI components defined in XML layouts (e.g., `activity_main.xml`) |
| **Lifecycle Awareness** | Managed by the Android Framework through the **Activity Lifecycle** callback methods |
| **Back Stack Behaviour** | Activities are pushed onto and popped from the **Activity Stack** (LIFO structure) |
| **Launching Mechanism** | Activated via **Intents** (explicit or implicit) |
| **Configuration Persistence** | State managed through `onSaveInstanceState()` and Bundle objects |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Room" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A House with Multiple Rooms**
> Imagine your Android application as a **large house**. Each Activity is an **individual room** in that house:
> - When you enter a room (launch an activity), you see its furniture (UI elements like buttons, text fields, images).
> - You can walk from one room to another through a **doorway** (this doorway is called an **Intent**).
> - The house manager (the **Android OS / Activity Manager**) keeps a record of the rooms you've visited — this record is the **Back Stack**.
> - Pressing the **Back button** is like stepping back into the previous room (popping the stack).
> - If the house manager finds that a room has been idle for too long or the device is running low on memory, they may **dispose of the room's contents** (the OS destroys the activity to reclaim memory).

This analogy demystifies why activities exist, how they communicate, and how the operating system manages their existence.

## 1.4 Distinguishing Activities from Other Android Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Activities must not be confused with other Android components:
> - **Service** — Runs in the background without a UI (e.g., music playback).
> - **Broadcast Receiver** — Responds to system-wide announcements (e.g., low battery).
> - **Content Provider** — Manages shared application data.
> - **Activity** — The *only* component that provides a visual UI and direct user interaction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Activity Class Hierarchy

Every Activity in Java for Android inherits from a well-defined class hierarchy. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for KTU board examinations, as it is a frequent Part-A question.

```
java.lang.Object
   └── android.content.Context
         └── android.app.ApplicationContext
               └── android.app.Activity
                     └── androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity
                           └── (Your Custom Activity, e.g., MainActivity)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `AppCompatActivity` is preferred over `Activity` in modern development:**
> - Provides backward compatibility for Material Design components on older Android versions (API levels < 21).
> - Includes support for the `androidx.appcompat` toolbar and theming.
> - Required for using `AppCompatDelegate` and modern UI patterns taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 2.2 The Seven Activity Lifecycle Methods — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Android framework invokes specific callback methods at defined states in an activity's life. These methods form the **Activity Lifecycle**, a high-yield topic for 14-mark questions.

| # | Callback Method | Visibility State | Purpose / Action |
|---|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | `onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)` | Created (not visible) | Inflates layout, initializes views, sets up listeners. Called **once per instance**. |
| 2 | `onStart()` | Visible but not focused | Prepares the UI to become interactive. Re-registers UI-related listeners. |
| 3 | `onResume()` | Visible AND focused (foreground) | Activity is now in the "Resumed" state — the user can interact. Resume paused operations (animations, sensors). |
| 4 | `onPause()` | Partially obscured | Release CPU-intensive resources, commit unsaved changes (e.g., drafts). Called when another activity comes in front. |
| 5 | `onStop()` | Not visible | Activity is no longer visible. Release heavy resources (network connections, camera). |
| 6 | `onDestroy()` | Destroyed | Final cleanup. Called either because `finish()` was invoked or the system is reclaiming memory. |
| 7 | `onRestart()` | Coming back from stopped state | Invoked after `onStop()` when the activity is being shown to the user again. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`onCreate()` vs `onStart()` vs `onResume()` — The Visibility vs Focus Distinction:**
> - `onCreate()` → Activity object exists in memory but window is **not drawn yet**.
> - `onStart()` → Window becomes **visible** but does not yet have **input focus**.
> - `onResume()` → Activity has **focus** — it is at the top of the back stack.

## 2.3 Activity State Diagram Transitions

The transitions between the four primary states (Created, Started, Resumed, Paused, Stopped, Destroyed) follow deterministic rules:

| Trigger Event | Previous State | Method Invoked | Next State |
|---------------|----------------|----------------|------------|
| Activity first launched | Does not exist | `onCreate()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()` | Resumed |
| Another activity comes to foreground | Resumed | `onPause()` → `onStop()` | Stopped |
| User returns from another activity | Stopped | `onRestart()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()` | Resumed |
| User presses Back button | Resumed | `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onDestroy()` | Destroyed |
| User presses Home button | Resumed | `onPause()` → `onStop()` | Stopped |
| System kills process (memory pressure) | Paused/Stopped | *(No callback)* | Destroyed |
| Configuration change (e.g., screen rotation) | Resumed | `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onDestroy()` → `onCreate()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()` | Resumed (new instance) |

## 2.4 The Activity Stack (Back Stack) — Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Activity Stack** (also called the **Back Stack**) is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure maintained by the Android system through the `ActivityManager` service. It governs the navigation history of activities within a task.

**Operational Rules of the Back Stack:**

1. **Push Operation:** When a new activity is launched (via `startActivity()`), it is pushed onto the **top** of the stack.
2. **Pop Operation:** When the user presses the Back button or `finish()` is called, the topmost activity is popped and destroyed; the previous activity resumes.
3. **Stack Overflow:** By default, the OS will terminate activities at the bottom of the stack if memory is exhausted — these are the *least recently used* activities.
4. **Single Task Affinity:** Activities sharing the same `taskAffinity` in the manifest will belong to the same back stack.

**Mathematical Model of the Stack:**

Let $S_t$ denote the stack at time $t$. For $n$ activities $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$:

$$S_t = [A_1 \rightarrow A_2 \rightarrow A_3 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow A_n]_{\text{top}}$$

When the Back button is pressed:

$$S_{t+1} = [A_1 \rightarrow A_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow A_{n-1}]_{\text{top}}$$

This means $A_n$ is popped and the new top becomes $A_{n-1}$.

## 2.5 Intents — The Communication Bridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intent** is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component. It is the mechanism through which activities are launched.

| Intent Type | Description | Example |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| **Explicit Intent** | Specifies the exact target component by class name. | `Intent i = new Intent(this, SecondActivity.class);` |
| **Implicit Intent** | Declares an action; the system finds a matching component. | `Intent i = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://ktu.edu.in"));` |

**Intent Method Overloads for Activity Launching:**

| Method Signature | Behaviour |
|------------------|-----------|
| `startActivity(Intent intent)` | Launches a new activity without expecting a result. |
| `startActivityForResult(Intent intent, int requestCode)` | **(Deprecated)** — Launches an activity expecting a result back. |
| `registerForActivityResult(ActivityResultContract<I, O>, ActivityResultCallback<O>)` | **Modern AndroidX approach** — Registers a launcher for receiving results. |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Activity Communication

| Concept | Equation / Notation | Meaning |
|---------|--------------------|---------| 
| Back Stack Push | $S_t \xrightarrow{\text{startActivity}(i)} S_{t+1} = S_t \cup \{A_{\text{new}}\}$ | New activity added to top |
| Back Stack Pop | $S_t \xrightarrow{\text{Back/finish()}} S_{t-1} = S_t \setminus \{A_{\text{top}}\}$ | Top activity removed |
| Intent Filter Match Score | $M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot c_i$ where $c_i \in \{0,1\}$ | Weights of matched intent filter components |
| Configuration Change Penalty | $T_{\text{recovery}} = t_{\text{destroy}} + t_{\text{create}} + t_{\text{start}} + t_{\text{resume}}$ | Total time to recover from rotation |
| Activity Instance Retention (saved state) | $\text{State} \subseteq \text{Bundle} \xrightarrow{\text{onSaveInstanceState}} \text{Persisted}$ | State saved across destruction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The activity lifecycle and back-stack model are critical in:
> - **Mobile UX Engineering** — designing smooth navigation transitions.
> - **Memory-constrained systems** — Android runs on devices with as little as **1 GB RAM**; lifecycle awareness prevents crashes.
> - **State preservation** — ensuring user data (form inputs, scroll positions) survives configuration changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Creating a Custom Activity in Java

This derivation follows the **exact sequence of steps** a developer performs when scaffolding a new activity, as required by the KTU practical examination pattern.

### Step 1: Declare the Activity in the Android Manifest

The manifest file `AndroidManifest.xml` is the application-level configuration. Every activity must be declared, or it will not be recognized by the Android system.

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    package="com.example.ktuactivities">

    <application
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.KTUActivities">

        <!-- Step 1a: Declaring the launcher activity -->
        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <!-- Step 1b: Declaring a secondary activity -->
        <activity
            android:name=".SecondActivity"
            android:exported="false"
            android:parentActivityName=".MainActivity" />
    </application>
</manifest>
```

**Logical Conversion:** The `<intent-filter>` with `MAIN` action and `LAUNCHER` category designates `MainActivity` as the **entry point** of the application. Without this, the app icon will not appear in the device launcher.

### Step 2: Create the Layout XML Resource

The UI is defined declaratively in `res/layout/activity_main.xml`:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center"
    android:padding="24dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/titleText"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Welcome to KTU Activities Demo"
        android:textSize="22sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/goToSecondBtn"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:layout_marginTop="32dp"
        android:text="Open Second Activity" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Logical Conversion:** Each UI component is given a unique `android:id` so it can be referenced from Java code via `findViewById()`. The IDs are converted to integer constants at compile time by the `aapt2` resource compiler.

### Step 3: Implement the Activity Java Class

```java
package com.example.ktuactivities;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.util.Log;
import android.widget.Button;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    // Class-level constant for log tagging (best practice)
    private static final String TAG = "MainActivityLifecycle";

    // UI component references — declared at class level for lifecycle efficiency
    private Button goToSecondBtn;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        Log.d(TAG, "onCreate() invoked");
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        goToSecondBtn = findViewById(R.id.goToSecondBtn);
        goToSecondBtn.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            Intent intent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, SecondActivity.class);
            intent.putExtra("USER_NAME", "KTU Student");
            startActivity(intent);
        });
    }

    @Override
    protected void onStart() {
        super.onStart();
        Log.d(TAG, "onStart() invoked");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onResume() {
        super.onResume();
        Log.d(TAG, "onResume() invoked");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onPause() {
        super.onPause();
        Log.d(TAG, "onPause() invoked");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onStop() {
        super.onStop();
        Log.d(TAG, "onStop() invoked");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onDestroy() {
        super.onDestroy();
        Log.d(TAG, "onDestroy() invoked");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onRestart() {
        super.onRestart();
        Log.d(TAG, "onRestart() invoked");
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Derive the Lifecycle Invocation Order

Let us trace what happens when the user launches the app, presses Home, returns to the app, and finally presses Back. This is a high-yield derivation for KTU board exams.

$$L_{\text{launch}} = [\text{onCreate} \rightarrow \text{onStart} \rightarrow \text{onResume}]$$

When the user presses **Home**:

$$L_{\text{home}} = [\text{onPause} \rightarrow \text{onStop}]$$

When the user **returns**:

$$L_{\text{return}} = [\text{onRestart} \rightarrow \text{onStart} \rightarrow \text{onResume}]$$

When the user presses **Back**:

$$L_{\text{back}} = [\text{onPause} \rightarrow \text{onStop} \rightarrow \text{onDestroy}]$$

**Complete sequence for a typical session:**

$$\Sigma = L_{\text{launch}} \cup L_{\text{home}} \cup L_{\text{return}} \cup L_{\text{back}}$$

$$\Sigma = \text{onCreate}, \text{onStart}, \text{onResume}, \text{onPause}, \text{onStop}, \text{onRestart}, \text{onStart}, \text{onResume}, \text{onPause}, \text{onStop}, \text{onDestroy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** `onCreate()` and `onDestroy()` are called **only once per activity instance**. The methods `onStart()`, `onResume()`, `onPause()`, `onStop()` may be called **multiple times** during the activity's lifetime.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Lifecycle Method Execution Conditions

This matrix is highly valuable for solving KTU objective questions:

| User Action / System Event | Methods Called (in order) |
|----------------------------|---------------------------|
| First time opening the app | `onCreate` → `onStart` → `onResume` |
| Pressing the Back button | `onPause` → `onStop` → `onDestroy` |
| Pressing the Home button | `onPause` → `onStop` |
| Returning to app from Home | `onRestart` → `onStart` → `onResume` |
| Receiving a phone call (dialog appears) | `onPause` |
| Hanging up the phone call | `onResume` |
| Rotating the device | `onPause` → `onStop` → `onDestroy` → `onCreate` → `onStart` → `onResume` |
| Opening a new activity via Intent | `onPause` (current) → `onCreate` → `onStart` → `onResume` (new) → `onStop` (current) |
| Closing a dialog-themed activity | `onResume` (current activity resumes immediately) |

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation — Activity Instance Count

Let $n$ be the total number of activities launched in a session. Let $r$ be the number of times the user rotates the device. Each rotation **destroys and recreates** the activity (unless `configChanges` is declared in the manifest). The total number of `onCreate` invocations is:

$$N_{\text{onCreate}} = n \cdot (1 + r)$$

The total number of `onDestroy` invocations is:

$$N_{\text{onDestroy}} = n \cdot (1 + r) - k$$

where $k$ is the number of activities still alive (e.g., backgrounded but not destroyed).

The total number of `onResume` invocations per activity is:

$$N_{\text{onResume}} = r + 1$$

This demonstrates that `onResume` is called **once per activity instance creation**, plus once per return from a paused state.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activity Lifecycle State Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete state transitions of an Activity. It is rendered as a **state machine** with labelled transitions representing the callback methods.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : onCreate invoked
    Created --> Started : onStart invoked
    Started --> Resumed : onResume invoked
    Resumed --> Paused : onPause invoked
    Paused --> Resumed : onResume invoked
    Paused --> Stopped : onStop invoked
    Stopped --> Destroyed : onDestroy invoked
    Stopped --> Started : onRestart then onStart
    Destroyed --> [*]
    Resumed --> Stopped : onPause then onStop
    Resumed --> Destroyed : onPause then onStop then onDestroy
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Resumed** is the only state where the activity is fully interactive and has input focus.
> - **Paused** state occurs when the activity is partially obscured (e.g., a transparent dialog is on top).
> - **Stopped** state occurs when the activity is completely hidden (e.g., another activity occupies the entire screen).
> - From **Stopped**, the system may kill the process without calling `onDestroy()` — this is a common point of confusion in KTU exams.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Activity Communication Flow

This block diagram maps how multiple activities interact via Intents and the back stack. This is the **Fallback Architecture Diagram** as recommended for complex interaction patterns.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MainActivity Launched] --> B[Intent Object Created]
    B --> C{Intent Type}
    C -->|Explicit| D[Target Class Specified]
    C -->|Implicit| E[Action + Data URI]
    D --> F[ActivityManager Service]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Activity Stack Push]
    G --> H[SecondActivity Instantiated]
    H --> I[onCreate onStart onResume]
    I --> J{User Action}
    J -->|Back Button| K[Pop Activity Stack]
    J -->|Home Button| L[Move to Background]
    J -->|New Intent| M[Push Another Activity]
    K --> N[onPause onStop onDestroy]
    L --> O[onPause onStop]
    M --> P[ThirdActivity Created]
    N --> Q[Return to Previous Activity]
    O --> R[Process May Be Killed]
    P --> S[New Top of Stack]
```

## 4.3 Class Hierarchy and Method Override Structure

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Object {
        <<java.lang>>
    }
    class Context {
        <<android.content>>
    }
    class Activity {
        <<android.app>>
        +onCreate(Bundle)
        +onStart()
        +onResume()
        +onPause()
        +onStop()
        +onDestroy()
        +onRestart()
    }
    class AppCompatActivity {
        <<androidx.appcompat.app>>
    }
    class MainActivity {
        +onCreate(Bundle)
        +onStart()
        +onResume()
    }
    class SecondActivity {
        +onCreate(Bundle)
    }
    Object <|-- Context
    Context <|-- Activity
    Activity <|-- AppCompatActivity
    AppCompatActivity <|-- MainActivity
    AppCompatActivity <|-- SecondActivity
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows that `MainActivity` and `SecondActivity` are sibling subclasses of `AppCompatActivity`. They inherit all lifecycle methods from `Activity` but are free to override only those methods relevant to their functionality.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Activity in Android. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An Activity in Android is a fundamental component that represents a **single screen with a user interface** in an application. It extends the `android.app.Activity` class (or `AppCompatActivity` from AndroidX) and serves as the entry point for user interaction. Each activity is independent and can be launched using Intents. Activities follow a well-defined lifecycle managed by the Android framework, transitioning through states such as Created, Started, Resumed, Paused, Stopped, and Destroyed through corresponding callback methods like `onCreate()`, `onStart()`, `onResume()`, `onPause()`, `onStop()`, and `onDestroy()`. Activities are stacked in a LIFO structure called the **Back Stack**, where pressing the back button pops the current activity and reveals the previous one.

*(Examiner's Note: Award full marks only if the student mentions both the inheritance hierarchy and at least three lifecycle methods.)*

### Question 2: Differentiate between Explicit Intent and Implicit Intent. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Target Specification** | Specifies the exact target component (e.g., class name). | Does not specify a target; declares an action for the system to resolve. |
| **Security** | Safe — only the specified component is invoked. | Less safe — any matching app on the device can respond. |
| **Use Case** | Navigating between activities within the same app. | Performing common actions across apps (dial a number, open a URL). |
| **Example** | `new Intent(this, SecondActivity.class)` | `new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://ktu.edu.in"))` |

**Code Sample for Explicit Intent:**
```java
Intent explicitIntent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, SecondActivity.class);
explicitIntent.putExtra("key", "value");
startActivity(explicitIntent);
```

**Code Sample for Implicit Intent:**
```java
Intent implicitIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://ktu.edu.in"));
if (implicitIntent.resolveActivity(getPackageManager()) != null) {
    startActivity(implicitIntent);
}
```
