## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Lifecycle Implementation with State Management

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Question:** With a neat diagram, explain the Android Activity Lifecycle. List all the callback methods in their correct order of invocation and describe the purpose of each.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

**Activity Lifecycle Diagram (Text Representation for Answer Sheet):**

The Android Activity Lifecycle consists of **six core callback methods** plus one conditional method (`onRestart`). The complete invocation sequence is shown below:

```
onCreate() → onStart() → onResume() → [Activity Running]
    ↓ (User navigates away or system event)
onPause() → onStop() → [Activity in Background]
    ↓ (System reclaims memory OR user finishes)
onDestroy() → [Activity Destroyed]
```

**Or, if returning from background:**

```
onStop() → onRestart() → onStart() → onResume()
```

**Description of Each Callback Method:**

1. **`onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)` [1 mark]** — Called when the activity is **first created**. This is where you initialize essential components: set the layout using `setContentView()`, bind UI elements via `findViewById()`, configure adapters, and set click listeners. The `savedInstanceState` parameter contains previously saved state data if the activity is being recreated after destruction.

2. **`onStart()` [1 mark]** — Called when the activity becomes **visible to the user** but is not yet in the foreground. This is where you should make the UI visible and register any `BroadcastReceiver`s that should be active only when the activity is visible.

3. **`onResume()` [1 mark]** — Called when the activity **gains focus** and becomes the foreground activity. The user can now interact with it. Resume animations, sensor listeners, GPS updates, or any other resource-intensive operations that were paused in `onPause()`.

4. **`onPause()` [1 mark]** — Called when the activity **loses focus** but is still partially visible (e.g., a dialog or another translucent activity is on top). Release or pause resources that should not consume CPU when the activity is not in focus — for example, pausing a video player or releasing the camera.

5. **`onStop()` [1 mark]** — Called when the activity is **no longer visible**. Release heavy resources such as network connections, database cursors, or registered listeners that are not needed when the activity is hidden. The activity instance is still kept in memory.

6. **`onDestroy()` [1 mark]** — Called before the activity is **destroyed**. This is the final cleanup point. The activity can be destroyed because `finish()` was called, the user pressed Back, or the system is reclaiming memory due to configuration changes. Release all remaining resources to prevent memory leaks.

7. **`onRestart()` [1 mark — conditional]** — Called when an activity that was stopped is being restarted. Always followed by `onStart()` and then `onResume()`.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Question:** Write a Java program to demonstrate the Activity Lifecycle by overriding all the callback methods in `MainActivity` and printing log statements. Also, show the manifest declaration of this activity.

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Manifest Declaration** (`AndroidManifest.xml`)

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    package="com.example.lifecycle">

    <application
        android:label="Lifecycle Demo"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MaterialComponents">
        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>
    </application>
</manifest>
```

**Step 2: MainActivity.java**

```java
package com.example.lifecycle;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.util.Log;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private static final String TAG = "KtuActivityLifecycle";

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);
        Log.d(TAG, "1. onCreate() — Activity Created");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onStart() {
        super.onStart();
        Log.d(TAG, "2. onStart() — Activity Visible");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onResume() {
        super.onResume();
        Log.d(TAG, "3. onResume() — Activity in Foreground");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onPause() {
        super.onPause();
        Log.d(TAG, "4. onPause() — Activity Losing Focus");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onStop() {
        super.onStop();
        Log.d(TAG, "5. onStop() — Activity Not Visible");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onDestroy() {
        super.onDestroy();
        Log.d(TAG, "6. onDestroy() — Activity Destroyed");
    }

    @Override
    protected void onRestart() {
        super.onRestart();
        Log.d(TAG, "7. onRestart() — Activity Restarting from Stopped State");
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Expected Logcat Output**

When the user launches the app:
```
1. onCreate() — Activity Created
2. onStart() — Activity Visible
3. onResume() — Activity in Foreground
```

When the user presses the Home button:
```
4. onPause() — Activity Losing Focus
5. onStop() — Activity Not Visible
```

When the user returns to the app:
```
7. onRestart() — Activity Restarting from Stopped State
2. onStart() — Activity Visible
3. onResume() — Activity in Foreground
```

When the user presses the Back button:
```
4. onPause() — Activity Losing Focus
5. onStop() — Activity Not Visible
6. onDestroy() — Activity Destroyed
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing `super.onXxx()` call** — Students often forget to call the parent class method. This is a **2-mark deduction** per occurrence in the exam.
> 2. **Incorrect log tag** — Using `System.out.println()` instead of `Log.d()` will not produce output in Logcat. Use `android.util.Log` for proper logging.
> 3. **Missing `setContentView()` in `onCreate()`** — Without this call, the activity will have a blank screen.
> 4. **Confusing `onPause()` with `onStop()`** — `onPause()` is called when the activity *partially* loses focus (e.g., dialog overlay). `onStop()` is called when the activity is *completely* hidden.
> 5. **Forgetting the `LAUNCHER` intent filter** — Without it, the activity cannot be launched from the device home screen.

### Question B (14 Marks): Activity Navigation with Intent-Based Data Passing

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Question:** Explain the concept of Intents in Android. Differentiate between Explicit and Implicit Intents with suitable Java code examples. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Intents:**

An Intent in Android is a **messaging object** that facilitates communication between components. It is an abstract description of an operation to be performed. Intents serve three primary purposes:

1. **Activity Navigation** — Launching one activity from another.
2. **Service Invocation** — Starting background services.
3. **Broadcast Triggering** — Sending system-wide announcements.

**Explicit Intent — Theory [2 marks]:**

An Explicit Intent specifies the **exact target component** (by its Java class name) that should receive the intent. It is typically used for in-app navigation where the developer knows precisely which component to invoke. Explicit intents are the safest form of intent because they guarantee the intended target receives the message.

**Code Example:**
```java
Intent explicitIntent = new Intent(CurrentActivity.this, TargetActivity.class);
explicitIntent.putExtra("username", "admin");
explicitIntent.putExtra("age", 21);
startActivity(explicitIntent);
```

**Implicit Intent — Theory [2 marks]:**

An Implicit Intent declares a **general action** (e.g., `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_DIAL`) along with optional data (e.g., a URI). The Android system inspects all installed apps' manifest files to find a component whose `<intent-filter>` matches the declared action and data type. If multiple components match, the user is presented with a chooser dialog.

**Code Example:**
```java
// Opening a web page
Intent webIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://www.ktu.edu.in"));
startActivity(webIntent);

// Dialing a phone number
Intent dialIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_DIAL, Uri.parse("tel:+911234567890"));
startActivity(dialIntent);

// Sending an email
Intent emailIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_SEND);
emailIntent.setType("text/plain");
emailIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_EMAIL, new String[]{"admissions@ktu.edu.in"});
emailIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_SUBJECT, "Inquiry");
startActivity(Intent.createChooser(emailIntent, "Send Email via:"));
```

**Comparison Table [3 marks]:**

| Parameter | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Target | Specific component (class name) | Abstract action resolved by OS |
| Resolution | Direct call | Via Intent Resolution mechanism |
| Cross-app communication | No (same app) | Yes (across apps) |
| Security risk | Low | Potential (intent redirection) |
| Use case | Internal navigation | Common actions (web, dial, email) |
| Manifest declaration | Not required in target | Required (intent-filter) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Question:** Write a complete Java program that demonstrates launching a `SecondActivity` from `MainActivity` using an Explicit Intent and passing a user's name and age. The `SecondActivity` should display the received data. Show both the XML layout and Java code. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Layout for `MainActivity` (`activity_main.xml`)**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="24dp"
    android:gravity="center">

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Main Activity"
        android:textSize="28sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/nameInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter your name"
        android:inputType="textPersonName"
        android:layout_marginTop="24dp" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/ageInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter your age"
        android:inputType="number"
        android:layout_marginTop="16dp" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/submitBtn"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Send to Second Activity"
        android:layout_marginTop="24dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 2: Layout for `SecondActivity` (`activity_second.xml`)**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="24dp"
    android:gravity="center">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/displayText"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Received data will appear here"
        android:textSize="22sp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 3: `MainActivity.java`**

```java
package com.example.intentdemo;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.Button;
import android.widget.EditText;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private EditText nameInput, ageInput;
    private Button submitBtn;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        nameInput = findViewById(R.id.nameInput);
        ageInput = findViewById(R.id.ageInput);
        submitBtn = findViewById(R.id.submitBtn);

        submitBtn.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            String name = nameInput.getText().toString().trim();
            String ageStr = ageInput.getText().toString().trim();

            if (name.isEmpty() || ageStr.isEmpty()) {
                nameInput.setError("Required");
                ageInput.setError("Required");
                return;
            }

            int age = Integer.parseInt(ageStr);

            Intent intent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, SecondActivity.class);
            intent.putExtra("USER_NAME", name);
            intent.putExtra("USER_AGE", age);
            startActivity(intent);
        });
    }
}
```

**Step 4: `SecondActivity.java`**

```java
package com.example.intentdemo;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.TextView;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class SecondActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private TextView displayText;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_second);

        displayText = findViewById(R.id.displayText);

        Intent incomingIntent = getIntent();
        String name = incomingIntent.getStringExtra("USER_NAME");
        int age = incomingIntent.getIntExtra("USER_AGE", 0);

        String displayMessage = "Hello, " + name + "!\nYou are " + age + " years old.";
        displayText.setText(displayMessage);
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Manifest Declaration**

```xml
<activity
    android:name=".SecondActivity"
    android:exported="false"
    android:parentActivityName=".MainActivity">
    <meta-data
        android:name="android.support.PARENT_ACTIVITY"
        android:value=".MainActivity" />
</activity>
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Intent-Based Questions:**
> 1. **Key Mismatch** — Using different keys in `putExtra()` and `getStringExtra()` (e.g., `"USERNAME"` vs `"USER_NAME"`). This will result in a `null` value at runtime. Always double-check the key strings — **1 mark deduction** per mismatch.
> 2. **Type Mismatch** — Using `getStringExtra()` for an integer value or vice versa will throw a `ClassCastException`. Use the corresponding typed getter method.
> 3. **Missing `setContentView()`** — `findViewById()` will return `null` if the layout is not set first.
> 4. **Forgetting `parentActivityName`** — This prevents the Up button from working in the ActionBar.
> 5. **Not handling empty input** — Real-world applications must validate user input before passing it as extras.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Preparation:**

- **Definition [Critical]:** An Activity is a single-screen UI component extending `AppCompatActivity` (or `Activity`). It is the entry point for user interaction in an Android app.

- **Lifecycle Methods [Critical — 14-mark favorite]:** Memorize the seven callbacks in their exact invocation order:
  - `onCreate()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()` → [running] → `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onDestroy()`
  - Conditional: `onRestart()` is called between `onStop()` and `onStart()` when returning from background.

- **State vs Method Distinction [Critical]:**
  - `onCreate()` = **Created** state (not visible).
  - `onStart()` = **Started** state (visible but not focused).
  - `onResume()` = **Resumed** state (visible AND focused — interactive).
  - `onPause()` = **Paused** state (partially obscured).
  - `onStop()` = **Stopped** state (completely hidden).
  - `onDestroy()` = **Destroyed** state (instance finalized).

- **Back Stack [Critical]:** A LIFO data structure maintained by the Android `ActivityManager`. `startActivity()` pushes; Back button or `finish()` pops.

- **Intents [High Yield]:**
  - **Explicit** → specifies target class: `new Intent(this, TargetActivity.class)`
  - **Implicit** → declares action: `new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, uri)`
  - Data passing: `intent.putExtra("KEY", value)` and `getIntent().getXxxExtra("KEY")`

- **Manifest Declaration [Required]:** Every activity must be declared in `AndroidManifest.xml`. The launcher activity must include the `<intent-filter>` with `MAIN` action and `LAUNCHER` category.

- **Best Practices [Coding Exam]:**
  1. Always call `super.onXxx()` as the **first statement** in overridden lifecycle methods.
  2. Use `findViewById()` only after `setContentView()`.
  3. Release heavy resources in `onStop()`, not `onDestroy()` (because `onDestroy()` may not be called under memory pressure).
  4. Use `Log.d(TAG, message)` for lifecycle debugging.
  5. Validate input data before passing as intent extras.

- **Common Configuration Changes:** Screen rotation triggers full activity recreation. To handle this without recreation, declare `android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize"` in the manifest (rarely recommended in modern apps).

- **Modern Android Note:** `startActivityForResult()` is **deprecated** since AndroidX Activity 1.2.0 (2020). Use `registerForActivityResult()` with `ActivityResultContracts` instead. This is a **probable follow-up question** in advanced KTU papers.

- **Memory Rule of Thumb:** Activities at the bottom of the back stack are the **first to be killed** under memory pressure. Do not store critical state only in memory — use `onSaveInstanceState()` for transient UI state and persistent storage (SharedPreferences, Room DB) for permanent data.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Activities Notes**
> **Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Pattern**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Managing Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Managing Analysis** in Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD) refers to the disciplined process of organizing, documenting, controlling, and refining the artifacts produced during the analysis phase of the Unified Process (or any iterative OO methodology). It encompasses the management of **analysis documents** (such as use-case realizations, analysis classes, and package diagrams), the **iteration planning** of analysis activities, and the **transition control** from analysis to design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Managing Analysis involves the systematic organization of analysis artifacts (use cases, analysis classes, packages), documentation standards, iteration management within the Inception and Elaboration phases, and the disciplined evolution of the analysis model toward a design-ready state.*

In KTU's 2024 scheme context, this topic falls under **Module 2: Analysis & Design** and directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply object-oriented analysis and design techniques to model real-world systems."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **building a house**:
- The **Analysis phase** is when an architect creates the blueprint (rooms, dimensions, flow).
- **Managing Analysis** is like the **project manager** who organizes all blueprint sheets into folders, labels them by floor/wing, reviews them iteratively with the client, marks changes, ensures consistency between electrical and plumbing blueprints, and finally hands them to the construction team (designers) only when the blueprint is complete and approved.

Without management:
- Blueprints get lost
- Inconsistencies creep in (a wall is electrical in one sheet, load-bearing in another)
- The construction team gets confused

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Managing analysis is NOT about doing analysis — it is about **organizing what was analyzed**, **tracking changes**, and **ensuring the analysis output is ready for design handoff.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Use-Case Description Length:** 1–3 pages per use case (industry norm).
- **Analysis Class Cardinality per Use Case:** typically **5–15 classes** for a medium-complexity use case.
- **Iterative Analysis Cycle Length:** **2–6 weeks** per iteration in Elaboration phase.
- **Documentation Standard:** **IEEE 830-1998** for SRS (Software Requirements Specification).

### 1.4 Core Components of Managing Analysis (KTU High-Yield List)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects students to know these management components:

1. **Documenting Analysis** (Use-Case Descriptions, Supplementary Specifications, Glossary)
2. **Managing the Analysis Model** (class stereotypes, packages)
3. **Iterative Refinement** of analysis
4. **From Analysis to Design** (transition)
5. **Use-Case Realizations** (analysis-level sequence and class diagrams)
6. **Architecture Description** at the analysis level
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Analysis Workflow — Structured Logic Steps

The management of analysis in OOAD follows a controlled, iterative workflow. Here is the breakdown:

#### Step 1: Establish the Analysis Model Skeleton
- Create initial **analysis packages** (logical groupings of analysis classes).
- Define **architecturally significant use cases** (the ones that shape the system).
- Identify the **architectural analysis patterns** (Layered, MVC, Client-Server, etc.).

#### Step 2: Document Use Cases at the Analysis Level
- Each use case is documented in two forms:
  1. **Brief description** (1–2 paragraphs)
  2. **Fully dressed description** (complete flow: preconditions, postconditions, main flow, alternate flows, exception flows)
- Attach **use-case diagrams** to the model.

#### Step 3: Identify Analysis Classes
For each use case, perform **noun-phrase analysis** on the use-case text to identify candidate classes. Then classify each class into one of three stereotypes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Analysis Class Stereotypes (UML Standard):**
> - **Boundary Class** (denoted as `<<boundary>>`) — mediates between system and actors (UI, API gateway, device interface)
> - **Entity Class** (denoted as `<<entity>>`) — represents persistent information (Customer, Order, Account)
> - **Control Class** (denoted as `<<control>>`) — encapsulates use-case specific logic (OrderController, LoginController)

#### Step 4: Distribute Behavior Across Classes
- Apply the **GRASP principles** (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns):
  - **Information Expert**
  - **Creator**
  - **Controller**
  - **Low Coupling**
  - **High Cohesion**

#### Step 5: Use-Case Realization
- For each use case, create:
  - An **analysis-level sequence diagram** (objects from boundary, entity, control classes interact)
  - An **analysis-level class diagram** (showing the classes involved and their relationships)

#### Step 6: Package the Analysis Model
- Group related analysis classes into **packages**.
- Apply **package dependencies** carefully (avoid circular dependencies).
- Common packaging strategies:
  - **Use-case based packaging** (one package per use case)
  - **Layered packaging** (boundary, control, entity as separate packages)
  - **Domain packaging** (one package per sub-domain)

#### Step 7: Iterate and Refine
- Each iteration adds detail, fixes inconsistencies, and may discover new requirements.
- The analysis model is **not static** — it evolves.

#### Step 8: Transition to Design
- Analysis classes map to design classes.
- Stereotypes may be removed or refined.
- Persistence, concurrency, distribution, and UI technology decisions are added.

### 2.2 Why "Manage" the Analysis?

> **The Why:**
> - Without management, the analysis model becomes a **dumping ground** of unrelated diagrams.
> - Without management, **inconsistencies** between use cases cause design rework.
> - Without management, **traceability** from requirements → analysis → design → code is lost.
> - Without management, the team **cannot collaborate** — multiple analysts would produce overlapping/conflicting artifacts.

> **The How:**
> - By applying **package organization** (modularity).
> - By enforcing **documentation standards** (templates, naming conventions).
> - By **version control** of all artifacts.
> - By **review checkpoints** at each iteration boundary.
> - By maintaining a **traceability matrix** (requirements ↔ use cases ↔ analysis classes).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a design/management topic (not physics or math), the "formulas" below are **structural formulas and rules of thumb** that KTU examiners expect you to recall.

| **Rule / Formula** | **Statement** | **When to Apply** |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary Class Rule | A class that mediates between actor and system is `<<boundary>>` | UI screens, API endpoints, device drivers |
| Entity Class Rule | A class that stores long-lived business info is `<<entity>>` | Customer, Product, Order, Invoice |
| Control Class Rule | One `<<control>>` per use case (typically) | OrderController, LoginController |
| CRC Card Formula | $\text{Class} = \langle \text{Name}, \text{Responsibilities}, \text{Collaborators} \rangle$ | Class responsibility assignment |
| Use-Case Realization | 1 use case = 1 sequence diagram + participation in 1 class diagram | Per use case in analysis model |
| Package Coupling Target | Acyclic dependency graph (no cycles) | Review of package diagram |
| Cohesion Target | $\text{Cohesion}(C) = \dfrac{\text{Related responsibilities of }C}{\text{Total responsibilities of }C}$ | Class quality check |
| Coupling Target | $\text{Coupling}(A,B) = \text{Inter-class dependencies between } A \text{ and } B$ | Aim for minimum |
| Estimation Formula | $\text{Effort} = \text{UCP} \times \text{Productivity Factor}$ | Use-case based project estimation |
| Iterative Formula | $\text{Progress} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Increment}_i$ | Each iteration adds increment |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing the **Cohesion formula** in your exam answer sheet, write it as: $\text{Cohesion}(C) = \dfrac{\text{Related responsibilities of }C}{\text{Total responsibilities of }C}$ — never use vertical pipes like `|x|` in markdown tables, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Managing analysis is used in:

- **Software Architecture Reviews** — managers ensure analysis packages are coherent.
- **CMMI Level 3+ organizations** — require formal analysis management artifacts.
- **Agile + Disciplined Agile Delivery** — analysis is managed via "initial domain modeling" sprints.
- **Safety-critical systems** (aerospace, medical) — traceability from requirement to class is legally mandated.
- **Large-scale Java enterprise projects** — Spring/Java EE apps begin with managed analysis packages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping Use Case to Analysis Classes (The "Noun-Phrase → Stereotype" Algorithm)

This is a **structural derivation** — given a use-case description, derive the set of analysis classes and their stereotypes.

**Step 1:** Take the fully-dressed use-case text.

> **Example Use Case:** *"Process Sale" — A Customer arrives at a POS terminal with items. The Cashier records each item. The System computes the total. The Customer pays; the System records the payment and issues a receipt.*

**Step 2:** Identify noun phrases (candidates for classes).

Nouns found: **Customer, Cashier, POS terminal, Item, System, Total, Payment, Receipt**

**Step 3:** Apply elimination rules:

| **Candidate** | **Decision** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Customer | Keep as **actor** | External to system |
| Cashier | Keep as **actor** | External to system |
| POS terminal | Keep as **actor** | External device |
| Item | **Entity** | Long-lived product info |
| System | **Eliminate** | Too vague; do not model |
| Total | **Eliminate** | Derived value, not an object |
| Payment | **Entity** | Persistent financial record |
| Receipt | **Entity** | Persistent record |

**Step 4:** Add control and boundary classes:

- **Boundary class:** `SaleUI` (handles cashier interaction with terminal)
- **Control class:** `ProcessSaleController` (one per use case)
- **Additional Entity:** `Sale` (encapsulates the in-progress transaction)

**Step 5:** Final analysis class set:

$$
\text{AnalysisClasses} = \{\text{Sale}, \text{Item}, \text{Payment}, \text{Receipt}\} \cup \{\text{SaleUI}\} \cup \{\text{ProcessSaleController}\}
$$

$$
\text{Entity} = \{\text{Sale}, \text{Item}, \text{Payment}, \text{Receipt}\}, \quad \text{Boundary} = \{\text{SaleUI}\}, \quad \text{Control} = \{\text{ProcessSaleController}\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why one Control per use case?** The Controller GRASP pattern recommends that **system events** (not sub-functions) be handled by a controller. Each use case typically corresponds to one system-level event, hence one control class.

### 3.2 Derivation: Packaging Strategy Decision

Given $N$ analysis classes grouped into $U$ use cases, the packaging decision is derived as follows:

**Step 1:** Count classes per use case.

$$
\forall u \in U : n_u = \vert C_u \vert
$$

where $C_u$ is the set of classes participating in use case $u$.

**Step 2:** Compute total coupling cost of a packaging strategy $S$:

$$
\text{CouplingCost}(S) = \sum_{p_i, p_j \in \text{Packages}(S)} \text{Inter-package dependencies}
$$

**Step 3:** Choose $S^*$ that minimizes coupling cost while keeping package sizes in the range of **10–50 classes** (recommended):

$$
S^* = \arg\min_{S} \left( \text{CouplingCost}(S) + \alpha \cdot \sum_{p \in S} \max(0, \vert p \vert - 50) \right)
$$

where $\alpha$ is a penalty weight for oversized packages.

**Step 4 (Example):** Suppose we have 3 use cases, 30 classes total. Two strategies:

- **Strategy A (Layered):** 3 packages (Boundary, Control, Entity) of sizes 8, 5, 17. Coupling cost = 12 cross-package deps.
- **Strategy B (Use-Case Based):** 3 packages, one per use case. Sizes: 10, 12, 8. Coupling cost = 4 cross-package deps.

$$
\text{Strategy B wins} \quad \therefore S^* = \text{Use-Case Based Packaging}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Analysis → Design

This matrix is **frequently asked in KTU exams**. Memorize it.

| **Analysis Artifact** | **Design Artifact** | **What Changes** | **What is Preserved** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `<<boundary>>` class | UI class (Swing/FXML/HTML) | Technology, layout, widgets | Public methods, responsibilities |
| `<<control>>` class | Application/Service class | Statelessness, transaction mgmt | Use-case flow logic |
| `<<entity>>` class | Domain class (POJO/JavaBean) | Persistence annotations, validation | Attributes, business methods |
| Analysis package | Design package / module | Layering, dependency direction | Class names, relationships |
| Use-case description | Use-case realization | Sequence diagram, methods | Pre/postconditions |
| N/A | New design classes | DAO, Factory, Strategy, etc. | — |

### 3.4 Derivation: Iteration Management

In Iterative-Incremental OOAD, total work $W$ is split across iterations:

$$
W = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the work done in iteration $i$, and $k$ is the total number of iterations.

**Velocity** (a Scrum-derived metric) is used to monitor progress:

$$
V_i = \frac{w_i^{\text{planned}} - w_i^{\text{actual}}}{w_i^{\text{planned}}}
$$

**Acceptable velocity range:**

$$
-0.2 \leq V_i \leq 0.3
$$

If $V_i < -0.2$: iteration is overloaded; defer use cases.
If $V_i > 0.3$: iteration is underloaded; pull in more use cases.

### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** *"For a Library Management System, perform Managing Analysis: identify analysis classes with stereotypes, draw the use-case realization, and propose a packaging strategy."*

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Use cases identified:** Issue Book, Return Book, Register Member, Search Catalog.

2. **For "Issue Book" use case, identify classes:**

$$
\text{IssueBookClasses} = \{\text{Book}, \text{Member}, \text{Loan}, \text{IssueBookUI}, \text{IssueBookController}\}
$$

$$
\text{Stereotypes} = \{ \text{Book: entity}, \text{Member: entity}, \text{Loan: entity}, \text{IssueBookUI: boundary}, \text{IssueBookController: control} \}
$$

3. **Relationships (analysis class diagram):**
   - `IssueBookController` depends on `Loan`, `Book`, `Member` (control uses entities)
   - `IssueBookUI` depends on `IssueBookController` (boundary uses control)
   - `Loan` is associated with exactly one `Book` and one `Member` (multiplicity)

4. **Sequence diagram (analysis level):**
   - `Cashier → IssueBookUI: enterMemberID()`
   - `IssueBookUI → IssueBookController: issueBook(memberID, bookID)`
   - `IssueBookController → Member: getMember(memberID)`
   - `IssueBookController → Book: getBook(bookID)`
   - `IssueBookController → Loan: new Loan(member, book, today)`
   - `IssueBookController → Book: setStatus("issued")`
   - `IssueBookController → IssueBookUI: confirmIssue()`

5. **Packaging decision:** Use **Layered Packaging** because:
   - 4 use cases share many entity classes (Book, Member).
   - Use-case based packaging would create circular dependencies between Book and Member packages.
   - Layered: `boundary` (4 classes), `control` (4 classes), `entity` (3 classes). No cycles.

6. **Transition to design:** 
   - `Book` entity → `Book` POJO with `@Entity` (Hibernate) annotation.
   - `IssueBookController` → `IssueBookService` (Spring `@Service`).
   - `IssueBookUI` → `issueBook.fxml` (JavaFX).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Managing Analysis — Overall Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start Analysis Phase]) --> B0[Identify Use Cases]
    B0 --> C0[Document Use Cases]
    C0 --> D0[Identify Candidate Classes via Noun Analysis]
    D0 --> E0[Apply Stereotype Rules]
    E0 --> F0[Boundary Classes]
    E0 --> G0[Entity Classes]
    E0 --> H0[Control Classes]
    F0 --> I0[Apply GRASP Principles]
    G0 --> I0
    H0 --> I0
    I0 --> J0[Draw Use Case Realization]
    J0 --> K0[Sequence Diagram]
    J0 --> L0[Class Diagram]
    K0 --> M0[Package the Analysis Model]
    L0 --> M0
    M0 --> N0{Iteration Boundary}
    N0 -->|More Iterations Needed| C0
    N0 -->|Complete| O0[Analysis Model Stable]
    O0 --> P0[Transition to Design Phase]
    P0 --> Q0([Design Phase])
```

### 4.2 Analysis Class Stereotype Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Actors
        A1[Customer]
        A2[Cashier]
        A3[Admin]
    end
    subgraph BoundaryLayer
        B1[SaleUI]
        B2[LoginUI]
    end
    subgraph ControlLayer
        C1[ProcessSaleController]
        C2[LoginController]
    end
    subgraph EntityLayer
        E1[Sale]
        E2[Customer]
        E3[Payment]
        E4[Receipt]
        E5[Product]
    end
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    C1 --> E1
    C1 --> E3
    C1 --> E4
    C1 --> E5
    C2 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
    E1 --> E4
    E1 --> E5
```

### 4.3 Use-Case Realization Block Diagram (Process Sale Example)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Cashier as Actor: Cashier
    participant UI as Boundary: SaleUI
    participant Ctrl as Control: ProcessSaleController
    participant SaleEnt as Entity: Sale
    participant ProductEnt as Entity: Product
    participant PaymentEnt as Entity: Payment
    Cashier->>UI: enterItem(itemId)
    UI->>Ctrl: addItemToSale(itemId)
    Ctrl->>ProductEnt: getProduct(itemId)
    ProductEnt-->>Ctrl: productDetails
    Ctrl->>SaleEnt: addLineItem(product)
    Cashier->>UI: completeSale()
    UI->>Ctrl: endSale()
    Ctrl->>SaleEnt: computeTotal()
    SaleEnt-->>Ctrl: totalAmount
    Ctrl-->>UI: displayTotal(total)
    Cashier->>UI: makePayment(amount)
    UI->>Ctrl: makePayment(amount)
    Ctrl->>PaymentEnt: new Payment(amount)
    PaymentEnt-->>Ctrl: paymentRecord
    Ctrl->>SaleEnt: setStatus(paid)
    Ctrl-->>UI: printReceipt()
```

### 4.4 Packaging Strategy Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph StrategyA[Strategy A: Layered Packaging]
        A1[boundary pkg] --- A2[control pkg]
        A2 --- A3[entity pkg]
    end
    subgraph StrategyB[Strategy B: Use Case Packaging]
        B1[IssueBook pkg] --- B2[ReturnBook pkg]
        B2 --- B3[Catalog pkg]
        B1 --- B3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In Strategy A, dependencies flow one direction (boundary → control → entity). In Strategy B, use-case packages share the `Catalog` package, creating potential cycles if not managed carefully.

### 4.5 Iteration Management Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Inception
    Inception --> Elaboration1: Iteration 1
    Elaboration1 --> Elaboration2: Iteration 2
    Elaboration2 --> Construction: Architecture Baseline
    Construction --> Transition: Beta Testing
    Transition --> [*]
    note right of Elaboration1: Analysis Classes Identified
    note right of Elaboration2: Use Case Realizations Complete
    note right of Construction: Design Classes Implemented
```

### 4.6 Traceability Matrix Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Requirement REQ-001] --> UC[Use Case UC-01: Issue Book]
    UC --> AC[Analysis Class IssueBookController]
    AC --> DC[Design Class IssueBookService]
    DC --> CODE[Java Source: IssueBookService.java]
    AC -.->|trace| TEST[Test Case TC-001]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the three analysis class stereotypes defined in UML and state the role of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three UML analysis class stereotypes are:

1. **Boundary Class (`<<boundary>>`):** Models the interaction between the system and its external actors. Examples include UI windows, API endpoints, device interfaces, and communication protocols. **(1 mark)**

2. **Entity Class (`<<entity>>`):** Models long-lived, persistent information of the business domain. Examples include Customer, Order, Product, Invoice. Entity classes usually map to database tables in design. **(1 mark)**

3. **Control Class (`<<control>>`):** Encapsulates the use-case specific control logic and coordinates the flow of events for a use case. Typically, one control class is created per use case. Examples include `ProcessSaleController`, `IssueBookController`. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between analysis model and design model in OOAD.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Analysis Model** | **Design Model** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Captures **what** the system must do | Capties **how** the system will be built |
| **Abstraction** | Conceptual; technology-agnostic | Technical; technology-specific |
| **Class Stereotypes** | `<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<control>>` | POJO, Service, DAO, Controller (no stereotypes) |
| **Focus** | Functional requirements | Non-functional requirements, patterns, frameworks |
| **Audience** | End-users, business analysts | Developers, architects |
| **Time** | Inception + Elaboration phases | Elaboration + Construction phases |

(1 mark for purpose distinction, 1 mark for abstraction, 1 mark for at least 2 rows of comparison)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of Managing Analysis in OOAD. List and briefly describe the key activities involved in managing the analysis phase of an OO project.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For an "Online Bookstore System", identify the major use cases. For the use case "Place Order", perform the following analysis management activities:*
- *(i) Identify analysis classes with stereotypes (minimum 5).*
- *(ii) Draw the use-case realization as an analysis sequence diagram (textual form acceptable).*
- *(iii) Propose a packaging strategy and justify.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Managing Analysis is the disciplined organization, documentation, and control of the artifacts produced during the analysis phase. It ensures the analysis model is **complete**, **consistent**, **traceable**, and **design-ready**.

**Key Activities (5 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Documenting Use Cases:** Write fully dressed use-case descriptions with preconditions, postconditions, main flow, alternate flows.
2. **Maintaining the Glossary:** Define domain terms to ensure shared understanding.
3. **Organizing Analysis Classes into Packages:** Group related classes for manageability.
4. **Creating Use-Case Realizations:** Sequence + class diagrams for each use case.
5. **Managing Iterations:** Schedule analysis increments, review at iteration boundaries.
6. **Version Control & Traceability:** Track changes, link requirements to analysis to design.

**(b) — 7 marks:**

**Use Cases for Online Bookstore:** Browse Catalog, Search Books, Add to Cart, Place Order, Make Payment, Track Order, Register User, Manage Inventory.

**For "Place Order":**

**(i) Analysis Classes (3 marks):**

| **Class** | **Stereotype** | **Responsibility** |
|---|---|---|
| `Order` | `<<entity>>` | Represents customer's order with items, status, total |
| `Cart` | `<<entity>>` | Holds selected items before order placement |
| `Customer` | `<<entity>>` | Registered user placing the order |
| `Book` | `<<entity>>` | The book being ordered |
| `OrderUI` | `<<boundary>>` | Web form for entering shipping, confirming order |
| `PlaceOrderController` | `<<control>>` | Coordinates the order placement flow |

**(ii) Use-Case Realization — Sequence Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Customer -> OrderUI: submitOrder(cartID, shippingInfo)
OrderUI -> PlaceOrderController: placeOrder(cartID, shippingInfo)
PlaceOrderController -> Cart: getCart(cartID)
Cart --> PlaceOrderController: cartItems
PlaceOrderController -> Customer: getCustomer(customerID)
Customer --> PlaceOrderController: customerDetails
PlaceOrderController -> Order: new Order(customer, cartItems, shippingInfo)
Order --> PlaceOrderController: newOrder
PlaceOrderController -> Order: computeTotal()
Order --> PlaceOrderController: totalAmount
PlaceOrderController -> Order: setStatus("Pending")
PlaceOrderController -> OrderUI: confirmOrder(orderID)
OrderUI -> Customer: displayOrderConfirmation()
```

**(iii) Packaging Strategy (2 marks):**

**Chosen Strategy: Layered Packaging**

- `boundary` package: contains all UI classes (OrderUI, CartUI, SearchUI, etc.)
- `control` package: contains all controllers (PlaceOrderController, etc.)
- `entity` package: contains all domain entities (Order, Cart, Customer, Book, etc.)

**Justification:** Layered packaging avoids circular dependencies, supports high cohesion within layers, and is the standard for Java enterprise applications (matches Spring's layered architecture). The use-case based strategy would cause cycles because `Order`, `Cart`, and `Book` are shared across multiple use cases.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** *Discuss the importance of packaging in managing the analysis model. Compare and contrast two major packaging strategies with suitable diagrams.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Apply the GRASP Controller pattern to assign responsibilities in a "Withdraw Cash" use case of an ATM system. Identify all analysis classes, their stereotypes, and show the responsibility assignment.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

**Importance of Packaging (3 marks):**
- **Modularity:** Divides large analysis model into manageable units.
- **Reduced Coupling:** Packages minimize inter-package dependencies.
- **Improved Cohesion:** Related classes stay together.
- **Parallel Development:** Multiple analysts can work on different packages simultaneously.
- **Reuse:** Well-designed packages can be reused across projects.

**Two Strategies (4 marks — 2 each):**

**1. Use-Case Based Packaging:** One package per use case, containing all classes (boundary, control, entity) needed for that use case.
- **Pros:** Easy traceability; each package maps to one user-visible feature.
- **Cons:** Heavy duplication of entity classes (e.g., `Customer` appears in 5 packages); circular dependencies if entities are shared.

**2. Layered Packaging:** Separate packages for boundary, control, and entity classes.
- **Pros:** Eliminates duplication; supports standard Java/Spring layering; low coupling.
- **Cons:** Less traceability from package to use case.

**Diagrams:** (Textual description acceptable)

Use-Case Based:
```
[IssueBook pkg] -- [ReturnBook pkg]
[IssueBook pkg] -- [Catalog pkg]
[ReturnBook pkg] -- [Catalog pkg]   (cycle risk if Catalog uses IssueBook)
```

Layered:
```
[boundary pkg] --> [control pkg] --> [entity pkg]
(Clean one-directional dependency, no cycles)
```

**(b) — 7 marks:**

**Use Case: Withdraw Cash** — Customer inserts card, enters PIN, specifies amount, receives cash and receipt.

**Analysis Classes with Stereotypes (3 marks):**

| **Class** | **Stereotype** | **Responsibility** |
|---|---|---|
| `ATM` | `<<boundary>>` | Physical device interface (card reader, keypad, dispenser) |
| `WithdrawalUI` | `<<boundary>>` | Screen for amount entry and messages |
| `WithdrawalController` | `<<control>>` | Coordinates withdrawal flow (one per use case) |
| `Account` | `<<entity>>` | Customer's bank account with balance |
| `Transaction` | `<<entity>>` | Record of withdrawal (audit log) |
| `CashDispenser` | `<<entity>>` (or control) | Manages physical cash inventory |

**GRASP Controller Application (2 marks):**

The Controller GRASP principle says: *"Assign the responsibility for handling a system event to a controller class that represents the overall system or use case scenario."*

**Assignment:**
- The system event "Customer wants to withdraw cash" is handled by **`WithdrawalController`** (not by `ATM` or `Account`).
- `WithdrawalController` delegates to `Account.withdraw(amount)` (Information Expert) and `Transaction.record()` (Creator — Transaction is created by WithdrawalController because WithdrawalController aggregates the necessary data).
- `ATM` (boundary) captures input and calls `WithdrawalController`.
- `WithdrawalController` calls `Account` (entity) to verify balance and debit.
- `WithdrawalController` calls `Transaction` (entity) to log.
- `WithdrawalController` calls `CashDispenser` to dispense cash.

**Responsibility Distribution (2 marks):**

```
WithdrawalController. handleWithdrawal(cardInfo, amount, pin):
    account = Account.findByCard(cardInfo)
    if not account.verifyPIN(pin): return "Invalid PIN"
    if not account.hasSufficientBalance(amount): return "Insufficient Funds"
    account.debit(amount)
    transaction = new Transaction(account, amount, "WITHDRAWAL")
    cashDispenser.dispense(amount)
    return "Withdrawal Successful"
```

This satisfies:
- **High Cohesion:** WithdrawalController has a focused responsibility.
- **Low Coupling:** WithdrawalController communicates with entities via simple messages.
- **Controller:** WithdrawalController represents the use case scenario.
- **Information Expert:** Account manages its own balance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Managing Analysis" Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Analysis with Design (–2 marks):** Students write `Spring @Service` or `Hibernate @Entity` in the analysis model. **These belong to design, not analysis.** Use stereotypes `<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<control>>`.
>
> 2. **Missing Use-Case Realization (–3 marks):** A common 14-mark question expects both a **sequence diagram** AND a **class diagram** for the use case. If you draw only one, you lose half the marks for that part.
>
> 3. **Wrong Number of Control Classes (–1 mark):** Examiners expect **one control class per use case** as a rule of thumb. Creating `LoginValidatorController` AND `LoginController` for one use case is overkill.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Pre/Postconditions (–1 mark):** Use-case documentation must include preconditions (e.g., "Customer must be logged in") and postconditions (e.g., "Order is saved with status = PENDING").
>
> 5. **No Traceability (–2 marks):** Always mention that analysis classes **trace back to** use cases and **forward to** design classes. Use a traceability matrix if possible.
>
> 6. **Vague Packaging Justification (–1 mark):** Just "we use layered packaging" is not enough. You must justify **why** (e.g., "to avoid cycles because `Book` is shared by Issue and Return use cases").
>
> 7. **Forgetting Iteration Aspect (–1 mark):** Managing analysis is iterative. Mention that the model evolves through iterations and that architecture is solidified at the end of Elaboration.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Managing Analysis**

- [ ] **Definition:** Managing Analysis = organizing, documenting, controlling analysis artifacts.
- [ ] **3 Stereotypes:** `<<boundary>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<control>>` — memorize roles.
- [ ] **Rule of Thumb:** 1 control class per use case.
- [ ] **Entity classes** = long-lived domain info; map to DB tables in design.
- [ ] **Boundary classes** = system ↔ actor interface; one per actor/use case touchpoint.
- [ ] **Noun-phrase analysis** is the technique to find candidate classes.
- [ ] **Use-Case Realization** = sequence diagram + class diagram per use case.
- [ ] **GRASP Principles** guide responsibility assignment (Controller, Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion).
- [ ] **Packaging Strategies:** Layered vs. Use-Case Based vs. Domain.
- [ ] **Layered Packaging** is preferred for Java enterprise systems; it avoids cycles.
- [ ] **Iteration Management:** Analysis is iterative; architecture is solidified at end of Elaboration.
- [ ] **Traceability Matrix** is mandatory for large projects: Requirement → Use Case → Analysis Class → Design Class → Code → Test.
- [ ] **Transition to Design:** Analysis classes evolve into design classes; technology decisions (Spring, JavaFX, Hibernate) are added.
- [ ] **Documentation Standards:** IEEE 830, use-case template (Cockburn), UML 2.5 notation.
- [ ] **Architecture Description at Analysis Level:** Captures layers, packages, architecturally significant use cases.
- [ ] **Deliverables of Managing Analysis:** Use-Case Model, Analysis Model, Supplementary Specification, Glossary, Architecture Document (analysis-level).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** For 14-mark questions on this topic, always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Activities → (3) Class identification with stereotypes → (4) Use-case realization → (5) Packaging strategy with justification → (6) Transition to design notes.** This structure matches the KTU valuation key and maximizes your score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decomposing the System - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decomposing the System - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Syllabus Definition (PEITT411 – Module 3)**
> **System Decomposition** is the process of breaking down a complex software system into a hierarchy of smaller, loosely-coupled, and highly-cohesive sub-systems, components, and objects, each with well-defined responsibilities and interfaces. It forms the foundational analytical step in Object-Oriented Design (OOD) before classes, methods, and relationships are formalized in code.

In the simplest possible terms: **Decomposition is the art of "cutting a big problem into small, solvable pieces."** Before we can build software, we must first understand *what* we are building — and the only practical way to do that for a non-trivial system is to break it apart.

### Conceptual Analogy: Building a House 🏠
Imagine you are asked to *"build a house."* You cannot hand a single brick to a customer and call it done. Instead, you decompose the problem:

- **Level 1 (System):** The complete house.
- **Level 2 (Sub-systems):** Foundation, Structure, Electrical, Plumbing, Interior.
- **Level 3 (Components / Objects):** Each room, each wire, each pipe.
- **Level 4 (Responsibilities):** A specific door *opens*; a specific light *switches on*.

Each piece is small enough to be designed, built, and tested independently. **This is exactly what system decomposition does for software.**

### Why Decomposition Matters in OOD
| Reason | Plain-English Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Manageability** | Humans can only hold ~7 items in working memory at once (Miller's Law); decomposition respects this cognitive limit. |
| **Reusability** | Small, focused components (objects) can be reused across projects. |
| **Maintainability** | A bug in a *Login* module is easier to fix than one scattered across a monolithic codebase. |
| **Parallelism** | Different teams can work on different decomposed sub-systems simultaneously. |
| **Testability** | Small units can be unit-tested in isolation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Decomposition is **not the same as coding**. It is a *design-time* activity that produces logical/architectural artifacts (sub-system diagrams, class candidates, responsibility lists) which then *guide* coding. Examiners often award 2–3 marks simply for correctly identifying *why* decomposition precedes class design.

### Two Flavors of Decomposition
1. **Functional (Algorithmic) Decomposition** — Breaks the system by *what it does* (functions, processes). Used in structured/procedural design (C, Pascal).
2. **Object-Oriented Decomposition** — Breaks the system by *what it knows and does* (objects combining data + behavior). Used in OOD with Java, C++, etc.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Trick:** Functional = *verbs* (`calculateTax`, `printReport`). Object-Oriented = *nouns* (`Customer`, `Order`, `Invoice`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Decomposition Hierarchy (KTU's Favourite Diagram)

Object-oriented decomposition produces a **layered tree of abstractions**. The deeper you go, the more concrete the elements become.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Layers of Decomposition (Memorize for KTU)**
> 1. **System Layer** — The entire application (e.g., *Online Banking System*).
> 2. **Sub-system Layer** — Major logical divisions (e.g., *Account Management*, *Transaction Processing*, *User Authentication*).
> 3. **Class / Object Layer** — Blueprints of entities (e.g., `Account`, `Transaction`, `Customer`).
> 4. **Method / Responsibility Layer** — Individual behaviors (e.g., `deposit()`, `withdraw()`, `validatePin()`).

### 2.2 Core Principles Governing Decomposition

Every KTU textbook highlights **two opposing forces** that govern how *good* a decomposition is:

#### A. Cohesion (The "Glue" Inside a Module)
**Definition:** The degree to which the responsibilities inside a single module are *strongly related* and *focused* on a single purpose.

- **High cohesion** = the module does **one thing**, and does it well. ✅ *Good.*
- **Low cohesion** = the module does many unrelated things. ❌ *Bad.*

**Intuition:** A *Swiss Army knife* has low cohesion (knife, scissors, file, screwdriver all crammed in). A *chef's knife* has high cohesion (only cuts, but cuts excellently).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cognitive Hook:** "Aim for **high** cohesion; if you cannot describe a class in one sentence *without using 'and' or 'or'*, split it."

#### B. Coupling (The "Wires" Between Modules)
**Definition:** The degree of *interdependence* between two modules. How much one module knows about, or relies upon, the internals of another.

- **Low coupling** = modules talk through small, clean interfaces. ✅ *Good.*
- **High coupling** = modules know each other's private details. ❌ *Bad.*

**Intuition:** Two roommates (low coupling) only need to know *what time* the other wakes up. A conjoined twin (high coupling) shares everything — even pain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Decomposition:**
> **MAXIMIZE Cohesion + MINIMIZE Coupling** = "Strong insides, weak connections." This single sentence earns full marks in any KTU design question.

### 2.3 Types of Coupling (Worst → Best)

| Rank | Coupling Type | Description | KTU Verdict |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 (worst) | **Content** | One module directly modifies another's internal data | Avoid completely |
| 2 | **Common / Global** | Multiple modules share global variables | Avoid |
| 3 | **Control** | One module passes a *flag* that controls another's logic | Reduce |
| 4 | **Stamp** | Passes a whole data structure when only part is needed | Reduce |
| 5 | **Data** | Passes only primitive parameters (the cleanest) | ✅ Ideal |
| 6 (best) | **Message / Call** | Uses only an object's public interface (no globals, no struct passing) | ✅ Ideal for OOD |

### 2.4 Types of Cohesion (Worst → Best)

| Rank | Cohesion Type | Description | KTU Verdict |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 (worst) | **Coincidental** | Random, unrelated tasks grouped together | Refactor immediately |
| 2 | **Logical** | Similar-looking tasks grouped (e.g., all "I/O" routines) | Weak |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Tasks done at the same time grouped (e.g., `init()`) | Acceptable |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Tasks must be done in a specific order | OK |
| 5 | **Communicational** | Tasks use the *same* data | Good |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one task is input of the next | Strong |
| 7 (best) | **Functional** | All tasks contribute to *one single, well-defined function* | ✅ Ideal |

### 2.5 Strategies / Approaches to Decomposition

| Strategy | How It Works | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Top-Down** | Start from the system, recursively split into sub-systems until classes appear. | Green-field projects, clear requirements. |
| **Bottom-Up** | Start by identifying reusable classes/objects, then group them into sub-systems. | Reuse-heavy projects, libraries, frameworks. |
| **Use-Case Driven** | Use UML use-cases to discover actors → objects → classes. | KTU's preferred approach (OOAD by Bruegge & Hott is KTU reference style). |
| **Domain-Driven (DDD)** | Decompose by *bounded contexts* of the business domain. | Enterprise Java systems (Spring, microservices). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to decompose a system, **always justify your approach** by stating the strategy (e.g., *"I am using a use-case driven top-down approach because..."*). This alone often fetches 1–2 extra marks.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive Software:** An ECU is decomposed into sub-systems (engine control, braking, infotainment) with strict decoupling for safety.
- **Banking:** A core banking platform is decomposed into Account, Ledger, and Reporting sub-systems — each owned by a different team.
- **Java Reflection & Spring Beans:** Spring's `@Service`, `@Repository` annotations *enforce* low coupling at runtime by wiring components declaratively.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the *procedural logic* an examiner expects when you are asked: *"Decompose the given system into sub-systems and identify classes."*

### 3.1 The 5-Step Decomposition Procedure (Algorithm of Thought)

Given a problem statement, the *formal sequence* is:

**Step 1 — Identify the System Boundary**
Determine *what is in-scope* and *what is out-of-scope* for the system.

**Step 2 — Identify Actors and Use-Cases**
List external entities (actors) and the meaningful interactions (use-cases) they perform.

**Step 3 — Group Use-Cases into Sub-Systems**
Cluster related use-cases into *cohesive* sub-systems.

**Step 4 — Identify Candidate Classes (CRC Cards)**
For each sub-system, use **Class-Responsibility-Collaborator (CRC)** analysis:
- *Class* — A noun representing an entity.
- *Responsibility* — What it *knows* and *does*.
- *Collaborator* — Other classes it interacts with.

**Step 5 — Refactor for High Cohesion & Low Coupling**
Apply the rules in §2.3 and §2.4. If a class has more than ~5–7 responsibilities, split it.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: "Library Management System"

Let us formally apply the algorithm.

#### Step 1 — System Boundary
> The system automates book lending, returns, catalog search, and member management. *Out of scope:* book acquisition, fines payment gateway.

#### Step 2 — Actors and Use-Cases
- **Actors:** `Member`, `Librarian`, `System Administrator`.
- **Use-Cases:**
  - `Search Catalog`
  - `Issue Book`
  - `Return Book`
  - `Register Member`
  - `Generate Reports`

#### Step 3 — Sub-System Grouping (Decomposition Output)

$$
\text{Library System} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Sub-system 1: Catalog Management} \\
\text{Sub-system 2: Circulation Management} \\
\text{Sub-system 3: Member Management} \\
\text{Sub-system 4: Reporting}
\end{cases}
$$

#### Step 4 — CRC Card Sample (Catalog Management)

| Class | Responsibilities | Collaborators |
|---|---|---|
| `Book` | Knows title, author, ISBN, availability status | `Catalog` |
| `Catalog` | Stores and searches books | `Book`, `SearchService` |
| `SearchService` | Executes search queries | `Catalog` |

#### Step 5 — Cohesion / Coupling Evaluation

We apply a *qualitative metric* often seen in KTU questions:

$$
Q_{design} = \alpha \cdot \text{Cohesion} - \beta \cdot \text{Coupling}
$$

where $\alpha, \beta$ are positive weighting constants. The goal is to **maximize** $Q_{design}$.

- For our Library example: `Book` has **functional** cohesion (knows only book data + status) → cohesion score = 7/7.
- `Catalog` ↔ `SearchService` communicate *only* through method calls (no globals) → coupling score = data/message (rank 5–6).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix (Use this exact 4-column format in your KTU answer):**

| Sub-system | Classes | Primary Responsibility | Coupling to Others |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catalog Management | `Book`, `Catalog`, `SearchService` | Manage book inventory & search | Low (uses Book objects only) |
| Circulation Management | `Loan`, `Transaction`, `ReturnHandler` | Issue / return books | Medium (talks to Catalog, Member) |
| Member Management | `Member`, `MembershipCard`, `AuthService` | Register and authenticate members | Low (talking to itself) |
| Reporting | `ReportGenerator`, `StatsEngine` | Generate usage & fine reports | Medium (reads from others via interfaces) |

### 3.3 Decision Logic — When to *Stop* Decomposing

A common KTU pitfall is *over-decomposition* (creating too many tiny classes). The stopping rule is:

$$
\text{Stop when } \frac{\Delta \text{Cohesion}}{\Delta \text{Classes}} < \epsilon
$$

In plain English: stop splitting when the new classes no longer meaningfully increase cohesion — i.e., they become artificially small.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Decomposition of an "Online Shopping System"

This Mermaid block diagram captures the hierarchical decomposition a KTU examiner expects to see in any OOD question worth 7+ marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysRoot[Shopping System]
    sysRoot --> subA[Catalog Sub-System]
    sysRoot --> subB[Order Sub-System]
    sysRoot --> subC[Payment Sub-System]
    sysRoot --> subD[User Sub-System]
    sysRoot --> subE[Shipping Sub-System]

    subA --> clsA1[Product Class]
    subA --> clsA2[Category Class]
    subA --> clsA3[Inventory Class]

    subB --> clsB1[Order Class]
    subB --> clsB2[OrderItem Class]
    subB --> clsB3[Cart Class]

    subC --> clsC1[Payment Class]
    subC --> clsC2[Invoice Class]
    subC --> clsC3[RefundService Class]

    subD --> clsD1[Customer Class]
    subD --> clsD2[AuthService Class]
    subD --> clsD3[Profile Class]

    subE --> clsE1[Shipment Class]
    subE --> clsE2[Address Class]
    subE --> clsE3[TrackingService Class]

    clsB1 -. talks to .-> clsA1
    clsC1 -. talks to .-> clsB1
    clsD2 -. talks to .-> clsB1
    clsE1 -. talks to .-> clsB1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in a KTU answer:**
> - **Solid arrows (`-->`)** represent *decomposition / "part-of"* relationships.
> - **Dotted arrows (`-.->`)** represent *runtime collaboration / coupling* between classes in different sub-systems.
> - Notice how **dotted arrows always cross sub-system boundaries cautiously** — this visualizes the **"low coupling"** goal.

### 4.2 Sub-System Dependency Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SM1[Sub-System: User]
        D1[Customer]
        D2[AuthService]
    end
    subgraph SM2[Sub-System: Order]
        B1[Order]
        B2[Cart]
    end
    subgraph SM3[Sub-System: Catalog]
        A1[Product]
    end
    subgraph SM4[Sub-System: Payment]
        C1[Payment]
    end
    D2 -->|authenticates| B1
    B1 -->|reads products| A1
    B1 -->|initiates| C1
```

This second view emphasizes **how the coupling forms a directional acyclic graph** — the *cleanest* kind of dependency structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define system decomposition in object-oriented design. Mention any two benefits.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
System decomposition is the process of breaking a complex software system into a hierarchy of smaller, manageable, and loosely-coupled sub-systems and objects, each with well-defined responsibilities.
- **Benefit 1 — Manageability:** Large systems become easier to understand, design, and maintain.
- **Benefit 2 — Reusability:** Well-decomposed components (objects) can be reused across multiple projects, reducing development time and cost.
*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two benefits.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between functional decomposition and object-oriented decomposition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Functional Decomposition | Object-Oriented Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Breaks down by *functions / processes* (verbs) | Breaks down by *entities / objects* (nouns) |
| Data & Behavior | Kept separate | Bundled together inside objects (encapsulation) |
| Reusability | Functions are hard to reuse across programs | Objects are highly reusable |
| Example | `calculateTax()`, `printBill()` | `Customer`, `Invoice`, `Order` |
| Suitable for | Procedural languages (C, Pascal) | OO languages (Java, C++) |

*(1½ marks for any two differences + 1½ for remaining points.)*

---

### 📕 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For every "decompose the system" question, students commonly lose marks in these four ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to name the sub-systems** — they only list classes and skip the *middle* layer.
> 2. **Not justifying cohesion/coupling** — stating *what* you decomposed is not enough; you must defend *why* it is good decomposition.
> 3. **Confusing decomposition with class diagram** — decomposition is an *earlier* activity; do not jump straight to UML arrows.
> 4. **Ignoring actors and use-cases** — KTU expects use-case-driven decomposition as a *first step*.

---

### 🅰️ Question A — 14 Marks *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

> *"Design and decompose an **Online Examination System** used by a university. Identify sub-systems, list at least three classes per sub-system with their responsibilities, and evaluate the design using cohesion and coupling principles."*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Decomposition & Class Identification**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Actors & Use-Cases:**
- Actors: `Student`, `Examiner`, `Administrator`.
- Use-Cases: `Register for Exam`, `Take Exam`, `Evaluate Answers`, `Publish Results`, `Manage Question Bank`.

**Step 2 — Sub-Systems (with classes):**

| Sub-system | Classes | Key Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **User Management** | `Student`, `Examiner`, `Admin`, `AuthService` | Login, registration, role assignment |
| **Question Bank** | `Question`, `QuestionPaper`, `Subject`, `Option` | Store and retrieve questions |
| **Examination Engine** | `ExamSession`, `Timer`, `ResponseSheet`, `NavigationController` | Conduct the exam interactively |
| **Evaluation & Results** | `Evaluator`, `Result`, `GradeCalculator`, `ReportGenerator` | Auto/manual evaluation, generate report cards |

*(5 marks for sub-system identification + 2 marks for class listings.)*

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Cohesion / Coupling Evaluation**

**Model Solution:**

- The `Question` class exhibits **functional cohesion** (only stores question data + option list) — highest grade.
- The `ExamSession` class has **sequential cohesion** (load question → start timer → accept answer → save response) — second-best.
- The `AuthService` is **loosely coupled** to `ExamSession`: it only receives a verified `Student` object, no global state, no flags — **data coupling** (best type).
- The `Evaluator` ↔ `Question` interaction uses **message coupling** (calls `getCorrectAnswer()` interface).
- **Justification:** The decomposition satisfies the golden rule — *high cohesion, low coupling* — and the dependency graph between sub-systems is **acyclic**, confirming a clean design.

*(2 marks cohesion analysis + 2 marks coupling analysis + 2 marks for stating the golden rule + overall justification = 7 marks.)*

---

### 🅱️ Question B — 14 Marks *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

> *"Consider an **ATM (Automated Teller Machine) System**. Perform a top-down decomposition: list the sub-systems, propose candidate classes with responsibilities (use CRC technique), and draw a high-level block diagram showing sub-system interactions."*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Top-Down Decomposition + CRC**

**Model Solution:**

**Layer 1 — System:** ATM System.
**Layer 2 — Sub-systems:** (i) Authentication, (ii) Transaction, (iii) Cash Dispensing, (iv) Receipt & Logging, (v) Network/Bank Communication.

**Layer 3 — CRC Cards (sample):**

| Class | Responsibility | Collaborator |
|---|---|---|
| `CardReader` | Reads card data, sends to Auth | `Authentication` |
| `PinValidator` | Verifies PIN | `CardReader`, `Customer` |
| `Account` | Holds balance, supports debit/credit | `Transaction` |
| `Transaction` | Encapsulates a withdraw/deposit/balance-inquiry operation | `Account`, `CashDispenser` |
| `CashDispenser` | Physically dispenses cash; tracks denominations | `Transaction` |
| `ReceiptPrinter` | Prints transaction receipt | `Transaction` |
| `BankGateway` | Communicates with the bank server | `Transaction`, `AuthService` |

*(3 marks for sub-system layers + 4 marks for at least 4 CRC cards with all 3 columns populated.)*

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Block Diagram & Justification**

**Model Solution (textual block diagram):**

```
                ┌──────────────────────┐
                │      ATM SYSTEM      │
                └──────────┬───────────┘
                           │
        ┌──────────┬───────┼───────┬──────────┐
        ▼          ▼       ▼       ▼          ▼
   Authentication  Tx     Cash   Receipt   Network
   (PinValidator)  Engine Dispnsr Printer  Gateway
        │          │       │       │          │
        └──────────┴───────┴───────┴──────────┘
                Shared Domain Objects:
            (Account, CardReader, Customer)
```

- All sub-systems communicate via shared *domain objects* (e.g., `Account`) and **interfaces** — this represents **data + message coupling** (ranks 5 and 6).
- The `CashDispenser` is *completely decoupled* from `PinValidator`; they do not even know each other exists → **ideal low coupling**.
- Each sub-system has **one focused goal** (auth, dispense, log, etc.) → **functional cohesion**.

*(3 marks block diagram + 2 marks cohesion + 2 marks coupling = 7 marks.)*

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU**

- [x] **Decomposition** = breaking a system into sub-systems, classes, and responsibilities.
- [x] It is a **design-time** activity that *precedes* class diagram creation.
- [x] **Two main flavors:** Functional (verbs) vs Object-Oriented (nouns).
- [x] **Four layers:** System → Sub-system → Class → Method/Responsibility.
- [x] **Golden Rule:** *Maximize cohesion, minimize coupling.*
- [x] **Best cohesion** = Functional. **Worst** = Coincidental.
- [x] **Best coupling** = Data/Message. **Worst** = Content.
- [x] **Strategies:** Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Use-Case Driven (KTU favourite), Domain-Driven.
- [x] **CRC technique** = Class – Responsibility – Collaborator (used to identify classes).
- [x] **Stopping rule:** Stop decomposing when further splitting no longer improves cohesion.
- [x] **Always** mention actors and use-cases before listing sub-systems (use-case-driven approach).
- [x] **Always** draw a block diagram of sub-systems with labelled interactions.
- [x] **Always** justify your design by referring to cohesion and coupling types explicitly.
- [x] **Avoid over-decomposition** — a class with one trivial method is not a good design.
- [x] **Watch out** for the 4 common valuation pitfalls listed in the warning callout above.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 3 at a Glance — The "Reusability & Flexibility" Pillar

**Module 3** of *Object Oriented Design using Java (PEITT411)* is the conceptual heart of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP). While **Module 1** introduced the philosophy of OOP and **Module 2** dealt with the basic building block — the **class** and the **object** — Module 3 elevates the design from a collection of isolated blueprints to a **living, hierarchical, and polymorphic system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> Module 3 covers the advanced OO mechanisms that enable **code reuse, dynamic behavior, and contractual design** in Java. The four pillars addressed are:
> 1. **Inheritance** — *is-a* hierarchical relationship.
> 2. **Polymorphism** — *one interface, many implementations*.
> 3. **Abstract Classes & Interfaces** — *contractual and partial implementation blueprints*.
> 4. **Packages** — *logical grouping and access-controlled namespaces*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Manufacturing Plant"

Imagine a large automobile company:

- **Classes (Module 2)** are the **blueprints** for individual car models (e.g., a Sedan blueprint, an SUV blueprint).
- **Inheritance** is the engineering practice of creating a **new SUV blueprint from an existing Car blueprint**, inheriting the engine, wheels, and chassis design, and then *adding* off-road features. The new SUV blueprint **is a** Car, but with extra capabilities.
- **Polymorphism** is the universal **"startEngine()" button** on the dashboard. Whether you are sitting in a Sedan, an SUV, or a Truck, the button looks identical, but pressing it triggers a *different internal mechanism* depending on the actual vehicle type behind it.
- **Abstract Classes** are **incomplete prototype blueprints** — for example, a "Generic EV Blueprint" that defines *what* every electric vehicle must have (battery, motor) but leaves the *exact* battery chemistry for subclasses to decide.
- **Interfaces** are **certification contracts** — a "SafetyCertified" badge that any vehicle (Car, Bike, Truck) can earn by promising to implement certain methods (e.g., `applyBrakes()`), regardless of their inheritance lineage.
- **Packages** are the **factory departments** (Engine Department, Body Department, Electronics Department) — each keeps related blueprints together and controls who can access proprietary designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Module 3 Matters in KTU Exams:**
> Module 3 carries the **highest weightage** in both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** internal assessments and the **End Semester Examination (ESE)**. Expect at least one full 14-mark question and a guaranteed Part-A (3-mark) question on inheritance, polymorphism, or interfaces.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars — One-Line Summary

| Pillar | Core Keyword | Exam Buzzword |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | `extends` | "is-a" relationship |
| Polymorphism | Dynamic dispatch | One interface, many forms |
| Abstraction | `abstract` / `interface` | Contract enforcement |
| Packages | `package` / `import` | Namespace management |

## 1.4 Prerequisites Checklist (Revise Before Proceeding)

Before diving into Module 3's depth, ensure mastery of:
- **Class & Object creation** with `new` keyword
- **Encapsulation** via `private` fields and `public` getters/setters
- **Constructors** (default, parameterized, copy)
- **`this` keyword** for instance reference
- **Access modifiers**: `private`, default (package-private), `protected`, `public`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> A common mistake students make in Module 3 questions is confusing **"is-a"** (inheritance) with **"has-a"** (composition/aggregation). If a class *contains* a reference to another class, it is **NOT** inheritance. A `Car` *has-an* `Engine`, but a `SportsCar` *is-a* `Car`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inheritance — The "is-a" Reusability Engine

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition
**Inheritance** is the OOP mechanism by which a new class (**subclass / derived / child / extended class**) is created from an existing class (**superclass / base / parent class**), inheriting its **accessible** members (fields and methods) and optionally **overriding** methods to provide specialized behavior.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Declaration** — The subclass uses the `extends` keyword in its class header.
2. **Member Inheritance** — All `public` and `protected` members of the superclass are inherited. `private` members are **not directly accessible** but exist in the object.
3. **Constructor Chaining** — The subclass constructor **must** invoke a superclass constructor (explicitly via `super(...)` or implicitly as the first line).
4. **Method Overriding** — The subclass can redefine an inherited method with the **same signature** (name + parameter list) and a covariant return type.
5. **Type Compatibility** — A subclass object **is-a** superclass object (upcasting is implicit and safe).
6. **Access Augmentation** — Access level in override **cannot be reduced** (e.g., `public` cannot become `protected`).

### 2.1.3 Types of Inheritance Supported in Java

| Type | Java Support | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single | ✅ Yes | `class B extends A` |
