| Multilevel | ✅ Yes | `class C extends B extends A` |
| Hierarchical | ✅ Yes | `class B extends A`, `class C extends A` |
| Multiple (class) | ❌ No | Diamond problem; resolved via interfaces |
| Hybrid | ⚠️ Partial | Mix of class & multiple interface inheritance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Diamond Problem:**
> Java **disallows multiple class inheritance** because if class `C` extends both `A` and `B`, and both define `display()`, the compiler cannot decide which `display()` `C` inherits. Java solves this by allowing **multiple interface inheritance** (since Java 8, default methods can cause conflict, but must be explicitly disambiguated).

### 2.1.4 `super` Keyword — Three Critical Uses

| Use | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Call superclass constructor | `super(args)` | Must be first line of subclass constructor |
| Access superclass field | `super.fieldName` | Resolve name shadowing |
| Invoke superclass method | `super.methodName()` | Explicitly call overridden parent version |

## 2.2 Polymorphism — "One Interface, Many Implementations"

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition
**Polymorphism** (Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a single interface (method call, reference type) to invoke **different underlying behaviors** depending on the actual object type at runtime.

### 2.2.2 Two Sub-Types in Java

**A. Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding / Early Binding)**
- Achieved via **Method Overloading**.
- Compiler resolves the call at compile time based on method signature (name + parameter types).
- **No inheritance required** — can exist within a single class.

**B. Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding / Late Binding)**
- Achieved via **Method Overriding** + **Upcasting** + **Dynamic Method Dispatch**.
- JVM resolves the call at runtime by inspecting the **actual object type** in memory, not the reference type.
- **Requires inheritance hierarchy**.

### 2.2.3 Dynamic Method Dispatch — The Engine

**Definition:** The mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at **runtime** according to the actual type of the object referred to by the superclass reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Golden Rule for Polymorphism Questions:**
> *"Reference type decides WHAT can be called. Object type decides WHICH version executes."*
> - A `Vehicle` reference pointing to a `Car` object can call only methods declared in `Vehicle` (or inherited).
> - If `start()` is overridden in `Car`, the **`Car` version** of `start()` runs.

### 2.2.4 The `final` Keyword & Polymorphism Interaction

| Modifier | On Method | On Class | On Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| `final` | Cannot be overridden | Cannot be subclassed | Constant (cannot reassign) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** `final` and `finally` and `finalize()` are **three completely different things**. KTU loves to test this distinction in Part-A questions.

## 2.3 Abstract Classes & Interfaces — The Contract Designers

### 2.3.1 Abstract Classes

**Definition:** A class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated** and may contain **abstract methods** (methods with no body). Used to capture common behavior while forcing subclasses to provide specific implementations.

**Rules:**
- Declared with `abstract` keyword.
- May have **zero, one, or many** abstract methods.
- May have **concrete methods, fields, constructors**.
- A subclass must override **all** abstract methods or itself be declared `abstract`.
- Can extend **only one** abstract (or concrete) class.

### 2.3.2 Interfaces

**Definition:** A pure abstract type (since Java 8, it can also have `default` and `static` methods) that defines a **contract** of method signatures that implementing classes must fulfill.

**Rules:**
- Declared with `interface` keyword.
- All methods are **implicitly `public abstract`** (pre-Java 8).
- All fields are **implicitly `public static final`** (constants).
- A class uses `implements` to adopt an interface.
- A class can implement **multiple** interfaces (Java's answer to multiple inheritance).
- Since **Java 8**: `default` methods (with body) and `static` methods are allowed.
- Since **Java 9**: `private` methods are allowed.

### 2.3.3 Abstract Class vs Interface — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + Default + Static |
| Fields | Any (instance/static/final) | Only `public static final` |
| Constructors | Yes | No |
| Access Modifiers | Any | Implicitly `public` |
| Inheritance | `extends` (one only) | `implements` (many) |
| When to Use | Shared code + partial contract | Pure contract definition |

## 2.4 Packages — The Namespace Managers

### 2.4.1 Formal Definition
A **package** in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces. Conceptually similar to folders in a file system, packages provide:
1. **Logical grouping** of related types.
2. **Access protection** (package-private visibility).
3. **Name conflict resolution**.

### 2.4.2 Package Declaration & Import

- **Declaration:** First statement in a Java file: `package com.ktu.department;`
- **Built-in packages:** `java.lang` (auto-imported), `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`, `java.awt`, `javax.swing`.
- **Import variants:**
  - `import java.util.ArrayList;` — imports single class.
  - `import java.util.*;` — imports all classes in the package.
  - `import static java.lang.Math.PI;` — static import (since Java 5).
- **Fully Qualified Name:** Can be used without import: `java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in);`

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Exam Trigger Word |
|---|---|---|
| Single Inheritance | `class Child extends Parent` | "Create a derived class" |
| Method Override | Same signature in child | "Specialize the behavior" |
| Method Overload | Same name, different params | "Compile-time polymorphism" |
| Abstract Method | `abstract void draw();` | "Force subclass to implement" |
| Interface | `interface Drawable { void draw(); }` | "Define a contract" |
| Implements | `class Circle implements Drawable` | "Multiple contract adoption" |
| `super()` call | First line of constructor | "Chain to parent constructor" |
| Dynamic Dispatch | `Parent p = new Child(); p.method();` | "What is printed?" |
| Package | `package com.ktu.mypkg;` | "Group related classes" |
| Access Levels | `private < default < protected < public` | "Scope of members" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These four pillars are not academic curiosities — they power **every modern Java framework**. Spring Boot uses **interfaces and DI (Dependency Injection)** for loose coupling. JavaFX uses **inheritance and polymorphism** for UI component trees. Apache Maven organizes jars into **packages**. Mastering Module 3 = mastering real-world Java.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for "Which OOP Feature to Use?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is the **single most important analytical tool** for Module 3. KTU examiners expect students to *justify* design choices.

**Step 1 — Identify the Relationship:**
- If new class **is a specialized version** of existing class → **Inheritance**
- If new class **has a** component of existing class → **Composition**

**Step 2 — Identify the Behavior Pattern:**
- If common code exists **AND** some methods must be specialized → **Abstract Class**
- If only a contract is needed (no shared code) → **Interface**

**Step 3 — Identify the Binding Requirement:**
- If method to call is determined at **compile time** → **Overloading**
- If method to call is determined at **runtime** based on object → **Overriding + Upcasting**

**Step 4 — Identify the Namespace Need:**
- If classes are logically related and need grouping → **Package**

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Method Dispatch Resolution

Consider a classic KTU problem setup:

```java
class Animal {
    void speak() { System.out.println("Animal speaks"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void speak() { System.out.println("Dog barks"); }
}
class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void speak() { System.out.println("Cat meows"); }
}
```

**Step 1 — Reference Creation:**

$$ \text{Animal } a = \text{new Dog}(); $$

*Conversion logic:* The compiler allows this because `Dog` **is-a** `Animal` (upcasting is implicit and safe). The reference variable `a` is of compile-time type `Animal`, but the object it points to in the heap is of runtime type `Dog`.

**Step 2 — Method Call:**

$$ a.speak() \rightarrow \text{?} $$

*Conversion logic:* Compiler checks: *"Is `speak()` declared in `Animal`?"* → **Yes**. So the call is **type-checked successfully** at compile time. The compiler emits an `invokevirtual` bytecode instruction.

**Step 3 — Runtime Resolution by JVM:**

$$ \text{JVM inspects object header} \rightarrow \text{Actual class} = Dog $$

*Conversion logic:* The JVM walks the object's class table, finds `Dog.speak()` overrides `Animal.speak()`, and dispatches to the `Dog` version. The output is:

$$ \text{Output: } \texttt{Dog barks} $$

## 3.3 Derivation: Constructor Chaining Order in Multilevel Inheritance

Consider:

```java
class A {
    A() { System.out.println("A"); }
}
class B extends A {
    B() { System.out.println("B"); }
}
class C extends B {
    C() { System.out.println("C"); }
}
// new C();
```

**Step 1 — `new C()` is invoked.**

The JVM allocates memory and calls `C()`.

**Step 2 — Implicit `super()` execution.**

Since `C()` does not explicitly call `super()`, the compiler inserts `super();` as the first line. This calls `B()`.

**Step 3 — Chained `super()` call.**

`B()` has no explicit `super()`, so compiler inserts `super();` calling `A()`.

**Step 4 — Topmost constructor runs first.**

`A()` prints `A`, then returns to `B()` which prints `B`, then returns to `C()` which prints `C`.

**Final Output Order:**

$$ \text{Output sequence} = A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C $$

*Conversion logic:* Construction always proceeds **from the root of the hierarchy downward**. Destruction (in languages with destructors) would proceed in the **reverse** order. Java has no destructors, but the same pattern applies to `finally` blocks during exception unwinding.

## 3.4 Derivation: Method Overloading Resolution Algorithm

When the compiler encounters an overloaded call `obj.method(5, 10.5)`, it uses the **Most Specific Method Selection** algorithm in three phases:

**Phase 1 — Exact Match Search:**
$$ \text{Search for method with signature matching exactly } (int, double) $$

If found, use it. If not, proceed.

**Phase 2 — Widening Primitive Conversion:**
$$ \text{Attempt: } (int, double) \rightarrow (long, double) \rightarrow (double, double) \rightarrow (\text{Object}, \text{Object}) $$

The compiler picks the **closest** match in the widening hierarchy:

$$
\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

**Phase 3 — Autoboxing and Varargs:**

$$ (int, double) \rightarrow (Integer, Double) \rightarrow (int\ldots, double\ldots) $$

Autoboxing has **lower priority** than widening; varargs is the **last resort**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Phase priority order in Java overload resolution:**
> 1. Exact match
> 2. Widening primitive conversion
> 3. Autoboxing / Unboxing
> 4. Varargs
>
> Mixing phases (e.g., autoboxing + widening) is **not allowed** and causes compile error.

## 3.5 Analytical Table — `instanceof` Operator Behavior

The `instanceof` operator checks whether an object is an instance of a specific class or interface. Since Java 16, **pattern matching for instanceof** is standard:

```java
if (obj instanceof Dog d) {
    d.fetch();  // d is auto-cast and available
}
```

| Expression | Returns `true` if... |
|---|---|
| `dog instanceof Animal` | `dog` is `Dog`, `Puppy`, or any `Dog` subclass |
| `dog instanceof Dog` | `dog` is `Dog` or subclass of `Dog` |
| `null instanceof X` | Always `false` (compile-time) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** `instanceof` returns `false` for `null` checks. This is a frequently tested edge case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
      +String brand
      +int year
      +start() void
      +stop() void
    }
    class Car {
      +int numDoors
      +honk() void
    }
    class SportsCar {
      +int topSpeed
      +turboBoost() void
    }
    class ElectricCar {
      +int batteryCapacity
      +charge() void
    }
    Vehicle <|-- Car
    Car <|-- SportsCar
    Car <|-- ElectricCar
```

*Conversion logic:* Solid arrows with hollow triangles point from child to parent, representing the `extends` relationship. `SportsCar` and `ElectricCar` both ultimately inherit from `Vehicle` via `Car`.

## 4.2 Polymorphism Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Code: Parent p = new Child()"] --> B["Compile Time Check"]
    B --> C{"Is method declared in Parent?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Bytecode: invokevirtual p.method()"]
    C -->|"No"| E["COMPILE ERROR"]
    D --> F["Runtime: JVM reads object header"]
    F --> G{"Object's actual class is Child?"}
    G -->|"Yes"| H["Invoke Child's overridden method"]
    G -->|"No, check parent"| I["Walk up hierarchy"]
    I --> H
    H --> J["Execute and return"]
```

*Conversion logic:* The flowchart shows the two-stage binding of polymorphic calls — compile-time **type checking** and runtime **method dispatch**.

## 4.3 Abstract Class vs Interface — Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ABSTRACT["Abstract Class Layer"]
        A1["abstract class Shape"]
        A2["abstract double area()"]
        A3["void display() - concrete"]
    end
    subgraph INTERFACE["Interface Layer"]
        I1["interface Drawable"]
        I2["void draw() - implicit public abstract"]
        I3["default void erase() - Java 8+"]
    end
    subgraph CONCRETE["Concrete Implementation"]
        C1["class Circle extends Shape implements Drawable"]
        C2["Must override area() from Shape"]
        C3["Must override draw() from Drawable"]
    end
    ABSTRACT --> CONCRETE
    INTERFACE --> CONCRETE
```

*Conversion logic:* A concrete class can simultaneously inherit from **one** abstract class and implement **multiple** interfaces, combining both contract enforcement and shared code.

## 4.4 Package and Access Modifier Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Package: com.ktu.banking"] --> C1["class Account"]
    P1 --> C2["class SavingsAccount"]
    P2["Package: com.ktu.audit"] --> C3["class AuditLog"]
    C1 -->|"public members"| X["Visible everywhere"]
    C1 -->|"protected members"| Y["Visible in same pkg + subclasses"]
    C1 -->|"default members"| Z["Visible only in com.ktu.banking"]
    C1 -->|"private members"| W["Visible only inside Account"]
    C3 -->|"import com.ktu.banking.Account"| X
```

*Conversion logic:* Packages act as **visibility boundaries** for default-access members. Cross-package access requires either `public` visibility or an explicit `extends`/`implements` relationship for `protected`.

## 4.5 Lifecycle of an Object Through Module 3 Concepts

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Reference
    participant Heap
    participant JVM
    Client->>Reference: Animal a = new Dog()
    Reference->>Heap: Allocate Dog object
    Heap->>JVM: Object header marks class = Dog
    Client->>Reference: a.speak()
    Reference->>JVM: invokevirtual speak
    JVM->>Heap: Inspect actual class
    Heap-->>JVM: Class is Dog
    JVM->>Heap: Invoke Dog.speak()
    Heap-->>Client: Output: Dog barks
```

*Conversion logic:* The sequence captures the runtime contract between the **reference** (compile-time view) and the **heap object** (runtime reality) that defines Java's polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of method overriding in Java. How is it different from method overloading?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Method Overriding* is the process of redefining a method inherited from a superclass in a subclass with the **same name, same parameter list, and a covariant return type**. It enables **runtime polymorphism** — the JVM decides which version to call based on the actual object type. It requires an inheritance hierarchy, and the access level **cannot be reduced**.

*Method Overloading* is defining multiple methods with the **same name but different parameter lists** (number, type, or order of parameters) within the **same class** (or with a subclass). It is resolved at **compile time** based on the method signature, and is an example of **static polymorphism**. Overloading does not require inheritance.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1.5 marks: Overriding definition + runtime polymorphism link
- 1.5 marks: Overloading definition + compile-time binding + contrast

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is an abstract class in Java? When would you prefer it over an interface?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **abstract class** in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated** and may contain **abstract methods** (methods without a body) as well as **concrete methods** and instance fields. It is used to capture common behavior and state among related classes while forcing subclasses to provide specific implementations.

An abstract class is preferred over an interface when:
1. The related classes share **common code or state** (fields, constructors, concrete methods).
2. The related classes have an **"is-a" relationship** rather than just a behavioral contract.
3. Future versions need to **add new concrete methods** without breaking implementing classes (interfaces would force changes in all implementers).

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1.5 marks: Abstract class definition + instantiation rule
- 1.5 marks: Justification of preference over interface with valid use cases

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the four types of inheritance with suitable Java code examples. Why does Java not support multiple inheritance using classes? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **runtime polymorphism** using a superclass `Shape` with subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle`. Include a method `draw()` in each class and show how a single reference of type `Shape` invokes the correct `draw()` method at runtime. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**The four types of inheritance are:**

**1. Single Inheritance:** One subclass inherits from one superclass.

```java
class Animal { void eat() { System.out.println("eating"); } }
class Dog extends Animal { void bark() { System.out.println("barking"); } }
```

**2. Multilevel Inheritance:** A chain of inheritance (grandparent → parent → child).

```java
class Grandparent { void legacy() { } }
class Parent extends Grandparent { void guide() { } }
class Child extends Parent { void learn() { } }
```

**3. Hierarchical Inheritance:** Multiple subclasses inherit from a single superclass.

```java
class Vehicle { }
class Car extends Vehicle { }
class Truck extends Vehicle { }
class Bike extends Vehicle { }
```

**4. Multiple Inheritance (via interfaces):** A class inherits from multiple sources (interfaces only).

```java
interface Flyable { void fly(); }
interface Swimmable { void swim(); }
class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable {
    public void fly() { }
    public void swim() { }
}
```

**Why Java Disallows Multiple Class Inheritance:**

Java disallows multiple class inheritance to avoid the **Diamond Problem / Ambiguity Problem**. If a class `C` extends both `A` and `B`, and both `A` and `B` define a method `display()` with the same signature, the compiler cannot determine **which version** `C` inherits. This creates ambiguity in the object layout and method resolution. Java resolves this by allowing a class to implement **multiple interfaces** instead — interfaces are pure contracts (mostly abstract), and any conflict in default methods (Java 8+) must be explicitly resolved by the implementing class.

**Mark Distribution for Part (a):**
- 1 mark each for the four types with code (4 marks)
- 3 marks: Diamond problem explanation + Java's solution

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }
}
class Rectangle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Rectangle");
    }
}
public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;  // superclass reference

        s = new Circle();
        s.draw();  // Calls Circle's draw()

        s = new Rectangle();
        s.draw();  // Calls Rectangle's draw()
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\texttt{Drawing a Circle} \newline \texttt{Drawing a Rectangle}
$$

**Explanation of Runtime Dispatch:**

- The reference `s` is of type `Shape` (compile-time type).
- The objects created are `Circle` and `Rectangle` (runtime types).
- When `s.draw()` is called, the compiler verifies that `draw()` is declared in `Shape` (line 4).
- At runtime, the JVM inspects the actual object class in memory and dispatches to the **overridden** version.
- This is **dynamic method dispatch**, the mechanism that enables runtime polymorphism.

**Mark Distribution for Part (b):**
- 2 marks: Correct class hierarchy with override annotation
- 2 marks: Correct polymorphic main method
- 2 marks: Accurate output prediction
- 1 mark: Explanation of dynamic dispatch

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is an interface in Java? Explain with an example how a class can implement **multiple interfaces**. How does Java 8's `default` method feature enhance interfaces? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Java program using an **abstract class** `Employee` with abstract method `calculateSalary()`. Extend it with two subclasses `FullTimeEmployee` and `PartTimeEmployee` that provide their own salary calculation logic. Demonstrate calling `calculateSalary()` polymorphically. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

An **interface** in Java is a reference type, similar to a class, that can contain **abstract methods**, **default methods** (since Java 8), **static methods** (Java 8+), and **constants** (implicitly `public static final`). It defines a **contract** that implementing classes must fulfill. Interfaces support Java's form of **multiple inheritance of type**.

```java
interface Printable {
    void print();  // implicitly public abstract
}
interface Scannable {
    void scan();
}
class MultifunctionPrinter implements Printable, Scannable {
    public void print() { System.out.println("Printing..."); }
    public void scan()  { System.out.println("Scanning..."); }
}
```

Here, `MultifunctionPrinter` adopts **two contracts** (Printable and Scannable), demonstrating multiple interface implementation.

**Java 8 Default Methods Enhancement:**

Before Java 8, adding a new method to an interface **broke all existing implementations** (compile error). Java 8 introduced `default` methods — methods in an interface with a **concrete implementation** — that all implementing classes **inherit automatically** without being forced to override. This enables **backward-compatible API evolution**.

```java
interface Printer {
    void print();
    default void status() {           // default method
        System.out.println("Ready");
    }
}
```

If a class implements two interfaces that both define a `default` method with the same signature, the implementing class **must** explicitly override and disambiguate, or the compiler raises an error.

**Mark Distribution for Part (a):**
- 2 marks: Interface definition with rules
- 2 marks: Multiple interface implementation example
- 3 marks: Java 8 default method feature with example and conflict resolution

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

```java
abstract class Employee {
    String name;
    double baseRate;
    Employee(String name, double baseRate) {
        this.name = name;
        this.baseRate = baseRate;
    }
    abstract double calculateSalary();

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Salary: " + calculateSalary());
    }
}
class FullTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    FullTimeEmployee(String n, double r) { super(n, r); }
    @Override
    double calculateSalary() { return baseRate * 1.0; }  // fixed monthly
}
class PartTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    int hoursWorked;
    PartTimeEmployee(String n, double r, int h) {
        super(n, r);
        hoursWorked = h;
    }
    @Override
    double calculateSalary() { return baseRate * hoursWorked; }
}
public class Payroll {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee[] staff = {
            new FullTimeEmployee("Alice", 50000),
            new PartTimeEmployee("Bob", 500, 40)
        };
        for (Employee e : staff) {
            e.display();  // Polymorphic call
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\texttt{Name: Alice, Salary: 50000.0} \newline \texttt{Name: Bob, Salary: 20000.0}
$$

**Polymorphism Justification:**
- The reference type `Employee` (in the array) decides what can be called (`calculateSalary()` is declared in `Employee`).
- The actual object type (`FullTimeEmployee` or `PartTimeEmployee`) decides which version runs.
- This is the **open-closed principle** in action: the loop is closed for modification, open for extension — any new `Employee` subclass plugs in without changing `main()`.

**Mark Distribution for Part (b):**
- 2 marks: Abstract class with abstract + concrete method
- 2 marks: Both subclass overrides with distinct logic
- 2 marks: Polymorphic array iteration in main
- 1 mark: Correct output

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 3 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Constructor without `super()` call** — *(-2 marks)*: If a subclass constructor does not explicitly call `super(args)`, the compiler inserts a no-argument `super()` call. If the parent has **no default constructor**, this causes a compile error. Always chain explicitly.
>
> 2. **Reducing access in override** — *(-1 mark)*: Declaring a `public` parent method as `protected` in the child is a **compile error**, not just bad practice.
>
> 3. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** — *(-0.5 mark)*: Not a functional error, but a **style penalty** in KTU valuation; also loses marks for weak method signature detection.
>
> 4. **Static methods "overriding"** — *(-2 marks)*: Static methods are **hidden**, not overridden. Calling a static method via a superclass reference invokes the **parent's** static method, not the child's. This is a classic KTU trick question.
>
> 5. **Abstract method body** — *(-2 marks)*: An abstract method **cannot have a body** — `abstract void draw() { }` is a compile error.
>
> 6. **Interface field mutation** — *(-2 marks)*: All interface fields are `public static final`; attempting to modify them is a compile error.
>
> 7. **Confusing `final` class with `final` method** — *(-1 mark)*: A `final` class **cannot be extended**; a `final` method **cannot be overridden**. These are independent restrictions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Inheritance** uses the `extends` keyword and models an **"is-a"** relationship.
- [ ] Java supports **single, multilevel, and hierarchical** class inheritance — **not multiple** class inheritance.
- [ ] Multiple inheritance is achieved through **interfaces** using the `implements` keyword.
- [ ] **`super(args)`** must be the **first line** of a subclass constructor.
- [ ] **Method Overriding** = same signature + covariant return type + access not reduced.
- [ ] **Method Overloading** = same name, different parameter list — resolved at **compile time**.
- [ ] **Runtime polymorphism** = override + upcasting + dynamic method dispatch.
- [ ] **Abstract class** may have abstract + concrete members; **cannot be instantiated**.
- [ ] **Interface** fields are implicitly `public static final`; methods implicitly `public abstract` (pre-Java 8).
- [ ] **Java 8** added `default` and `static` methods to interfaces; **Java 9** added `private` methods.
- [ ] **`final` keyword** prevents overriding (method), extension (class), or reassignment (variable).
- [ ] **Packages** group related classes; `java.lang` is auto-imported.
- [ ] **Access hierarchy**: `private < default (package) < protected < public`.
- [ ] **`protected`** members are accessible in the **same package** and in **subclasses** (even outside the package).
- [ ] **`instanceof` returns `false` for `null`** — a frequently tested edge case.
- [ ] **Static methods are hidden, not overridden** — polymorphism does not apply to them.
- [ ] **`Object` class** is the **root** of every Java class hierarchy — directly or via `Object` superclass.
- [ ] **Polymorphism rule**: *Reference type decides WHAT; Object type decides WHICH.*
- [ ] Constructor chaining executes from **root → leaf** (parent first, then child).
- [ ] **Object slicing** does not occur in Java because all references are uniformly typed by class hierarchy.
- [ ] Use **abstract class** for shared code + partial contract; use **interface** for pure contract.
- [ ] A class can **extend one** class and **implement many** interfaces simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: CORE OBJECT-ORIENTED CONCEPTS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 3 Focus Area**
> This module builds the conceptual backbone of Java OOP. The four pillars covered are **Inheritance**, **Polymorphism**, **Abstraction (Abstract Classes)**, and **Interfaces**. Mastery of these is mandatory for ESE, university lab viva, and placement coding rounds.

---

## 1.1 🧬 INHERITANCE

### 📐 Formal Academic Definition
**Inheritance** is a mechanism in object-oriented programming by which one class (the *subclass* / *child* / *derived* class) acquires the properties (fields) and behaviors (methods) of another class (the *superclass* / *parent* / *base* class). In Java, it is implemented using the `extends` keyword and supports **single, multilevel, and hierarchical** inheritance. Java **does not** support multiple inheritance of classes to avoid the *Diamond Problem*; this is achieved through **interfaces**.

$$ \text{class SubClass } \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{ class SuperClass} $$

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
Think of inheritance like a **family tree**. A child inherits traits (eye color, height) and behaviors (walking style, language) from parents. Similarly, in Java, a subclass inherits fields and methods from its superclass — but it can also **add new features** or **override** inherited behaviors to make them its own. A `SavingsAccount` "is-a" `BankAccount` — it inherits the basic banking features but adds interest calculation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** *IS-A* relationship (inheritance) vs *HAS-A* relationship (composition/aggregation). Examiners love asking you to identify these in design questions.

---

## 1.2 🎭 POLYMORPHISM

### 📐 Formal Academic Definition
**Polymorphism** (Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a single interface, method, or object to take on **multiple forms**. In Java, polymorphism is of two types:

1. **Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding / Early Binding):** Achieved via *method overloading*.
2. **Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding / Late Binding):** Achieved via *method overriding* with *upcasting* and *dynamic method dispatch* through the Java Virtual Machine (JVM).

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a single person acting as a **Doctor** at a hospital, a **Parent** at home, and a **Customer** at a shop. The same person (`person.speak()`) responds differently based on the context (role). Similarly, in Java, a `Shape` reference can call `draw()` and depending on whether the actual object is a `Circle`, `Square`, or `Triangle`, the appropriate method executes at runtime.

---

## 1.3 🧩 ABSTRACTION (Abstract Classes & Methods)

### 📐 Formal Academic Definition
**Abstraction** is the process of **hiding implementation details** and exposing only the **essential features/functionality** to the user. In Java, abstraction is implemented through:
- **Abstract classes** (declared with the `abstract` keyword; can have 0 or more abstract methods and concrete methods).
- **Interfaces** (a pure abstract type — Java 8+ allows `default` and `static` methods).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of the examiner:** An abstract class **cannot be instantiated**. Any subclass that does not implement all abstract methods of its parent must itself be declared `abstract`.

---

## 1.4 🔌 INTERFACES

### 📐 Formal Academic Definition
An **interface** in Java is a **blueprint of a class** that contains **abstract methods** (pre-Java 8), `default` methods, `static` methods, and **constants** (`public static final` by default). A class implements an interface using the `implements` keyword. Since **Java 8**, interfaces can have `default` and `static` methods. Since **Java 9**, interfaces can also have `private` methods.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy
An interface is like a **contract or job description**. A job description for "Driver" lists responsibilities (`drive()`, `navigate()`) but does not specify *how* to drive. Any person (class) hired as a Driver must fulfill (implement) those responsibilities, but each driver may fulfill them differently (Tesla driver vs. truck driver).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A class can implement **multiple interfaces** (Java's way of multiple inheritance). Example: `class SmartPhone extends Gadget implements Camera, Phone, GPS { ... }`

---

## 1.5 🔑 THE `super` KEYWORD

The `super` keyword in Java refers to the **immediate parent class object**. It is used to:
1. Call the **parent class constructor** → `super(args)`.
2. Access **parent class methods** that have been overridden → `super.methodName()`.
3. Access **parent class fields** → `super.fieldName`.

`super()` **must be the first statement** in a constructor if used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPT MATRIX

## 2.1 🧬 Types of Inheritance (KTU Diagram-Favorite)

Java supports the following inheritance relationships:

| Type | Definition | Supported in Java? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single** | One subclass inherits one superclass | ✅ Yes | `B extends A` |
| **Multilevel** | Chain: Grandparent → Parent → Child | ✅ Yes | `C extends B extends A` |
| **Hierarchical** | One superclass, multiple subclasses | ✅ Yes | `B, C both extend A` |
| **Multiple** | One subclass inherits multiple superclasses | ❌ No (Diamond Problem) | `C extends A, B` |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more types | ❌ No (achievable via interfaces) | Mixed hierarchy |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** "Does Java support multiple inheritance?" The answer is: **No for classes, Yes for interfaces.** Many students lose marks by saying Java supports multiple inheritance without the qualifier.

---

## 2.2 🎭 Polymorphism: The Deep Mechanics

### Compile-Time Polymorphism (Method Overloading)
- Same method name, **different parameter list** (number, type, or order).
- Return type alone **cannot** differentiate overloaded methods.
- Resolved by the **compiler** → hence *static binding*.

### Run-Time Polymorphism (Method Overriding)
- Subclass provides a **specific implementation** of a method already defined in the parent.
- Rules:
  * Same method signature (name + parameters).
  * Access modifier cannot be more restrictive.
  * `final`, `static`, and `private` methods **cannot** be overridden.
  * Return type can be a **covariant** subtype (covariant return).
- Resolved by the **JVM at runtime** using the actual object type → hence *dynamic dispatch*.

$$ \text{Parent Reference} \xrightarrow{\text{upcast}} \text{Child Object} \xrightarrow{\text{parent.method()}} \text{Child's overridden method} $$

---

## 2.3 🧩 Abstract Class vs Interface — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + (default, static, private since Java 8/9) |
| Variables | Any (instance/static) | `public static final` only |
| Constructor | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Inheritance | `extends` (single) | `implements` (multiple) |
| Access Modifiers | Any | Implicitly `public` |
| When to use | Shared code + common state | Pure contract / capability |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mental Model:** Use an **abstract class** when subclasses share a common base implementation. Use an **interface** when unrelated classes must share a capability (e.g., `Comparable`, `Serializable`).

---

## 2.4 ⚙️ The `Object` Class — Root of All Classes

Every class in Java implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`. Key overridable methods:

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `toString()` | Returns string representation |
| `equals(Object o)` | Value-based equality |
| `hashCode()` | Hash value for hash-based collections |
| `getClass()` | Returns runtime class object |
| `finalize()` | Called by GC (deprecated since Java 9) |
| `clone()` | Creates object copy |
| `wait(), notify(), notifyAll()` | Thread synchronization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contract Rule (asked in ESE):** If you override `equals()`, you **must** override `hashCode()` to maintain the general contract for `HashMap`, `HashSet`, etc.

---

## 2.5 🔁 Dynamic Method Dispatch — The Engine of Runtime Polymorphism

The JVM maintains a **vtable (virtual method table)** internally for each class. When an overridden method is called via a parent reference:
1. The compiler checks that the method exists in the parent (compile-time).
2. At runtime, the JVM looks up the actual object's class in the vtable.
3. The most-specific (overriding) version is executed.

This is why **upcasting** is safe but **downcasting** requires an explicit cast and may throw `ClassCastException`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP LOGICAL DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 🧬 Logical Derivation: Why Java Disallows Multiple Inheritance of Classes

**Step 1: The Diamond Problem Statement**
Suppose Java allowed: `class C extends A, B`. Both `A` and `B` have a method `display()`.

**Step 2: Ambiguity Creation**
If `A.display()` and `B.display()` differ, and `C` does not override `display()`, the compiler/JVM cannot decide which version to inherit.

**Step 3: Resolution Attempt — Name Conflict**
Even if `C` overrides `display()`, complexity arises for fields, constructors, and state initialization order.

**Step 4: Java's Final Design Decision**
To maintain **simplicity, safety, and unambiguity**, Java designers forbade multiple class inheritance and provided **interfaces** as the alternative, since interfaces carry only **method signatures (contracts)**, not state, eliminating the diamond conflict for state.

$$ \text{Diamond Ambiguity} \Rightarrow \text{Java bans multiple class inheritance} \Rightarrow \text{Uses interfaces for multiple type inheritance} $$

---

## 3.2 🎭 Logical Derivation: How Runtime Polymorphism Works

**Step 1: Reference Creation (Upcasting)**
```java
Animal a = new Dog();   // Upcasting: Dog IS-A Animal
a.sound();              // Compiler checks: does Animal have sound()? YES
```

**Step 2: Compile-Time Check**
The compiler verifies the method exists in the **reference type** (`Animal`). It does not know `a` actually points to a `Dog`.

**Step 3: Runtime Resolution**
At execution, the JVM checks the **actual object type** in heap memory:
- Heap object is `Dog` → JVM dispatches to `Dog.sound()` → outputs "Bark".
- If heap object were `Cat`, JVM would dispatch to `Cat.sound()` → "Meow".

**Step 4: Why This Matters**
This enables **loose coupling** and **extensibility** — you can add a `Lion` class later without modifying the existing code that holds `Animal` references. This is the **Open/Closed Principle** of SOLID design.

$$ \text{Reference Type} \neq \text{Object Type} \Rightarrow \text{Dynamic Dispatch} \Rightarrow \text{Extensible Design} $$

---

## 3.3 🧩 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What (KTU Design Question Favorite)

| Design Requirement | Use |
|---|---|
| Share common code + state among related classes | **Abstract Class** |
| Define a contract for unrelated classes | **Interface** |
| Reuse code from an existing class | **Inheritance** |
| Combine two capabilities into one class | **Multiple Interfaces** |
| Allow same method to behave differently at runtime | **Method Overriding** |
| Provide multiple constructors / static methods with same name | **Method Overloading** |

---

## 3.4 🔑 Constructor Chaining with `super()` — Step-by-Step

When a subclass object is created:

**Step 1:** `new Dog()` invoked.
**Step 2:** Compiler implicitly inserts `super()` as the **first line** of `Dog()` constructor.
**Step 3:** `Animal()` constructor executes first → initializes parent fields.
**Step 4:** Control returns to `Dog()` constructor → initializes child fields.
**Step 5:** Object is fully constructed and returned.

$$ \text{Object Construction Order} = \text{Parent Static} \rightarrow \text{Child Static} \rightarrow \text{Parent Instance} \rightarrow \text{Parent Constructor} \rightarrow \text{Child Instance} \rightarrow \text{Child Constructor} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** If the parent has a **parameterized constructor** and **no default constructor**, the child constructor **must explicitly call** `super(args)`; otherwise, a compile-time error occurs.

---

## 3.5 🔬 Covariant Return Types — A Subtle Java Feature

When overriding a method, the return type can be a **subclass** of the original return type.

**Logical Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Parent class declares: `Animal getPet() { return new Animal(); }`
**Step 2:** Child class overrides: `Dog getPet() { return new Dog(); }`
**Step 3:** This is valid because `Dog IS-A Animal` (covariance).
**Step 4:** Benefits type safety and reduces the need for explicit downcasting.

$$ \text{Override Return Type} \in \text{Subtypes of Original Return Type} \Rightarrow \text{Covariant Return} $$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 🧬 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
        +String name
        +void eat()
        +void sound()
    }
    class Dog {
        +void sound()
        +void fetch()
    }
    class Cat {
        +void sound()
        +void climb()
    }
    class Puppy {
        +void weep()
    }
    Animal <|-- Dog
    Animal <|-- Cat
    Dog <|-- Puppy
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** `Dog` and `Cat` both inherit from `Animal` (hierarchical inheritance). `Puppy` inherits from `Dog` (multilevel inheritance). The arrows point from child to parent (UML convention).

---

## 4.2 🎭 Runtime Polymorphism Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Code: Animal a = new Dog] --> B{Compiler: Does Animal have sound?}
    B -- YES --> C[Compile OK]
    C --> D[Runtime: a.sound called]
    D --> E{JVM: Check actual object type}
    E -- Dog Object --> F[Invoke Dog.sound]
    E -- Cat Object --> G[Invoke Cat.sound]
    E -- Animal Object --> H[Invoke Animal.sound]
    F --> I[Output: Bark]
    G --> J[Output: Meow]
    H --> K[Output: Generic Sound]
```

---

## 4.3 🧩 Abstract Class vs Interface — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Abstract_Class
        A1[Fields: Any Type] --> A2[Methods: Abstract + Concrete]
        A2 --> A3[Constructor: Yes]
        A3 --> A4[Inheritance: extends - single]
    end
    subgraph Interface
        B1[Fields: public static final only] --> B2[Methods: abstract + default + static]
        B2 --> B3[Constructor: No]
        B3 --> B4[Inheritance: implements - multiple]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 🔁 Constructor Chaining Order

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main
    participant Dog
    participant Animal
    Main->>Dog: new Dog()
    activate Dog
    Dog->>Animal: super() implicit
    activate Animal
    Animal->>Animal: Initialize parent fields
    Animal-->>Dog: parent constructed
    deactivate Animal
    Dog->>Dog: Initialize child fields
    Dog-->>Main: object returned
    deactivate Dog
```

---

## 4.5 🔌 Multiple Interface Implementation

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Printable {
        <<interface>>
        +print()
    }
    class Serializable {
        <<interface>>
        +serialize()
    }
    class Document {
        -String content
        +print()
        +serialize()
        +open()
    }
    Printable <|.. Document
    Serializable <|.. Document
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed arrow with hollow triangle denotes **implementation** (UML notation for interface realization), whereas a solid arrow with hollow triangle denotes **inheritance/extension**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding in Java. Give a one-line example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Compile-time polymorphism | Run-time polymorphism |
| Signature | Different parameter list | Same parameter list |
| Class | Same class | Parent-child relationship |
| Binding | Static | Dynamic |
| Return type | Can differ (with diff params) | Same or covariant |
| Example: `add(int a, int b)` and `add(double a, double b)` in same class | Example: `Vehicle.run()` overridden as `Car.run()` | |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why does Java not support multiple inheritance through classes? How is it achieved using interfaces?**

**Model Answer:**
Java does not support multiple inheritance of classes to avoid the **Diamond Problem** — when a class inherits from two classes that have a method with the same signature, ambiguity arises about which version to inherit. Multiple inheritance is achieved using **interfaces** because interfaces only declare contracts (no state/implementation), eliminating ambiguity. A class can implement multiple interfaces, gaining their type contracts without state conflicts.

---

## 📌 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A — 14 Marks** [Inheritance + Polymorphism + Abstract Class Design]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Design a Java class hierarchy for a **Banking System** with `Account` as an abstract base class, and `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` as subclasses. Include appropriate fields, constructors, an abstract method `calculateInterest()`, and demonstrate method overriding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **runtime polymorphism** using a `Shape` abstract class with subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle`. Show how a single `Shape` reference can invoke different `draw()` methods at runtime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

```java
// Abstract base class
abstract class Account {
    protected String accountHolder;
    protected double balance;

    public Account(String name, double balance) {
        this.accountHolder = name;
        this.balance = balance;
    }

    public void displayBalance() {
        System.out.println("Account Holder: " + accountHolder);
        System.out.println("Balance: " + balance);
    }

    // Abstract method to be overridden
    public abstract void calculateInterest();
}

// Savings Account subclass
class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    private double interestRate = 0.04; // 4% annual

    public SavingsAccount(String name, double balance) {
        super(name, balance);  // call parent constructor
    }

    @Override
    public void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * interestRate;
        System.out.println("Savings Interest: " + interest);
    }
}

// Current Account subclass
class CurrentAccount extends Account {
    private double interestRate = 0.01; // 1% annual

    public CurrentAccount(String name, double balance) {
        super(name, balance);
    }

    @Override
    public void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * interestRate;
        System.out.println("Current Interest: " + interest);
    }
}

// Main class
public class BankingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account a1 = new SavingsAccount("Alice", 100000);
        Account a2 = new CurrentAccount("Bob", 200000);

        a1.displayBalance();
        a1.calculateInterest();   // Calls SavingsAccount version

        a2.displayBalance();
        a2.calculateInterest();   // Calls CurrentAccount version
    }
}
```

**Key Concepts Demonstrated:**
- `abstract` keyword for abstract class & method.
- `extends` for inheritance.
- `super()` to call parent constructor.
- `@Override` annotation for method overriding.
- Runtime polymorphism via parent reference.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

```java
// Abstract base class
abstract class Shape {
    public abstract void draw();
    public abstract double area();
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }

    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }

    @Override
    public double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    private double length, width;

    public Rectangle(double l, double w) {
        this.length = l;
        this.width = w;
    }

    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Rectangle");
    }

    @Override
    public double area() {
        return length * width;
    }
}

public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Runtime polymorphism in action
        Shape s;

        s = new Circle(5.0);
        s.draw();    // Output: Drawing a Circle
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());

        s = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);
        s.draw();    // Output: Drawing a Rectangle
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());
    }
}
```

**Key Concepts Demonstrated:**
- Parent reference (`Shape s`) holding child objects (upcasting).
- JVM dispatches to the **actual object's overridden method** at runtime.
- Single interface, multiple behaviors — true polymorphism.

---

### ⭐ **Question B — 14 Marks** [Alternative Choice — Interfaces + Multiple Inheritance Design]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **interfaces in Java** with suitable example. Discuss how interfaces support **multiple inheritance** and the role of `default` methods introduced in Java 8. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Java program where a `SmartPhone` class **extends `Device`** and **implements `Camera`, `MusicPlayer`, and `GPS` interfaces**. Define the interfaces with at least one abstract method each and a `default` method in at least one interface. Show how the `SmartPhone` resolves method calls. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** An interface in Java is a **pure abstract type** that defines a contract of abstract methods that implementing classes must fulfill. Interfaces cannot be instantiated.

**Syntax:**
```java
interface Drawable {
    void draw();             // implicitly public abstract
    default void show() {    // default method (Java 8+)
        System.out.println("Default show");
    }
}
```

**Multiple Inheritance Support:** Java does not allow multiple inheritance of classes (Diamond Problem), but a class can implement **multiple interfaces**:
```java
class Tablet implements Touchable, Portable { ... }
```

**Role of `default` methods (Java 8):**
- Allow interfaces to provide **concrete implementation** without breaking existing implementations.
- Used to add new methods to interfaces while maintaining **backward compatibility**.
- If a class implements two interfaces with the **same default method**, the class **must override** that method to resolve ambiguity (compile-time error otherwise).

**Key Points:**
- All interface variables are implicitly `public static final` (constants).
- A class implementing an interface must implement **all its abstract methods** or be declared `abstract`.
- Interfaces support multiple inheritance through `interface C extends A, B { ... }`.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

```java
// Interfaces
interface Camera {
    void takePhoto();
    default void recordVideo() {
        System.out.println("Recording video in 1080p");
    }
}

interface MusicPlayer {
    void playMusic();
    void pauseMusic();
}

interface GPS {
    void navigate(String destination);
}

// Parent class
class Device {
    String brand;
    Device(String brand) { this.brand = brand; }
    void powerOn() { System.out.println(brand + " is ON"); }
}

// SmartPhone extends Device and implements three interfaces
class SmartPhone extends Device implements Camera, MusicPlayer, GPS {

    SmartPhone(String brand) {
        super(brand);
    }

    @Override
    public void takePhoto() {
        System.out.println("Photo clicked");
    }

    @Override
    public void playMusic() {
        System.out.println("Playing music");
    }

    @Override
    public void pauseMusic() {
        System.out.println("Music paused");
    }

    @Override
    public void navigate(String destination) {
        System.out.println("Navigating to " + destination);
    }
}

public class SmartPhoneDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SmartPhone sp = new SmartPhone("Samsung");
        sp.powerOn();
        sp.takePhoto();
        sp.recordVideo();     // inherited default method
        sp.playMusic();
        sp.navigate("Kochi");
    }
}
```

**Key Concepts Demonstrated:**
- Hybrid inheritance: single class inheritance + multiple interface implementation.
- `super(brand)` calls parent `Device` constructor.
- The `recordVideo()` method is **inherited as a default method** without overriding.
- The `SmartPhone` class is a concrete class fulfilling all interface contracts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation:** Not wrong, but shows poor practice. Examiners may deduct 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Not calling `super()` explicitly when parent has no default constructor:** Causes compile-time error — student often gets 0 for the entire program.
> 3. **Confusing overloading with overriding** in the answer — be very precise with terminology.
> 4. **Missing the `default` method explanation** in Java 8+ questions — high-weightage topic.
> 5. **Writing `interface Phone { void call(); }` without explaining implicit `public abstract`** — examiners want that detail.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Inheritance** = `extends` keyword; supports single, multilevel, hierarchical; **NOT multiple** for classes.
- ✅ **Polymorphism** = Overloading (compile-time) + Overriding (run-time via dynamic dispatch).
- ✅ **Abstract class** = Cannot be instantiated; can have abstract + concrete methods + constructors.
- ✅ **Interface** = Pure contract; `public static final` fields; `default` & `static` methods from Java 8.
- ✅ **`super`** = Refers to immediate parent; must be the first line in constructor if used.
- ✅ **`Object` class** = Root of all Java classes; 11 key methods (know `equals` + `hashCode` contract).
- ✅ **Covariant return** = Override return type can be a subtype of original.
- ✅ **Final/static/private methods** cannot be overridden.
- ✅ **Diamond problem** = Reason Java disallows multiple class inheritance.
- ✅ **Upcasting** = Implicit & safe; **Downcasting** = Explicit, can throw `ClassCastException`.
- ✅ **Open/Closed Principle** = Achieved via polymorphism (open for extension, closed for modification).
- ✅ **IS-A vs HAS-A** = IS-A → inheritance; HAS-A → composition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Design (OOD)**, an **Activity** is a fundamental behavioral modeling construct defined under the **Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5 specification)**. An *Activity* represents a major unit of executable behavior of a system, encompassing a sequential (and sometimes concurrent) flow of coordinated steps.

An **Activity Diagram** is a **UML behavioral diagram** that visualizes the workflow of stepwise activities and actions with support for choice, iteration, and concurrency. It models the **dynamic aspects of a system** — i.e., *what happens*, *when it happens*, and *what the order of occurrences is*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Activities and Activity Diagrams are part of the **OOAD (Object Oriented Analysis and Design)** methodology that precedes Java implementation. In KTU valuation, an Activity is graded as a *node* inside an Activity Diagram that represents a unit of work performed within a state machine or workflow.

### Formal UML 2.5 Definition (Object Management Group)
> An *Activity* is the specification of parameterized behavior as the coordinated sequencing of subordinate units whose individual elements are actions. — **OMG UML 2.5.1 Specification**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **Activity Diagram as a flowchart of a recipe while cooking a dish**.

- **Each cooking step** (chopping onions, boiling water, adding salt) = an **Activity / Action node**.
- **The decision** ("Do you want it spicy or mild?") = a **Decision (Branch) node**.
- **Two parallel tasks** (boiling rice AND making curry) = **Fork/Join (Concurrency)**.
- **The starting point** ("Begin cooking") = **Initial Node**.
- **The ending point** ("Dish is ready") = **Final / Activity-Final Node**.
- **The chef and the helper** doing different tasks = **Swimlanes (Partitions)**.

Just as a recipe tells you the *order* and *parallelism* of cooking steps, an Activity Diagram tells developers the *order* and *parallelism* of business processes, method calls, or business workflows.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Terms (bolded for emphasis):**
> - **Activity** — a behavior represented as a coordinated flow of actions.
> - **Action** — the fundamental unit of executable functionality inside an activity.
> - **Token** — a conceptual traversal marker; one token = one thread of control.
> - **Pin** — an input/output parameter holder on an action.
> - **Control Flow** — directed edge connecting nodes.
> - **Object Flow** — edge carrying data objects between nodes.
> - **Initial Node** — exactly one per activity; marks start (filled circle).
> - **Activity Final Node** — terminates the entire activity (bullseye circle).
> - **Flow Final Node** — terminates only that specific path (circle with X).
> - **Decision Node** — a diamond representing branching.
> - **Merge Node** — a diamond merging multiple alternative paths.
> - **Fork Node** — splits one flow into concurrent flows (thick bar).
> - **Join Node** — synchronizes concurrent flows back into one (thick bar).
> - **Swimlane (Partition)** — organizes activities by responsible entity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of an Activity Diagram

An Activity Diagram is composed of the following building blocks. Each block has a strict UML 2.5 notation, and KTU examiners expect these *exact* shapes in board answers.

### 2.1.1 Nodes (Vertices)

| Node Type | Notation Shape | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Node** | Filled black circle ● | Start of the activity (mandatory, exactly one) |
| **Activity Final Node** | Bullseye ⊙ | Terminates the entire activity |
| **Flow Final Node** | Circle with cross ⊗ | Terminates only that control flow path |
| **Action / Activity** | Rounded rectangle | Represents a step of work |
| **Decision Node** | Diamond ◇ | Branches flow based on a guard condition |
| **Merge Node** | Diamond ◇ | Unifies alternative paths back to one |
| **Fork Node** | Thick black bar ▬ | Splits flow into concurrent threads |
| **Join Node** | Thick black bar ▬ | Synchronizes concurrent flows |
| **Object Node** | Rectangle | Holds data flowing between actions |
| **Send Signal Action** | Convex pentagon | Sends a signal to another activity |
| **Receive Signal Action** | Concave pentagon | Waits for a signal |
| **Time Event** | Hourglass | Delays a flow by elapsed time |

### 2.1.2 Edges (Connectors)

- **Control Flow** — solid arrow → (no data, just sequencing).
- **Object Flow** — solid arrow → carrying an object, with optional *pin* labels.

### 2.1.3 Partitions (Swimlanes)

Partitions divide the activity into regions, each representing a **responsible entity** (Class, Actor, Department). They are drawn as **vertical or horizontal bands**, each labeled at the top with the responsible unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Swimlanes are *extremely* frequently asked in KTU ESE Part B questions. A 7-mark question typically asks: "Draw an activity diagram for an ATM transaction system with swimlanes for Customer, ATM Machine, and Bank Server."

## 2.2 Token Flow Semantics (Why does it work?)

The UML 2.5 execution model uses **tokens** to track flow.

- A token is offered to an outgoing edge of a node only when the node's *activity* is complete.
- At a **Fork node**, a single incoming token is replicated into *N* outgoing tokens (one per parallel branch).
- At a **Join node**, the activity waits until **all** incoming branches have offered tokens, then produces a single outgoing token.
- At a **Decision node**, a guard condition `[condition]` on each outgoing edge determines which branch the token takes. **Exactly one** guard must be true at a time.
- At a **Merge node**, no synchronization occurs — the first token to arrive is passed through.

## 2.3 Activity vs. State Machine vs. Sequence Diagram

| Property | Activity Diagram | State Machine | Sequence Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Workflow of actions | Lifecycle of an object | Time-ordered message exchange |
| **Primary Use** | Business process modeling | Reactive object behavior | Inter-object communication |
| **Concurrency** | Native support (Fork/Join) | Limited | Limited (par, alt fragments) |
| **Time Dimension** | Implicit (top-to-bottom) | Explicit (state residency) | Explicit (vertical lifeline) |
| **Best For (KTU)** | Modeling methods, use-case flows | Modeling reactive subsystems | Modeling interaction protocols |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Shape | Edge Type | Cardinality Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | Filled ● | None | **Exactly 1** per activity |
| Activity-Final | ⊙ | None | At least 0; commonly 1 |
| Flow-Final | ⊗ | None | Any number |
| Action | Rounded Rect | Control or Object | Min 1 in/out |
| Decision | ◇ | Outgoing guards `[…]` | 1 in, 2+ out |
| Merge | ◇ | No guard | 2+ in, 1 out |
| Fork | ▬ | Implicit AND | 1 in, 2+ out |
| Join | ▬ | Implicit AND-sync | 2+ in, 1 out |
| Partition | Swimlane band | Encloses nodes | Each node ∈ 1 partition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Activity Diagrams are the foundation of **Business Process Modeling (BPMN)**, **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) workflow design**, **embedded systems state-flow design**, and **UML-driven code generation tools** (e.g., Rational Rose, Enterprise Architect, Visual Paradigm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm: Constructing a KTU-Standard Activity Diagram

Every board-answer diagram follows a deterministic 6-step construction. We will derive this algorithmically.

**Step 1 — Identify the use case scope.**
Read the problem statement; determine the *single high-level process* to be modeled (e.g., "Online Book Purchase").

**Step 2 — Enumerate the activities.**
List the discrete actions: `Login → Search Book → Add to Cart → Checkout → Pay → Confirm`.

**Step 3 — Identify decision points.**
Look for conditional words: *if, whether, depending on*. Each maps to a **Decision/Merge diamond** with guards.

**Step 4 — Identify concurrent flows.**
Look for *parallel* words: *simultaneously, while, in parallel*. Each maps to a **Fork/Join** bar.

**Step 5 — Assign swimlanes.**
Map each activity to the responsible entity (Customer, System, Payment Gateway).

**Step 6 — Attach initial and final nodes.**
Place **one** Initial node at start. Attach **one or more** Activity-Final nodes at the end.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — ATM Cash Withdrawal

We will derive the Activity Diagram for a 7-mark KTU question:

> *"Draw an Activity Diagram with swimlanes for an ATM cash withdrawal process."*

### Step-by-step textual derivation:

1. **Initial node** placed in the **Customer** swimlane.
2. Customer performs: *Insert Card*, *Enter PIN*, *Select Withdrawal*, *Enter Amount*, *Collect Cash*, *Take Receipt*.
3. After *Enter PIN*, the **ATM Machine** validates PIN → Decision: *Valid?*
4. If Invalid → *Display Error* → *Eject Card* → Flow-Final Node.
5. If Valid → ATM sends request to **Bank Server** → Server checks balance.
6. Decision: *Sufficient Balance?*
7. Fork into two parallel actions: *Dispense Cash* and *Print Receipt*.
8. Join → *Eject Card* → *Take Card*.
9. Activity-Final node in Customer lane.

### Equivalent Mermaid Flowchart Representation

The following Mermaid block models the **logical flow** of the ATM process (note: Mermaid does not natively support swimlanes, so we partition via subgraphs — this is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture* mapping).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initC([Initial Node - Customer])
    act1[/Insert Card/]
    act2[/Enter PIN/]
    dec1{Diamond Decision - PIN Valid}
    act3[/Display Error/]
    act4[/Eject Card Invalid/]
    flowEnd1([Flow-Final - Invalid Path])
    act5[/Select Withdrawal/]
    act6[/Enter Amount/]
    act7[/ATM Validates with Bank Server/]
    dec2{Diamond Decision - Sufficient Balance}
    act8[/Display Insufficient Funds/]
    flowEnd2([Flow-Final - No Funds])
    fork1[["Fork Node - Parallel"]]
    act9[/Dispense Cash/]
    act10[/Print Receipt/]
    join1[["Join Node - Synchronize"]]
    act11[/Eject Card/]
    act12[/Take Card and Cash/]
    finalNode([Activity-Final Node])

    initC --> act1 --> act2 --> dec1
    dec1 -- No --> act3 --> act4 --> flowEnd1
    dec1 -- Yes --> act5 --> act6 --> act7 --> dec2
    dec2 -- No --> act8 --> flowEnd2
    dec2 -- Yes --> fork1
    fork1 --> act9 --> join1
    fork1 --> act10 --> join1
    join1 --> act11 --> act12 --> finalNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how the **Fork node** splits one control flow into *two parallel paths* (Dispense Cash + Print Receipt), and the **Join node** synchronizes them back. This is the formal notation for concurrency in UML 2.5.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Online Shopping Checkout

For a 7-mark Part B sub-question:
> *"Draw an Activity Diagram for an Online Shopping Checkout process including user authentication, payment validation, and concurrent email/SMS confirmation."*

### Derived Step-by-Step Solution:

1. **Swimlanes:** *Customer*, *Web Application*, *Payment Gateway*, *Notification Service*.
2. Activities: *Browse → Add to Cart → Login → Checkout → Choose Payment → Pay → [Fork: Send Email + Send SMS] → Join → Display Order Confirmation → Logout*.
3. Decisions: *Login Successful?*, *Payment Approved?*, *Cart Empty?*
4. Flow-Final nodes for: failed login, declined payment.

### Mermaid Block Diagram (Logical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Initial - Customer Lane])
    B[/Browse Products/]
    C[/Add to Cart/]
    D[/Proceed to Checkout/]
    E[/Enter Credentials/]
    F{Login Successful?}
    G[/Show Error/]
    H([Flow-Final - Login Failed])
    I[/Choose Payment Method/]
    J[/Submit Payment/]
    K{Payment Approved?}
    L[/Show Declined Message/]
    M([Flow-Final - Payment Declined])
    N[[Fork - Notification Parallel]]
    O[/Send Confirmation Email/]
    P[/Send Confirmation SMS/]
    Q[[Join - Sync Notifications]]
    R[/Show Order Confirmation/]
    S[/Logout/]
    T([Activity-Final])

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
    F -- No --> G --> H
    F -- Yes --> I --> J --> K
    K -- No --> L --> M
    K -- Yes --> N
    N --> O --> Q
    N --> P --> Q
    Q --> R --> S --> T
```

## 3.4 Guard Condition Grammar (Formal Derivation)

A guard condition is a **Boolean expression** placed in square brackets `[...]` on the outgoing edge of a Decision node.

$$
\text{Guard} ::= \texttt{[} \;\; \text{BooleanExpression} \;\; \texttt{]}
$$

$$
\text{BooleanExpression} ::= \text{Expression} \;\; ( \; \texttt{AND} \mid \texttt{OR} \; ) \;\; \text{Expression} \mid \text{UnaryCondition}
$$

$$
\text{UnaryCondition} ::= \texttt{NOT} \;\; \text{Expression} \mid \text{Variable} \;\; ( \; \texttt{>} \mid \texttt{<} \mid \texttt{==} \mid \texttt{>=} \mid \texttt{<=} \mid \texttt{!=} \; ) \;\; \text{Value}
$$

### KTU Board Convention for Guard Labeling:
- Use **square brackets** `[]` around the condition.
- Place the label **directly on the edge**, not on the node.
- Each outgoing edge of a Decision node must have **at most one** guard.
- The special guard `else` is permitted for the *default* path.

> [!TIP]
> **Example valid guard labels:** `[balance >= amount]`, `[isValid == true]`, `[userType == "Admin"]`, `[else]`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Notation Reference — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following block diagram exhaustively maps the **notation → meaning → KTU usage** of every Activity Diagram component.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NODES["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM NODE TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        N1["INITIAL NODE - Filled Black Circle - Marks Start"]
        N2["ACTIVITY FINAL - Bullseye Circle - Ends Entire Activity"]
        N3["FLOW FINAL - Circle with X - Ends One Path Only"]
        N4["ACTION - Rounded Rectangle - Single Unit of Work"]
        N5["ACTIVITY - Rounded Rectangle with Icon - Compound Behavior"]
        N6["OBJECT NODE - Rectangle - Holds Data in Object Flow"]
        N7["DECISION OR MERGE - Diamond - Branching Logic"]
        N8["FORK OR JOIN - Thick Black Bar - Concurrency Boundary"]
        N9["SEND SIGNAL - Convex Pentagon - Outbound Asynchronous"]
        N10["RECEIVE SIGNAL - Concave Pentagon - Inbound Asynchronous"]
        N11["TIME EVENT - Hourglass - Temporal Delay"]
    end

    subgraph EDGES["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM EDGE TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        E1["CONTROL FLOW - Solid Arrow - Sequencing Without Data"]
        E2["OBJECT FLOW - Solid Arrow - Carries Object Token"]
    end

    subgraph GROUPING["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM GROUPING TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        G1["SWIMLANE PARTITION - Vertical or Horizontal Band - Responsibility"]
        G2["ACTIVITY GROUP - Dashed Rounded Rectangle - Logical Grouping"]
    end

    subgraph GUARDS["GUARD CONDITION TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        Q1["BOOLEAN EXPRESSION - Square Bracket Label - Decision Branch"]
        Q2["ELSE CLAUSE - Default Fallback - Catch All"]
    end

    N1 --> N4
    N4 --> N7
    N7 --> N2
    N4 --> N8
    N8 --> N4
    E1 --> E2
    G1 --> N4
    Q1 --> N7
```

## 4.2 Activity Diagram Construction Workflow — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 - Read Problem Statement] --> stepB[Step 2 - Identify Actors and Lanes]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 - Enumerate Sequential Actions]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 - Locate Decision Points]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 - Locate Parallel Sections]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 - Insert Initial and Final Nodes]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 - Apply Guard Conditions]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 - Validate with KTU Notation Rules]
```

## 4.3 Activity Diagram vs. State Machine vs. Sequence — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPARISON["PURPOSE COMPARISON OF UML BEHAVIORAL DIAGRAMS"]
        direction TB
        A1["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM - Workflow and Business Process Modeling"]
        A2["STATE MACHINE DIAGRAM - Reactive Object Lifecycle Modeling"]
        A3["SEQUENCE DIAGRAM - Time-Ordered Inter Object Messaging"]
    end

    A1 --> USE1[Use Case - ATM Cash Withdrawal]
    A2 --> USE2[Use Case - Login Session States]
    A3 --> USE3[Use Case - Method Call Between Classes]
```

## 4.4 Token Flow Semantics — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0([Token at Initial Node])
    T0 --> T1[Token Traverses Action A]
    T1 --> T2[Token Reaches Decision Node]
    T2 -- Guard True --> T3[Token Moves to Branch X]
    T2 -- Guard Else --> T4[Token Moves to Branch Y]
    T1 --> F1[Token Reaches Fork Node]
    F1 --> F2[Token Replicated into Two]
    F2 --> F3[Branch Alpha Executes]
    F2 --> F4[Branch Beta Executes]
    F3 --> J1[Join Node Waits]
    F4 --> J1
    J1 --> J2[Synchronized Single Token Continues]
    J2 --> FN([Activity Final Node Consumed])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1(a)]**
*Define an Activity Diagram. List any four components used in it.*

#### Model Answer:
An **Activity Diagram** is a UML behavioral diagram used to model the dynamic aspects of a system. It represents the workflow of activities and actions, including sequential, conditional, and concurrent flows.

Four components are:
1. **Initial Node** — Filled circle marking the start.
2. **Activity Final Node** — Bullseye circle marking termination.
3. **Decision Node** — Diamond for branching with guard conditions.
4. **Fork/Join Node** — Thick bar for concurrent flows.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2(b)]**
*What is the difference between a Decision node and a Fork node in an Activity Diagram?*

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Decision Node | Fork Node |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Diamond ◇ | Thick black bar ▬ |
| **Purpose** | Branches flow based on guard conditions | Splits flow into concurrent parallel paths |
| **Synchronization** | Does NOT synchronize | No synchronization (Join node does that) |
| **Tokens** | Routes a single token to one branch | Replicates a single token to multiple parallel branches |
| **Use Case** | *if-else* logic | Parallel activities |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q11(a,b)]**

> **(a)** Draw an Activity Diagram with swimlanes for an **Online Train Reservation System**. The diagram should include user login, train search, seat availability check, payment processing, and ticket generation. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the roles of **Decision node**, **Fork-Join node**, **Swimlane**, and **Activity Final node** with reference to the diagram drawn in part (a). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Initial - Customer Lane])
    B[/Open Website/]
    C[/Enter Username and Password/]
    D{Login Successful?}
    E[/Show Error Message/]
    F([Flow-Final - Login Failed])
    G[/Search Train by Date and Destination/]
    H[/Select Train/]
    I{Seats Available?}
    J[/Add to Waitlist/]
    K[/Display No Seats Message/]
    L([Flow-Final - No Seats])
    M[/Proceed to Payment/]
    N[/Enter Card Details/]
    O{Payment Approved?}
    P[/Show Declined Notification/]
    Q[[Fork - Parallel Notification]]
    R[/Send Email Ticket/]
    S[/Send SMS Confirmation/]
    T[[Join - Sync Notifications]]
    U[/Display Booking Confirmation/]
    V[/Logout/]
    W([Activity-Final - End])

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -- No --> E --> F
    D -- Yes --> G --> H --> I
    I -- No --> J
    J --> K --> L
    I -- Yes --> M --> N --> O
    O -- No --> P
    P --> W
    O -- Yes --> Q
    Q --> R --> T
    Q --> S --> T
    T --> U --> V --> W
```

**Part (b) — Explanation:**

1. **Decision Node** (D, I, O): These diamonds represent conditional branching. At *Login Successful?*, the flow routes to either *Show Error* (false) or *Search Train* (true). Guards like `[loginValid == true]` should be labeled on each outgoing edge.

2. **Fork–Join Nodes** (Q and T): The **Fork** splits the flow into two parallel branches: *Send Email Ticket* and *Send SMS Confirmation*. The **Join** synchronizes them — the system only proceeds to *Display Booking Confirmation* once both notifications are dispatched.

3. **Swimlane (Partition)**: Although the Mermaid approximation is a single flow, in hand-drawn exam answers the diagram must be divided into vertical swimlanes for **Customer**, **Web Application Server**, and **Payment Gateway** to clarify responsibility.

4. **Activity Final Node** (W): The bullseye circle ⊙ terminates the entire activity, reached after *Logout*. In contrast, the *Flow-Final* nodes (F, L) terminate only their respective failed-login and no-seat branches.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Activity-Final (⊙) with Flow-Final (⊗).** Many students draw the wrong symbol — this costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Do not place a guard on a Merge node** — guards belong only on outgoing edges of Decision nodes.
> 3. **Do not skip the Initial node.** Even if obvious, it is mandatory and omitting it costs a mark.
> 4. **Swimlane labels must be at the top of the band** — examiners check this strictly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q12(a,b)]**

> **(a)** Draw an Activity Diagram for a **Hospital Patient Admission System** including registration, doctor assignment, ward allocation, and billing. Show at least one **Fork-Join** and one **Decision-Merge**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **Activity Diagram** and **Sequence Diagram**. Mention any five differences. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Initial - Patient Lane])
    B[/Patient Arrives at Reception/]
    C[/Fill Registration Form/]
    D[/Enter Details into System/]
    E{New Patient?}
    F[/Create New Medical Record/]
    G[/Retrieve Existing Medical Record/]
    H[/Assign Doctor Based on Symptoms/]
    I{Doctor Available?}
    J[/Add to Appointment Queue/]
    K[/Generate Token Number/]
    L[/Doctor Consultation/]
