```mermaid
    Dog->>Dog: Initialize child fields
    Dog-->>Main: object returned
    deactivate Dog
```

---

## 4.5 🔌 Multiple Interface Implementation

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Printable {
        <<interface>>
        +print()
    }
    class Serializable {
        <<interface>>
        +serialize()
    }
    class Document {
        -String content
        +print()
        +serialize()
        +open()
    }
    Printable <|.. Document
    Serializable <|.. Document
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed arrow with hollow triangle denotes **implementation** (UML notation for interface realization), whereas a solid arrow with hollow triangle denotes **inheritance/extension**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding in Java. Give a one-line example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Compile-time polymorphism | Run-time polymorphism |
| Signature | Different parameter list | Same parameter list |
| Class | Same class | Parent-child relationship |
| Binding | Static | Dynamic |
| Return type | Can differ (with diff params) | Same or covariant |
| Example: `add(int a, int b)` and `add(double a, double b)` in same class | Example: `Vehicle.run()` overridden as `Car.run()` | |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why does Java not support multiple inheritance through classes? How is it achieved using interfaces?**

**Model Answer:**
Java does not support multiple inheritance of classes to avoid the **Diamond Problem** — when a class inherits from two classes that have a method with the same signature, ambiguity arises about which version to inherit. Multiple inheritance is achieved using **interfaces** because interfaces only declare contracts (no state/implementation), eliminating ambiguity. A class can implement multiple interfaces, gaining their type contracts without state conflicts.

---

## 📌 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A — 14 Marks** [Inheritance + Polymorphism + Abstract Class Design]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Design a Java class hierarchy for a **Banking System** with `Account` as an abstract base class, and `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` as subclasses. Include appropriate fields, constructors, an abstract method `calculateInterest()`, and demonstrate method overriding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **runtime polymorphism** using a `Shape` abstract class with subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle`. Show how a single `Shape` reference can invoke different `draw()` methods at runtime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

```java
// Abstract base class
abstract class Account {
    protected String accountHolder;
    protected double balance;

    public Account(String name, double balance) {
        this.accountHolder = name;
        this.balance = balance;
    }

    public void displayBalance() {
        System.out.println("Account Holder: " + accountHolder);
        System.out.println("Balance: " + balance);
    }

    // Abstract method to be overridden
    public abstract void calculateInterest();
}

// Savings Account subclass
class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    private double interestRate = 0.04; // 4% annual

    public SavingsAccount(String name, double balance) {
        super(name, balance);  // call parent constructor
    }

    @Override
    public void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * interestRate;
        System.out.println("Savings Interest: " + interest);
    }
}

// Current Account subclass
class CurrentAccount extends Account {
    private double interestRate = 0.01; // 1% annual

    public CurrentAccount(String name, double balance) {
        super(name, balance);
    }

    @Override
    public void calculateInterest() {
        double interest = balance * interestRate;
        System.out.println("Current Interest: " + interest);
    }
}

// Main class
public class BankingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account a1 = new SavingsAccount("Alice", 100000);
        Account a2 = new CurrentAccount("Bob", 200000);

        a1.displayBalance();
        a1.calculateInterest();   // Calls SavingsAccount version

        a2.displayBalance();
        a2.calculateInterest();   // Calls CurrentAccount version
    }
}
```

**Key Concepts Demonstrated:**
- `abstract` keyword for abstract class & method.
- `extends` for inheritance.
- `super()` to call parent constructor.
- `@Override` annotation for method overriding.
- Runtime polymorphism via parent reference.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

```java
// Abstract base class
abstract class Shape {
    public abstract void draw();
    public abstract double area();
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }

    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }

    @Override
    public double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    private double length, width;

    public Rectangle(double l, double w) {
        this.length = l;
        this.width = w;
    }

    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Rectangle");
    }

    @Override
    public double area() {
        return length * width;
    }
}

public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Runtime polymorphism in action
        Shape s;

        s = new Circle(5.0);
        s.draw();    // Output: Drawing a Circle
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());

        s = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);
        s.draw();    // Output: Drawing a Rectangle
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());
    }
}
```

**Key Concepts Demonstrated:**
- Parent reference (`Shape s`) holding child objects (upcasting).
- JVM dispatches to the **actual object's overridden method** at runtime.
- Single interface, multiple behaviors — true polymorphism.

---

### ⭐ **Question B — 14 Marks** [Alternative Choice — Interfaces + Multiple Inheritance Design]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **interfaces in Java** with suitable example. Discuss how interfaces support **multiple inheritance** and the role of `default` methods introduced in Java 8. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Java program where a `SmartPhone` class **extends `Device`** and **implements `Camera`, `MusicPlayer`, and `GPS` interfaces**. Define the interfaces with at least one abstract method each and a `default` method in at least one interface. Show how the `SmartPhone` resolves method calls. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** An interface in Java is a **pure abstract type** that defines a contract of abstract methods that implementing classes must fulfill. Interfaces cannot be instantiated.

**Syntax:**
```java
interface Drawable {
    void draw();             // implicitly public abstract
    default void show() {    // default method (Java 8+)
        System.out.println("Default show");
    }
}
```

**Multiple Inheritance Support:** Java does not allow multiple inheritance of classes (Diamond Problem), but a class can implement **multiple interfaces**:
```java
class Tablet implements Touchable, Portable { ... }
```

**Role of `default` methods (Java 8):**
- Allow interfaces to provide **concrete implementation** without breaking existing implementations.
- Used to add new methods to interfaces while maintaining **backward compatibility**.
- If a class implements two interfaces with the **same default method**, the class **must override** that method to resolve ambiguity (compile-time error otherwise).

**Key Points:**
- All interface variables are implicitly `public static final` (constants).
- A class implementing an interface must implement **all its abstract methods** or be declared `abstract`.
- Interfaces support multiple inheritance through `interface C extends A, B { ... }`.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

```java
// Interfaces
interface Camera {
    void takePhoto();
    default void recordVideo() {
        System.out.println("Recording video in 1080p");
    }
}

interface MusicPlayer {
    void playMusic();
    void pauseMusic();
}

interface GPS {
    void navigate(String destination);
}

// Parent class
class Device {
    String brand;
    Device(String brand) { this.brand = brand; }
    void powerOn() { System.out.println(brand + " is ON"); }
}

// SmartPhone extends Device and implements three interfaces
class SmartPhone extends Device implements Camera, MusicPlayer, GPS {

    SmartPhone(String brand) {
        super(brand);
    }

    @Override
    public void takePhoto() {
        System.out.println("Photo clicked");
    }

    @Override
    public void playMusic() {
        System.out.println("Playing music");
    }

    @Override
    public void pauseMusic() {
        System.out.println("Music paused");
    }

    @Override
    public void navigate(String destination) {
        System.out.println("Navigating to " + destination);
    }
}

public class SmartPhoneDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SmartPhone sp = new SmartPhone("Samsung");
        sp.powerOn();
        sp.takePhoto();
        sp.recordVideo();     // inherited default method
        sp.playMusic();
        sp.navigate("Kochi");
    }
}
```

**Key Concepts Demonstrated:**
- Hybrid inheritance: single class inheritance + multiple interface implementation.
- `super(brand)` calls parent `Device` constructor.
- The `recordVideo()` method is **inherited as a default method** without overriding.
- The `SmartPhone` class is a concrete class fulfilling all interface contracts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation:** Not wrong, but shows poor practice. Examiners may deduct 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Not calling `super()` explicitly when parent has no default constructor:** Causes compile-time error — student often gets 0 for the entire program.
> 3. **Confusing overloading with overriding** in the answer — be very precise with terminology.
> 4. **Missing the `default` method explanation** in Java 8+ questions — high-weightage topic.
> 5. **Writing `interface Phone { void call(); }` without explaining implicit `public abstract`** — examiners want that detail.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Inheritance** = `extends` keyword; supports single, multilevel, hierarchical; **NOT multiple** for classes.
- ✅ **Polymorphism** = Overloading (compile-time) + Overriding (run-time via dynamic dispatch).
- ✅ **Abstract class** = Cannot be instantiated; can have abstract + concrete methods + constructors.
- ✅ **Interface** = Pure contract; `public static final` fields; `default` & `static` methods from Java 8.
- ✅ **`super`** = Refers to immediate parent; must be the first line in constructor if used.
- ✅ **`Object` class** = Root of all Java classes; 11 key methods (know `equals` + `hashCode` contract).
- ✅ **Covariant return** = Override return type can be a subtype of original.
- ✅ **Final/static/private methods** cannot be overridden.
- ✅ **Diamond problem** = Reason Java disallows multiple class inheritance.
- ✅ **Upcasting** = Implicit & safe; **Downcasting** = Explicit, can throw `ClassCastException`.
- ✅ **Open/Closed Principle** = Achieved via polymorphism (open for extension, closed for modification).
- ✅ **IS-A vs HAS-A** = IS-A → inheritance; HAS-A → composition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Design (OOD)**, an **Activity** is a fundamental behavioral modeling construct defined under the **Unified Modeling Language (UML 2.5 specification)**. An *Activity* represents a major unit of executable behavior of a system, encompassing a sequential (and sometimes concurrent) flow of coordinated steps.

An **Activity Diagram** is a **UML behavioral diagram** that visualizes the workflow of stepwise activities and actions with support for choice, iteration, and concurrency. It models the **dynamic aspects of a system** — i.e., *what happens*, *when it happens*, and *what the order of occurrences is*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Activities and Activity Diagrams are part of the **OOAD (Object Oriented Analysis and Design)** methodology that precedes Java implementation. In KTU valuation, an Activity is graded as a *node* inside an Activity Diagram that represents a unit of work performed within a state machine or workflow.

### Formal UML 2.5 Definition (Object Management Group)
> An *Activity* is the specification of parameterized behavior as the coordinated sequencing of subordinate units whose individual elements are actions. — **OMG UML 2.5.1 Specification**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **Activity Diagram as a flowchart of a recipe while cooking a dish**.

- **Each cooking step** (chopping onions, boiling water, adding salt) = an **Activity / Action node**.
- **The decision** ("Do you want it spicy or mild?") = a **Decision (Branch) node**.
- **Two parallel tasks** (boiling rice AND making curry) = **Fork/Join (Concurrency)**.
- **The starting point** ("Begin cooking") = **Initial Node**.
- **The ending point** ("Dish is ready") = **Final / Activity-Final Node**.
- **The chef and the helper** doing different tasks = **Swimlanes (Partitions)**.

Just as a recipe tells you the *order* and *parallelism* of cooking steps, an Activity Diagram tells developers the *order* and *parallelism* of business processes, method calls, or business workflows.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Terms (bolded for emphasis):**
> - **Activity** — a behavior represented as a coordinated flow of actions.
> - **Action** — the fundamental unit of executable functionality inside an activity.
> - **Token** — a conceptual traversal marker; one token = one thread of control.
> - **Pin** — an input/output parameter holder on an action.
> - **Control Flow** — directed edge connecting nodes.
> - **Object Flow** — edge carrying data objects between nodes.
> - **Initial Node** — exactly one per activity; marks start (filled circle).
> - **Activity Final Node** — terminates the entire activity (bullseye circle).
> - **Flow Final Node** — terminates only that specific path (circle with X).
> - **Decision Node** — a diamond representing branching.
> - **Merge Node** — a diamond merging multiple alternative paths.
> - **Fork Node** — splits one flow into concurrent flows (thick bar).
> - **Join Node** — synchronizes concurrent flows back into one (thick bar).
> - **Swimlane (Partition)** — organizes activities by responsible entity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of an Activity Diagram

An Activity Diagram is composed of the following building blocks. Each block has a strict UML 2.5 notation, and KTU examiners expect these *exact* shapes in board answers.

### 2.1.1 Nodes (Vertices)

| Node Type | Notation Shape | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Node** | Filled black circle ● | Start of the activity (mandatory, exactly one) |
| **Activity Final Node** | Bullseye ⊙ | Terminates the entire activity |
| **Flow Final Node** | Circle with cross ⊗ | Terminates only that control flow path |
| **Action / Activity** | Rounded rectangle | Represents a step of work |
| **Decision Node** | Diamond ◇ | Branches flow based on a guard condition |
| **Merge Node** | Diamond ◇ | Unifies alternative paths back to one |
| **Fork Node** | Thick black bar ▬ | Splits flow into concurrent threads |
| **Join Node** | Thick black bar ▬ | Synchronizes concurrent flows |
| **Object Node** | Rectangle | Holds data flowing between actions |
| **Send Signal Action** | Convex pentagon | Sends a signal to another activity |
| **Receive Signal Action** | Concave pentagon | Waits for a signal |
| **Time Event** | Hourglass | Delays a flow by elapsed time |

### 2.1.2 Edges (Connectors)

- **Control Flow** — solid arrow → (no data, just sequencing).
- **Object Flow** — solid arrow → carrying an object, with optional *pin* labels.

### 2.1.3 Partitions (Swimlanes)

Partitions divide the activity into regions, each representing a **responsible entity** (Class, Actor, Department). They are drawn as **vertical or horizontal bands**, each labeled at the top with the responsible unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Swimlanes are *extremely* frequently asked in KTU ESE Part B questions. A 7-mark question typically asks: "Draw an activity diagram for an ATM transaction system with swimlanes for Customer, ATM Machine, and Bank Server."

## 2.2 Token Flow Semantics (Why does it work?)

The UML 2.5 execution model uses **tokens** to track flow.

- A token is offered to an outgoing edge of a node only when the node's *activity* is complete.
- At a **Fork node**, a single incoming token is replicated into *N* outgoing tokens (one per parallel branch).
- At a **Join node**, the activity waits until **all** incoming branches have offered tokens, then produces a single outgoing token.
- At a **Decision node**, a guard condition `[condition]` on each outgoing edge determines which branch the token takes. **Exactly one** guard must be true at a time.
- At a **Merge node**, no synchronization occurs — the first token to arrive is passed through.

## 2.3 Activity vs. State Machine vs. Sequence Diagram

| Property | Activity Diagram | State Machine | Sequence Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Workflow of actions | Lifecycle of an object | Time-ordered message exchange |
| **Primary Use** | Business process modeling | Reactive object behavior | Inter-object communication |
| **Concurrency** | Native support (Fork/Join) | Limited | Limited (par, alt fragments) |
| **Time Dimension** | Implicit (top-to-bottom) | Explicit (state residency) | Explicit (vertical lifeline) |
| **Best For (KTU)** | Modeling methods, use-case flows | Modeling reactive subsystems | Modeling interaction protocols |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Shape | Edge Type | Cardinality Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | Filled ● | None | **Exactly 1** per activity |
| Activity-Final | ⊙ | None | At least 0; commonly 1 |
| Flow-Final | ⊗ | None | Any number |
| Action | Rounded Rect | Control or Object | Min 1 in/out |
| Decision | ◇ | Outgoing guards `[…]` | 1 in, 2+ out |
| Merge | ◇ | No guard | 2+ in, 1 out |
| Fork | ▬ | Implicit AND | 1 in, 2+ out |
| Join | ▬ | Implicit AND-sync | 2+ in, 1 out |
| Partition | Swimlane band | Encloses nodes | Each node ∈ 1 partition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Activity Diagrams are the foundation of **Business Process Modeling (BPMN)**, **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) workflow design**, **embedded systems state-flow design**, and **UML-driven code generation tools** (e.g., Rational Rose, Enterprise Architect, Visual Paradigm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm: Constructing a KTU-Standard Activity Diagram

Every board-answer diagram follows a deterministic 6-step construction. We will derive this algorithmically.

**Step 1 — Identify the use case scope.**
Read the problem statement; determine the *single high-level process* to be modeled (e.g., "Online Book Purchase").

**Step 2 — Enumerate the activities.**
List the discrete actions: `Login → Search Book → Add to Cart → Checkout → Pay → Confirm`.

**Step 3 — Identify decision points.**
Look for conditional words: *if, whether, depending on*. Each maps to a **Decision/Merge diamond** with guards.

**Step 4 — Identify concurrent flows.**
Look for *parallel* words: *simultaneously, while, in parallel*. Each maps to a **Fork/Join** bar.

**Step 5 — Assign swimlanes.**
Map each activity to the responsible entity (Customer, System, Payment Gateway).

**Step 6 — Attach initial and final nodes.**
Place **one** Initial node at start. Attach **one or more** Activity-Final nodes at the end.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — ATM Cash Withdrawal

We will derive the Activity Diagram for a 7-mark KTU question:

> *"Draw an Activity Diagram with swimlanes for an ATM cash withdrawal process."*

### Step-by-step textual derivation:

1. **Initial node** placed in the **Customer** swimlane.
2. Customer performs: *Insert Card*, *Enter PIN*, *Select Withdrawal*, *Enter Amount*, *Collect Cash*, *Take Receipt*.
3. After *Enter PIN*, the **ATM Machine** validates PIN → Decision: *Valid?*
4. If Invalid → *Display Error* → *Eject Card* → Flow-Final Node.
5. If Valid → ATM sends request to **Bank Server** → Server checks balance.
6. Decision: *Sufficient Balance?*
7. Fork into two parallel actions: *Dispense Cash* and *Print Receipt*.
8. Join → *Eject Card* → *Take Card*.
9. Activity-Final node in Customer lane.

### Equivalent Mermaid Flowchart Representation

The following Mermaid block models the **logical flow** of the ATM process (note: Mermaid does not natively support swimlanes, so we partition via subgraphs — this is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture* mapping).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initC([Initial Node - Customer])
    act1[/Insert Card/]
    act2[/Enter PIN/]
    dec1{Diamond Decision - PIN Valid}
    act3[/Display Error/]
    act4[/Eject Card Invalid/]
    flowEnd1([Flow-Final - Invalid Path])
    act5[/Select Withdrawal/]
    act6[/Enter Amount/]
    act7[/ATM Validates with Bank Server/]
    dec2{Diamond Decision - Sufficient Balance}
    act8[/Display Insufficient Funds/]
    flowEnd2([Flow-Final - No Funds])
    fork1[["Fork Node - Parallel"]]
    act9[/Dispense Cash/]
    act10[/Print Receipt/]
    join1[["Join Node - Synchronize"]]
    act11[/Eject Card/]
    act12[/Take Card and Cash/]
    finalNode([Activity-Final Node])

    initC --> act1 --> act2 --> dec1
    dec1 -- No --> act3 --> act4 --> flowEnd1
    dec1 -- Yes --> act5 --> act6 --> act7 --> dec2
    dec2 -- No --> act8 --> flowEnd2
    dec2 -- Yes --> fork1
    fork1 --> act9 --> join1
    fork1 --> act10 --> join1
    join1 --> act11 --> act12 --> finalNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how the **Fork node** splits one control flow into *two parallel paths* (Dispense Cash + Print Receipt), and the **Join node** synchronizes them back. This is the formal notation for concurrency in UML 2.5.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Online Shopping Checkout

For a 7-mark Part B sub-question:
> *"Draw an Activity Diagram for an Online Shopping Checkout process including user authentication, payment validation, and concurrent email/SMS confirmation."*

### Derived Step-by-Step Solution:

1. **Swimlanes:** *Customer*, *Web Application*, *Payment Gateway*, *Notification Service*.
2. Activities: *Browse → Add to Cart → Login → Checkout → Choose Payment → Pay → [Fork: Send Email + Send SMS] → Join → Display Order Confirmation → Logout*.
3. Decisions: *Login Successful?*, *Payment Approved?*, *Cart Empty?*
4. Flow-Final nodes for: failed login, declined payment.

### Mermaid Block Diagram (Logical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Initial - Customer Lane])
    B[/Browse Products/]
    C[/Add to Cart/]
    D[/Proceed to Checkout/]
    E[/Enter Credentials/]
    F{Login Successful?}
    G[/Show Error/]
    H([Flow-Final - Login Failed])
    I[/Choose Payment Method/]
    J[/Submit Payment/]
    K{Payment Approved?}
    L[/Show Declined Message/]
    M([Flow-Final - Payment Declined])
    N[[Fork - Notification Parallel]]
    O[/Send Confirmation Email/]
    P[/Send Confirmation SMS/]
    Q[[Join - Sync Notifications]]
    R[/Show Order Confirmation/]
    S[/Logout/]
    T([Activity-Final])

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
    F -- No --> G --> H
    F -- Yes --> I --> J --> K
    K -- No --> L --> M
    K -- Yes --> N
    N --> O --> Q
    N --> P --> Q
    Q --> R --> S --> T
```

## 3.4 Guard Condition Grammar (Formal Derivation)

A guard condition is a **Boolean expression** placed in square brackets `[...]` on the outgoing edge of a Decision node.

$$
\text{Guard} ::= \texttt{[} \;\; \text{BooleanExpression} \;\; \texttt{]}
$$

$$
\text{BooleanExpression} ::= \text{Expression} \;\; ( \; \texttt{AND} \mid \texttt{OR} \; ) \;\; \text{Expression} \mid \text{UnaryCondition}
$$

$$
\text{UnaryCondition} ::= \texttt{NOT} \;\; \text{Expression} \mid \text{Variable} \;\; ( \; \texttt{>} \mid \texttt{<} \mid \texttt{==} \mid \texttt{>=} \mid \texttt{<=} \mid \texttt{!=} \; ) \;\; \text{Value}
$$

### KTU Board Convention for Guard Labeling:
- Use **square brackets** `[]` around the condition.
- Place the label **directly on the edge**, not on the node.
- Each outgoing edge of a Decision node must have **at most one** guard.
- The special guard `else` is permitted for the *default* path.

> [!TIP]
> **Example valid guard labels:** `[balance >= amount]`, `[isValid == true]`, `[userType == "Admin"]`, `[else]`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Notation Reference — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following block diagram exhaustively maps the **notation → meaning → KTU usage** of every Activity Diagram component.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NODES["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM NODE TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        N1["INITIAL NODE - Filled Black Circle - Marks Start"]
        N2["ACTIVITY FINAL - Bullseye Circle - Ends Entire Activity"]
        N3["FLOW FINAL - Circle with X - Ends One Path Only"]
        N4["ACTION - Rounded Rectangle - Single Unit of Work"]
        N5["ACTIVITY - Rounded Rectangle with Icon - Compound Behavior"]
        N6["OBJECT NODE - Rectangle - Holds Data in Object Flow"]
        N7["DECISION OR MERGE - Diamond - Branching Logic"]
        N8["FORK OR JOIN - Thick Black Bar - Concurrency Boundary"]
        N9["SEND SIGNAL - Convex Pentagon - Outbound Asynchronous"]
        N10["RECEIVE SIGNAL - Concave Pentagon - Inbound Asynchronous"]
        N11["TIME EVENT - Hourglass - Temporal Delay"]
    end

    subgraph EDGES["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM EDGE TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        E1["CONTROL FLOW - Solid Arrow - Sequencing Without Data"]
        E2["OBJECT FLOW - Solid Arrow - Carries Object Token"]
    end

    subgraph GROUPING["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM GROUPING TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        G1["SWIMLANE PARTITION - Vertical or Horizontal Band - Responsibility"]
        G2["ACTIVITY GROUP - Dashed Rounded Rectangle - Logical Grouping"]
    end

    subgraph GUARDS["GUARD CONDITION TAXONOMY"]
        direction TB
        Q1["BOOLEAN EXPRESSION - Square Bracket Label - Decision Branch"]
        Q2["ELSE CLAUSE - Default Fallback - Catch All"]
    end

    N1 --> N4
    N4 --> N7
    N7 --> N2
    N4 --> N8
    N8 --> N4
    E1 --> E2
    G1 --> N4
    Q1 --> N7
```

## 4.2 Activity Diagram Construction Workflow — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 - Read Problem Statement] --> stepB[Step 2 - Identify Actors and Lanes]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 - Enumerate Sequential Actions]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 - Locate Decision Points]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 - Locate Parallel Sections]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 - Insert Initial and Final Nodes]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 - Apply Guard Conditions]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 - Validate with KTU Notation Rules]
```

## 4.3 Activity Diagram vs. State Machine vs. Sequence — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPARISON["PURPOSE COMPARISON OF UML BEHAVIORAL DIAGRAMS"]
        direction TB
        A1["ACTIVITY DIAGRAM - Workflow and Business Process Modeling"]
        A2["STATE MACHINE DIAGRAM - Reactive Object Lifecycle Modeling"]
        A3["SEQUENCE DIAGRAM - Time-Ordered Inter Object Messaging"]
    end

    A1 --> USE1[Use Case - ATM Cash Withdrawal]
    A2 --> USE2[Use Case - Login Session States]
    A3 --> USE3[Use Case - Method Call Between Classes]
```

## 4.4 Token Flow Semantics — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0([Token at Initial Node])
    T0 --> T1[Token Traverses Action A]
    T1 --> T2[Token Reaches Decision Node]
    T2 -- Guard True --> T3[Token Moves to Branch X]
    T2 -- Guard Else --> T4[Token Moves to Branch Y]
    T1 --> F1[Token Reaches Fork Node]
    F1 --> F2[Token Replicated into Two]
    F2 --> F3[Branch Alpha Executes]
    F2 --> F4[Branch Beta Executes]
    F3 --> J1[Join Node Waits]
    F4 --> J1
    J1 --> J2[Synchronized Single Token Continues]
    J2 --> FN([Activity Final Node Consumed])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1(a)]**
*Define an Activity Diagram. List any four components used in it.*

#### Model Answer:
An **Activity Diagram** is a UML behavioral diagram used to model the dynamic aspects of a system. It represents the workflow of activities and actions, including sequential, conditional, and concurrent flows.

Four components are:
1. **Initial Node** — Filled circle marking the start.
2. **Activity Final Node** — Bullseye circle marking termination.
3. **Decision Node** — Diamond for branching with guard conditions.
4. **Fork/Join Node** — Thick bar for concurrent flows.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2(b)]**
*What is the difference between a Decision node and a Fork node in an Activity Diagram?*

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Decision Node | Fork Node |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Diamond ◇ | Thick black bar ▬ |
| **Purpose** | Branches flow based on guard conditions | Splits flow into concurrent parallel paths |
| **Synchronization** | Does NOT synchronize | No synchronization (Join node does that) |
| **Tokens** | Routes a single token to one branch | Replicates a single token to multiple parallel branches |
| **Use Case** | *if-else* logic | Parallel activities |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q11(a,b)]**

> **(a)** Draw an Activity Diagram with swimlanes for an **Online Train Reservation System**. The diagram should include user login, train search, seat availability check, payment processing, and ticket generation. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the roles of **Decision node**, **Fork-Join node**, **Swimlane**, and **Activity Final node** with reference to the diagram drawn in part (a). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Initial - Customer Lane])
    B[/Open Website/]
    C[/Enter Username and Password/]
    D{Login Successful?}
    E[/Show Error Message/]
    F([Flow-Final - Login Failed])
    G[/Search Train by Date and Destination/]
    H[/Select Train/]
    I{Seats Available?}
    J[/Add to Waitlist/]
    K[/Display No Seats Message/]
    L([Flow-Final - No Seats])
    M[/Proceed to Payment/]
    N[/Enter Card Details/]
    O{Payment Approved?}
    P[/Show Declined Notification/]
    Q[[Fork - Parallel Notification]]
    R[/Send Email Ticket/]
    S[/Send SMS Confirmation/]
    T[[Join - Sync Notifications]]
    U[/Display Booking Confirmation/]
    V[/Logout/]
    W([Activity-Final - End])

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -- No --> E --> F
    D -- Yes --> G --> H --> I
    I -- No --> J
    J --> K --> L
    I -- Yes --> M --> N --> O
    O -- No --> P
    P --> W
    O -- Yes --> Q
    Q --> R --> T
    Q --> S --> T
    T --> U --> V --> W
```

**Part (b) — Explanation:**

1. **Decision Node** (D, I, O): These diamonds represent conditional branching. At *Login Successful?*, the flow routes to either *Show Error* (false) or *Search Train* (true). Guards like `[loginValid == true]` should be labeled on each outgoing edge.

2. **Fork–Join Nodes** (Q and T): The **Fork** splits the flow into two parallel branches: *Send Email Ticket* and *Send SMS Confirmation*. The **Join** synchronizes them — the system only proceeds to *Display Booking Confirmation* once both notifications are dispatched.

3. **Swimlane (Partition)**: Although the Mermaid approximation is a single flow, in hand-drawn exam answers the diagram must be divided into vertical swimlanes for **Customer**, **Web Application Server**, and **Payment Gateway** to clarify responsibility.

4. **Activity Final Node** (W): The bullseye circle ⊙ terminates the entire activity, reached after *Logout*. In contrast, the *Flow-Final* nodes (F, L) terminate only their respective failed-login and no-seat branches.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Activity-Final (⊙) with Flow-Final (⊗).** Many students draw the wrong symbol — this costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Do not place a guard on a Merge node** — guards belong only on outgoing edges of Decision nodes.
> 3. **Do not skip the Initial node.** Even if obvious, it is mandatory and omitting it costs a mark.
> 4. **Swimlane labels must be at the top of the band** — examiners check this strictly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q12(a,b)]**

> **(a)** Draw an Activity Diagram for a **Hospital Patient Admission System** including registration, doctor assignment, ward allocation, and billing. Show at least one **Fork-Join** and one **Decision-Merge**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **Activity Diagram** and **Sequence Diagram**. Mention any five differences. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Initial - Patient Lane])
    B[/Patient Arrives at Reception/]
    C[/Fill Registration Form/]
    D[/Enter Details into System/]
    E{New Patient?}
    F[/Create New Medical Record/]
    G[/Retrieve Existing Medical Record/]
    H[/Assign Doctor Based on Symptoms/]
    I{Doctor Available?}
    J[/Add to Appointment Queue/]
    K[/Generate Token Number/]
    L[/Doctor Consultation/]
    M{Admission Required?}
    N[/Discharge with Prescription/]
    O([Flow-Final - No Admission])
    P[[Fork - Parallel Ward Tasks]]
    Q[/Allocate Ward and Bed/]
    R[/Generate Admission Slip/]
    S[[Join - Sync Ward Tasks]]
    T[/Update Ward Occupancy/]
    U[/Calculate Treatment Cost/]
    V[/Generate Bill/]
    W[/Patient Pays and Receives Receipt/]
    X([Activity-Final - End])

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F --> H
    E -- No --> G --> H
    H --> I
    I -- No --> J --> K --> L --> M
    I -- Yes --> L --> M
    M -- No --> N --> O
    M -- Yes --> P
    P --> Q --> S
    P --> R --> S
    S --> T --> U --> V --> W --> X
```

**Part (b) — Five Differences (Activity vs Sequence Diagram):**

| # | Aspect | Activity Diagram | Sequence Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary Focus** | Workflow of activities inside a process | Time-ordered message passing between objects |
| 2 | **Notation Center** | Activities, actions, decisions, forks | Objects, lifelines, activations, messages |
| 3 | **Concurrency** | First-class support (Fork/Join) | Limited (only `par` and `alt` fragments) |
| 4 | **Time Dimension** | Implicit (top-to-bottom flow) | Explicit (vertical lifeline with time progressing downward) |
| 5 | **Best Use Case** | Business process modeling, use-case bodies | Method invocation flow between classes |
| 6 | **UML Category** | Behavior diagram (process view) | Behavior diagram (interaction view) |
| 7 | **Swimlanes** | Supported natively | Not supported |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Q-B:**
> 1. Students often confuse *Activity* with *State*. Remember: an **Action** in an Activity Diagram is **instantaneous**, while a **State** in a State Machine has **duration**.
> 2. The Fork bar must be drawn as a **solid thick black bar** — not a diamond. Drawing a diamond for fork will lose 2 marks.
> 3. Guard conditions must be in **square brackets** `[]` — parentheses `()` or curly braces `{}` are incorrect UML notation and lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Activity Diagram = UML 2.5 behavioral diagram** for modeling workflow and business processes.
- **Initial Node** = filled black circle ●, **exactly one** per activity.
- **Activity Final Node** = bullseye ⊙, **terminates the entire activity**.
- **Flow Final Node** = circle with cross ⊗, **terminates only one path**.
- **Decision Node** = diamond ◇, branches using **guard conditions in square brackets** `[]`.
- **Merge Node** = diamond ◇, no guards, just unites alternative paths.
- **Fork Node** = thick black bar ▬, splits into **parallel concurrent** flows.
- **Join Node** = thick black bar ▬, **synchronizes** parallel flows into one.
- **Swimlane (Partition)** = vertical/horizontal band **labeled at the top** with the responsible entity (Actor, Class, System).
- **Action** = rounded rectangle, represents a **single unit of work**.
- **Object Node** = rectangle, holds **data** flowing between actions.
- **Control Flow** = simple arrow (sequencing, no data).
- **Object Flow** = arrow carrying a data object.
- **Guard syntax** = `[condition]`, e.g., `[balance >= 1000]`, `[else]`.
- **Concurrency** is the **killer feature** of Activity Diagrams — always look for parallel words in the problem statement.
- **Difference table** must be memorized: Activity vs Sequence vs State Machine (Focus, Concurrency, Time, Use Case).
- **Token-based execution**: tokens flow through nodes; Join waits for all incoming tokens.
- **Send/Receive Signal** actions = pentagon shapes (convex = send, concave = receive).
- **KTU marking** — typically 7 marks for the diagram and 7 marks for the explanation/justification. **Drawing accuracy is half the marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Managing System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Managing System Design" in OOD using Java?

In the context of **Object Oriented Design using Java**, **Managing System Design** refers to the strategic organization, persistence, and manipulation of objects and data within a software system. It encompasses the architectural decisions and Java mechanisms used to **store, retrieve, serialize, and manipulate collections of objects** efficiently while preserving the principles of OOD (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction).

The three pillars of Managing System Design in KTU's PEITT411 syllabus are:

1. **File I/O Management** — Persisting object state to secondary storage.
2. **Serialization & Deserialization** — Converting objects to byte streams and vice versa.
3. **Collections Framework & Generics** — Managing groups of objects dynamically with type safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Managing System Design* is the phase of OO software engineering where the developer decides **how objects will be stored, accessed, transferred, and grouped** using Java's built-in infrastructure (`java.io`, `java.nio`, `java.util` packages).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a **large postal warehouse** 📦:

| Warehouse Element | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Different **storage boxes** for different parcel types | **Collections** (`ArrayList`, `HashSet`, `HashMap`) |
| A **shipping manifest** that records parcel details on paper | **Serialization** (writing object state to a file) |
| A **conveyor belt** carrying parcels in/out | **Streams** (`InputStream`, `OutputStream`) |
| A **label printer** ensuring boxes are correctly categorized | **Generics** (`<String>`, `<Integer>`) |
| The **delivery truck** (the actual program using these) | The **Application / Driver Class** |

Just as a warehouse manager chooses *which box* to use for *which parcel* and *how to log* every shipment, a Java developer managing system design chooses *which collection* to use, *how to persist* objects, and *how to ensure type safety*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The "managing" in Managing System Design does **NOT** mean project management. It means **runtime data management** — how your objects live, persist, and interact with each other in memory and on disk.

---

## 1.3 The Three Core Java Packages Involved

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU question on this topic will revolve around these three packages:
> - `java.io` → File and Stream operations
> - `java.util` → Collections Framework
> - `java.lang` → Auto-boxing, generics support, `Comparable` interface

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in OOD

In real-world OO systems (banking, e-commerce, hospital management), objects must be:
- **Grouped** logically (e.g., list of all `Customer` objects) → **Collections**
- **Saved** when the program ends (e.g., account data must survive a restart) → **File I/O + Serialization**
- **Transferred** across networks (e.g., sending an `Order` object to a server) → **Serialization**
- **Type-safe** to avoid runtime `ClassCastException` → **Generics**

Without these management mechanisms, an OO design is **incomplete and non-functional** in any enterprise scenario.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 File I/O Management — The Two Stream Hierarchies

Java treats all I/O as **streams** — a sequence of data flowing from a source to a destination. There are **two parallel hierarchies** in `java.io`:

### 2.1.1 Byte Streams (Binary Data — 8-bit)
Used for **images, audio, PDFs, serialized objects**.

```
InputStream  (abstract)
   ├── FileInputStream
   ├── BufferedInputStream
   ├── ObjectInputStream      ← for Deserialization
   └── DataInputStream

OutputStream (abstract)
   ├── FileOutputStream
   ├── BufferedOutputStream
   ├── ObjectOutputStream     ← for Serialization
   └── PrintStream
```

### 2.1.2 Character Streams (Text Data — 16-bit Unicode)
Used for **text files (.txt, .csv, .json)**.

```
Reader (abstract)
   ├── FileReader
   ├── BufferedReader
   └── InputStreamReader   (bridge: bytes → chars)

Writer (abstract)
   ├── FileWriter
   ├── BufferedWriter
   ├── PrintWriter
   └── OutputStreamWriter  (bridge: chars → bytes)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Why do we wrap FileInputStream inside BufferedInputStream?"*
> **Answer:** `BufferedInputStream` adds an internal buffer (default **8192 bytes**) that **reduces direct disk access**, dramatically improving read performance. This is the **Decorator Design Pattern** in action.

---

## 2.2 Serialization Theory

**Serialization** is the process of converting a Java object's state into a **byte stream**, so it can be:
- Saved to a file
- Sent over a network
- Stored in a database BLOB column

**Deserialization** is the reverse process.

### 2.2.1 Conditions for Serialization
1. The class must implement the **`java.io.Serializable`** marker interface (it has no methods).
2. All instance variables must be either:
   - `Serializable`, OR
   - Marked **`transient`** (excluded from serialization).
3. The class should declare a **`serialVersionUID`** to control versioning.

### 2.2.2 The `transient` Keyword
Fields marked `transient` are **skipped** during serialization. Upon deserialization, they receive **default values** (`null` for objects, `0` for `int`, `false` for `boolean`).

### 2.2.3 The `serialVersionUID`
A `long` constant used for **version control**. If the class structure changes incompatibly, deserialization throws `InvalidClassException`.

---

## 2.3 Collections Framework — The Master Architecture

The **Java Collections Framework (JCF)** is a unified architecture for representing and manipulating groups of objects.

### 2.3.1 The Core Interface Hierarchy

```
                Iterable<T>
                    |
                Collection<T>
            /--------+--------\
           /         |         \
         List       Set       Queue
          |          |          |
     (Ordered,   (No           (FIFO /
     duplicates) duplicates)  Priority)
```

### 2.3.2 The Map Hierarchy (Separate from Collection)

```
                Map<K,V>  (not a Collection!)
            /------+------+------\
           /       |      |       \
       HashMap   TreeMap Hashtable  LinkedHashMap
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** `Map<K,V>` does **NOT** extend `Collection<E>`. It is a **separate root interface** because it stores **key-value pairs**, not single elements.

---

## 2.4 Generics — Type Safety at Compile Time

**Generics** enable types (classes and methods) to be **parameters** when defining classes, interfaces, and methods — much like formal parameters in method declarations.

### Syntax:
```java
ClassName<TypeParameter> variable = new ClassName<>();
```

### Why Generics?
| Without Generics | With Generics |
|---|---|
| `List list = new ArrayList();` | `List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();` |
| Allows mixed types (unsafe) | Compile-time type check |
| Requires manual casting | No casting needed (`Diamond operator <>`, Java 7+) |
| Runtime `ClassCastException` risk | Errors caught at compile time |

### Generic Class Declaration:
```java
class Box<T> {
    private T content;
    public void set(T item) { this.content = item; }
    public T get() { return content; }
}
```

### Bounded Type Parameters:
```java
<T extends Number>   // T must be Number or subclass
<T super Integer>    // T must be Integer or superclass (wildcard lower bound)
```

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU ESE 2024**

| Concept | Key Class/Interface | Package | Critical Method | Return Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Read text file line-by-line | `BufferedReader` | `java.io` | `readLine()` | `String` |
| Write text to file | `FileWriter` | `java.io` | `write(String)` | `void` |
| Read binary file | `FileInputStream` | `java.io` | `read()` | `int` |
| Serialize object | `ObjectOutputStream` | `java.io` | `writeObject(Object)` | `void` |
| Deserialize object | `ObjectInputStream` | `java.io` | `readObject()` | `Object` |
| Dynamic resizable array | `ArrayList<E>` | `java.util` | `add(E)`, `get(int)` | `boolean`, `E` |
| Hash table based set | `HashSet<E>` | `java.util` | `add(E)`, `contains(Object)` | `boolean` |
| Key-value pairs | `HashMap<K,V>` | `java.util` | `put(K,V)`, `get(K)` | `V`, `V` |
| Sorted keys (Red-Black tree) | `TreeMap<K,V>` | `java.util` | `put(K,V)` | `V` |
| Type-safe collection | `Collection<E>` | `java.util` | All methods use `E` | varies |
| Marker interface for persistence | `Serializable` | `java.io` | *(no methods)* | — |
| Exclude field from serialization | `transient` keyword | `java.lang` | — | — |

### 2.5.1 Performance Characteristics (Time Complexity)

| Collection | `add` | `get` | `contains` | `remove` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ArrayList` | Amortized O(1) | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) |
| `LinkedList` | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) |
| `HashSet` | O(1) avg | — | O(1) avg | O(1) avg |
| `TreeSet` | O(log n) | — | O(log n) | O(log n) |
| `HashMap` | O(1) avg | O(1) avg | O(1) avg | O(1) avg |
| `TreeMap` | O(log n) | O(log n) | O(log n) | O(log n) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Choosing the wrong collection is the **#1 cause of performance bottlenecks** in Java enterprise applications. KTU expects you to justify your collection choice in design questions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking System** → `HashMap<AccountNumber, Account>` for O(1) account lookup.
- **Student Records** → `ArrayList<Student>` for sequential, indexed access.
- **Unique Usernames** → `HashSet<String>` to prevent duplicates.
- **Saving Game State** → `ObjectOutputStream` to serialize the entire `GameState` object.
- **Configuration Files** → `Properties` (extends `Hashtable`) for key-value text config.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Writing to and Reading from a Text File

**Problem:** Create a Java program that writes three student names to a file `students.txt`, then reads them back and displays them on the console.

### Step 1: Understand the requirement
We need **two streams**:
- A `Writer` (output) to save data
- A `Reader` (input) to load data

### Step 2: Write operation (Save)

```java
import java.io.FileWriter;
import java.io.IOException;

public class WriteDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            FileWriter fw = new FileWriter("students.txt");
            fw.write("Anand\n");
            fw.write("Bhavna\n");
            fw.write("Chitra\n");
            fw.close();
            System.out.println("Data written successfully.");
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Logic explanation (each line):**

| Line | Why it is written |
|---|---|
| `FileWriter fw = new FileWriter("students.txt");` | Opens file for writing. **Throws `FileNotFoundException` (a subclass of `IOException`)** if path is invalid. |
| `fw.write("Anand\n");` | Writes the string and an explicit newline character. |
| `fw.close();` | **Critical:** Flushes the OS buffer to disk. Without this, data may be lost. |
| `catch (IOException e)` | Catches any I/O failure (disk full, permission denied, etc.). |

### Step 3: Read operation (Load)

```java
import java.io.BufferedReader;
import java.io.FileReader;
import java.io.IOException;

public class ReadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("students.txt"));
            String line;
            while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
                System.out.println("Student: " + line);
            }
            br.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Logic explanation:**

| Line | Why it is written |
|---|---|
| `BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("students.txt"));` | **Decorator pattern** — `BufferedReader` wraps `FileReader` to add a 8192-char buffer. |
| `while ((line = br.readLine()) != null)` | `readLine()` returns `null` at **End Of File (EOF)** — the loop termination condition. |
| `br.close();` | Closes the **outermost** wrapper; it cascades to close `FileReader` too. |

### Step 4: Try-with-resources (Modern Java — KTU expects this!)

```java
try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("students.txt"))) {
    String line;
    while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
        System.out.println(line);
    }
} catch (IOException e) {
    e.printStackTrace();
}
```

**Why preferred:** The resource `br` is **automatically closed** even if an exception occurs (AutoCloseable interface — Java 7+).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Serialization of a Custom Object

**Problem:** Serialize and deserialize a `Student` object containing `name`, `rollNo`, and a `transient` field `password`.

### Step 1: Define the Serializable class

```java
import java.io.Serializable;

public class Student implements Serializable {
    private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;

    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private transient String password;  // Will NOT be serialized

    public Student(String name, int rollNo, String password) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.password = password;
    }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Student{name='" + name + "', rollNo=" + rollNo 
               + ", password='" + password + "'}";
    }
}
```

### Step 2: Serialize (Save to file `student.ser`)

```java
import java.io.FileOutputStream;
import java.io.ObjectOutputStream;

public class SerializeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student("Anand", 101, "secret123");
        try (ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream(
                new FileOutputStream("student.ser"))) {
            oos.writeObject(s);
            System.out.println("Object serialized.");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Deserialize (Load from file)

```java
import java.io.FileInputStream;
import java.io.ObjectInputStream;

public class DeserializeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try (ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream(
                new FileInputStream("student.ser"))) {
            Student s = (Student) ois.readObject();
            System.out.println("Deserialized: " + s);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Trace the output

The deserialized object's `password` will be **`null`** because:
1. `password` is declared `transient`.
2. The JVM skips it during `writeObject`.
3. During `readObject`, the field is initialized to its **default value** (`null` for reference types).

**Expected Output:**

$$ \text{Deserialized: Student\{name='Anand', rollNo=101, password='null'\}} $$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Generics with Bounded Type Parameter

**Problem:** Create a generic class `Calculator<T extends Number>` that can add two numbers and return their sum as a `double`.

```java
class Calculator<T extends Number> {
    private T a, b;

    public Calculator(T a, T b) {
        this.a = a;
        this.b = b;
    }

    public double add() {
        return a.doubleValue() + b.doubleValue();
    }
}

public class GenericDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator<Integer> intCalc = new Calculator<>(10, 20);
        System.out.println("Integer Sum: " + intCalc.add());

        Calculator<Double> dblCalc = new Calculator<>(5.5, 4.5);
        System.out.println("Double Sum: " + dblCalc.add());

        // Calculator<String> strCalc = new Calculator<>("Hi", "Hello");
        // COMPILE ERROR! String does not extend Number.
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$ \text{Integer Sum: 30.0} $$
$$ \text{Double Sum: 10.0} $$

**Why the bound works:** `T extends Number` guarantees that `T` is either `Number` or a subclass (`Integer`, `Double`, `Float`, etc.). This allows safe invocation of `a.doubleValue()` and `b.doubleValue()`.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Collection

| Requirement | Best Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Frequent `get(index)` access | `ArrayList` | O(1) random access via internal array |
| Frequent insert/delete in middle | `LinkedList` | O(1) insertion/deletion at known position |
| No duplicates, fast lookup | `HashSet` | O(1) average `contains()` |
| Sorted unique elements | `TreeSet` | O(log n) operations + sorted iteration |
| Key-value mapping, fast lookup | `HashMap` | O(1) average `get(key)` |
| Sorted key iteration | `TreeMap` | O(log n) operations + sorted keys |
| Thread-safe legacy | `Hashtable`, `Vector` | Synchronized (legacy) — avoid in new code |
| Insertion-order preserved map | `LinkedHashMap` | Hash table + doubly-linked list |
| Concurrent access from multiple threads | `ConcurrentHashMap` | High-performance thread-safe map |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** If the question says *"fast lookup of student roll numbers"* → answer is **`HashSet<Integer>`** (or `HashMap<Integer, Student>`). If it says *"display students in alphabetical order"* → answer is **`TreeSet<String>`** or **`TreeMap`**.

---

## 3.5 Common Pitfalls and Their Mathematical/Logical Analysis

| Pitfall | What Happens | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting `close()` on stream | Data loss (buffer not flushed) | Use try-with-resources |
| Serializing a non-Serializable field | `NotSerializableException` | Mark field `transient` or make class `Serializable` |
| Modifying class after serialization | `InvalidClassException` | Use `serialVersionUID` |
| Using raw `List` then casting | `ClassCastException` at runtime | Use `List<String>` generics |
| Calling `get(int)` on empty `ArrayList` | `IndexOutOfBoundsException` | Check `isEmpty()` first |
| `HashMap` with custom object keys | `get()` returns `null` | Override `equals()` and `hashCode()` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Java I/O Class Hierarchy (The Two Towers)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Object - root of all Java classes]
    A0 --> B1[InputStream - abstract]
    A0 --> B2[OutputStream - abstract]
    A0 --> C1[Reader - abstract]
    A0 --> C2[Writer - abstract]

    B1 --> B1a[FileInputStream]
    B1 --> B1b[BufferedInputStream]
    B1 --> B1c[ObjectInputStream]
    B1 --> B1d[DataInputStream]

    B2 --> B2a[FileOutputStream]
    B2 --> B2b[BufferedOutputStream]
    B2 --> B2c[ObjectOutputStream]
    B2 --> B2d[PrintStream]

    C1 --> C1a[FileReader]
    C1 --> C1b[BufferedReader]
    C1 --> C1c[InputStreamReader]

    C2 --> C2a[FileWriter]
    C2 --> C2b[BufferedWriter]
    C2 --> C2c[PrintWriter]
    C2 --> C2d[OutputStreamWriter]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The left side (`InputStream`/`OutputStream`) handles **8-bit binary data**. The right side (`Reader`/`Writer`) handles **16-bit Unicode characters**. Bridge classes (`InputStreamReader`/`OutputStreamWriter`) allow interconversion.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Collections Framework Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT0[Iterable T]
    ROOT1[Map K V - separate root]

    ROOT0 --> COLL[Collection T]
    COLL --> LIST[List - ordered duplicates allowed]
    COLL --> SET[Set - no duplicates]
    COLL --> QUEUE[Queue - FIFO ordering]

    LIST --> AL[ArrayList]
    LIST --> LL[LinkedList]
    LIST --> VEC[Vector legacy]

    SET --> HS[HashSet]
    SET --> TS[TreeSet sorted]
    SET --> LS[LinkedHashSet insertion order]

    QUEUE --> PQ[PriorityQueue]
    QUEUE --> LQ[LinkedList also Queue]

    ROOT1 --> HM[HashMap]
    ROOT1 --> TM[TreeMap sorted keys]
    ROOT1 --> LHM[LinkedHashMap insertion order]
    ROOT1 --> HT[Hashtable legacy synchronized]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Serialization Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBJ1[Java Object in Memory] -->|writeObject| OOS[ObjectOutputStream]
    OOS -->|byte stream| FOS[FileOutputStream]
    FOS -->|writes| DISK[(File on Disk .ser)]

    DISK -->|reads| FIS[FileInputStream]
    FIS -->|byte stream| OIS[ObjectInputStream]
    OIS -->|readObject| OBJ2[Reconstructed Java Object]

    CHECK{Class implements Serializable?}
    OBJ1 --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|No| ERR1[Throws NotSerializableException]
    CHECK -->|Yes| OOS

    CHECK2{Field marked transient?}
    OOS --> CHECK2
    CHECK2 -->|Yes| SKIP[Field skipped default value used]
    CHECK2 -->|No| DISK
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decorator Pattern in I/O (Why We Wrap Streams)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Client Code] --> B[BufferedReader - outermost wrapper]
    B --> C[FileReader - middle wrapper]
    C --> D[File students.txt - actual resource]

    B -.->|adds 8192 char buffer| BX[Performance boost]
    C -.->|reads raw chars| CX[Connects to file]
    D -.->|stores data| DX[Persistent storage]
```

**Key concept:** Each wrapper **adds a responsibility** without modifying the inner class. This is the **Decorator Design Pattern** — a KTU high-weightage topic often asked alongside Managing System Design.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Try-With-Resources Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as main method
    participant T as try-with-resources block
    participant R as Resource e.g. BufferedReader
    participant FS as FileSystem

    M->>T: Enter try block
    T->>R: Open resource
    R->>FS: Connect to file
    Note over T,R: If exception occurs
    T->>R: Auto-close invoked
    R->>FS: Close file handle
    T->>M: Exception caught in catch
    Note over T,R: If no exception
    T->>R: Normal close at block end
    T->>M: Continue execution
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**What is serialization in Java? Explain the role of the `transient` keyword.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Serialization is the process of converting a Java object's state into a **byte stream** so that it can be saved to a file, sent over a network, or stored in a database. It is achieved by implementing the **`java.io.Serializable`** marker interface and using `ObjectOutputStream.writeObject()`.

The `transient` keyword is used to **exclude specific instance variables** from the serialization process. When an object is serialized, transient fields are skipped. Upon deserialization, these fields are restored to their **default values** (`null` for objects, `0` for numeric types, `false` for booleans).

**Example:**
```java
private transient String password;  // Not serialized
```

**Use case:** Sensitive data like passwords, security tokens, or session-specific fields should be marked `transient` to prevent them from being written to disk.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, S3 CSE]**
**Differentiate between `ArrayList` and `LinkedList` in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `ArrayList` | `LinkedList` |
|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Data Structure** | Dynamic resizable array | Doubly-linked list |
| **Access (`get(i)`)** | O(1) — direct index | O(n) — traversal needed |
| **Insertion/Deletion at end** | Amortized O(1) | O(1) |
| **Insertion/Deletion in middle** | O(n) — shifting required | O(1) — pointer update |
| **Memory Overhead** | Less (only data) | More (data + 2 pointers per node) |
| **Best Use Case** | Frequent read/random access | Frequent insert/delete |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B has **Module Internal Choice**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions below are complete and independent.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain the Java I/O stream hierarchy with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **byte streams** and **character streams**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to read a text file `input.txt` line by line using `BufferedReader` and count the number of lines, words, and characters in the file. Use **try-with-resources**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The Java I/O stream hierarchy is divided into **two parallel class trees** rooted at abstract classes in the `java.io` package:

**1. Byte Stream Hierarchy (Binary Data — 8 bits):**

```
InputStream (abstract)
   ├── FileInputStream
   ├── BufferedInputStream
   ├── ObjectInputStream   (for deserialization)
   ├── DataInputStream
   └── ByteArrayInputStream

OutputStream (abstract)
   ├── FileOutputStream
   ├── BufferedOutputStream
   ├── ObjectOutputStream  (for serialization)
   ├── PrintStream
   └── DataOutputStream
```

**2. Character Stream Hierarchy (Text Data — 16-bit Unicode):**

```
Reader (abstract)
   ├── FileReader
   ├── BufferedReader
   ├── InputStreamReader   (bridge: bytes → chars)
   └── StringReader

Writer (abstract)
   ├── FileWriter
   ├── BufferedWriter
   ├── PrintWriter
   ├── OutputStreamWriter  (bridge: chars → bytes)
   └── StringWriter
```

**Differentiation Table:**

| Aspect | Byte Streams | Character Streams |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit of data** | 1 byte (8 bits) | 1 character (16-bit Unicode) |
| **Root classes** | `InputStream`, `OutputStream` | `Reader`, `Writer` |
| **Use for** | Images, audio, video, `.class`, `.ser` files | Text files (`.txt`, `.csv`, `.html`, `.java`) |
| **Encoding handling** | No encoding awareness | Unicode-aware (handles UTF-8, UTF-16, etc.) |
| **Performance for text** | Inefficient (manual char-to-byte conversion) | Efficient (native Unicode support) |
| **Example** | `FileInputStream`, `ObjectInputStream` | `FileReader`, `BufferedReader` |

**Bridge Classes:** `InputStreamReader` converts byte stream → character stream, and `OutputStreamWriter` converts character stream → byte stream. They are essential when reading text from a binary source (e.g., socket).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

```java
import java.io.BufferedReader;
import java.io.FileReader;
import java.io.IOException;

public class FileStats {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int lineCount = 0, wordCount = 0, charCount = 0;

        // Try-with-resources auto-closes br
        try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("input.txt"))) {
            String line;
            while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
                lineCount++;
                charCount += line.length() + 1; // +1 for newline
                String[] words = line.trim().split("\\s+");
                if (!line.trim().isEmpty()) {
                    wordCount += words.length;
                }
            }

            System.out.println("Lines: " + lineCount);
            System.out.println("Words: " + wordCount);
            System.out.println("Characters: " + charCount);

        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.err.println("I/O Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Explanation (7 marks distribution):**

1. **Import statements (1 mark):** `BufferedReader`, `FileReader`, `IOException`.
2. **Variable initialization (1 mark):** `lineCount`, `wordCount`, `charCount` set to 0.
3. **Try-with-resources (2 marks):** `BufferedReader` wraps `FileReader` for efficiency.
4. **Loop logic (2 marks):** `readLine()` returns `null` at EOF; counting logic for lines/words/chars.
5. **Exception handling and output (1 mark):** Proper catch block and formatted output.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
> - ❌ Using `Scanner` instead of `BufferedReader` — slower, not expected for I/O questions.
> - ❌ Forgetting `br.close()` when not using try-with-resources — **−2 marks**.
> - ❌ Counting empty lines as words — use `line.trim().isEmpty()` check.
> - ❌ Not handling the `null` returned at EOF — infinite loop possible.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Choice 2]**

**(a)** What is the Java Collections Framework? Draw the **hierarchy of the Collection interface** and explain any **three commonly used implementations** of `List`, `Set`, and `Map`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program using **generics** to create a class `Pair<A, B>` that stores two heterogeneous objects. Demonstrate it with `(String, Integer)` and `(Double, Boolean)` instances. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The **Java Collections Framework (JCF)** is a unified, reusable, and interoperable architecture introduced in **Java 2 (JDK 1.2)** for representing and manipulating groups of objects. It provides:
- **Interfaces** — abstract data types (e.g., `List`, `Set`, `Map`).
- **Implementations** — concrete classes (e.g., `ArrayList`, `HashSet`).
- **Algorithms** — reusable methods (e.g., `Collections.sort()`, `Collections.binarySearch()`).

**Hierarchy Diagram:**

```
                Iterable<T>
                    |
                Collection<T>
            /--------+--------\
           /         |         \
         List       Set       Queue
          |          |
   ArrayList,    HashSet,
   LinkedList,   TreeSet,
   Vector        LinkedHashSet

                Map<K,V>  (separate root)
            /------+------+------\
       HashMap   TreeMap  Hashtable  LinkedHashMap
```

**Three Commonly Used Implementations:**

**1. `ArrayList<E>` (implements `List`):**
- **Backed by:** A dynamically resizable array.
- **When to use:** Frequent random access via index (`get(i)`).
- **Performance:** O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion in middle.
- **Example:** `List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();`

**2. `HashSet<E>` (implements `Set`):**
- **Backed by:** A `HashMap` internally.
- **When to use:** Storing **unique** elements with O(1) lookup.
- **Performance:** O(1) average for `add`, `contains`, `remove`.
- **Example:** `Set<Integer> uniqueIds = new HashSet<>();`

**3. `HashMap<K,V>` (implements `Map`):**
- **Backed by:** Hash table (array of buckets).
- **When to use:** Key-value associations with O(1) average `get(key)`.
- **Performance:** O(1) average for `put`, `get`, `remove`; O(n) worst case.
- **Example:** `Map<String, Integer> ageMap = new HashMap<>();`

> **Key Distinction:** `Map<K,V>` is **NOT** a `Collection`. It does not extend `Collection` because it stores pairs, not individual elements.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

```java
// Generic class with two type parameters
class Pair<A, B> {
    private A first;
    private B second;

    public Pair(A first, B second) {
        this.first = first;
        this.second = second;
    }

    public A getFirst() { return first; }
    public B getSecond() { return second; }

    public void setFirst(A first) { this.first = first; }
    public void setSecond(B second) { this.second = second; }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "(" + first + ", " + second + ")";
    }
}

public class PairDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Pair of String and Integer
        Pair<String, Integer> nameAge = new Pair<>("Anand", 21);
        System.out.println("Name-Age Pair: " + nameAge);

        // Pair of Double and Boolean
        Pair<Double, Boolean> scorePass = new Pair<>(85.5, true);
        System.out.println("Score-Pass Pair: " + scorePass);

        // Accessing individual elements with type safety
        String name = nameAge.getFirst();   // No cast needed!
        Integer age = nameAge.getSecond();
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + age);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

$$ \text{Name-Age Pair: (Anand, 21)} $$
$$ \text{Score-Pass Pair: (85.5, true)} $$
$$ \text{Name: Anand, Age: 21} $$

**Step-by-step Explanation (7 marks distribution):**

1. **Generic class declaration (2 marks):** `class Pair<A, B>` — note the comma-separated type parameters.
2. **Encapsulation of fields (1 mark):** Private `first` (type `A`) and `second` (type `B`).
3. **Constructor and getters/setters (2 marks):** Parameterized constructor, type-safe accessors.
4. **Demonstration with two different type instantiations (1 mark):** `(String, Integer)` and `(Double, Boolean)`.
5. **Type-safe retrieval without casting (1 mark):** Shows the compile-time safety of generics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
> - ❌ Writing `class Pair<T>` with a single parameter when the question asks for **two** heterogeneous types — **−2 marks**.
> - ❌ Using raw types: `Pair p = new Pair();` — loses type safety, **−1 mark**.
> - ❌ Forgetting to override `toString()` — poor output, **−1 mark**.
> - ❌ Using `Object` instead of generics — defeats the purpose, **−3 marks** (zero for type safety point).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

### 🔹 File I/O Essentials
- **Two hierarchies:** `InputStream`/`OutputStream` (bytes) **vs.** `Reader`/`Writer` (chars).
- **Text files → use `FileReader`/`FileWriter` or `BufferedReader`/`BufferedWriter`.**
- **Binary files (images, serialized objects) → use `FileInputStream`/`FileOutputStream`.**
- **Always close streams** — use **try-with-resources** for automatic closing.
- **Wrapping a `FileReader` inside `BufferedReader`** is a **Decorator Pattern** + performance boost (8 KB internal buffer).

### 🔹 Serialization Essentials
- **Class must `implements Serializable`** — it is a **marker interface** (no methods).
- **`transient` fields are excluded** from serialization → deserialized to **default values**.
- **`serialVersionUID`** ensures version compatibility during deserialization.
- **`writeObject()`** and **`readObject()`** are the magic methods in `ObjectOutputStream`/`ObjectInputStream`.

### 🔹 Collections Essentials
- **List** = ordered + duplicates allowed → `ArrayList`, `LinkedList`.
- **Set** = unique elements only → `HashSet` (fast), `TreeSet` (sorted).
- **Map** = key-value pairs → `HashMap` (fast), `TreeMap` (sorted), `Hashtable` (legacy/synchronized).
- **`Map` does NOT extend `Collection`** — separate root interface.
- **Choose `ArrayList` for read-heavy, `LinkedList` for write-heavy operations.**

### 🔹 Generics Essentials
- **Syntax:** `ClassName<T>` or `ClassName<K, V>` for two parameters.
- **Benefit:** Compile-time type safety, eliminates explicit casting.
- **Diamond operator `<>`** (Java 7+) — `new ArrayList<>()` infers type from left side.
- **Bounded types:** `<T extends Number>` — restricts T to a subtype.
- **Wildcards:** `<?>` (any), `<? extends T>` (upper bound), `<? super T>` (lower bound).

### 🔹 KTU Exam-Specific Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing `Comparable` (in class) with `Comparator` (separate class).
- ❌ Forgetting that `Iterator` is **fail-fast** — throws `ConcurrentModificationException` if collection is modified during iteration (use `ListIterator` or `CopyOnWriteArrayList` to avoid).
- ❌ Using `Vector` or `Hashtable` in new code — they are legacy and synchronized (slower).
- ❌ Mixing up `throw` (keyword) and `throws` (declaration) — both appear in I/O exception handling.

### 🔹 One-Line Definitions (Memorize These!)
- **Serialization** = Object → Byte stream.
- **Deserialization** = Byte stream → Object.
- **Marker Interface** = Empty interface used for type tagging (e.g., `Serializable`, `Cloneable`).
- **Decorator Pattern** = Wrapping an object to add behavior dynamically (used by all `Buffered*` classes).
- **Generic Class** = Class with one or more type parameters, enabling type-safe reusable code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 7-mark "explain with example" question, follow this structure:
> 1. **Definition** (1 mark) → 2. **Syntax/Code** (2 marks) → 3. **Step-by-step explanation** (2 marks) → 4. **Output/Use case** (1 mark) → 5. **Diagram where applicable** (1 mark bonus).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Design: Reusing Pattern Solutions
Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 3 at a Glance — The "Reusability & Flexibility" Pillar

**Module 3** of *Object Oriented Design using Java (PEITT411)* is the conceptual heart of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP). While **Module 1** introduced the philosophy of OOP and **Module 2** dealt with the basic building block — the **class** and the **object** — Module 3 elevates the design from a collection of isolated blueprints to a **living, hierarchical, and polymorphic system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> Module 3 covers the advanced OO mechanisms that enable **code reuse, dynamic behavior, and contractual design** in Java. The four pillars addressed are:
> 1. **Inheritance** — *is-a* hierarchical relationship.
> 2. **Polymorphism** — *one interface, many implementations*.
> 3. **Abstract Classes & Interfaces** — *contractual and partial implementation blueprints*.
> 4. **Packages** — *logical grouping and access-controlled namespaces*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Manufacturing Plant"

Imagine a large automobile company:

- **Classes (Module 2)** are the **blueprints** for individual car models (e.g., a Sedan blueprint, an SUV blueprint).
- **Inheritance** is the engineering practice of creating a **new SUV blueprint from an existing Car blueprint**, inheriting the engine, wheels, and chassis design, and then *adding* off-road features. The new SUV blueprint **is a** Car, but with extra capabilities.
- **Polymorphism** is the universal **"startEngine()" button** on the dashboard. Whether you are sitting in a Sedan, an SUV, or a Truck, the button looks identical, but pressing it triggers a *different internal mechanism* depending on the actual vehicle type behind it.
- **Abstract Classes** are **incomplete prototype blueprints** — for example, a "Generic EV Blueprint" that defines *what* every electric vehicle must have (battery, motor) but leaves the *exact* battery chemistry for subclasses to decide.
- **Interfaces** are **certification contracts** — a "SafetyCertified" badge that any vehicle (Car, Bike, Truck) can earn by promising to implement certain methods (e.g., `applyBrakes()`), regardless of their inheritance lineage.
- **Packages** are the **factory departments** (Engine Department, Body Department, Electronics Department) — each keeps related blueprints together and controls who can access proprietary designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Module 3 Matters in KTU Exams:**
> Module 3 carries the **highest weightage** in both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** internal assessments and the **End Semester Examination (ESE)**. Expect at least one full 14-mark question and a guaranteed Part-A (3-mark) question on inheritance, polymorphism, or interfaces.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars — One-Line Summary

| Pillar | Core Keyword | Exam Buzzword |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | `extends` | "is-a" relationship |
| Polymorphism | Dynamic dispatch | One interface, many forms |
| Abstraction | `abstract` / `interface` | Contract enforcement |
| Packages | `package` / `import` | Namespace management |

## 1.4 Prerequisites Checklist (Revise Before Proceeding)

Before diving into Module 3's depth, ensure mastery of:
- **Class & Object creation** with `new` keyword
- **Encapsulation** via `private` fields and `public` getters/setters
- **Constructors** (default, parameterized, copy)
- **`this` keyword** for instance reference
- **Access modifiers**: `private`, default (package-private), `protected`, `public`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> A common mistake students make in Module 3 questions is confusing **"is-a"** (inheritance) with **"has-a"** (composition/aggregation). If a class *contains* a reference to another class, it is **NOT** inheritance. A `Car` *has-an* `Engine`, but a `SportsCar` *is-a* `Car`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inheritance — The "is-a" Reusability Engine

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition
**Inheritance** is the OOP mechanism by which a new class (**subclass / derived / child / extended class**) is created from an existing class (**superclass / base / parent class**), inheriting its **accessible** members (fields and methods) and optionally **overriding** methods to provide specialized behavior.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Declaration** — The subclass uses the `extends` keyword in its class header.
