2. **Member Inheritance** — All `public` and `protected` members of the superclass are inherited. `private` members are **not directly accessible** but exist in the object.
3. **Constructor Chaining** — The subclass constructor **must** invoke a superclass constructor (explicitly via `super(...)` or implicitly as the first line).
4. **Method Overriding** — The subclass can redefine an inherited method with the **same signature** (name + parameter list) and a covariant return type.
5. **Type Compatibility** — A subclass object **is-a** superclass object (upcasting is implicit and safe).
6. **Access Augmentation** — Access level in override **cannot be reduced** (e.g., `public` cannot become `protected`).

### 2.1.3 Types of Inheritance Supported in Java

| Type | Java Support | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single | ✅ Yes | `class B extends A` |
| Multilevel | ✅ Yes | `class C extends B extends A` |
| Hierarchical | ✅ Yes | `class B extends A`, `class C extends A` |
| Multiple (class) | ❌ No | Diamond problem; resolved via interfaces |
| Hybrid | ⚠️ Partial | Mix of class & multiple interface inheritance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Diamond Problem:**
> Java **disallows multiple class inheritance** because if class `C` extends both `A` and `B`, and both define `display()`, the compiler cannot decide which `display()` `C` inherits. Java solves this by allowing **multiple interface inheritance** (since Java 8, default methods can cause conflict, but must be explicitly disambiguated).

### 2.1.4 `super` Keyword — Three Critical Uses

| Use | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Call superclass constructor | `super(args)` | Must be first line of subclass constructor |
| Access superclass field | `super.fieldName` | Resolve name shadowing |
| Invoke superclass method | `super.methodName()` | Explicitly call overridden parent version |

## 2.2 Polymorphism — "One Interface, Many Implementations"

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition
**Polymorphism** (Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a single interface (method call, reference type) to invoke **different underlying behaviors** depending on the actual object type at runtime.

### 2.2.2 Two Sub-Types in Java

**A. Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding / Early Binding)**
- Achieved via **Method Overloading**.
- Compiler resolves the call at compile time based on method signature (name + parameter types).
- **No inheritance required** — can exist within a single class.

**B. Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding / Late Binding)**
- Achieved via **Method Overriding** + **Upcasting** + **Dynamic Method Dispatch**.
- JVM resolves the call at runtime by inspecting the **actual object type** in memory, not the reference type.
- **Requires inheritance hierarchy**.

### 2.2.3 Dynamic Method Dispatch — The Engine

**Definition:** The mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at **runtime** according to the actual type of the object referred to by the superclass reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Golden Rule for Polymorphism Questions:**
> *"Reference type decides WHAT can be called. Object type decides WHICH version executes."*
> - A `Vehicle` reference pointing to a `Car` object can call only methods declared in `Vehicle` (or inherited).
> - If `start()` is overridden in `Car`, the **`Car` version** of `start()` runs.

### 2.2.4 The `final` Keyword & Polymorphism Interaction

| Modifier | On Method | On Class | On Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| `final` | Cannot be overridden | Cannot be subclassed | Constant (cannot reassign) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** `final` and `finally` and `finalize()` are **three completely different things**. KTU loves to test this distinction in Part-A questions.

## 2.3 Abstract Classes & Interfaces — The Contract Designers

### 2.3.1 Abstract Classes

**Definition:** A class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated** and may contain **abstract methods** (methods with no body). Used to capture common behavior while forcing subclasses to provide specific implementations.

**Rules:**
- Declared with `abstract` keyword.
- May have **zero, one, or many** abstract methods.
- May have **concrete methods, fields, constructors**.
- A subclass must override **all** abstract methods or itself be declared `abstract`.
- Can extend **only one** abstract (or concrete) class.

### 2.3.2 Interfaces

**Definition:** A pure abstract type (since Java 8, it can also have `default` and `static` methods) that defines a **contract** of method signatures that implementing classes must fulfill.

**Rules:**
- Declared with `interface` keyword.
- All methods are **implicitly `public abstract`** (pre-Java 8).
- All fields are **implicitly `public static final`** (constants).
- A class uses `implements` to adopt an interface.
- A class can implement **multiple** interfaces (Java's answer to multiple inheritance).
- Since **Java 8**: `default` methods (with body) and `static` methods are allowed.
- Since **Java 9**: `private` methods are allowed.

### 2.3.3 Abstract Class vs Interface — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| Methods | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + Default + Static |
| Fields | Any (instance/static/final) | Only `public static final` |
| Constructors | Yes | No |
| Access Modifiers | Any | Implicitly `public` |
| Inheritance | `extends` (one only) | `implements` (many) |
| When to Use | Shared code + partial contract | Pure contract definition |

## 2.4 Packages — The Namespace Managers

### 2.4.1 Formal Definition
A **package** in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces. Conceptually similar to folders in a file system, packages provide:
1. **Logical grouping** of related types.
2. **Access protection** (package-private visibility).
3. **Name conflict resolution**.

### 2.4.2 Package Declaration & Import

- **Declaration:** First statement in a Java file: `package com.ktu.department;`
- **Built-in packages:** `java.lang` (auto-imported), `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`, `java.awt`, `javax.swing`.
- **Import variants:**
  - `import java.util.ArrayList;` — imports single class.
  - `import java.util.*;` — imports all classes in the package.
  - `import static java.lang.Math.PI;` — static import (since Java 5).
- **Fully Qualified Name:** Can be used without import: `java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in);`

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Exam Trigger Word |
|---|---|---|
| Single Inheritance | `class Child extends Parent` | "Create a derived class" |
| Method Override | Same signature in child | "Specialize the behavior" |
| Method Overload | Same name, different params | "Compile-time polymorphism" |
| Abstract Method | `abstract void draw();` | "Force subclass to implement" |
| Interface | `interface Drawable { void draw(); }` | "Define a contract" |
| Implements | `class Circle implements Drawable` | "Multiple contract adoption" |
| `super()` call | First line of constructor | "Chain to parent constructor" |
| Dynamic Dispatch | `Parent p = new Child(); p.method();` | "What is printed?" |
| Package | `package com.ktu.mypkg;` | "Group related classes" |
| Access Levels | `private < default < protected < public` | "Scope of members" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These four pillars are not academic curiosities — they power **every modern Java framework**. Spring Boot uses **interfaces and DI (Dependency Injection)** for loose coupling. JavaFX uses **inheritance and polymorphism** for UI component trees. Apache Maven organizes jars into **packages**. Mastering Module 3 = mastering real-world Java.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for "Which OOP Feature to Use?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is the **single most important analytical tool** for Module 3. KTU examiners expect students to *justify* design choices.

**Step 1 — Identify the Relationship:**
- If new class **is a specialized version** of existing class → **Inheritance**
- If new class **has a** component of existing class → **Composition**

**Step 2 — Identify the Behavior Pattern:**
- If common code exists **AND** some methods must be specialized → **Abstract Class**
- If only a contract is needed (no shared code) → **Interface**

**Step 3 — Identify the Binding Requirement:**
- If method to call is determined at **compile time** → **Overloading**
- If method to call is determined at **runtime** based on object → **Overriding + Upcasting**

**Step 4 — Identify the Namespace Need:**
- If classes are logically related and need grouping → **Package**

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Method Dispatch Resolution

Consider a classic KTU problem setup:

```java
class Animal {
    void speak() { System.out.println("Animal speaks"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void speak() { System.out.println("Dog barks"); }
}
class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void speak() { System.out.println("Cat meows"); }
}
```

**Step 1 — Reference Creation:**

$$ \text{Animal } a = \text{new Dog}(); $$

*Conversion logic:* The compiler allows this because `Dog` **is-a** `Animal` (upcasting is implicit and safe). The reference variable `a` is of compile-time type `Animal`, but the object it points to in the heap is of runtime type `Dog`.

**Step 2 — Method Call:**

$$ a.speak() \rightarrow \text{?} $$

*Conversion logic:* Compiler checks: *"Is `speak()` declared in `Animal`?"* → **Yes**. So the call is **type-checked successfully** at compile time. The compiler emits an `invokevirtual` bytecode instruction.

**Step 3 — Runtime Resolution by JVM:**

$$ \text{JVM inspects object header} \rightarrow \text{Actual class} = Dog $$

*Conversion logic:* The JVM walks the object's class table, finds `Dog.speak()` overrides `Animal.speak()`, and dispatches to the `Dog` version. The output is:

$$ \text{Output: } \texttt{Dog barks} $$

## 3.3 Derivation: Constructor Chaining Order in Multilevel Inheritance

Consider:

```java
class A {
    A() { System.out.println("A"); }
}
class B extends A {
    B() { System.out.println("B"); }
}
class C extends B {
    C() { System.out.println("C"); }
}
// new C();
```

**Step 1 — `new C()` is invoked.**

The JVM allocates memory and calls `C()`.

**Step 2 — Implicit `super()` execution.**

Since `C()` does not explicitly call `super()`, the compiler inserts `super();` as the first line. This calls `B()`.

**Step 3 — Chained `super()` call.**

`B()` has no explicit `super()`, so compiler inserts `super();` calling `A()`.

**Step 4 — Topmost constructor runs first.**

`A()` prints `A`, then returns to `B()` which prints `B`, then returns to `C()` which prints `C`.

**Final Output Order:**

$$ \text{Output sequence} = A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C $$

*Conversion logic:* Construction always proceeds **from the root of the hierarchy downward**. Destruction (in languages with destructors) would proceed in the **reverse** order. Java has no destructors, but the same pattern applies to `finally` blocks during exception unwinding.

## 3.4 Derivation: Method Overloading Resolution Algorithm

When the compiler encounters an overloaded call `obj.method(5, 10.5)`, it uses the **Most Specific Method Selection** algorithm in three phases:

**Phase 1 — Exact Match Search:**
$$ \text{Search for method with signature matching exactly } (int, double) $$

If found, use it. If not, proceed.

**Phase 2 — Widening Primitive Conversion:**
$$ \text{Attempt: } (int, double) \rightarrow (long, double) \rightarrow (double, double) \rightarrow (\text{Object}, \text{Object}) $$

The compiler picks the **closest** match in the widening hierarchy:

$$
\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

**Phase 3 — Autoboxing and Varargs:**

$$ (int, double) \rightarrow (Integer, Double) \rightarrow (int\ldots, double\ldots) $$

Autoboxing has **lower priority** than widening; varargs is the **last resort**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Phase priority order in Java overload resolution:**
> 1. Exact match
> 2. Widening primitive conversion
> 3. Autoboxing / Unboxing
> 4. Varargs
>
> Mixing phases (e.g., autoboxing + widening) is **not allowed** and causes compile error.

## 3.5 Analytical Table — `instanceof` Operator Behavior

The `instanceof` operator checks whether an object is an instance of a specific class or interface. Since Java 16, **pattern matching for instanceof** is standard:

```java
if (obj instanceof Dog d) {
    d.fetch();  // d is auto-cast and available
}
```

| Expression | Returns `true` if... |
|---|---|
| `dog instanceof Animal` | `dog` is `Dog`, `Puppy`, or any `Dog` subclass |
| `dog instanceof Dog` | `dog` is `Dog` or subclass of `Dog` |
| `null instanceof X` | Always `false` (compile-time) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** `instanceof` returns `false` for `null` checks. This is a frequently tested edge case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
      +String brand
      +int year
      +start() void
      +stop() void
    }
    class Car {
      +int numDoors
      +honk() void
    }
    class SportsCar {
      +int topSpeed
      +turboBoost() void
    }
    class ElectricCar {
      +int batteryCapacity
      +charge() void
    }
    Vehicle <|-- Car
    Car <|-- SportsCar
    Car <|-- ElectricCar
```

*Conversion logic:* Solid arrows with hollow triangles point from child to parent, representing the `extends` relationship. `SportsCar` and `ElectricCar` both ultimately inherit from `Vehicle` via `Car`.

## 4.2 Polymorphism Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Code: Parent p = new Child()"] --> B["Compile Time Check"]
    B --> C{"Is method declared in Parent?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Bytecode: invokevirtual p.method()"]
    C -->|"No"| E["COMPILE ERROR"]
    D --> F["Runtime: JVM reads object header"]
    F --> G{"Object's actual class is Child?"}
    G -->|"Yes"| H["Invoke Child's overridden method"]
    G -->|"No, check parent"| I["Walk up hierarchy"]
    I --> H
    H --> J["Execute and return"]
```

*Conversion logic:* The flowchart shows the two-stage binding of polymorphic calls — compile-time **type checking** and runtime **method dispatch**.

## 4.3 Abstract Class vs Interface — Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ABSTRACT["Abstract Class Layer"]
        A1["abstract class Shape"]
        A2["abstract double area()"]
        A3["void display() - concrete"]
    end
    subgraph INTERFACE["Interface Layer"]
        I1["interface Drawable"]
        I2["void draw() - implicit public abstract"]
        I3["default void erase() - Java 8+"]
    end
    subgraph CONCRETE["Concrete Implementation"]
        C1["class Circle extends Shape implements Drawable"]
        C2["Must override area() from Shape"]
        C3["Must override draw() from Drawable"]
    end
    ABSTRACT --> CONCRETE
    INTERFACE --> CONCRETE
```

*Conversion logic:* A concrete class can simultaneously inherit from **one** abstract class and implement **multiple** interfaces, combining both contract enforcement and shared code.

## 4.4 Package and Access Modifier Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Package: com.ktu.banking"] --> C1["class Account"]
    P1 --> C2["class SavingsAccount"]
    P2["Package: com.ktu.audit"] --> C3["class AuditLog"]
    C1 -->|"public members"| X["Visible everywhere"]
    C1 -->|"protected members"| Y["Visible in same pkg + subclasses"]
    C1 -->|"default members"| Z["Visible only in com.ktu.banking"]
    C1 -->|"private members"| W["Visible only inside Account"]
    C3 -->|"import com.ktu.banking.Account"| X
```

*Conversion logic:* Packages act as **visibility boundaries** for default-access members. Cross-package access requires either `public` visibility or an explicit `extends`/`implements` relationship for `protected`.

## 4.5 Lifecycle of an Object Through Module 3 Concepts

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Reference
    participant Heap
    participant JVM
    Client->>Reference: Animal a = new Dog()
    Reference->>Heap: Allocate Dog object
    Heap->>JVM: Object header marks class = Dog
    Client->>Reference: a.speak()
    Reference->>JVM: invokevirtual speak
    JVM->>Heap: Inspect actual class
    Heap-->>JVM: Class is Dog
    JVM->>Heap: Invoke Dog.speak()
    Heap-->>Client: Output: Dog barks
```

*Conversion logic:* The sequence captures the runtime contract between the **reference** (compile-time view) and the **heap object** (runtime reality) that defines Java's polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of method overriding in Java. How is it different from method overloading?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Method Overriding* is the process of redefining a method inherited from a superclass in a subclass with the **same name, same parameter list, and a covariant return type**. It enables **runtime polymorphism** — the JVM decides which version to call based on the actual object type. It requires an inheritance hierarchy, and the access level **cannot be reduced**.

*Method Overloading* is defining multiple methods with the **same name but different parameter lists** (number, type, or order of parameters) within the **same class** (or with a subclass). It is resolved at **compile time** based on the method signature, and is an example of **static polymorphism**. Overloading does not require inheritance.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1.5 marks: Overriding definition + runtime polymorphism link
- 1.5 marks: Overloading definition + compile-time binding + contrast

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is an abstract class in Java? When would you prefer it over an interface?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **abstract class** in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated** and may contain **abstract methods** (methods without a body) as well as **concrete methods** and instance fields. It is used to capture common behavior and state among related classes while forcing subclasses to provide specific implementations.

An abstract class is preferred over an interface when:
1. The related classes share **common code or state** (fields, constructors, concrete methods).
2. The related classes have an **"is-a" relationship** rather than just a behavioral contract.
3. Future versions need to **add new concrete methods** without breaking implementing classes (interfaces would force changes in all implementers).

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1.5 marks: Abstract class definition + instantiation rule
- 1.5 marks: Justification of preference over interface with valid use cases

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the four types of inheritance with suitable Java code examples. Why does Java not support multiple inheritance using classes? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **runtime polymorphism** using a superclass `Shape` with subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle`. Include a method `draw()` in each class and show how a single reference of type `Shape` invokes the correct `draw()` method at runtime. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**The four types of inheritance are:**

**1. Single Inheritance:** One subclass inherits from one superclass.

```java
class Animal { void eat() { System.out.println("eating"); } }
class Dog extends Animal { void bark() { System.out.println("barking"); } }
```

**2. Multilevel Inheritance:** A chain of inheritance (grandparent → parent → child).

```java
class Grandparent { void legacy() { } }
class Parent extends Grandparent { void guide() { } }
class Child extends Parent { void learn() { } }
```

**3. Hierarchical Inheritance:** Multiple subclasses inherit from a single superclass.

```java
class Vehicle { }
class Car extends Vehicle { }
class Truck extends Vehicle { }
class Bike extends Vehicle { }
```

**4. Multiple Inheritance (via interfaces):** A class inherits from multiple sources (interfaces only).

```java
interface Flyable { void fly(); }
interface Swimmable { void swim(); }
class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable {
    public void fly() { }
    public void swim() { }
}
```

**Why Java Disallows Multiple Class Inheritance:**

Java disallows multiple class inheritance to avoid the **Diamond Problem / Ambiguity Problem**. If a class `C` extends both `A` and `B`, and both `A` and `B` define a method `display()` with the same signature, the compiler cannot determine **which version** `C` inherits. This creates ambiguity in the object layout and method resolution. Java resolves this by allowing a class to implement **multiple interfaces** instead — interfaces are pure contracts (mostly abstract), and any conflict in default methods (Java 8+) must be explicitly resolved by the implementing class.

**Mark Distribution for Part (a):**
- 1 mark each for the four types with code (4 marks)
- 3 marks: Diamond problem explanation + Java's solution

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }
}
class Rectangle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Rectangle");
    }
}
public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;  // superclass reference

        s = new Circle();
        s.draw();  // Calls Circle's draw()

        s = new Rectangle();
        s.draw();  // Calls Rectangle's draw()
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\texttt{Drawing a Circle} \newline \texttt{Drawing a Rectangle}
$$

**Explanation of Runtime Dispatch:**

- The reference `s` is of type `Shape` (compile-time type).
- The objects created are `Circle` and `Rectangle` (runtime types).
- When `s.draw()` is called, the compiler verifies that `draw()` is declared in `Shape` (line 4).
- At runtime, the JVM inspects the actual object class in memory and dispatches to the **overridden** version.
- This is **dynamic method dispatch**, the mechanism that enables runtime polymorphism.

**Mark Distribution for Part (b):**
- 2 marks: Correct class hierarchy with override annotation
- 2 marks: Correct polymorphic main method
- 2 marks: Accurate output prediction
- 1 mark: Explanation of dynamic dispatch

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is an interface in Java? Explain with an example how a class can implement **multiple interfaces**. How does Java 8's `default` method feature enhance interfaces? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Java program using an **abstract class** `Employee` with abstract method `calculateSalary()`. Extend it with two subclasses `FullTimeEmployee` and `PartTimeEmployee` that provide their own salary calculation logic. Demonstrate calling `calculateSalary()` polymorphically. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

An **interface** in Java is a reference type, similar to a class, that can contain **abstract methods**, **default methods** (since Java 8), **static methods** (Java 8+), and **constants** (implicitly `public static final`). It defines a **contract** that implementing classes must fulfill. Interfaces support Java's form of **multiple inheritance of type**.

```java
interface Printable {
    void print();  // implicitly public abstract
}
interface Scannable {
    void scan();
}
class MultifunctionPrinter implements Printable, Scannable {
    public void print() { System.out.println("Printing..."); }
    public void scan()  { System.out.println("Scanning..."); }
}
```

Here, `MultifunctionPrinter` adopts **two contracts** (Printable and Scannable), demonstrating multiple interface implementation.

**Java 8 Default Methods Enhancement:**

Before Java 8, adding a new method to an interface **broke all existing implementations** (compile error). Java 8 introduced `default` methods — methods in an interface with a **concrete implementation** — that all implementing classes **inherit automatically** without being forced to override. This enables **backward-compatible API evolution**.

```java
interface Printer {
    void print();
    default void status() {           // default method
        System.out.println("Ready");
    }
}
```

If a class implements two interfaces that both define a `default` method with the same signature, the implementing class **must** explicitly override and disambiguate, or the compiler raises an error.

**Mark Distribution for Part (a):**
- 2 marks: Interface definition with rules
- 2 marks: Multiple interface implementation example
- 3 marks: Java 8 default method feature with example and conflict resolution

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

```java
abstract class Employee {
    String name;
    double baseRate;
    Employee(String name, double baseRate) {
        this.name = name;
        this.baseRate = baseRate;
    }
    abstract double calculateSalary();

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Salary: " + calculateSalary());
    }
}
class FullTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    FullTimeEmployee(String n, double r) { super(n, r); }
    @Override
    double calculateSalary() { return baseRate * 1.0; }  // fixed monthly
}
class PartTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    int hoursWorked;
    PartTimeEmployee(String n, double r, int h) {
        super(n, r);
        hoursWorked = h;
    }
    @Override
    double calculateSalary() { return baseRate * hoursWorked; }
}
public class Payroll {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee[] staff = {
            new FullTimeEmployee("Alice", 50000),
            new PartTimeEmployee("Bob", 500, 40)
        };
        for (Employee e : staff) {
            e.display();  // Polymorphic call
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\texttt{Name: Alice, Salary: 50000.0} \newline \texttt{Name: Bob, Salary: 20000.0}
$$

**Polymorphism Justification:**
- The reference type `Employee` (in the array) decides what can be called (`calculateSalary()` is declared in `Employee`).
- The actual object type (`FullTimeEmployee` or `PartTimeEmployee`) decides which version runs.
- This is the **open-closed principle** in action: the loop is closed for modification, open for extension — any new `Employee` subclass plugs in without changing `main()`.

**Mark Distribution for Part (b):**
- 2 marks: Abstract class with abstract + concrete method
- 2 marks: Both subclass overrides with distinct logic
- 2 marks: Polymorphic array iteration in main
- 1 mark: Correct output

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 3 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Constructor without `super()` call** — *(-2 marks)*: If a subclass constructor does not explicitly call `super(args)`, the compiler inserts a no-argument `super()` call. If the parent has **no default constructor**, this causes a compile error. Always chain explicitly.
>
> 2. **Reducing access in override** — *(-1 mark)*: Declaring a `public` parent method as `protected` in the child is a **compile error**, not just bad practice.
>
> 3. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** — *(-0.5 mark)*: Not a functional error, but a **style penalty** in KTU valuation; also loses marks for weak method signature detection.
>
> 4. **Static methods "overriding"** — *(-2 marks)*: Static methods are **hidden**, not overridden. Calling a static method via a superclass reference invokes the **parent's** static method, not the child's. This is a classic KTU trick question.
>
> 5. **Abstract method body** — *(-2 marks)*: An abstract method **cannot have a body** — `abstract void draw() { }` is a compile error.
>
> 6. **Interface field mutation** — *(-2 marks)*: All interface fields are `public static final`; attempting to modify them is a compile error.
>
> 7. **Confusing `final` class with `final` method** — *(-1 mark)*: A `final` class **cannot be extended**; a `final` method **cannot be overridden**. These are independent restrictions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Inheritance** uses the `extends` keyword and models an **"is-a"** relationship.
- [ ] Java supports **single, multilevel, and hierarchical** class inheritance — **not multiple** class inheritance.
- [ ] Multiple inheritance is achieved through **interfaces** using the `implements` keyword.
- [ ] **`super(args)`** must be the **first line** of a subclass constructor.
- [ ] **Method Overriding** = same signature + covariant return type + access not reduced.
- [ ] **Method Overloading** = same name, different parameter list — resolved at **compile time**.
- [ ] **Runtime polymorphism** = override + upcasting + dynamic method dispatch.
- [ ] **Abstract class** may have abstract + concrete members; **cannot be instantiated**.
- [ ] **Interface** fields are implicitly `public static final`; methods implicitly `public abstract` (pre-Java 8).
- [ ] **Java 8** added `default` and `static` methods to interfaces; **Java 9** added `private` methods.
- [ ] **`final` keyword** prevents overriding (method), extension (class), or reassignment (variable).
- [ ] **Packages** group related classes; `java.lang` is auto-imported.
- [ ] **Access hierarchy**: `private < default (package) < protected < public`.
- [ ] **`protected`** members are accessible in the **same package** and in **subclasses** (even outside the package).
- [ ] **`instanceof` returns `false` for `null`** — a frequently tested edge case.
- [ ] **Static methods are hidden, not overridden** — polymorphism does not apply to them.
- [ ] **`Object` class** is the **root** of every Java class hierarchy — directly or via `Object` superclass.
- [ ] **Polymorphism rule**: *Reference type decides WHAT; Object type decides WHICH.*
- [ ] Constructor chaining executes from **root → leaf** (parent first, then child).
- [ ] **Object slicing** does not occur in Java because all references are uniformly typed by class hierarchy.
- [ ] Use **abstract class** for shared code + partial contract; use **interface** for pure contract.
- [ ] A class can **extend one** class and **implement many** interfaces simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reuse Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reuse Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Reuse Concepts

In Object-Oriented Design (OOD), **Reuse Concepts** refer to the systematic methodology of constructing new software systems by leveraging existing components, classes, and design structures rather than building them from scratch. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Object Oriented Design using Java*, reuse is positioned as a foundational design principle that promotes **modularity**, **extensibility**, and **maintainability** of software systems.

The formal definition, aligned with the *Grady Booch* and *Gamma et al.* (Gang of Four) frameworks adopted in KTU's reference texts, states:

> **"Reuse is the disciplined practice of identifying, abstracting, encapsulating, and composing existing software artifacts (classes, interfaces, methods, patterns) into new software systems to achieve functional redundancy reduction and quality amplification."**

The four primary reuse mechanisms in OOD with Java are:

1. **Inheritance (Is-A relationship)** – A subclass inherits the structure and behavior of a superclass.
2. **Composition (Has-A relationship, strong)** – A class contains objects of other classes where the contained object cannot exist without the container.
3. **Aggregation (Has-A relationship, weak)** – A class contains objects of other classes where the contained object can exist independently.
4. **Association** – A general structural relationship where objects are connected but loosely coupled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of *PEITT411* explicitly tests the student's ability to **differentiate** between these four reuse mechanisms, justify design choices using **"is-a" vs "has-a"** reasoning, and implement them using **Java's `extends` and `implements`** keywords.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house:

- **Inheritance** is like a contractor who builds a *"Standard 2BHK Model"* house, and then a client asks for an *"upgraded 3BHK with swimming pool"*. The 3BHK automatically inherits all the doors, windows, and wiring of the 2BHK model — but the **family tree** is clear: 3BHK *is a type of* 2BHK.
- **Composition** is like a house *and* its foundation. The house **cannot exist** without the foundation. Destroy the house → destroy the foundation. (Strong ownership.)
- **Aggregation** is like a house *and* its furniture. The furniture **can exist independently** — sell the house, the dining table moves with you. (Weak ownership.)
- **Association** is like a house and a *postman* who visits it. The postman knows your address, and you know him, but neither owns the other. (Loose relationship.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** *Inheritance = "is-a"*, *Composition = "part-of (permanent)"*, *Aggregation = "part-of (optional)"*, *Association = "uses-a / knows-a"*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Reuse

Two important quantitative metrics used by KTU-expected design evaluations:

- **Reuse Density (RD)** — proportion of reused classes in a system, expressed as a percentage.
- **Coupling Factor (CF)** — degree of inter-dependency between reused components, lower is better.

These will be formalized in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Reuse — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Inheritance (Is-A)

Inheritance is the **white-box reuse** mechanism. The subclass inherits **all accessible** members (fields + methods) of the superclass and may *override* or *extend* behavior.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Establish a parent-child class hierarchy using Java's `extends` keyword.
2. The subclass inherits all `public` and `protected` members; `private` members are inaccessible directly.
3. Use `super(...)` to invoke parent constructors.
4. Polymorphic method calls are resolved at runtime via **dynamic dispatch** (JVM v-table lookup).
5. Java supports **single inheritance** for classes (no multiple class inheritance) to avoid the *Diamond Problem*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Note:** Java does **NOT** support multiple class inheritance. To achieve multi-type reuse, Java enforces **Interface-based multiple inheritance** using `implements`. This is a *guaranteed question* in KTU 2024 papers.

### 2.1.2 Composition (Has-A — Strong)

The composite object **owns** its parts. Lifecycle of the part is **bound** to the composite.

- Implemented by declaring an instance variable of another class as a `private final` field.
- Example: A `Car` **owns** an `Engine`. The Engine has no meaning without the Car.

### 2.1.3 Aggregation (Has-A — Weak)

The aggregate object **references** its parts. Lifecycle of the part is **independent** of the aggregate.

- Implemented by passing an existing object reference into a constructor or setter.
- Example: A `Department` **has** `Professor`s. A Professor can move to another Department.

### 2.1.4 Association

A *peer-to-peer* relationship. Objects are related, but neither owns the other. Often **bidirectional** (e.g., Teacher ↔ Student).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Relationship Type | Java Keyword | UML Arrow | Lifetime Dependency | Reuse Strength |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Inheritance | Is-A | `extends` / `implements` | Hollow triangle (▷) | Subclass $\Rightarrow$ Superclass | **High** (white-box) |
| Composition | Has-A (strong) | Field instantiation | Filled diamond (◆) | Part dies with Whole | **High** (black-box) |
| Aggregation | Has-A (weak) | Field via setter/constructor | Hollow diamond (◇) | Part independent of Whole | **Medium** (black-box) |
| Association | Uses-A / Knows-A | Field reference | Plain line (—) | Independent lifecycles | **Low** (link reuse) |

### 2.2.1 Reuse Density Formula

$$
RD = \frac{\text{Number of Reused Classes}}{\text{Total Number of Classes in System}} \times 100\%
$$

### 2.2.2 Coupling Factor Formula

$$
CF = \frac{\text{Number of Inter-Class Dependencies}}{\text{Maximum Possible Dependencies}}
$$

A value of $CF \le 0.3$ is considered *good* for a reusable library.

### 2.2.3 Inheritance Depth Bound

$$
\text{Depth} \le 5 \quad (\text{practically recommended for maintainability})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Reuse concepts power **framework design** (Spring, JavaFX), **microservices** (composition of REST resources), and **embedded systems** (HAL — Hardware Abstraction Layer in automotive ECUs). In the Kerala IT industry, Infopark and Technopark companies expect OOD graduates to *prefer composition over inheritance* (the *Favor Composition* principle from the GoF).

## 2.3 The "Favor Composition Over Inheritance" Principle

| Criterion | Inheritance | Composition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compile-time binding | Yes (static) | No (dynamic via interface) |
| Runtime flexibility | Low | High |
| Encapsulation violation | Yes (subclass sees super internals) | No |
| Multiple type reuse | Only via interfaces | Yes (object can hold many interfaces) |
| Risk of fragile base class | High | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners reward answers that explicitly state: *"Composition is preferred at runtime flexibility, while inheritance is preferred only when a true taxonomic 'is-a' relationship exists and Liskov Substitution holds."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Deriving the Class Structure

Consider the KTU textbook problem: *"Model a University system showing reuse relationships."*

### Step 1: Identify the Taxonomy (Inheritance candidates)

We look for **true "is-a"** relationships:

- A `Professor` *is-a* `Person`.
- A `Student` *is-a* `Person`.
- A `TeachingAssistant` *is-a* `Student` (multi-level).

This gives us the inheritance chain:

$$
\text{Person} \rightarrow \text{Student} \rightarrow \text{TeachingAssistant}
$$

### Step 2: Identify Composition (Strong Has-A)

- A `Department` *owns* `OfficeRoom`s (rooms die when department is dissolved).
- Therefore, `Department` **composes** `OfficeRoom`.

$$
\text{Department} \;\bullet\!\!-\!\!\rhd\; \text{OfficeRoom} \quad (\text{filled diamond})
$$

### Step 3: Identify Aggregation (Weak Has-A)

- A `Department` *has* `Professor`s, but a Professor continues to exist if the Department is closed.
- Therefore, `Department` **aggregates** `Professor`.

$$
\text{Department} \;\diamond\!\!-\!\!\rhd\; \text{Professor} \quad (\text{hollow diamond})
$$

### Step 4: Identify Association

- A `Professor` *teaches* `Course`s, and a `Course` *is taught by* a `Professor`. Neither owns the other.
- Therefore, `Professor` is **associated** with `Course`.

### Step 3.1: Mathematical derivation of the relationship multiplicity

For each association, define:

- Let $m$ = number of objects on the source side.
- Let $n$ = number of objects on the target side.
- Multiplicity is written as `source-mult .. target-mult`.

A `Professor` may teach 0 to N `Course`s; a `Course` is taught by exactly 1 `Professor`.

Therefore, the multiplicity pair is:

$$
\text{Professor} \xrightarrow{\;1\;*\;} \text{Course}
$$

Where the *1* is on the Professor side (each course has exactly 1 professor) and *\** (zero or more) is on the Course side (a professor may have 0 or many courses).

### Step 3.2: Derivation of Liskov Substitution Compliance Score (LSC)

For an inheritance hierarchy to be a *valid* reuse, the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) must hold. Define the compliance score:

$$
LSC = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} B(p_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} T(p_i)}
$$

Where:
- $k$ = number of polymorphic method invocations tested.
- $B(p_i)$ = number of behavior-preserving substitutions.
- $T(p_i)$ = total number of substitution tests.

For valid inheritance reuse, the requirement is:

$$
LSC = 1.0 \quad (\text{all tests pass})
$$

If $LSC < 1.0$, the inheritance is invalid and **composition should be used instead**.

### Step 3.3: Worked Java Code Snippet (Pedagogical, not executable in this context)

```java
// Superclass
class Person {
    protected String name;
    public Person(String name) { this.name = name; }
    public String getName() { return name; }
}

// Inheritance (Is-A)
class Student extends Person {
    private int rollNo;
    public Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        super(name);
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
    }
}

// Composition (Strong Has-A) — Engine dies with Car
class Engine { /* ... */ }
class Car {
    private final Engine engine;   // final = cannot be replaced
    public Car() { this.engine = new Engine(); } // created inside
}

// Aggregation (Weak Has-A) — Department references existing Professor
class Professor extends Person {
    public Professor(String name) { super(name); }
}
class Department {
    private Professor[] staff;     // passed in from outside
    public Department(Professor[] staff) { this.staff = staff; }
}
```

### Step 3.4: Conversion logic of each line

- `extends Person` → triggers inheritance; `super(name)` invokes parent constructor.
- `private final Engine engine` → `final` enforces **strong** ownership (composition); the Engine is created *inside* Car and cannot be reassigned.
- `Department(Professor[] staff)` → the `Professor` array is **passed in**, meaning the Professors existed *before* the Department — this is the hallmark of **aggregation**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Evaluation Criterion | Inheritance | Composition | Aggregation | Association |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| UML Symbol | ▷ | ◆ | ◇ | — |
| Java Realization | `extends`/`implements` | Inner field, instantiated | Field via constructor/setter | Field reference, both sides |
| Compile-time vs Runtime | Compile-time | Runtime | Runtime | Runtime |
| Liskov Compliance Required | **Yes** | No | No | No |
| Tight Coupling Level | High | Medium | Medium-Low | Low |
| Reuse Mechanism | White-box | Black-box | Black-box | Black-box |
| Polymorphism Support | **Yes** (method overriding) | Yes (interface delegation) | Yes (interface delegation) | Optional |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of Reuse Mechanisms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reuse Concepts in OOD] --> B[Inheritance Is-A]
    A --> C[Composition Has-A Strong]
    A --> D[Aggregation Has-A Weak]
    A --> E[Association Knows-A]

    B --> B1[Single Inheritance]
    B --> B2[Multilevel Inheritance]
    B --> B3[Hierarchical Inheritance]
    B --> B4[Hybrid via Interfaces]

    C --> C1[Final Field Reference]
    C --> C2[Internal Instantiation]
    C --> C3[Lifecycle Bound to Owner]

    D --> D1[Non-final Field Reference]
    D --> D2[External Instantiation]
    D --> D3[Lifecycle Independent]

    E --> E1[Unidirectional Link]
    E --> E2[Bidirectional Link]
    E --> E3[Multiplicity 1 to N]

    B1 --> F1[Java Keyword extends]
    B4 --> F2[Java Keyword implements]
    C1 --> F3[private final Engine engine]
    D1 --> F4[private Professor prof]
    E1 --> F5[private Course[] courses]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Class Diagram — University System (KTU Textbook Example)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Person {
        -String name
        -int age
        +getName() String
        +getAge() int
    }
    class Professor {
        -String empId
        -String specialization
        +teach() void
    }
    class Student {
        -int rollNo
        -String branch
        +register() void
    }
    class TeachingAssistant {
        -String labName
        +assistLab() void
    }
    class Department {
        -String deptName
        -OfficeRoom[] rooms
        -Professor[] staff
    }
    class OfficeRoom {
        -int roomNumber
        -int capacity
    }
    class Course {
        -String courseCode
        -String title
    }

    Person <|-- Professor
    Person <|-- Student
    Student <|-- TeachingAssistant
    Department *-- OfficeRoom : composition
    Department o-- Professor : aggregation
    Professor --> Course : teaches
    Course --> Professor : taughtBy
```

## 4.3 Reuse Decision Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to reuse class B in class A] --> Q1{Is A a type of B?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does LSP hold?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Does A own B for its lifetime?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> INH[Use Inheritance extends]
    Q2 -- No --> COMP[Use Composition final field]
    Q3 -- Yes --> COMP
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Does A reference B temporarily?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> ASSOC[Use Association]
    Q4 -- No --> AGG[Use Aggregation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for students:** The decision tree maps directly to the KTU 2024 exam's classic *"Justify your choice of relationship"* questions. Memorize the order: **LSP check → Ownership check → Lifecycle check**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define software reuse. List any four reuse mechanisms supported in Java with one-line examples.*

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

**Software Reuse:** Software reuse is the process of creating new software systems by integrating existing software components, classes, or modules, thereby reducing development time, cost, and redundancy.

**Four Reuse Mechanisms in Java:**

1. **Inheritance (Is-A):** `class Dog extends Animal` — Dog reuses Animal's fields and methods.
2. **Composition (Has-A, strong):** `class Car { private final Engine engine = new Engine(); }` — Car owns its Engine.
3. **Aggregation (Has-A, weak):** `class Department { private Professor[] prof; }` — Department references external Professors.
4. **Association (Knows-A):** `class Teacher { private Student[] students; }` — bidirectional peer link.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often *omit* the keyword `final` when describing composition. Marks are deducted because **`final`** is what *enforces* the strong-ownership semantic in Java. Always mention it.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) and state why it is critical for inheritance-based reuse.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)**, formulated by *Barbara Liskov (1987)*, states:

> *"Objects of a superclass shall be replaceable with objects of a subclass without altering the desirable properties of the program."*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
\forall\, S \in \text{Subclass}, \quad \forall\, P \in \text{Program}, \quad P(S) \equiv P(\text{Superclass instance})
$$

**Why it is critical for inheritance-based reuse:**

- It guarantees that **subclass objects can be used wherever the superclass is expected** (polymorphic substitution).
- It ensures **behavioral correctness** — overridden methods must not violate the parent's contract.
- Violation of LSP leads to the *fragile base class problem* and breaks runtime polymorphism.
- In Java, violating LSP is detected only at runtime via `ClassCastException` or failed unit tests, hence the design responsibility lies with the developer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with *internal choice* — i.e., the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) Differentiate between Inheritance, Composition, and Aggregation with Java code examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Inheritance | Composition | Aggregation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Relationship | Is-A | Has-A (strong) | Has-A (weak) |
| Keyword in Java | `extends` | Field with `new` | Field via setter/ctor |
| UML Symbol | Hollow triangle | Filled diamond | Hollow diamond |
| Lifetime coupling | Coupled at compile-time | Part dies with whole | Part independent |

**Java Code Snippet:**

```java
// Inheritance
class Vehicle { void start() { System.out.println("Start"); } }
class Bike extends Vehicle { }   // Bike IS-A Vehicle

// Composition
class Wheel { }
class Car {
    private final Wheel w1 = new Wheel();   // created INSIDE
    private final Wheel w2 = new Wheel();
}

// Aggregation
class Player { }
class Team {
    private Player[] players;   // passed from outside
    public Team(Player[] p) { this.players = p; }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- In `Bike extends Vehicle`, the Bike inherits `start()`. This is *compile-time* reuse.
- In `Car`, the `Wheel` objects are created *inside* the Car class and marked `final`, enforcing that no Wheel can outlive the Car — this is **composition**.
- In `Team`, the `Player[]` is passed in via the constructor, meaning the Players existed *before* the Team — this is **aggregation**.

**(b) Explain the "Favor Composition Over Inheritance" principle. Justify it using the Fragile Base Class problem. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Principle:** Proposed in *Effective Java* (Bloch) and the *Gang of Four* (GoF) book, this principle states that **class reuse should prefer object composition over class inheritance**, unless a true "is-a" relationship exists.

**Justification via Fragile Base Class Problem:**

- In inheritance, the subclass depends on the *internal implementation details* of the superclass.
- If the superclass is later modified (e.g., a method's internal logic changes), subclass behavior may break **even if the superclass's public API is unchanged**.
- This is called the **fragile base class problem**, and it has caused famous production bugs (e.g., the Java AWT `Component` class hierarchy).

**Mathematical Justification using Reuse Density:**

$$
RD_{composition} > RD_{inheritance} \quad \text{when } n_{parts} \ge 3
$$

Because composition can dynamically swap parts at runtime (via interfaces), it achieves higher reuse density across more contexts.

**Real-world Example:** The Java `InputStream` hierarchy uses **decorator composition** (`BufferedInputStream(InputStream in)`) rather than subclassing, allowing unlimited stacking of behaviors at runtime.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Option 2*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) With a suitable UML class diagram, model a Library system demonstrating Inheritance, Aggregation, and Association. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Entities and their relationships:

- `Member` (base class) → specialized into `StudentMember` and `FacultyMember` (**Inheritance**).
- `Library` **aggregates** `Book` objects (Books can exist outside the Library).
- `Member` is **associated** with `Book` (a Member borrows many Books, a Book is borrowed by one Member at a time).

**UML Class Diagram (textual representation):**

```
         Member  (abstract)
            △
           / \
          /   \
StudentMember  FacultyMember

Library  ◇----------*  Book
   |
   |
   |   borrows
   ↓
   Member  -------- *  Book    (Association: 1 Member borrows 0..N Books)
```

**Java Implementation Sketch:**

```java
abstract class Member {
    protected int memberId;
    abstract void borrow(Book b);
}

class StudentMember extends Member {
    void borrow(Book b) { /* ... */ }
}

class Book {
    private String isbn;
    private String title;
    private Member currentBorrower;  // association
}

class Library {
    private List<Book> books;        // aggregation
    public Library(List<Book> books) { this.books = books; }
}
```

**(b) Discuss the diamond problem. How does Java resolve it? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diamond Problem Definition:**

The diamond problem arises in multiple inheritance when a class `D` inherits from two classes `B` and `C`, both of which inherit from a common class `A`. This creates an *ambiguity*: if `A` defines a method `m()`, and both `B` and `C` override it differently, then `D.m()` is **ambiguous**.

**Visual representation (Diamond):**

$$
A \rightarrow B \;\diagdown\;\; C \rightarrow D
$$

Class $D$ has two paths to $A$, hence the shape resembles a diamond.

**Java's Resolution Strategy:**

1. **Disallow multiple class inheritance:** Java forbids `class D extends B, C`. Only one class can be extended.
2. **Allow multiple interface inheritance:** Java allows `class D implements B, C` where `B` and `C` are interfaces.
3. **Diamond-safe interface rules:**
   - Interfaces may declare `default` methods (Java 8+).
   - If two interfaces provide conflicting `default` methods, the implementing class **must override** the method explicitly to resolve the conflict.
   - Example:

```java
interface A { default void m() { System.out.println("A.m"); } }
interface B extends A { default void m() { System.out.println("B.m"); } }
interface C extends A { default void m() { System.out.println("C.m"); } }
class D implements B, C {
    @Override
    public void m() { B.super.m(); }  // explicit disambiguation
}
```

This is *deterministic*, *compiler-checked*, and *maintainable* — hence Java's approach is considered superior to C++'s virtual inheritance resolution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering diamond problem questions, students frequently forget to mention **default methods** (introduced in Java 8). Always state that the problem *re-emerged* with default methods and Java requires *explicit override disambiguation* to resolve it. Skipping this loses **3 of 7 marks** in this sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reuse Concepts** in OOD = Inheriting or composing existing class structures to build new functionality.
- The **four reuse mechanisms** are: **Inheritance, Composition, Aggregation, Association**.
- **Inheritance** uses the Java keyword `extends` for classes and `implements` for interfaces; it represents an **"is-a"** relationship.
- **Composition** is a strong "has-a" relationship; the part's lifetime is bound to the whole; use `private final` fields instantiated inside the class.
- **Aggregation** is a weak "has-a" relationship; the part exists independently; the part is passed in via constructor or setter.
- **Association** is a "uses-a / knows-a" relationship with independent lifecycles on both sides; often bidirectional.
- Java **does not support multiple class inheritance** to avoid the diamond problem; multiple interface inheritance is allowed.
- **Favor Composition Over Inheritance** is a GoF/Bloch design maxim that reduces coupling and avoids the *fragile base class problem*.
- **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** must hold for inheritance to be a valid reuse — quantified via the LSC score requiring $LSC = 1.0$.
- **UML symbols to memorize cold:** ▷ (inheritance), ◆ (composition), ◇ (aggregation), ─ (association).
- **Reuse Density** $RD$ measures the fraction of reused classes; higher is better, but **Coupling Factor** $CF$ must stay $\le 0.3$ for a healthy system.
- **Inheritance depth** should be kept $\le 5$ levels for maintainability (McCabe-style complexity heuristic).
- **Default methods in interfaces (Java 8+)** reintroduced a limited form of diamond problem; explicit override disambiguation is mandatory.
- For KTU 2024 exams, always support a relationship choice with **three justifications**: (1) UML symbol, (2) Java keyword, (3) lifetime dependency argument.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reuse Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reuse Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reuse Activities** are the systematic, procedural, and organizational set of engineering operations performed across the software development lifecycle to identify, classify, select, adapt, integrate, and maintain **reusable software assets** (components, classes, patterns, frameworks) for the purpose of constructing new systems or evolving existing ones, while ensuring functional correctness, architectural coherence, and lifecycle cost efficiency.

In the context of **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, Reuse Activities constitute the operational bridge between the *philosophy of reuse* and the *practical implementation of reuse mechanisms* such as **Inheritance, Composition, Aggregation, Delegation, Parameterized Classes, Frameworks, and Design Patterns**.

The canonical classification of Reuse Activities as adopted in academic literature (and consistent with KTU Module 3 syllabus) includes **seven core activities**:

1. **Conceptualization** — Recognizing reuse opportunities from problem-domain analysis.
2. **Analysis & Specification** — Formalizing the *contract* of the reusable asset (interface, behavior, constraints).
3. **Identification & Search** — Locating candidate reusable assets in the asset repository (library, catalog).
4. **Classification & Cataloging** — Organizing assets taxonomically (e.g., by domain, granularity, abstraction level).
5. **Selection & Evaluation** — Choosing the best-fit asset using qualitative and quantitative criteria.
6. **Adaptation & Customization** — Modifying (or wrapping) the asset to fit the new context.
7. **Integration & Maintenance** — Incorporating the asset into the new system and managing its evolution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Toolbox" Analogy:** Imagine you are a carpenter building furniture. Do you forge a new hammer for every chair you build? **No.** You maintain a **toolbox** (the asset library), **classify** tools by purpose (cataloging), **pick** the right hammer (selection), **sharpen or modify** it slightly for a special job (adaptation), and **use** it in your workshop (integration). **Reuse Activities** are precisely this disciplined engineering process applied to software — instead of writing every class from scratch, an OOD developer follows these activities to leverage existing, battle-tested assets.

A second useful analogy: **Reuse Activities are to software what a library's acquisition workflow is to a librarian.** A librarian doesn't just *buy books*. They **identify gaps (Conceptualization), negotiate specifications with publishers (Specification), search catalogs (Identification), classify using Dewey Decimal (Classification), select based on patron needs (Selection), bind or repair (Adaptation), and shelve for circulation (Integration)**.

## 1.3 Why Reuse Activities Matter in KTU 2024 Context

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** where Course Outcomes (COs) require students to **design**, not merely *code*. Reuse Activities directly address higher-order Bloom's levels (**Analyze, Evaluate, Create**), which is reflected in the 14-mark Part B questions typically allocated to this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric — Industry Data:** Empirical industry studies (publicly reported metrics) indicate that systematic reuse can reduce development effort by **40%–60%**, defect density by **50%–90%**, and time-to-market by **30%–50%**. These figures are not KTU-prescribed constants, but are industry-validated benchmarks commonly cited in software engineering literature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Reuse Activities — Operational Breakdown

### Activity 1: Conceptualization
- **Goal:** Recognize that a problem has a reusable solution waiting to be leveraged.
- **How:** Conduct *domain analysis* — examine commonality and variability across problem requirements.
- **Output:** A *reuse opportunity report* listing candidate areas (e.g., persistence, UI rendering, logging, communication).
- **Why it matters:** Without conceptualization, every project reinvents the wheel, leading to duplicated effort and inconsistent design.

### Activity 2: Analysis & Specification
- **Goal:** Define *what* the reusable asset must do, independent of *how*.
- **How:** Specify the asset's **interface (contract)**, **pre-conditions**, **post-conditions**, and **invariants**.
- **Output:** A *component specification document* or, in Java terms, an **interface or abstract class definition**.
- **Why it matters:** A well-specified contract enables **black-box reuse**, decoupling clients from implementation details.

### Activity 3: Identification & Search
- **Goal:** Locate existing candidate assets in the asset base.
- **How:** Use *faceted classification*, *keyword search*, *signature matching*, or *pattern recognition* to scan the repository.
- **Output:** A *shortlist* of candidate components with their specifications.
- **Why it matters:** The best reuse is the reuse you actually find; poor discoverability kills reuse programs.

### Activity 4: Classification & Cataloging
- **Goal:** Organize assets in a structured repository for efficient retrieval.
- **How:** Apply taxonomies such as **domain-based**, **functionality-based**, or **abstraction-level-based** (e.g., object → class → package → framework).
- **Output:** A *structured asset catalog* (e.g., Maven repository, internal asset library).
- **Why it matters:** Without classification, the asset base becomes a *digital graveyard* of unused code.

### Activity 5: Selection & Evaluation
- **Goal:** Choose the asset that best satisfies the new context's quality, functional, and non-functional requirements.
- **How:** Apply evaluation criteria:
  - **Functional fit** (does it do what we need?)
  - **Quality metrics** (reliability, performance, maintainability)
  - **Compatibility** (architectural, technological, licensing)
  - **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — including adaptation, integration, and future maintenance.
- **Output:** A *selection decision record* justifying the choice.
- **Why it matters:** Premature or biased selection leads to **forced-fit reuse**, which often costs more than greenfield development.

### Activity 6: Adaptation & Customization
- **Goal:** Modify the asset to fit the new context without breaking its core contract.
- **How:** Apply *wrapping* (Adapter pattern), *parameterization* (Generics/Template Method), *subclassing* (Inheritance), or *configuration*.
- **Output:** An *adapted asset* that satisfies both the original contract and the new requirements.
- **Why it matters:** Off-the-shelf rarely fits perfectly; controlled adaptation is the heart of practical reuse.

### Activity 7: Integration & Maintenance
- **Goal:** Incorporate the asset into the target system and manage its evolution.
- **How:** Use **dependency injection**, **service locators**, **OSGi**, or **Java Module System** for integration. Maintain via version control, regression testing, and **backward compatibility** discipline.
- **Output:** A *deployed system* plus an *updated asset repository* (with feedback loop).
- **Why it matters:** Reuse is not a one-shot activity; without maintenance, the asset becomes legacy and decays in value.

## 2.2 Reuse Granularity Hierarchy

Reuse Activities operate at different **granularities**. The KTU examiner often asks students to compare these levels.

| Granularity Level | Reuse Mechanism | Java Construct | Coupling Type | Flexibility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Function** | Code snippet / utility | Static method | None | Low |
| **Class** | Inheritance / Composition | `class`, `extends`, `has-a` | Tight (Inh.) / Loose (Comp.) | Medium |
| **Abstract Class** | Template Method | `abstract class` | Medium | Medium |
| **Interface** | Polymorphism | `interface`, `implements` | Loose | High |
| **Package** | API reuse | `import`, JAR | Loose | High |
| **Framework** | Inversion of Control | Spring, JavaFX | Configured | Very High |
| **Pattern** | Architectural template | GoF patterns | Conceptual | Very High |
| **Application** | COTS / Product-line | Full app | N/A | Variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coupling & Flexibility Trade-off:** As you climb the granularity hierarchy, the *flexibility* of reuse generally increases, but so does the *architectural commitment*. A student must understand that **white-box reuse (Inheritance)** gives high control but high coupling, while **black-box reuse (Composition/Delegation)** gives lower coupling but requires more design discipline.

## 2.3 Reuse Mechanisms — Comparative Matrix

| Mechanism | Type | Java Syntax | Advantages | Disadvantages |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inheritance** | White-box | `class B extends A` | Code reuse, polymorphism | Breaks encapsulation, fragile base class |
| **Composition** | Black-box | `class B { A a; }` | Loose coupling, runtime flexibility | More boilerplate, delegation overhead |
| **Aggregation** | Black-box (weak) | `class B { A a; }` (shared lifetime) | Models "has-a" with shared parts | Lifecycle management complexity |
| **Delegation** | Black-box | `b.doX() { a.doX(); }` | Decouples interface from impl. | Verbose, many forwarding methods |
| **Generics** | Parametric | `class Box<T>` | Type safety, no casting | Cannot reuse primitive-specific code easily |
| **Interfaces** | Contractual | `interface I` | Multiple inheritance of type | No implementation reuse |
| **Default Methods** | Limited white-box | `default void m(){}` in interface | Backward-compatible evolution | Diamond inheritance ambiguity |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **quantitative and qualitative** metrics, formulas, and decision rules students must memorize for KTU examinations. Pay special attention to the **Reuse Effectiveness Index** — it is a derivable metric often tested.

| Metric / Formula | Equation | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reuse Ratio (RR)** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{\text{Reused LOC}}{\text{Total LOC}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of lines-of-code reused. | Percent (%) |
| **Reuse Effectiveness Index (REI)** | $\text{REI} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Avoided}}{\text{Defects Introduced by Reuse}} + \text{RR}$ | Quality-adjusted reuse score. | Dimensionless |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(\text{Gain} - \text{Cost})}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Financial justification for reuse investment. | Percent (%) |
| **Adaptation Cost Ratio (ACR)** | $\text{ACR} = \dfrac{\text{Adaptation Effort}}{\text{Total Reuse Effort}}$ | Fraction of effort spent adapting. | Dimensionless |
| **Coupling Coefficient (C)** | $C = \dfrac{\text{External References}}{\text{Total References}}$ | Lower is better (loose coupling). | Dimensionless |
| **Black-box vs White-box rule** | If $\text{IS-A} \to$ White-box (Inh.) | Decision rule for mechanism choice. | Logical |
| **Composite Reuse Principle (CRP)** | Favor $\text{HAS-A}$ over $\text{IS-A}$ | Object-oriented design guideline. | Logical |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Software:** Banks reuse *authentication frameworks* (e.g., Spring Security) across hundreds of applications rather than building one per app.
- **Embedded Systems:** Automotive ECU developers reuse *AUTOSAR components* to standardize software across car models — this is reuse at industrial scale.
- **Web Development:** Frontend developers reuse *React component libraries* (Material-UI, Bootstrap) — this is composition-based reuse.
- **AI/ML Pipelines:** Data scientists reuse *TensorFlow/PyTorch layers* — this is parameteric/generic reuse.
- **Telecommunications:** Telecom software reuses *protocol stacks* (e.g., SIP, Diameter) — this is framework-level reuse.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Applying Reuse Activities to a Java Case Study

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this section, we will **exhaustively walk through** the seven Reuse Activities applied to a real Java case study. The case study: a **University wants to build a "Course Management System" (CMS)** and has an **existing library system** that handles user authentication, logging, and notifications. We will systematically apply each reuse activity.

## 3.1 Step 1: Conceptualization

**Input:** New CMS requirements — students enroll, faculty upload grades, admin manages courses.

**Analysis:**
- Commonality with existing Library System: **user authentication**, **audit logging**, **email/SMS notifications**, **database persistence**.
- Variability: New entities (Course, Enrollment, Grade), new UI workflows, new business rules.

**Reuse Opportunities Identified:**
1. Authentication service
2. Audit logging framework
3. Notification service
4. Generic CRUD persistence layer

**Output:** Reuse Opportunity Report (textual artifact).

## 3.2 Step 2: Analysis & Specification

For each candidate, we specify the **contract**. In Java, this means defining an **interface**.

```java
// Step 2 Output: Interface Specification (Contract)

public interface AuthenticationService {
    boolean authenticate(String username, String password);
    User getCurrentUser();
    void logout();
}

public interface NotificationService {
    void sendEmail(String to, String subject, String body);
    void sendSMS(String phoneNumber, String message);
}

public interface AuditLogger {
    void logAction(String userId, String action, String resource);
}
```

**Specification Discipline:**
- *Pre-condition:* `authenticate` requires non-null, non-empty credentials.
- *Post-condition:* On success, `getCurrentUser()` returns a non-null `User` object.
- *Invariant:* A logged-out user cannot access protected resources.

## 3.3 Step 3: Identification & Search

Search the existing asset repository (which we can model as a package or Maven dependency):

```
com.university.library
    ├── authentication
    │   └── LibraryAuthenticationService.java   ← CANDIDATE
    ├── notification
    │   ├── EmailNotifier.java                  ← CANDIDATE
    │   └── SMSNotifier.java                    ← CANDIDATE
    └── audit
        └── DatabaseAuditLogger.java            ← CANDIDATE
```

**Search Technique Used:** *Keyword search* on interface names, *faceted search* by domain (auth, notification, audit).

## 3.4 Step 4: Classification & Cataloging

| Asset | Domain | Granularity | License | Version | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `LibraryAuthenticationService` | Security | Class | Internal | 2.3.1 | Stable |
| `EmailNotifier` | Communication | Class | Internal | 1.7.0 | Stable |
| `SMSNotifier` | Communication | Class | Internal | 1.7.0 | Beta |
| `DatabaseAuditLogger` | Observability | Class | Internal | 3.0.0 | Deprecated (v3.1 available) |

**Taxonomy Used:** *Domain-based primary key + Granularity-based secondary key + Lifecycle-status tertiary key*.

## 3.5 Step 5: Selection & Evaluation

Applying the **Selection Criteria** from Section 2:

**Candidate 1: `LibraryAuthenticationService`**
- ✅ Functional fit: Implements `AuthenticationService` interface (the contract we defined in Step 2).
- ✅ Quality: 99.2% test coverage, no critical bugs in last 12 months.
- ✅ Compatible: Uses same JDK (17) and dependency versions.
- ✅ TCO: Estimated adaptation = 4 person-days; new build = 30 person-days.
- **Decision: REUSE** ✅

**Candidate 2: `DatabaseAuditLogger`**
- ❌ Version 3.0 is deprecated; v3.1 has breaking changes.
- ⚠️ Requires migration script and schema change.
- **Decision: REUSE WITH UPGRADE** ⚠️

**Candidate 3: `SMSNotifier`**
- ❌ Still in Beta; not production-ready.
- **Decision: DO NOT REUSE — BUILD NEW** ❌

**Quantitative Justification (using formulas from Section 2):**

Let us compute the Reuse Ratio for the CMS project:

$$
\text{Total LOC}_{\text{project}} = 25{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Reused LOC} = 4{,}200 \quad (\text{auth} = 1{,}800 + \text{email} = 1{,}200 + \text{audit adapter} = 1{,}200)
$$
$$
\text{RR} = \frac{4{,}200}{25{,}000} \times 100\% = 16.8\%
$$

This is modest; we target **30%+** by also reusing the persistence layer and UI components in subsequent iterations.

## 3.6 Step 6: Adaptation & Customization

The `LibraryAuthenticationService` returns a `LibraryUser`, but CMS needs a `CMSUser`. We apply the **Adapter Pattern** (a reuse activity best practice):

```java
// Step 6 Output: Adapter Pattern for cross-domain user mapping

// Target interface (what CMS expects)
public interface AuthenticationService {
    CMSUser authenticate(String username, String password);
}

// Adaptee (existing library class — DO NOT MODIFY)
public class LibraryAuthenticationService {
    public LibraryUser authenticate(String username, String password) { /* ... */ }
    // ... existing implementation
}

// Adapter (reuse mechanism)
public class AuthServiceAdapter implements AuthenticationService {
    private final LibraryAuthenticationService libraryAuth;
    
    public AuthServiceAdapter(LibraryAuthenticationService libraryAuth) {
        this.libraryAuth = libraryAuth;
    }
    
    @Override
    public CMSUser authenticate(String username, String password) {
        LibraryUser libUser = libraryAuth.authenticate(username, password);
        if (libUser == null) return null;
        // Map LibraryUser -> CMSUser
        return new CMSUser(
            libUser.getId(),
            libUser.getFullName(),
            mapRoles(libUser.getRoles())
        );
    }
    
    private Set<Role> mapRoles(Set<LibraryRole> libRoles) {
        // Domain translation logic
    }
}
```

**Adaptation Cost Ratio Calculation:**

$$
\text{Adaptation Effort} = 4 \text{ person-days}
$$
$$
\text{Total Reuse Effort} = 4 + 2 \text{ (integration)} = 6 \text{ person-days}
$$
$$
\text{ACR} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.667 = 66.7\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An ACR above **50%** is a yellow flag — the asset required significant adaptation. In a mature reuse program, we would refactor the library service to be more **domain-agnostic** to reduce future ACR.

## 3.7 Step 7: Integration & Maintenance

Integration via **Dependency Injection** (Spring-style, demonstrating framework-level reuse):

```java
// Step 7 Output: Integration in CMS Application

@Configuration
public class CMSConfig {
    
    @Bean
    public AuthenticationService authenticationService() {
        LibraryAuthenticationService libAuth = new LibraryAuthenticationService();
        return new AuthServiceAdapter(libAuth);
    }
    
    @Bean
    public NotificationService notificationService() {
        return new EmailNotifier();  // direct reuse, no adaptation needed
    }
    
    @Bean
    public AuditLogger auditLogger() {
        return new DatabaseAuditLogger();  // upgraded to v3.1
    }
}
```

**Maintenance Loop (the feedback to the asset repository):**
1. CMS discovers a **bug** in `EmailNotifier`'s handling of Unicode subjects.
2. Bug is **reported back** to the library team's repository.
3. Library team **patches** and releases v1.7.1.
4. CMS **upgrades** dependency — all consumers of the library benefit.

This is the **Reuse Virtuous Cycle** — every consumer's bug report improves the asset for everyone.

## 3.8 Summary Table — The Reuse Activity Walkthrough

| Step | Activity | Output Artifact | Reuse Mechanism |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Conceptualization | Reuse Opportunity Report | Domain analysis |
| 2 | Analysis & Spec. | Interface definitions | Contract-first design |
| 3 | Identification | Candidate list | Repository search |
| 4 | Classification | Asset catalog | Taxonomy application |
| 5 | Selection | Decision record | Evaluation criteria |
| 6 | Adaptation | Adapter class | Adapter Pattern |
| 7 | Integration | Configured beans | Dependency Injection |
