# MODULE 4: OVERVIEW — OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Module 4** of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Object Oriented Design using Java* (PEITT411) provides a panoramic introduction to the **advanced type systems and data-handling pillars of Java**. Specifically, this module consolidates the conceptual foundations of **Java Generics**, the **Java Collections Framework (JCF)**, **Utility Classes (Arrays, Objects)**, and **Lambda-based Stream processing**. Collectively, these constructs enable programmers to write *type-safe*, *reusable*, and *algorithmically efficient* code — the trifecta demanded by modern enterprise software engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> This module is the *bridge* between core OOP (Modules 1–3: Classes, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Exception Handling) and applied Java development (Modules 5–6: GUI, JDBC, Networking). Mastery here is non-negotiable for solving 14-mark Part B questions and the comprehensive viva voce.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are moving houses. In the old world, you have **one cardboard box** for *everything* — books, glassware, clothes — and you must label each item by hand to know what's inside. This is **pre-Generics Java** (raw `Object` types) — flexible but unsafe, requiring manual `Casting` at every step.

In the modern world, you have **specialized containers**:
- A **Wardrobe** (a `List`) for clothes in order.
- A **Bookshelf** (a `Set`) where duplicates are automatically rejected.
- A **Phonebook** (a `Map`) for quick name-to-number lookups.
- A **Refrigerator dispenser** (a `Queue`) — first item in, first item out.

This is the **Java Collections Framework**. Add a **sturdy, custom-built wardrobe** (a `Generic` class parameterized by `<T>`), and you have the full power of Module 4: *type safety + structured data handling + reusable code*.

### 1.3 Why Module 4 Matters — The "Big Picture"

| Engineering Pain Point | Module 4 Solution |
|---|---|
| `ClassCastException` at runtime | **Generics** — compile-time type safety |
| Reinventing data structures (linked lists, trees) | **Collections Framework** — pre-built, tested, optimized |
| Verbose iteration with `Iterator` | **Enhanced `for` loop / `forEach()`** — concise traversal |
| Brittle algorithms tied to data types | **Lambda expressions + Streams** — functional style |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Kotlin/Java Industry Standard)**: A well-designed generic collection reduces `RuntimeException` rates by **~40%** and improves code maintainability scores by **~30%** in enterprise codebases (measured via tools like SonarQube).

### 1.4 Module 4 — Conceptual Roadmap
The module logically progresses through five interlocking sub-topics:

1. **Generics** — parameterized types (`<T>`, `<K, V>`)
2. **Wrapper Classes & Autoboxing** — bridging primitives to objects
3. **The `java.util.Collection` Hierarchy** — `List`, `Set`, `Queue`
4. **The `java.util.Map` Hierarchy** — key-value stores
5. **Utility Algorithms** — `Collections` class, `Arrays` class, comparators, streams

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generics — The Theory of Parameterized Types

Generics enable **type parameters** to be defined for classes, interfaces, and methods, allowing the same code to operate on different data types *without sacrificing compile-time safety*.

**Core Theoretical Principles:**

- **Type Erasure (JVM Reality)**: The Java compiler performs *type erasure* — generic type information is removed at runtime, replaced with `Object` (or its upper bound). This ensures **backward compatibility** with pre-Java 5 code.
- **Bounded Type Parameters**: Restrict generic types using `extends` (upper bound) or `super` (lower bound, used in wildcards).
- **Wildcards `?`**: Represent an *unknown type*. Three flavors: unbounded (`?`), upper-bounded (`? extends T`), lower-bounded (`? super T`).
- **PECS Principle (Producer Extends, Consumer Super)**: The golden rule for wildcards in KTU examinations.

**Why Generics Exist — The "Why" Explained:**
- **Eliminates casts**: No more `(String) list.get(i)`.
- **Stronger type checks at compile time**: Bugs caught early, not in production.
- **Enables generic algorithms**: Write once, use on any type.

### 2.2 The Java Collections Framework (JCF) — Architecture

The JCF is built on **four core interfaces**:

1. **`Collection<E>`** — root of the collection hierarchy
2. **`List<E>`** — ordered, allows duplicates, index-based access
3. **`Set<E>`** — no duplicates, mathematical set semantics
4. **`Queue<E>`** — typically FIFO (First-In-First-Out) for processing
5. **`Map<K, V>`** — key-value pairs (NOT a subtype of `Collection`)

### 2.3 Wrapper Classes & Autoboxing

Java has **8 primitive types** that are *not* objects. To use them in generics (which require reference types), Java provides **wrapper classes**:

| Primitive | Wrapper Class | Default Value (Object) |
|---|---|---|
| `byte` | `Byte` | `null` |
| `short` | `Short` | `null` |
| `int` | `Integer` | `null` |
| `long` | `Long` | `null` |
| `float` | `Float` | `null` |
| `double` | `Double` | `null` |
| `char` | `Character` | `null` |
| `boolean` | `Boolean` | `null` |

**Autoboxing** = automatic conversion `int → Integer` (compile-time syntactic sugar).
**Unboxing** = automatic conversion `Integer → int` (compile-time syntactic sugar).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Generic Class | `class Box<T> { T item; }` | `T` is a *type parameter* |
| Generic Method | `public <T> void show(T item)` | Type inferred at call site |
| Upper Bounded Wildcard | `? extends Number` | Accepts `Integer`, `Double`, etc. |
| Lower Bounded Wildcard | `? super Integer` | Accepts `Integer`, `Number`, `Object` |
| Unbounded Wildcard | `?` | Any type |
| Diamond Operator | `List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();` | Type inference (Java 7+) |
| Autoboxing | `Integer x = 10;` | `int` → `Integer` (implicit) |
| Unboxing | `int y = new Integer(20);` | `Integer` → `int` (implicit) |
| `List` time complexity (ArrayList) | Access: **O(1)**, Insert/Delete at end: **O(1)** amortized, Insert/Delete at middle: **O(n)** | Indexed, resizable array |
| `List` time complexity (LinkedList) | Access: **O(n)**, Insert/Delete at ends: **O(1)** | Doubly-linked nodes |
| `HashSet` time complexity | Add/Contains/Remove: average **O(1)**, worst **O(n)** | Backed by `HashMap` |
| `TreeSet` time complexity | Add/Contains/Remove: **O(log n)** | Red-Black tree, sorted |
| `HashMap` time complexity | Get/Put: average **O(1)**, worst **O(n)** | Hash table backing |
| `TreeMap` time complexity | Get/Put: **O(log n)** | Red-Black tree, sorted keys |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction for Exams**: `Collection<E>` is the root, but `Map<K, V>` is a *separate hierarchy* — it does **not** extend `Collection`.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Type-Safety Improvement Using Generics

**Step 1 — Define the Raw (Unsafe) Class**:
A pre-Generics container stores `Object`, requiring explicit casts:

```java
class RawBox {
    private Object item;
    public void set(Object item) { this.item = item; }
    public Object get() { return item; }
}
```

**Step 2 — Identify the Risk**:
At runtime, a wrong cast causes `ClassCastException`:

```java
RawBox box = new RawBox();
box.set("Hello");
// box.set(123); // Compiles fine, breaks later
String s = (String) box.get(); // Manual cast required
```

**Step 3 — Define the Generic (Safe) Class**:
Parameterize the type:

```java
class Box<T> {
    private T item;
    public void set(T item) { this.item = item; }
    public T get() { return item; }
}
```

**Step 4 — Use the Generic Class**:
The compiler enforces type safety:

```java
Box<String> stringBox = new Box<>();
stringBox.set("Hello");
// stringBox.set(123); // COMPILE-TIME ERROR — caught here!
String s = stringBox.get(); // No cast needed
```

**Analytical Matrix: Raw vs. Generic Containers**

| Aspect | Raw Container (`Object`) | Generic Container (`<T>`) |
|---|---|---|
| Compile-time type check | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Manual casting required | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Runtime `ClassCastException` risk | ⚠️ High | ✅ Eliminated |
| Code reusability | Limited | High |
| Backward compatible with pre-Java 5 | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (type erasure) |

### 3.2 Derivation: Bounded Type Parameter — Upper Bound

To restrict generic types to a specific family, use bounded type parameters:

```java
public class NumberBox<T extends Number> {
    private T number;
    public NumberBox(T number) { this.number = number; }
    public double getDoubleValue() {
        return number.doubleValue(); // Safe — Number guarantees this
    }
}
```

**Analytical Reasoning**:
- `T extends Number` means `T` can be `Integer`, `Double`, `Float`, `Long`, etc.
- The compiler now knows `T` is a subtype of `Number`, so methods like `doubleValue()` are *guaranteed* to exist.
- `NumberBox<String>` would produce a **compile-time error** — `String` is not a `Number`.

### 3.3 Derivation: PECS Principle Application

The **PECS rule** (Producer Extends, Consumer Super) determines which wildcard to use:

- If a structure **produces** (outputs) values → use `? extends T`
- If a structure **consumes** (inputs) values → use `? super T`

**Example: Producer (Extends)**:
```java
public void processNumbers(List<? extends Number> list) {
    for (Number n : list) { // Read-only safe
        System.out.println(n.doubleValue());
    }
    // list.add(10); // COMPILE ERROR — cannot add, only read
}
```

**Example: Consumer (Super)**:
```java
public void addIntegers(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(10); // Safe — Integer is acceptable
    list.add(20);
    // Integer x = list.get(0); // COMPILE ERROR — only Object is safe to read
}
```

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Collection

| Requirement | Recommended Collection | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Fast random access by index | `ArrayList` | Access **O(1)**, Insert **O(n)** |
| Frequent insertion/deletion in middle | `LinkedList` | Insert/Delete at known position **O(1)** |
| Unique elements, no order needed | `HashSet` | **O(1)** average |
| Unique elements, sorted | `TreeSet` | **O(log n)** |
| Unique elements, insertion-order preserved | `LinkedHashSet` | **O(1)** average |
| Key-value lookups, no order | `HashMap` | **O(1)** average |
| Key-value lookups, sorted by key | `TreeMap` | **O(log n)** |
| Key-value lookups, insertion-order preserved | `LinkedHashMap` | **O(1)** average |
| FIFO processing | `ArrayDeque` / `LinkedList` | **O(1)** for enqueue/dequeue |
| Thread-safe legacy code | `Vector` / `Hashtable` | Synchronized (slower) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: 90% of Module 4 Part B questions ask: *"Which collection class would you use for scenario X, and why?"* Memorize this matrix.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Java Collections Framework — Complete Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Iterable] --> B[java.util.Collection]
    B --> C[List]
    B --> D[Set]
    B --> E[Queue]
    C --> C1[ArrayList]
    C --> C2[LinkedList]
    C --> C3[Vector]
    C3 --> C3a[Stack]
    D --> D1[HashSet]
    D --> D2[LinkedHashSet]
    D --> D3[TreeSet]
    D1 --> D1a[LinkedHashSet]
    E --> E1[ArrayDeque]
    E --> E2[PriorityQueue]
    E --> E3[LinkedList]

    F[java.util.Map] --> F1[HashMap]
    F --> F2[LinkedHashMap]
    F --> F3[TreeMap]
    F --> F4[Hashtable]
    F1 --> F2

    G[java.util.Collections] -->|Utility methods| B
    G -->|Utility methods| F
    H[java.util.Arrays] -->|Utility methods| I[Java Arrays]
```

### 4.2 Generics — Type Parameter Resolution Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source Code: Box String] --> COMP[Java Compiler]
    COMP -->|Type Check| SAFE{Type Safe?}
    SAFE -->|Yes| ERASE[Type Erasure]
    SAFE -->|No| ERR[Compile-Time Error]
    ERASE -->|Replace T with Object| CLASS[Compiled .class File]
    CLASS --> JVM[JVM Runtime]
    JVM --> EXEC[Execution with Object types]
    ERR --> FIX[Developer Fixes Code]
```

### 4.3 Wildcard Selection — Decision Tree (PECS Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Need a Wildcard ?] --> Q1{Reading or Writing?}
    Q1 -->|Reading from structure| Q2{Structure produces values?}
    Q1 -->|Writing to structure| Q3{Structure consumes values?}

    Q2 -->|Yes, want flexibility| EXT[Use ? extends T]
    Q2 -->|No specific bound| UNB[Use ? unbounded]

    Q3 -->|Yes, want flexibility| SUP[Use ? super T]
    Q3 -->|No, just any type| UNB2[Use ? unbounded]

    EXT --> EX1[Example: List ? extends Number]
    SUP --> EX2[Example: List ? super Integer]
    UNB --> EX3[Example: List ?]
    UNB2 --> EX3
```

### 4.4 Wrapper Class Conversion Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Primitive: int 42] -->|Autoboxing| W[Wrapper: Integer 42]
    W -->|Unboxing| P

    P2[Primitive: double 3.14] -->|Autoboxing| W2[Wrapper: Double 3.14]
    W2 -->|Unboxing| P2

    W -->|Used in Generics| G[List Integer allowed]
    P -->|Cannot use directly| ERR[Compile Error in List]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Module 4 Topic Interconnection

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Foundation
        GEN[Generics: Type Safety Layer]
        WC[Wrapper Classes: Primitive Bridge]
    end

    subgraph DataStructures
        COLL[Collection Framework: Pre-built Containers]
        LIST[List Interface]
        SET[Set Interface]
        QUE[Queue Interface]
        MAP[Map Interface]
    end

    subgraph Algorithms
        UTIL[Collections and Arrays Utility Classes]
        ITER[Iterators and Enhanced For Loop]
        STREAM[Stream API and Lambdas]
    end

    GEN --> COLL
    WC --> COLL
    COLL --> LIST
    COLL --> SET
    COLL --> QUE
    MAP -.->|Parallel Hierarchy| COLL
    LIST --> UTIL
    SET --> UTIL
    MAP --> UTIL
    UTIL --> ITER
    ITER --> STREAM
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Generics in Java. Mention any two advantages of using generics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Generics, introduced in Java 5, enable **type parameters** to be defined in classes, interfaces, and methods, allowing code to operate on different data types with **compile-time type safety**.

```java
class Box<T> {
    private T content;
    public void set(T content) { this.content = content; }
    public T get() { return content; }
}
```

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Type safety at compile time** — Eliminates `ClassCastException` by catching type mismatches during compilation.
2. **Eliminates explicit casting** — No need for `(String) obj.get()`; the compiler knows the return type.

*(1 mark definition + 1.5 marks for two advantages with example = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the difference between `ArrayList` and `LinkedList` in Java?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `ArrayList` | `LinkedList` |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying structure | Resizable dynamic array | Doubly-linked list |
| Random access (`get(i)`) | **O(1)** | **O(n)** |
| Insertion/Deletion at end | **O(1)** amortized | **O(1)** |
| Insertion/Deletion at middle | **O(n)** (shifting) | **O(1)** (pointer reassignment) |
| Memory overhead | Lower (contiguous array) | Higher (node objects + pointers) |
| Best use case | Frequent reads, infrequent writes | Frequent insertions/deletions |

**Verdict**: Use `ArrayList` as default; choose `LinkedList` only when insertion/deletion at arbitrary positions is the dominant operation.

*(3 marks — full tabular comparison)*

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Generics + Collections Deep Dive**

**Q3 (a) [7 marks]**
*Explain the concept of bounded type parameters and wildcards in Java Generics. Write a Java program to create a generic class `Pair<K, V>` that stores two heterogeneous objects and provides methods to retrieve them.*

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

**Bounded type parameters** restrict the types that can be used as type arguments using the `extends` keyword:

```java
public class NumberBox<T extends Number> {
    private T value;
    public double reciprocal() {
        return 1.0 / value.doubleValue(); // Safe: Number guarantees doubleValue()
    }
}
```

This guarantees `T` is a subtype of `Number`, enabling the use of `Number`'s methods.

**Wildcards** represent an unknown type, denoted by `?`:
- `?` — unbounded, any type
- `? extends T` — upper bound, "any subtype of T" (used in **producers**)
- `? super T` — lower bound, "any supertype of T" (used in **consumers**)

**Code Implementation (4 marks):**

```java
public class Pair<K, V> {
    private K key;
    private V value;

    // Constructor
    public Pair(K key, V value) {
        this.key = key;
        this.value = value;
    }

    // Getter for key
    public K getKey() {
        return this.key;
    }

    // Getter for value
    public V getValue() {
        return this.value;
    }

    // Setter method
    public void setPair(K key, V value) {
        this.key = key;
        this.value = value;
    }

    // toString for display
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "(" + key + ", " + value + ")";
    }

    // Main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Pair<String, Integer> student = new Pair<>("Alice", 85);
        Pair<Integer, String> product = new Pair<>(101, "Laptop");

        System.out.println("Student: " + student);
        System.out.println("Product: " + product);

        System.out.println("Key: " + student.getKey());
        System.out.println("Value: " + student.getValue());
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Student: (Alice, 85)
Product: (101, Laptop)
Key: Alice
Value: 85
```

---

**Q3 (b) [7 marks]**
*Demonstrate the use of `HashMap` and `TreeMap` with a Java program that stores student roll numbers (Integer) and names (String). Display the entries sorted by roll number using `TreeMap` and in insertion order using `HashMap` with `LinkedHashMap`.*

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Difference (2 marks):**
- `HashMap` makes **no guarantees** about iteration order.
- `LinkedHashMap` preserves **insertion order**.
- `TreeMap` keeps keys **sorted in natural order** (or by a custom `Comparator`).

**Code (5 marks):**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class StudentMapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. HashMap - No order guaranteed
        Map<Integer, String> hashMap = new HashMap<>();
        hashMap.put(103, "Alice");
        hashMap.put(101, "Bob");
        hashMap.put(105, "Charlie");
        hashMap.put(102, "David");

        System.out.println("HashMap (No specific order):");
        for (Map.Entry<Integer, String> entry : hashMap.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + " => " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // 2. LinkedHashMap - Insertion order preserved
        Map<Integer, String> linkedHashMap = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        linkedHashMap.put(103, "Alice");
        linkedHashMap.put(101, "Bob");
        linkedHashMap.put(105, "Charlie");
        linkedHashMap.put(102, "David");

        System.out.println("\nLinkedHashMap (Insertion order):");
        for (Map.Entry<Integer, String> entry : linkedHashMap.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + " => " + entry.getValue());
        }

        // 3. TreeMap - Sorted by key (natural Integer order)
        Map<Integer, String> treeMap = new TreeMap<>();
        treeMap.put(103, "Alice");
        treeMap.put(101, "Bob");
        treeMap.put(105, "Charlie");
        treeMap.put(102, "David");

        System.out.println("\nTreeMap (Sorted by roll number):");
        for (Map.Entry<Integer, String> entry : treeMap.entrySet()) {
            System.out.println(entry.getKey() + " => " + entry.getValue());
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
HashMap (No specific order):
101 => Bob
102 => David
103 => Alice
105 => Charlie

LinkedHashMap (Insertion order):
103 => Alice
101 => Bob
105 => Charlie
102 => David

TreeMap (Sorted by roll number):
101 => Bob
102 => David
103 => Alice
105 => Charlie
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Using raw types (`Map map = new HashMap();`) — loses 1 mark for not using generics.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to import `java.util.*` — code won't compile, loses all marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Confusing `HashMap` and `Hashtable` — `Hashtable` is **synchronized** and **legacy**, do not recommend it for new code.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Not explaining the **time complexity** — examiners specifically look for `O(1)` vs `O(log n)` mention.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Streams + Utility Classes**

**Q4 (a) [7 marks]**
*Explain autoboxing and unboxing in Java with examples. What is the significance of wrapper classes in the Collections Framework?*

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

Java has **8 primitive types** (`int`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`, etc.) that are not objects. However, **Generics and Collections require reference types**. **Wrapper classes** (`Integer`, `Double`, `Character`, `Boolean`, etc.) wrap primitives into objects, enabling them to be used in collection types.

**Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion of a primitive to its corresponding wrapper object, performed by the compiler:

```java
Integer x = 10;      // Compiler: Integer x = Integer.valueOf(10);
```

**Unboxing** is the reverse — automatic conversion of a wrapper object back to its primitive:

```java
int y = x;            // Compiler: int y = x.intValue();
```

**Code Examples (4 marks):**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class AutoboxingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. Autoboxing - primitive to wrapper
        Integer intObj = 42;           // int 42 -> Integer 42
        Double doubleObj = 3.14;       // double 3.14 -> Double 3.14
        Boolean boolObj = true;        // boolean true -> Boolean true

        System.out.println("Autoboxed Integer: " + intObj);
        System.out.println("Autoboxed Double: " + doubleObj);
        System.out.println("Autoboxed Boolean: " + boolObj);

        // 2. Unboxing - wrapper to primitive
        int a = intObj;                // Integer 42 -> int 42
        double b = doubleObj;          // Double 3.14 -> double 3.14

        System.out.println("Unboxed int: " + a);
        System.out.println("Unboxed double: " + b);

        // 3. Significance in Collections
        List<Integer> numbers = new ArrayList<>();
        numbers.add(10);     // Autoboxing: int 10 -> Integer 10
        numbers.add(20);
        numbers.add(30);

        int sum = 0;
        for (Integer n : numbers) {
            sum += n;        // Unboxing: Integer n -> int n
        }
        System.out.println("Sum of list elements: " + sum);

        // 4. NullPointerException risk on unboxing
        Integer nullInteger = null;
        try {
            int value = nullInteger;   // NPE on unboxing null
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught NPE: Cannot unbox null Integer");
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Autoboxed Integer: 42
Autoboxed Double: 3.14
Autoboxed Boolean: true
Unboxed int: 42
Unboxed double: 3.14
Sum of list elements: 60
Caught NPE: Cannot unbox null Integer
```

**Significance in Collections:**
- `List<int>` is **illegal** — generics require reference types.
- `List<Integer>` works because of **autoboxing**.
- Wrapper classes also provide utility methods: `Integer.parseInt("100")`, `Double.parseDouble("3.14")`.

---

**Q4 (b) [7 marks]**
*Write a Java program using the `Collections` utility class to perform the following operations on a `List<Integer>`: sort, reverse, find maximum, find minimum, and shuffle.*

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation of `Collections` Class (2 marks):**
`java.util.Collections` is a utility class with **static methods** that operate on collections. It cannot be instantiated. Key methods:
- `sort(List)` — natural ascending order
- `reverse(List)` — reverses element order
- `max(Collection)` / `min(Collection)` — extremes
- `shuffle(List)` — random permutation
- `binarySearch(List, key)` — efficient searching (list must be sorted)
- `frequency(Collection, element)` — count occurrences

**Code Implementation (5 marks):**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class CollectionsUtilityDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create a List using Arrays.asList
        List<Integer> numbers = new ArrayList<>(
            Arrays.asList(45, 12, 78, 23, 56, 89, 34, 67, 90, 11)
        );

        System.out.println("Original List: " + numbers);

        // 1. Sort the list in ascending order
        Collections.sort(numbers);
        System.out.println("\nAfter sort (ascending): " + numbers);

        // 2. Reverse the list
        Collections.reverse(numbers);
        System.out.println("After reverse: " + numbers);

        // 3. Find maximum element
        int max = Collections.max(numbers);
        System.out.println("Maximum element: " + max);

        // 4. Find minimum element
        int min = Collections.min(numbers);
        System.out.println("Minimum element: " + min);

        // 5. Shuffle the list (random order)
        Collections.shuffle(numbers);
        System.out.println("After shuffle: " + numbers);

        // Bonus: Binary search (after sorting again)
        Collections.sort(numbers);
        int searchKey = 56;
        int index = Collections.binarySearch(numbers, searchKey);
        System.out.println("Binary search for " + searchKey + ": index = " + index);

        // Bonus: Frequency of an element
        int frequency = Collections.frequency(numbers, 45);
        System.out.println("Frequency of 45: " + frequency);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output (sample, shuffle output varies):**
```
Original List: [45, 12, 78, 23, 56, 89, 34, 67, 90, 11]

After sort (ascending): [11, 12, 23, 34, 45, 56, 67, 78, 89, 90]
After reverse: [90, 89, 78, 67, 56, 45, 34, 23, 12, 11]
Maximum element: 90
Minimum element: 11
After shuffle: [45, 23, 90, 12, 78, 11, 67, 34, 89, 56]
Binary search for 56: index = 4
Frequency of 45: 1
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Using `Collection` (interface) instead of `Collections` (utility class) — a classic blunder worth **−1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Calling `binarySearch` on an **unsorted** list — produces **undefined results**, not a compile error. Always sort first.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Importing wrong package — `java.util.Collections` is correct, not `java.util.Collection`.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Overview**

### 🔹 Generics Essentials
- ✅ Generic syntax: `class Box<T>`, `interface List<E>`, `public <T> void method(T item)`
- ✅ Type erasure happens at compile time — JVM sees only raw types
- ✅ Bounded types: `<T extends Number>`, `<T super Integer>` (method-level only)
- ✅ Wildcards: `?`, `? extends T` (producer), `? super T` (consumer)
- ✅ PECS Rule: **Producer Extends, Consumer Super** — *most-asked KTU question*
- ✅ Diamond operator `<>` enables type inference (Java 7+)

### 🔹 Wrapper Classes
- ✅ 8 wrappers: `Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Character`, `Boolean`
- ✅ Autoboxing: `Integer x = 10;` (primitive → wrapper, compiler-handled)
- ✅ Unboxing: `int y = x;` (wrapper → primitive, compiler-handled)
- ✅ Risk: unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException`
- ✅ `Integer.valueOf()` caches values from **−128 to 127** (interview favorite!)

### 🔹 Collections Hierarchy
- ✅ `Collection<E>` is the root; `Map<K, V>` is **separate** (not a Collection subtype)
- ✅ `List` → ordered, duplicates allowed → `ArrayList` (default), `LinkedList`
- ✅ `Set` → no duplicates → `HashSet` (fast), `TreeSet` (sorted), `LinkedHashSet` (insertion-order)
- ✅ `Queue` → FIFO → `ArrayDeque`, `PriorityQueue` (heap-ordered)
- ✅ `Map` → key-value → `HashMap` (default), `TreeMap` (sorted), `LinkedHashMap` (insertion-order)

### 🔹 Time Complexity Cheat Sheet
- ✅ `ArrayList.get(i)` → **O(1)**
- ✅ `LinkedList.addFirst/Last` → **O(1)**
- ✅ `HashMap.put/get` → **O(1)** average
- ✅ `TreeMap/TreeSet` → **O(log n)** always
- ✅ **KTU loves asking complexity** — memorize this table

### 🔹 Utility Classes
- ✅ `Collections` (with **s**) → static methods on `Collection` objects
- ✅ `Collection` (no **s**) → the **interface**
- ✅ `Arrays` → static methods for array manipulation (`sort`, `binarySearch`, `toString`, `asList`)
- ✅ `Objects` → null-safe utilities (`requireNonNull`, `equals`, `hash`)

### 🔹 Key Conceptual Distinctions (Frequent Exam Traps)
- ✅ **`Comparable` vs `Comparator`**: `Comparable` is *natural ordering* (in the class itself); `Comparator` is *external custom ordering* (separate class)
- ✅ **`Iterator` vs `ListIterator`**: `Iterator` traverses forward only; `ListIterator` can traverse both directions and modify
- ✅ **`fail-fast` vs `fail-safe` iterators**: `ArrayList`'s iterator is *fail-fast* (throws `ConcurrentModificationException`); `CopyOnWriteArrayList`'s iterator is *fail-safe*
- ✅ **`HashMap` vs `Hashtable`**: `Hashtable` is **synchronized** and **legacy**; `HashMap` is **non-synchronized** and **modern**

### 🔹 Must-Remember Programs for 14-Mark Questions
- ✅ Generic class with multiple type parameters (`Pair<K, V>`, `Triple<K, V, T>`)
- ✅ Bounded generic with `<T extends Comparable<T>>`
- ✅ Wildcard method signatures (`? extends`, `? super`)
- ✅ `HashMap` with custom object keys (override `equals()` and `hashCode()`)
- ✅ `TreeSet` with custom `Comparator` for descending order
- ✅ `Collections.sort()` with custom `Comparator` (lambda-based)
- ✅ Iterating a `Map` using `entrySet()`, `keySet()`, `values()`

### 🔹 Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns
- ✅ *"Implement a generic class that supports multiple types..."* → Use `<T>` or `<K, V>`.
- ✅ *"Explain the difference between `List`, `Set`, and `Map`..."* → Use the hierarchy diagram above.
- ✅ *"Demonstrate the use of `Comparator` interface..."* → Write a class implementing `Comparator<T>` and override `compare()`.
- ✅ *"Write a program to count word frequency using `HashMap`..."* → Classic string-processing problem.
- ✅ *"What happens if you use a raw type vs a parameterized type?"* → Compile-time vs runtime safety comparison.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy**: Always start your 14-mark answer with a **conceptual paragraph (2-3 lines)**, then provide a **clean, well-commented code snippet** with **expected output**. This structure matches KTU's official marking scheme and guarantees full marks if executed correctly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interface Specification Concepts
Interface Specification Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interface Specification Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Interface Specification Activities

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Interface Specification Activities** are a set of design activities within the **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** methodology that formally describe the **external view of an object or a subsystem** — i.e., *what* services it offers, *how* clients may invoke them, and *under what conditions* the contract holds — *without* revealing *how* the services are internally implemented.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, an **interface specification** captures the **syntactic signature** (operation name, parameter list, return type) and the **semantic contract** (preconditions, postconditions, exceptions) of each public operation exposed by a class or a subsystem. It is one of the cornerstones of the **"open–closed"** and **"information hiding"** principles that OOAD prescribes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (verbatim-style):**
> *"Interface specification is the design activity of describing the interaction points between objects — including operation signatures, type information, exception behavior, and semantic contracts — to decouple clients from servers and to support substitutability, polymorphism, and independent evolution."*

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Remote Control" Metaphor

Imagine you buy a brand-new **Smart TV**. You do **not** receive the internal circuit diagrams — you receive a **remote control** with a fixed set of **buttons** (Power, Volume, Channel, Menu, App buttons). The remote control is the **interface**.

- Each **button** = an **operation** (signature: `powerOn()`, `setVolume(int)`).
- The **labels** on the buttons tell you **what** each does, but **not how** it does it.
- If the manufacturer changes the internal board, your **remote still works** — because the *interface* is unchanged.

**Interface Specification Activities** are the engineering work the TV manufacturer performs to:
1. **Decide** which buttons (operations) to expose.
2. **Document** what each button does and the rules for pressing it.
3. **Commit** to that contract (if the user presses Power, the TV *guarantees* it will turn on or throw a clear error — it will not silently fail).

> [!NOTE]
> **In Java terms (since this is PEITT411):** the `interface` keyword in Java is a *language-level* realization of this design activity. However, in OOAD at the design stage, **interface specification exists at a higher abstraction** — it can describe interactions between subsystems, components, and even distributed services (e.g., REST endpoints, CORBA IDL), not just Java `interface` types.

### 🔑 Why Interface Specification Matters in KTU Syllabus

| # | Reason | Syllabus Hook |
|---|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Enables **modular design** | Module 4 — Subsystem & Component Design |
| 2 | Supports **polymorphism & substitutability** | Liskov Substitution Principle (Module 3 → Module 4 bridge) |
| 3 | Defines **clear contracts** between collaborating objects | Design by Contract (Bertrand Meyer) |
| 4 | Forms the **bridge between analysis & implementation** | Transition from use-case to code |
| 5 | Facilitates **parallel development** & **testing** | Unit testing, mocking, TDD |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Note:** Questions on "Interface Specification Activities" frequently appear as 7-mark subdivisions inside larger design questions (Part B). Examiners look for the *activities list*, *contract elements* (pre/post/invariant), and *UML notation*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Interface Specification Activities

According to standard OOAD references adopted by the KTU 2024 curriculum (notably influenced by **Booch, Rumbaugh, Jacobson** and **Bertrand Meyer's Design by Contract**), interface specification activities break down into **four iterative, refinement-driven tasks**:

### 🧩 Activity 1 — Interface Identification (Discovery)

**Goal:** Discover *all* the public interaction points a class/component must expose to fulfil its responsibilities.

**Inputs:**
- Use-case realizations (from Module 2 / Module 3)
- Class diagrams (from analysis)
- CRC cards (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator)
- Domain vocabulary (glossary)

**Outputs:**
- A candidate list of operations per class
- Identification of *provider* vs *requirer* interfaces
- Type signatures (operation name, parameters, return type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Provider Interface:** Operations a class *offers* to others.
> **Requirer Interface:** Operations a class *needs* from others.

### 🧩 Activity 2 — Interface Description (Specification)

**Goal:** Formally describe **what each operation does**, its **parameters**, **return values**, and **exception behavior**.

**Three Sub-Activities:**

| Sub-Activity | Description | KTU Marker |
|--------------|-------------|------------|
| **Syntactic Description** | Operation name, parameter list (types, order), return type, exceptions thrown | UML signature |
| **Semantic Description** | Meaning of the operation, side-effects, state transitions | Pre/Post-conditions |
| **Constraints & Invariants** | Global rules that must always hold before, during, after invocation | Class Invariant |

### 🧩 Activity 3 — Interface Refinement (Evolution)

**Goal:** Evolve the interface across iterations — adding, removing, or generalizing operations as the design matures.

**Refinement Heuristics:**
- **Generalization** — extract common operations into a *parent interface*.
- **Specialization** — add domain-specific operations in *sub-interfaces*.
- **Consolidation** — merge two redundant operations into one parameterized operation.
- **Decomposition** — break a complex operation into simpler sub-operations.

### 🧩 Activity 4 — Interface Verification (Validation)

**Goal:** Ensure the interface is **complete, consistent, and minimal**.

**Quality Criteria:**
- ✅ **Complete** — every public responsibility of the class is reachable.
- ✅ **Consistent** — no two operations have the same signature but different semantics.
- ✅ **Minimal** — no redundant or unused operations.
- ✅ **Cohesive** — operations belong to the same abstraction.
- ✅ **Loosely Coupled** — minimal dependencies on other classes' internals.

---

## 2.2 The "Design by Contract" (DbC) Triad — KTU High-Yield

The single most-tested sub-topic under Interface Specification in KTU 2024 is the **contract** that accompanies each operation:

### 📜 Formal Contract Elements

For every operation $op(args) \rightarrow ret$ in an interface, the specification describes three logical components:

$$
\text{Contract}(op) = \langle \textbf{Pre}(op),\ \textbf{Post}(op),\ \textbf{Inv}(C) \rangle
$$

| Element | Symbol | When Checked | Who Checks It |
|---------|--------|--------------|----------------|
| **Precondition** | $\text{Pre}(op)$ | *Before* invocation | The **client** (caller) |
| **Postcondition** | $\text{Post}(op)$ | *After* successful return | The **server** (callee) |
| **Class Invariant** | $\text{Inv}(C)$ | Before & after *every* public operation | The **server** (callee) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Pre is the caller's duty, Post is the server's duty, Invariant is the server's promise."*

### 📊 The Truth Table of Contract Enforcement

| Scenario | $\text{Pre}(op)$ | $\text{Post}(op)$ | $\text{Inv}(C)$ | Outcome |
|----------|:----------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------:|---------|
| Normal flow | ✅ Holds | ✅ Holds | ✅ Holds | Success |
| Caller error | ❌ Violated | — | — | Exception thrown by server (`IllegalArgumentException`) |
| Server bug | ✅ Holds | ❌ Violated | — | Assertion failure / Undefined behaviour |
| Persistent corruption | ✅ Holds | ✅ Holds | ❌ Violated | Assertion failure / System error |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Notation / Formula | KTU-Mandatory Unit / Symbol |
|---|---------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Operation signature | $op(p_1 : T_1,\ p_2 : T_2,\ \dots,\ p_n : T_n) \rightarrow T_{ret}$ | $T_i$ = types, $T_{ret}$ = return type |
| 2 | Contract tuple | $\langle \text{Pre},\ \text{Post},\ \text{Inv} \rangle$ | Boolean predicates |
| 3 | Precondition predicate | $\text{Pre}(op)(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ | Boolean: $T \mid F$ |
| 4 | Postcondition predicate | $\text{Post}(op)(p_1, \dots, p_n,\ ret,\ old(state))$ | References $old(s)$ for state changes |
| 5 | Class Invariant | $\text{Inv}(C) \Leftrightarrow \forall\ op \in \text{PublicOps}(C) : \text{Inv}(C) \text{ holds}$ | Quantified predicate |
| 6 | Liskov Substitutability | $\forall\ x : S.\ \forall\ y : S \rightarrow T.\ \text{Pre}_{T}(y(x)) \Rightarrow \text{Pre}_{S}(y(x))$ | Type-theoretic |
| 7 | Number of interface methods | $N_{methods} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i$ | $m_i$ = methods in interface $i$ |
| 8 | Coupling metric (informal) | $\text{Coupling}(C) = \vert\{ D \mid C \text{ uses } D \}\vert$ | Cardinality of dependency set |
| 9 | Cohesion metric (LCOM — Lack of Cohesion) | $\text{LCOM} = \frac{\vert P \vert - \vert Q \vert}{m}$ | $P$ = method pairs not sharing fields; $Q$ = pairs sharing fields; $m$ = method count |
| 10 | Interface completeness ratio | $\text{Completeness} = \frac{\text{Operations spec'd}}{\text{Operations required by use-cases}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER** use the literal pipe `|` symbol inside a markdown table cell (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value). It breaks the table parser. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX form.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Interface Specification |
|--------|----------------------------------------|
| **Banking Software** | `Account.debit(amount)` — pre: `amount > 0`; post: `balance = old(balance) - amount`; invariant: `balance >= 0` |
| **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) interfaces — drivers expose a fixed C/Java API so apps survive hardware changes |
| **Microservices / REST** | OpenAPI/Swagger specifications are literally *interface specifications* over HTTP |
| **Compiler Design** | AST node interfaces define how visitors traverse the tree |
| **Robotics** | ROS (Robot Operating System) uses *message* and *service* interfaces for inter-node communication |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Every ECU (Electronic Control Unit) is described by port interfaces — a perfect industry analogue |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process for Interface Specification

## 3.1 The "Spec-to-Signature" Derivation — Worked Algorithm

The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks: *"Given a use-case realization, derive the interface specification."* The derivation proceeds in **five rigorous steps**. Each step is shown with the exact transformation logic.

### 🎯 Step 1 — Extract Verbs from Use-Case Realization

**Logic:** Scan the *flow of events* in the use case. Each **imperative verb** typically maps to an operation on the *controlling* class.

**Example input use-case (Library System, KTU-style):**
> *"A member requests a book. The system checks availability, reserves the book, and confirms the reservation to the member."*

**Extracted verbs (operations):**
$$
\text{Verbs} = \{ \text{request},\ \text{checkAvailability},\ \text{reserve},\ \text{confirm} \}
$$

### 🎯 Step 2 — Map Each Verb to a Controller Class

**Logic:** Use the *controller* (from GRASP) that owns the workflow. For a Library System:
- `request` → `LibraryController.requestBook(...)`
- `checkAvailability` → `BookCatalog.isAvailable(...)`
- `reserve` → `ReservationService.makeReservation(...)`
- `confirm` → `NotificationService.sendConfirmation(...)`

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Verbs} &\xrightarrow{\text{GRASP: Controller}} \text{ControllerClass}(verb) \\
\text{request} &\rightarrow \text{LibraryController} \\
\text{checkAvailability} &\rightarrow \text{BookCatalog} \\
\text{reserve} &\rightarrow \text{ReservationService} \\
\text{confirm} &\rightarrow \text{NotificationService}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🎯 Step 3 — Build the Syntactic Signature

**Logic:** For each verb, attach the **nouns** as parameters and the **result** as the return type.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{request} &\rightarrow \text{requestBook}(memberId : \text{String},\ bookISBN : \text{String}) : \text{Reservation} \\
\text{checkAvailability} &\rightarrow \text{isAvailable}(bookISBN : \text{String}) : \text{boolean} \\
\text{reserve} &\rightarrow \text{makeReservation}(memberId : \text{String},\ bookISBN : \text{String}) : \text{Reservation} \\
\text{confirm} &\rightarrow \text{sendConfirmation}(reservation : \text{Reservation}) : \text{void}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Each noun-phrase in the use-case sentence becomes a parameter; the output noun-phrase becomes the return type; if there is none, the return type is `void`.

### 🎯 Step 4 — Add the Semantic Contract (Pre/Post/Inv)

**Logic:** For each operation, ask three questions:
1. *What must be true before the call?* → **Pre**
2. *What must be true after the call?* → **Post**
3. *What must always be true?* → **Inv**

**Derivation of `makeReservation` contract:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{makeReservation}) &: \text{memberId} \neq \text{null} \;\land\; \text{bookISBN} \neq \text{null} \;\land\; \text{isAvailable}(\text{bookISBN}) \\
\text{Post}(\text{makeReservation}) &: \text{result} \neq \text{null} \;\land\; \text{result.memberId} = \text{memberId} \;\land\; \text{result.bookISBN} = \text{bookISBN} \;\land\; \lnot \text{isAvailable}(\text{bookISBN}) \\
\text{Inv}(\text{ReservationService}) &: \forall\ r \in \text{reservations} : r.\text{id} \neq \text{null} \;\land\; r.\text{expiry} > \text{now}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Keyword `old()`:** In postconditions, `old(state)` refers to the value of `state` *before* the operation ran. This is KTU-graded as a 1-marker in Part A frequently.

### 🎯 Step 5 — Refine for Quality (Minimal, Cohesive, Loosely Coupled)

**Logic:** Apply the four refinement heuristics from §2.1.

**Refinement Walkthrough:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial interfaces} &= 4 \text{ candidate operations across 4 classes} \\
\text{Apply Generalization} &\rightarrow \text{Common parent interface } I\text{Notifier} \text{ for NotificationService} \\
\text{Apply Decomposition} &\rightarrow \text{Split } \text{requestBook} \text{ into } \text{validate} \rightarrow \text{reserve} \rightarrow \text{confirm} \\
\text{Apply Consolidation} &\rightarrow \text{Merge } \text{checkAvailability} \text{ into } \text{makeReservation} \text{ as a guard} \\
\text{Final interface count} &= 3 \text{ classes},\ 4 \text{ operations (minimal, cohesive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 The "Contract Verification" Derivation

Given a contract $\langle \text{Pre},\ \text{Post},\ \text{Inv} \rangle$ and an implementation $I$, **correctness** is established via:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specification is sound} \;\Leftrightarrow\; &\forall\ s \in \text{States}.\ \big( \text{Inv}(s) \;\land\; \text{Pre}(op)(s) \big) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Inv}(op(s)) \;\land\; \text{Post}(op)(s, op(s)) \\
\text{Specification is complete} \;\Leftrightarrow\; &\forall\ s \in \text{States}.\ \text{Inv}(s) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Pre}(op)(s) \lor \text{well-defined-outcome}(s) \\
\text{Subtype is substitutable} \;\Leftrightarrow\; &\forall\ op \in \text{Super}.\ \text{Pre}_{\text{Super}}(op) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Pre}_{\text{Sub}}(op)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Row 1:** "If the invariant holds and the precondition holds before the call, then the invariant AND the postcondition must hold after the call." This is the **standard soundness condition** of Hoare-style design-by-contract logic.
> **Row 3:** This is precisely **Liskov's Substitution Principle (LSP)** cast in predicate logic — a favourite KTU exam point.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Banking Account

**Problem:** Specify the interface of an `Account` class supporting `debit`, `credit`, and `getBalance`.

**Step A — Signatures:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{debit}(amount : \text{double}) &: \text{boolean} \\
\text{credit}(amount : \text{double}) &: \text{boolean} \\
\text{getBalance}() &: \text{double}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Contracts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{debit}) &: \text{amount} > 0 \;\land\; \text{this.balance} \geq \text{amount} \\
\text{Post}(\text{debit}) &: \text{this.balance} = \text{old}(\text{this.balance}) - \text{amount} \;\land\; \text{result} = \text{true} \\
\text{Pre}(\text{credit}) &: \text{amount} > 0 \\
\text{Post}(\text{credit}) &: \text{this.balance} = \text{old}(\text{this.balance}) + \text{amount} \;\land\; \text{result} = \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{getBalance}) &: \text{result} = \text{this.balance} \\
\text{Inv}(\text{Account}) &: \text{this.balance} \geq 0 \;\land\; \text{this.accountNo} \neq \text{null}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Verification of Soundness for `debit`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assume Inv holds pre-call: } &\text{balance} \geq 0,\ \text{accountNo} \neq \text{null} \\
\text{Pre(debit) holds: } &\text{amount} > 0,\ \text{balance} \geq \text{amount} \\
\text{After execution: } &\text{balance}' = \text{balance} - \text{amount} \\
\text{Check Inv': } &\text{balance}' = \text{balance} - \text{amount} \geq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Check Post': } &\text{balance}' = \text{old(balance)} - \text{amount},\ \text{result} = \text{true} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Contract is sound.** This is the kind of full derivation KTU examiners reward with 14 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activity Flow of Interface Specification (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Use-Case Realization Available] --> B[Activity 1: Interface Identification]
    B --> B1[Extract Verbs from Flow of Events]
    B1 --> B2[Map Verbs to Controller Classes]
    B2 --> B3[Identify Provider and Requirer Interfaces]
    B3 --> C[Activity 2: Interface Description]
    C --> C1[Syntactic Specification: Signatures and Types]
    C1 --> C2[Semantic Specification: Pre/Post/Inv]
    C2 --> C3[Constraint Specification: Exceptions and Side Effects]
    C3 --> D[Activity 3: Interface Refinement]
    D --> D1[Generalize: Extract Common Interfaces]
    D1 --> D2[Specialize: Add Domain Specifics]
    D2 --> D3[Consolidate: Remove Redundancies]
    D3 --> E[Activity 4: Interface Verification]
    E --> E1{Check: Complete, Consistent, Minimal?}
    E1 -->|No| F[Iterate back to Activity 1 or 2]
    F --> B
    E1 -->|Yes| G[Final Interface Specification Document]
    G --> H[End: Ready for Implementation]
```

## 4.2 UML Class Diagram — Account Example (Interface Specification View)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class IAccount {
        <<interface>>
        +debit(amount: double) boolean
        +credit(amount: double) boolean
        +getBalance() double
    }
    class SavingsAccount {
        -balance: double
        -accountNo: String
        -interestRate: double
        +debit(amount: double) boolean
        +credit(amount: double) boolean
        +getBalance() double
        +computeInterest() double
    }
    class CurrentAccount {
        -balance: double
        -accountNo: String
        -overdraftLimit: double
        +debit(amount: double) boolean
        +credit(amount: double) boolean
        +getBalance() double
    }
    class InvariantCheck {
        <<constraint>>
        balance >= 0
        accountNo != null
    }
    IAccount <|.. SavingsAccount : implements
    IAccount <|.. CurrentAccount : implements
    SavingsAccount ..> InvariantCheck : must satisfy
    CurrentAccount ..> InvariantCheck : must satisfy
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** The `<<interface>>` stereotype and the dashed realisation arrow (`<|..`) are the *only* correct UML 2.5 notations. Examiners deduct marks for using dashed *inheritance* arrows between interface and class.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Contract Enforcement at Runtime

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant InvariantChecker
    participant Server as Server Object
    
    Client->>Server: op(args)
    activate Server
    Server->>InvariantChecker: Check Inv(C)
    InvariantChecker-->>Server: Inv(C) holds?
    alt Pre violated
        Server-->>Client: Throw IllegalArgumentException
    else Pre holds and Inv holds
        Server->>Server: Execute op body
        Server->>InvariantChecker: Check Inv(C) post-call
        alt Post violated
            Server-->>Client: AssertionError / UndefinedBehaviour
        else Post holds
            Server-->>Client: return result
        end
    end
    deactivate Server
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Interface Specification in a Layered System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PresentationLayer [Presentation Layer]
        UI[User Interface Module]
    end
    subgraph ApplicationLayer [Application Layer]
        APP1[Controller A]
        APP2[Controller B]
    end
    subgraph DomainLayer [Domain Layer]
        IFACE[Interface Specification IAccount]
        IMPL1[SavingsAccount]
        IMPL2[CurrentAccount]
    end
    subgraph InfrastructureLayer [Infrastructure Layer]
        DB[(Database)]
        LOG[Logger]
    end
    UI -->|invokes| APP1
    UI -->|invokes| APP2
    APP1 -->|depends on contract| IFACE
    APP2 -->|depends on contract| IFACE
    IFACE -.->|realised by| IMPL1
    IFACE -.->|realised by| IMPL2
    IMPL1 -->|persists| DB
    IMPL2 -->|persists| DB
    IMPL1 -->|logs| LOG
    IMPL2 -->|logs| LOG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **dependency direction** — *Application* depends only on the **interface** (`IFACE`), not on the concrete classes (`IMPL1`, `IMPL2`). This is the **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** in action, and is the architectural payoff of doing interface specification rigorously.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define *Interface Specification*. List any two activities involved in interface specification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> Interface specification is a design activity that describes the external view of an object or subsystem — namely, the operations it exposes, their signatures (syntax), and the conditions under which they are invoked (semantics), without revealing the internal implementation.

**Two Activities (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Interface Identification:** Discovering candidate operations by analysing use-cases, CRC cards, and class responsibilities.
2. **Interface Description:** Formally specifying each operation with syntactic signature (name, parameters, return type) and semantic contract (preconditions, postconditions, invariants).

> Other valid activities: *Interface Refinement, Interface Verification.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between *precondition* and *postcondition* in the context of interface contracts.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Precondition | Postcondition |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| **When checked** | Before the operation executes | After the operation returns |
| **Who is responsible** | The **caller** (client) | The **callee** (server) |
| **What it guarantees** | Inputs are valid | Output and state changes are correct |
| **Example for `debit(amount)`** | `amount > 0 ∧ balance ≥ amount` | `balance = old(balance) − amount` |

(Any accurate comparison covering the above four dimensions fetches full 3 marks.)

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *Internal Choice Pattern*

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B.

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, OOAD]**

**(a)** Explain the **four major activities** involved in interface specification during object-oriented design. Illustrate with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Design by Contract**. For a `Stack` class with operations `push(int x)` and `pop()`, write the **complete contract** (precondition, postcondition, invariant) for each operation. **(7 Marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution

#### **Part (a) — Four Activities (7 Marks)**

| # | Activity | Purpose | KTU Marking Hint |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Interface Identification** | Discover operations from use-cases, CRC, and class responsibilities | 2 marks |
| 2 | **Interface Description** | Specify syntax (signature) and semantics (contract) | 2 marks |
| 3 | **Interface Refinement** | Generalize / specialize / consolidate operations across iterations | 1.5 marks |
| 4 | **Interface Verification** | Check completeness, consistency, minimality, and substitutability | 1.5 marks |

**Example — Library Management System (illustrative, 1.5 marks within the 7):**
- **Identification:** From the use-case *"Member reserves a book"*, the verbs `checkAvailability`, `reserve`, and `confirm` are extracted.
- **Description:** Each is given a signature, e.g., `reserve(memberId, ISBN): Reservation`, and a contract.
- **Refinement:** A common `INotifier` interface is extracted to support both email and SMS confirmation.
- **Verification:** Checking that no operation is unused (minimality) and that subtypes of `INotifier` are substitutable (LSP).

#### **Part (b) — Stack Contract (7 Marks)**

**Design by Contract Definition (2 Marks):**
> Design by Contract (DbC) is a software correctness methodology in which software designers define formal, precise, and verifiable interface specifications using *preconditions*, *postconditions*, and *class invariants* — establishing mutual obligations and benefits between client and server.

**Contracts (5 Marks — 1 mark per element + 2 marks for invariants/pop):**

For the class `Stack` with internal state `elements: Array`, `top: int`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inv}(\text{Stack}) &: \text{top} \geq -1 \;\land\; \text{top} < \text{elements.length} \;\land\; \text{elements} \neq \text{null} \\
\text{Pre}(\text{push}(x)) &: x \neq \text{null} \;\land\; \text{top} < \text{elements.length} - 1 \quad \text{(no overflow)} \\
\text{Post}(\text{push}(x)) &: \text{top} = \text{old}(\text{top}) + 1 \;\land\; \text{elements}[\text{old}(\text{top}) + 1] = x \\
\text{Pre}(\text{pop}()) &: \text{top} \geq 0 \quad \text{(stack non-empty)} \\
\text{Post}(\text{pop}()) &: \text{result} = \text{elements}[\text{top}] \;\land\; \text{top} = \text{old}(\text{top}) - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, OOAD]**

**(a)** What is an **interface contract**? Explain the role of **preconditions, postconditions, and class invariants** in ensuring robust object-oriented design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider an `Order` class in an e-commerce system with methods `placeOrder()`, `cancelOrder()`, and `getStatus()`. Specify the **complete interface** (signatures + contracts) for this class and **verify soundness** of the `cancelOrder()` postcondition against the class invariant. **(7 Marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution

#### **Part (a) — Interface Contract & Three Elements (7 Marks)**

**Interface Contract Definition (2 Marks):**
> An interface contract is a formal specification that precisely describes the responsibilities of an object and the expectations from its clients. It binds the supplier (server) and consumer (client) into a mutual agreement.

**Three Elements (5 Marks — roughly 1.5 + 1.5 + 2):**

1. **Precondition (1.5 Marks):** A predicate that must be true *before* the operation is invoked. It is the **caller's responsibility**. If violated, the server is not obliged to behave correctly and may throw an exception.

2. **Postcondition (1.5 Marks):** A predicate that must be true *after* the operation completes successfully. It is the **server's guarantee** regarding the return value and the resulting object state (using `old()` notation for pre-call state references).

3. **Class Invariant (2 Marks):** A predicate that must hold **before and after every public operation**. It defines the structural and semantic consistency of an object's state throughout its lifetime — bridging preconditions and postconditions.

> **Role in Robustness:** Together, these three elements enable **fault detection at the boundary**, **clear debugging**, **safe substitutability** (LSP), and **self-checking code** — drastically reducing the need for defensive programming.

#### **Part (b) — Order Interface Specification (7 Marks)**

**Syntactic Specification (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{placeOrder}() &: \text{OrderConfirmation} \\
\text{cancelOrder}() &: \text{boolean} \\
\text{getStatus}() &: \text{OrderStatus}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Semantic Specification (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inv}(\text{Order}) &: \text{this.orderId} \neq \text{null} \;\land\; \text{this.totalAmount} \geq 0 \;\land\; \text{this.items} \neq \text{null} \\
\text{Pre}(\text{placeOrder}) &: \text{this.items.size}() > 0 \;\land\; \text{this.status} = \text{PENDING} \\
\text{Post}(\text{placeOrder}) &: \text{this.status} = \text{PLACED} \;\land\; \text{result.orderId} = \text{this.orderId} \\
\text{Pre}(\text{cancelOrder}) &: \text{this.status} = \text{PLACED} \;\lor\; \text{this.status} = \text{PENDING} \\
\text{Post}(\text{cancelOrder}) &: \text{this.status} = \text{CANCELLED} \;\land\; \text{result} = \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{getStatus}) &: \text{result} = \text{this.status}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Soundness Verification of `cancelOrder()` (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given Inv pre-call: } & \text{orderId} \neq \text{null},\ \text{totalAmount} \geq 0,\ \text{items} \neq \text{null} \\
\text{Pre holds: } & \text{status} \in \{\text{PLACED},\ \text{PENDING}\} \\
\text{After execution: } & \text{status}' = \text{CANCELLED} \\
\text{Verify Inv': } & \text{orderId} \neq \text{null} \;\checkmark\ \text{(unchanged)} \\
                    & \text{totalAmount} \geq 0 \;\checkmark\ \text{(unchanged)} \\
                    & \text{items} \neq \text{null} \;\checkmark\ \text{(unchanged)} \\
\text{Verify Post': } & \text{status}' = \text{CANCELLED},\ \text{result} = \text{true} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Contract is sound.** All invariants preserved, postcondition holds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps
> 1. **Forgetting `old()` notation:** Writing `balance = balance - amount` in a postcondition (instead of `balance = old(balance) - amount`) loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up responsibilities:** Saying "the server checks the precondition" — WRONG. The **client** owns the precondition.
> 3. **Skipping the invariant:** Many students write Pre/Post but forget `Inv(C)`. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Using `extends` arrow in UML between interface and class:** Should be a **dashed realisation arrow** (`<|..` in Mermaid, hollow triangle in UML). Examiners are strict here.
> 5. **Confusing *interface specification* with Java's `interface` keyword:** Interface specification is a *design-time* activity. Java `interface` is a *language construct* that *implements* the design. Examiners deduct marks for conflating the two.
> 6. **Not writing the contract in predicate form:** Writing *"the balance should not go below zero"* in prose is worth 0.5 marks; writing `balance ≥ 0` formally is worth 1.5 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Pin This List**

- ⭐ **Interface Specification** = describing the *external* view of an object (what, not how).
- ⭐ Four activities: **Identification → Description → Refinement → Verification**.
- ⭐ Contract triad: **Pre (caller's duty) | Post (server's duty) | Inv (server's promise).**
- ⭐ Always use **`old(state)`** in postconditions when referencing pre-call state.
- ⭐ **LSP** in predicate form: $\text{Pre}_{\text{Super}} \Rightarrow \text{Pre}_{\text{Sub}}$ — memorize this for 14-mark questions.
- ⭐ **Provider interface** = what a class offers. **Requirer interface** = what a class needs.
- ⭐ **Refinement heuristics:** Generalize, Specialize, Consolidate, Decompose.
- ⭐ **Quality attributes of a good interface:** Complete, Consistent, Minimal, Cohesive, Loosely Coupled.
- ⭐ **UML notation:** `<<interface>>` stereotype + dashed realisation arrow (`<|..`). Solid line with hollow triangle is **inheritance** — different.
- ⭐ **Industry analogues:** Java `interface`, CORBA IDL, REST OpenAPI, ROS messages, AUTOSAR ports.
- ⭐ **Hook to next modules:** Interface specifications are inputs to **detailed design** (Module 5) and **unit testing** (via mocking).
- ⭐ **Mnemonic for contract checking order:** *"**P**re — **P**ost — **I**nv"* = **PPI** (Pre, Post, Invariant).
- ⭐ **High-yield keywords to drop in 14-mark answers:** *information hiding*, *substitutability*, *polymorphism*, *dependency inversion*, *soundness*, *completeness*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Object Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Managing Object Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Managing Object Design** is the systematic, iterative process of organizing, refining, and maintaining object-oriented software components throughout the software development lifecycle. It encompasses the strategic decisions made *after* initial object identification (analysis phase) but *before/during* implementation, focusing on how objects interact, how responsibilities are distributed, and how the design evolves to satisfy non-functional requirements such as maintainability, reusability, and performance.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT411)** perspective, Managing Object Design covers:

> The post-analysis activities that govern the structural quality of an OO system, specifically dealing with **composition vs. inheritance trade-offs**, **coupling and cohesion metrics**, the **Law of Demeter**, **Design by Contract (DbC)**, **refactoring techniques**, and the strategic reuse of **design patterns and frameworks**.

| Aspect | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Phase Position** | Bridges Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA) and Implementation (Coding) |
| **Primary Goal** | Transform conceptual objects into a high-quality, flexible, and reusable design |
| **Key Concerns** | Reusability, Extensibility, Robustness, Performance, Maintainability |
| **Owner Role** | Software Architect / Senior Developer |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍽️

Think of **Managing Object Design** like being a **Head Chef designing a restaurant kitchen**:

- **Objects = Kitchen Stations** (Grill Station, Pastry Station, Salad Station)
- **Responsibilities = What each station cooks**
- **Inheritance** is like saying "The Sushi Station *is a kind of* Cold Station" — they share some features (refrigeration) but forcing everything into a family tree gets messy.
- **Composition** is like saying "The Sushi Station *has a* Refrigerator" — it simply *uses* the refrigeration component without claiming a parent-child relationship. This is usually cleaner and more flexible.
- **Coupling** is how strongly the Pastry Station depends on the Oven Station. If changing the oven breaks the pastry station, they are *tightly coupled* — bad!
- **Cohesion** is how focused each station is. A "Does-Everything Station" is a *low-cohesion* disaster.
- **Refactoring** is rearranging the kitchen layout every few months to improve workflow *without changing the menu*.
- **Design by Contract** is the chef's agreement: "If you give me fresh dough (precondition), I will hand you a baked croissant within 10 minutes (postcondition), and my oven will always stay between 180–220 °C (invariant)."

## 1.3 Key Terminology Quick-Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these five terms — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this module:**

1. **Coupling** — The degree of interdependence between software modules.
2. **Cohesion** — The degree to which elements of a module belong together.
3. **Composition** — A "has-a" relationship where the child *cannot exist* without the parent (strong ownership).
4. **Aggregation** — A "has-a" relationship where the child *can exist* independently (weak ownership).
5. **Design by Contract (DbC)** — A methodology where software components define precise mutual obligations (Bertrand Meyer, Eiffel language).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically highlights *Craig Larman*'s GRASP principles (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns) as the conceptual backbone of this module. While Larman is not formally named in many official PDFs, his terminology ("Information Expert", "Creator", "Controller") dominates KTU question papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Managing Object Design

### 🧱 Pillar 1: Composition vs. Inheritance (The "Is-a" vs. "Has-a" Decision)

| Decision Criterion | Prefer **Inheritance** | Prefer **Composition** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Relationship nature | True taxonomic "is-a" | Functional "has-a" or "uses-a" |
| Subclass is a *specialization* of parent | ✔️ | ❌ |
| Need to reuse *behavior* dynamically at runtime | ❌ | ✔️ (via Strategy/Decorator patterns) |
| Parent class is **stable** and not likely to change | ✔️ | Always safer |
| Multiple "parent" behaviors needed (e.g., Flyable + Swimmable) | ❌ (Java no multiple class inheritance) | ✔️ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Golden Rule (frequently tested):** *"Favor composition over inheritance"* — because inheritance breaks encapsulation (subclass depends on parent internals), while composition delegates cleanly to encapsulated collaborators.

**Java Code Mental Model:**

```java
// INHERITANCE (Is-a)
class SportsCar extends Car { }   // SportsCar IS-A Car

// COMPOSITION (Has-a)
class Car {
    private Engine engine;        // Car HAS-A Engine
    public Car(Engine e) { this.engine = e; }
}
```

### 🧱 Pillar 2: Coupling and Cohesion

**Coupling** measures *inter-module dependency*. **Cohesion** measures *intra-module focus*.

| Coupling Type (Bad → Good) | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Content Coupling** (worst) | One module directly modifies another's internal data |
| **Common Coupling** | Modules share global variables |
| **Control Coupling** | One module passes a control flag to another |
| **Stamp Coupling** | Passes a whole data structure when only a field is needed |
| **Data Coupling** (best) | Modules share data via parameters only |

| Cohesion Type (Bad → Good) | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Coincidental** (worst) | Arbitrary grouping of unrelated functions |
| **Logical** | Grouped by category of operation |
| **Temporal** | Executed at the same time |
| **Procedural** | Must be executed in a specific order |
| **Communicational** | Operate on the same data |
| **Sequential** | Output of one is input to another |
| **Functional** (best) | All elements contribute to a single, well-defined task |

### 🧱 Pillar 3: Law of Demeter (Principle of Least Knowledge)

Formal statement: *A method `M` of object `O` may only invoke the methods of the following kinds of objects:*
1. `O` itself
2. `M`'s parameters
3. Any object created/instantiated within `M`
4. `O`'s direct component objects (instance variables)
5. Any global object

**Violation example:**

```java
// BAD — "train wreck" (a.b().c().d())
customer.getWallet().getMoney().subtract(amount);

// GOOD — Law of Demeter compliant
customer.pay(amount);
```

### 🧱 Pillar 4: Design by Contract (DbC)

Introduced by **Bertrand Meyer** for the Eiffel language. A contract has three parts:

| Contract Term | Definition | Violation Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Precondition** | What must be true *before* a method is called | Caller's fault |
| **Postcondition** | What must be true *after* a method returns | Callee's fault |
| **Invariant** | What must always be true about an object (except during internal transitions) | Object's fault |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Java, DbC is not natively supported, but is implemented via:
> - `assert` keyword (preconditions/postconditions)
> - `IllegalArgumentException`, `IllegalStateException`
> - Libraries like **Java's `java.util.Objects.requireNonNull`**
> - Tools: **iContract, Cofoja, JUnit assumptions**

### 🧱 Pillar 5: Refactoring

**Definition (Martin Fowler, 1999):** *A disciplined technique for restructuring an existing body of code, altering its internal structure without changing its external behavior.*

Common Refactorings (High-yield for KTU):
1. **Extract Method** — pull a code fragment into a new method
2. **Move Method** — relocate a method to a more appropriate class
3. **Replace Inheritance with Composition** — when is-a is no longer valid
4. **Rename Method/Variable** — improve clarity
5. **Replace Conditional with Polymorphism** — eliminate `switch`/`if-else` chains

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Managing Object Design is largely qualitative, but these quantitative "rules of thumb" appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions.

| Metric / Concept | Formula or Rule | Target Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Coupling Index (C)** | $C = \dfrac{\text{Number of inter-module connections}}{\text{Maximum possible connections}}$ | $C \to 0$ (lower is better) |
| **Cohesion Strength (qualitative)** | Functional $\gg$ Sequential $\gg$ Communicational $\gg$ Procedural $\gg$ Temporal $\gg$ Logical $\gg$ Coincidental | Aim for **Functional** |
| **Depth of Inheritance Tree (DIT)** | Longest path from class to root | $\text{DIT} \leq 5$ (good design) |
| **Number of Children (NOC)** | Count of immediate subclasses | Lower = less impact of parent change |
| **Response Set (RS)** | $\text{RS} = \text{Methods called} + \text{Methods called by them}$ | $\text{RS}$ should be small |
| **Lack of Cohesion (LCOM)** | $\text{LCOM} = P - Q$ where $P$ = pairs not sharing instance vars, $Q$ = pairs sharing | $\text{LCOM} \to 0$ |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (M)** | $M = E - N + 2P$ (E=edges, N=nodes, P=connected components) | $M \leq 10$ per method |
| **Law of Demeter Object Calls** | $\text{Max dot-chains} = 1$ (e.g., `obj.getX()` only) | $\text{Chains} = 1$ |
| **DbC Precondition Check** | Use `assert` or `Objects.requireNonNull` | Must be checked by **caller** |
| **Design Pattern Selection Heuristic** | See Section 2.3 | — |

## 2.3 Design Pattern Quick-Selection (KTU often asks: "Which pattern fits this scenario?")

| Problem | Recommended Pattern | Family |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Create object families without specifying concrete classes | **Abstract Factory** | Creational |
| Add responsibilities to objects dynamically | **Decorator** | Structural |
| Notify multiple objects of state change | **Observer** | Behavioral |
| Encapsulate a request as an object | **Command** | Behavioral |
| Provide a unified interface to incompatible interfaces | **Adapter** | Structural |
| Ensure a class has only one instance | **Singleton** | Creational |
| Simplify complex subsystem interface | **Facade** | Structural |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Software (AUTOSAR):** Composition is heavily favored for ECU (Electronic Control Unit) modules so a steering bug doesn't cascade into braking.
- **Banking Systems:** Design by Contract prevents illegal states (negative balances), enforced via `assert` and database CHECK constraints.
- **Android SDK:** Favors composition (`Activity` has-a `ViewModel`) over inheritance, avoiding deep class trees.
- **Spacecraft Control (NASA JPL):** Uses DbC rigorously — every method has documented pre/post-conditions; invariant violations trigger safe-mode shutdown.
