ORM is the **cornerstone of enterprise Java development**. It bridges the **object-oriented world** (classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism) and the **relational world** (tables, rows, columns, foreign keys).

### 2.1.1 The Impedance Mismatch Problem

There exists a fundamental conflict between how data is represented in OOP versus RDBMS:

| Feature | Object-Oriented Model | Relational Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | Object Reference (memory address) | Primary Key (logical value) |
| **Structure** | Class with attributes | Table with columns |
| **Relationships** | References (pointers) | Foreign Keys |
| **Inheritance** | Native support (`extends`) | No direct support |
| **Navigation** | Bidirectional via references | Unidirectional via JOINs |
| **Encapsulation** | Private fields with getters/setters | Direct column access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ORM frameworks like **Hibernate** (most widely used in KTU curriculum) resolve this impedance mismatch by providing metadata-driven translations.

### 2.1.2 Core ORM Mapping Components

1. **Entity Mapping** — Class ↔ Table
2. **Identifier Mapping** — Primary Key field ↔ Primary Key column
3. **Property Mapping** — Field ↔ Column
4. **Association Mapping** — Reference/Collection ↔ Foreign Key
5. **Inheritance Mapping** — Class Hierarchy ↔ Table Strategy

## 2.2 Association Mapping — The Most Tested Topic

Association mapping defines how relationships between classes are persisted. There are **four cardinalities** that KTU exams heavily test:

### 2.2.1 One-to-One (1:1) Mapping

- **Concept:** One instance of Class A is associated with exactly one instance of Class B.
- **Java Implementation:** Single object reference as a field.
- **Database Implementation:** Foreign Key in either table with a **UNIQUE** constraint.
- **Example:** `Person` ↔ `Passport` — one person has exactly one passport.

### 2.2.2 One-to-Many (1:M) Mapping

- **Concept:** One instance of Class A is associated with multiple instances of Class B.
- **Java Implementation:** Collection field (`List<B>`, `Set<B>`).
- **Database Implementation:** Foreign Key in the "many" side table.
- **Example:** `Department` → `Employee[]` — one department has many employees.

### 2.2.3 Many-to-One (M:1) Mapping

- **Concept:** Inverse perspective of 1:M — many instances of Class A point to one instance of Class B.
- **Java Implementation:** Single object reference.
- **Database Implementation:** Foreign Key in the "many" side table.
- **Example:** Many `Employee` objects → One `Department`.

### 2.2.4 Many-to-Many (M:M) Mapping

- **Concept:** Multiple instances of A relate to multiple instances of B.
- **Java Implementation:** Collections on both sides.
- **Database Implementation:** Requires a **join table** (associative entity) with two foreign keys.
- **Example:** `Student` ↔ `Course` — a student takes many courses, and a course has many students.

## 2.3 Inheritance Mapping Strategies (Hibernate)

When mapping a class hierarchy to relational tables, three strategies are used:

| Strategy | Table Count | NULL Columns | Performance | Polymorphic Queries |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SINGLE_TABLE** | 1 (for entire hierarchy) | Many | ★★★★★ Fast | ★★★★ Easy |
| **JOINED** | 1 per concrete class | None | ★★★ Moderate | ★★★ Moderate |
| **TABLE_PER_CLASS** | 1 per class (concrete) | None | ★★ Slow | ★★ Complex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the SINGLE_TABLE inheritance strategy with an example."* — Be prepared with the discriminator column concept.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Definition | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mapping Cardinality | $C(A \to B)$ | Number of B objects related to one A object | ★★★★★ |
| One-to-One | $C = 1, 1$ | $\vert A \vert = 1, \vert B \vert = 1$ | ★★★★★ |
| One-to-Many | $C = 1, N$ | $\vert A \vert = 1, \vert B \vert = N$ | ★★★★★ |
| Many-to-One | $C = N, 1$ | $\vert A \vert = N, \vert B \vert = 1$ | ★★★★ |
| Many-to-Many | $C = N, M$ | $\vert A \vert = N, \vert B \vert = M$ | ★★★★★ |
| HashMap Load Factor | $\alpha = n / k$ | $n$ = entries, $k$ = buckets | ★★★ |
| HashMap Complexity | $O(1)$ avg | $O(n)$ worst-case | ★★★★ |
| Hash Function | $h(k) = k \bmod m$ | $m$ = table size (prime) | ★★★ |
| TreeMap Complexity | $O(\log n)$ | Red-Black tree | ★★★ |
| Collision Resolution | Chaining / Open Addressing | Two main strategies | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** ORM mapping powers **Spring Boot, Hibernate, JPA, EJB, and Jakarta EE** applications. Every enterprise Java application in production (banking, e-commerce, healthcare) uses these mapping concepts to persist billions of records efficiently.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **E-Commerce Systems** — Product ↔ Category (M:M), Order ↔ Customer (M:1), User ↔ Profile (1:1).
2. **Hospital Management** — Doctor ↔ Patient (M:M via Appointments), Department ↔ Doctor (1:M).
3. **Social Networks** — User ↔ Friend (M:M self-referential), User ↔ Post (1:M).
4. **University Management** — Student ↔ Course (M:M), Department ↔ Professor (1:M).
5. **Banking Systems** — Account ↔ Customer (M:1), Account ↔ Transaction (1:M).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HashMap Index Calculation

The `HashMap` in Java uses a hash function to determine the bucket index for storing key-value pairs. Let us derive the exact formula.

### Step 1: The Raw Hash Code

Every Java object inherits the `hashCode()` method from `Object`. For a generic key $k$, the raw hash is:

$$
h_{raw}(k) = \text{key.hashCode()}
$$

### Step 2: Hash Spreading (XOR with Shifted Self)

Java applies a secondary hash-spreading function to prevent poor distribution in low-quality hash codes:

$$
h_{spread}(k) = h_{raw}(k) \oplus \left( h_{raw}(k) \ggg 16 \right)
$$

This right-shifts the raw hash by **16 bits** and XORs it with itself. The bitwise right shift operator $\ggg$ is logical (zero-fill) shift.

### Step 3: Bucket Index Calculation

The final bucket index is computed by taking modulo with the table length $n$, which is always a power of 2:

$$
\text{index}(k) = h_{spread}(k) \;\&\; (n - 1)
$$

The bitwise AND operator $\&\;$ is used because $n$ is a power of 2, making this operation equivalent to a fast modulo.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

Let us compute the index for a key with `hashCode() = 123456` in a HashMap with default capacity **16**.

**Step 4a:** Compute $h_{raw}$:

$$
h_{raw} = 123456
$$

**Step 4b:** Convert 123456 to binary (32-bit):
$$
123456 = 0000\,0001\,1110\,0010\,0100\,0000_2
$$

**Step 4c:** Right shift by 16 bits:
$$
123456 \ggg 16 = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001_2 = 1
$$

**Step 4d:** XOR operation:

$$
h_{spread} = 123456 \oplus 1 = 123457
$$

**Step 4e:** Compute $n - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15$:

$$
n - 1 = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,1111_2 = 15
$$

**Step 4f:** Bitwise AND:

$$
123457 \;\&\; 15
$$

$$
123457 = 0001\,1110\,0010\,0100\,0001_2
$$

$$
15 = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,1111_2
$$

$$
\text{Result} = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001_2 = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** The key-value pair is stored in **bucket index 1** of the HashMap.

### Step 5: Load Factor and Rehashing

The load factor $\alpha$ determines when the HashMap doubles in size:

$$
\alpha = \frac{n_{entries}}{n_{capacity}}
$$

The default load factor in Java's `HashMap` is $\alpha = 0.75$. When $\alpha > 0.75$, the table is rehashed with capacity doubled. This maintains the $O(1)$ average-case complexity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Many-to-Many Join Table Schema

Consider a Many-to-Many relationship between `Student` and `Course`. Neither entity table can hold foreign keys directly without violating **First Normal Form (1NF)** because it would create multi-valued dependencies.

### Step 1: Define the Entity Tables

**Student table** $S$:

$$
S(\underline{sid}, sname, semail)
$$

**Course table** $C$:

$$
C(\underline{cid}, cname, credits)
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Relationship Cardinality

Each student can enroll in $N$ courses, and each course can have $M$ students:

$$
C(S \to C) = N, \quad C(C \to S) = M
$$

### Step 3: Create the Associative Entity Table

To resolve the M:M relationship, we introduce a **join table** $SC$ (StudentCourse):

$$
SC(\underline{sid}, \underline{cid}, \text{enrollment\_date}, \text{grade})
$$

The composite primary key is $(\underline{sid}, \underline{cid})$ — both are foreign keys referencing the parent tables.

### Step 4: Define Foreign Key Constraints

The foreign key relationships are formally:

$$
SC.sid \to S.sid \quad \text{(FK constraint)}
$$

$$
SC.cid \to C.cid \quad \text{(FK constraint)}
$$

### Step 5: Cardinality Verification

For a student with $sid = 101$ enrolled in 3 courses, there will be **3 rows** in $SC$ with $sid = 101$ and varying $cid$ values. Conversely, for a course with $cid = 202$ having 50 students, there will be **50 rows** in $SC$ with $cid = 202$.

This proves the join table correctly represents the M:M relationship with **no redundancy** and **no data loss**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Mapping Strategies

| Parameter | Single Reference | Collection (List) | Collection (Set) | Join Table |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cardinality** | 1:1, M:1 | 1:M, M:M | 1:M, M:M (unique) | M:M |
| **Java Field** | `Object other` | `List<B> list` | `Set<B> set` | Two `Set<B>` fields |
| **DB Storage** | FK Column | FK Column on "many" side | FK Column on "many" side | Dedicated bridge table |
| **Uniqueness** | Single instance | Allows duplicates | No duplicates | Composite key enforces uniqueness |
| **Null Allowed** | Yes (optional) | Yes | Yes (empty set) | No (FK constraint) |
| **Lazy Loading** | Yes (proxy) | Yes (extra lazy) | Yes | Yes |

## 3.4 Complete Mapping Example — Hibernate JPA Annotations

Let us derive a complete bidirectional One-to-Many mapping between `Department` and `Employee`:

```java
// Department.java — The "One" side
@Entity
@Table(name = "department")
public class Department {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
    
    private String name;
    
    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "department", cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
    private List<Employee> employees = new ArrayList<>();
    
    // getters and setters
}

// Employee.java — The "Many" side
@Entity
@Table(name = "employee")
public class Employee {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
    
    private String name;
    
    @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    @JoinColumn(name = "dept_id")
    private Department department;
    
    // getters and setters
}
```

**Generated SQL Schema:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE department (
    id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name VARCHAR(255)
);

CREATE TABLE employee (
    id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    name VARCHAR(255),
    dept_id BIGINT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES department(id)
);
```

This demonstrates how the **logical object relationship** in Java (a `Department` holds a `List<Employee>`) is translated to a **physical database schema** (the `employee` table has a `dept_id` foreign key column).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ORM Mapping Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Application Layer] --> B[Service Layer with Transaction Manager]
    B --> C[JPA EntityManager Interface]
    C --> D[Hibernate ORM Engine]
    D --> E[Persistence Context Cache]
    D --> F[SQL Dialect Translator]
    F --> G[JDBC Driver]
    G --> H[Relational Database]
    
    H --> I[ResultSet Mapper]
    I --> D
    D --> J[Object Hydration]
    J --> K[Java Object Graph]
    K --> A
    
    L[Mapping Metadata XML or Annotations] --> D
    M[Hibernate Configuration hibernate.cfg.xml] --> D
    N[Connection Pool HikariCP or C3P0] --> G
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Association Mapping Cardinality Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ONE_TO_ONE["One to One Mapping"]
        P1[Person] ---|has exactly one| PA1[Passport]
    end
    
    subgraph ONE_TO_MANY["One to Many Mapping"]
        D1[Department] ---|has many| E1[Employee A]
        D1 ---|has many| E2[Employee B]
        D1 ---|has many| E3[Employee C]
    end
    
    subgraph MANY_TO_ONE["Many to One Mapping"]
        E4[Employee X] ---|belongs to| D2[Department]
        E5[Employee Y] ---|belongs to| D2
        E6[Employee Z] ---|belongs to| D2
    end
    
    subgraph MANY_TO_MANY["Many to Many Mapping"]
        S1[Student 1] ---|enrolled in| C1[Course A]
        S1 ---|enrolled in| C2[Course B]
        S2[Student 2] ---|enrolled in| C1
        S2 ---|enrolled in| C3[Course C]
    end
    
    style P1 fill:#ffcccc
    style PA1 fill:#ffcccc
    style D1 fill:#ccffcc
    style E1 fill:#ccffcc
    style E2 fill:#ccffcc
    style E3 fill:#ccffcc
    style D2 fill:#ccccff
    style E4 fill:#ccccff
    style E5 fill:#ccccff
    style E6 fill:#ccccff
    style S1 fill:#ffffcc
    style S2 fill:#ffffcc
    style C1 fill:#ffffcc
    style C2 fill:#ffffcc
    style C3 fill:#ffffcc
```

## 4.3 Inheritance Mapping Strategy Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Parent[Vehicle id name] --> Car[Car numDoors]
    Parent --> Bike[Bike hasCarrier]
    
    subgraph SINGLE["SINGLE_TABLE Strategy"]
        ST[Vehicle Table id name type numDoors hasCarrier]
    end
    
    subgraph JOINED["JOINED Strategy"]
        JT1[Vehicle Table id name]
        JT2[Car Table id numDoors FK]
        JT3[Bike Table id hasCarrier FK]
        JT1 -.->|JOIN| JT2
        JT1 -.->|JOIN| JT3
    end
    
    subgraph TPC["TABLE_PER_CLASS Strategy"]
        TC1[Car Table id name numDoors]
        TC2[Bike Table id name hasCarrier]
    end
    
    style Parent fill:#ffe1e1
    style Car fill:#ffe1e1
    style Bike fill:#ffe1e1
    style ST fill:#e1f0ff
    style JT1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style JT2 fill:#e1ffe1
    style JT3 fill:#e1ffe1
    style TC1 fill:#fff0e1
    style TC2 fill:#fff0e1
```

## 4.4 Java Map Collection Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Interface Map K V] --> B[Interface SortedMap K V]
    A --> C[Class HashMap K V]
    A --> D[Class Hashtable K V]
    A --> E[Class LinkedHashMap K V]
    B --> F[Class TreeMap K V]
    
    C -->|uses| G[Hashing]
    F -->|uses| H[Red Black Tree]
    E -->|uses| I[Hash Table plus Linked List]
    
    J[Interface Map Entry K V] -.->|inner| A
    
    style A fill:#ffccff,stroke:#990099,stroke-width:3px
    style C fill:#ccffcc
    style F fill:#ffcccc
    style E fill:#ccccff
    style D fill:#ffffcc
```

## 4.5 Mapping Workflow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Java Application
    participant EM as EntityManager
    participant HC as Hibernate Cache
    participant DB as Database
    
    App->>EM: persist departmentEntity
    EM->>HC: Check if entity is managed
    HC-->>EM: Not found, new entity
    EM->>HC: Add to persistence context
    App->>EM: commit transaction
    EM->>DB: INSERT INTO department VALUES
    DB-->>EM: Generated ID returned
    EM->>HC: Update entity with generated ID
    EM-->>App: Transaction committed
    
    App->>EM: find Department class id 1
    EM->>HC: Check L1 cache
    HC-->>EM: Cache miss
    EM->>DB: SELECT FROM department WHERE id equals 1
    DB-->>EM: Return row data
    EM->>HC: Cache the result
    EM-->>App: Return managed Department object
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** All Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric node identifiers prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `app`, `em`) to comply with the compilation safeguards. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without embedded markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Object-Relational Mapping (ORM). Mention any two advantages of using ORM in Java applications. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) is a programming technique that converts data between incompatible type systems — specifically, between the object-oriented model used in Java (classes, objects) and the relational model used in databases (tables, rows). It allows developers to manipulate database records as Java objects without writing complex SQL queries.

**Two Advantages:**

1. **Productivity:** Eliminates repetitive JDBC code. Developers work with objects rather than SQL, reducing development time by approximately 35-40%.
2. **Database Independence:** Applications become portable across different database systems (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL) with minimal configuration changes, because the ORM handles dialect-specific SQL generation.

### Question 2: Differentiate between `@OneToOne` and `@ManyToMany` association mapping in Hibernate with one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `@OneToOne` | `@ManyToMany` |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardinality** | One instance of A ↔ One instance of B | Multiple instances of A ↔ Multiple instances of B |
| **Java Implementation** | Single object reference | Collection of objects on both sides |
| **Database Schema** | Foreign key with UNIQUE constraint | Requires a separate join table |
| **Example** | `Person` ↔ `Passport` | `Student` ↔ `Course` |

**Example One-to-One:** A `Person` class has a single `Passport` field annotated with `@OneToOne`.

**Example Many-to-Many:** A `Student` class has a `Set<Course> courses` field annotated with `@ManyToMany`, and the `Course` class has a `Set<Student> students` field with `mappedBy`.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. A (a)** Explain the different types of association mappings in JPA/Hibernate with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**Q. A (b)** Describe the SINGLE_TABLE inheritance mapping strategy with a Java code example. What is a discriminator column? (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q. A (a)

The four types of association mappings in JPA/Hibernate are:

**1. One-to-One (@OneToOne):**
- **Definition:** A single instance of Entity A is associated with exactly one instance of Entity B.
- **Example:** `Employee` ↔ `ParkingSpot` (each employee gets exactly one parking spot).

```java
@Entity
public class Employee {
    @Id
    private Long id;
    
    @OneToOne
    @JoinColumn(name = "parking_spot_id")
    private ParkingSpot parkingSpot;
}

@Entity
public class ParkingSpot {
    @Id
    private Long id;
    private String location;
}
```

**2. One-to-Many (@OneToMany):**
- **Definition:** One instance of Entity A is associated with multiple instances of Entity B.
- **Example:** `Department` → `Employee[]` (one department contains many employees).

```java
@Entity
public class Department {
    @Id
    private Long id;
    
    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "department")
    private List<Employee> employees = new ArrayList<>();
}
```

**3. Many-to-One (@ManyToOne):**
- **Definition:** Inverse of One-to-Many. Many instances of Entity A reference a single instance of Entity B.
- **Example:** Many `Employee` objects belong to one `Department`.

```java
@Entity
public class Employee {
    @Id
    private Long id;
    
    @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    @JoinColumn(name = "department_id")
    private Department department;
}
```

**4. Many-to-Many (@ManyToMany):**
- **Definition:** Multiple instances of Entity A are associated with multiple instances of Entity B.
- **Example:** `Student` ↔ `Course` (a student takes many courses, a course has many students).

```java
@Entity
public class Student {
    @Id
    private Long id;
    
    @ManyToMany
    @JoinTable(name = "student_course",
        joinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "student_id"),
        inverseJoinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "course_id"))
    private Set<Course> courses = new HashSet<>();
}
```

---

### Model Solution for Q. A (b)

**SINGLE_TABLE Inheritance Mapping Strategy:**

This strategy maps an entire class hierarchy to a **single database table**. A **discriminator column** is added to differentiate between different subclass types stored in the same table.

**Java Code Example:**

```java
@Entity
@Table(name = "vehicle_single")
@Inheritance(strategy = InheritanceType.SINGLE_TABLE)
@DiscriminatorColumn(name = "vehicle_type", discriminatorType = DiscriminatorType.STRING)
public abstract class Vehicle {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;
    
    private String manufacturer;
    private int year;
}

@Entity
@DiscriminatorValue("CAR")
public class Car extends Vehicle {
    private int numDoors;
    private String fuelType;
}

@Entity
@DiscriminatorValue("BIKE")
public class Bike extends Vehicle {
    private boolean hasCarrier;
    private int engineCC;
}
```

**Generated SQL Schema:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE vehicle_single (
    id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
    manufacturer VARCHAR(255),
    year INT,
    vehicle_type VARCHAR(31),
    num_doors INT,
    fuel_type VARCHAR(255),
    has_carrier BOOLEAN,
    engine_cc INT
);
```

**Discriminator Column Explanation:**
- The `@DiscriminatorColumn` annotation specifies a special column (`vehicle_type`) in the single table that stores a **type identifier** (e.g., "CAR" or "BIKE") for each row.
- This allows Hibernate to **instantiate the correct subclass** when fetching a row from the table.
- The `@DiscriminatorValue` annotation on each subclass specifies what value is stored in the discriminator column for that subclass.
- **Advantages:** Fastest performance, no joins required for polymorphic queries, simple schema.
- **Disadvantages:** Subclass-specific columns will be NULL for unrelated subclasses, violating database normalization principles.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Hibernate ORM framework. Discuss the role of EntityManager. (7 marks)

**Q. B (b)** Compare JOINED and TABLE_PER_CLASS inheritance mapping strategies. Provide Java code examples for both. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q. B (a)

**Hibernate ORM Architecture (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram for visual representation):**

The Hibernate architecture consists of the following layers:

1. **Java Application Layer** — Contains the business logic and service classes.
2. **EntityManager (JPA) / Session (Hibernate native)** — The primary API for interacting with the persistence context. It manages the lifecycle of entity instances (persist, merge, remove, find).
3. **Persistence Context** — A first-level cache where managed entities are stored during a transaction.
4. **Hibernate Core Engine** — The brain of the framework that performs dirty checking, generates SQL, manages caching, and handles lazy loading.
5. **JDBC Layer** — The bridge to the underlying database, using standard JDBC APIs.
6. **Database** — The relational data store (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, etc.).

**Role of EntityManager:**

The `EntityManager` is the **central interface** for CRUD operations in JPA. Its key responsibilities include:

1. **Persist:** Save a new entity to the database (`em.persist(entity)`).
2. **Find/Retrieve:** Query the database by primary key (`em.find(Student.class, id)`).
3. **Merge:** Update a detached entity (`em.merge(entity)`).
4. **Remove:** Delete an entity (`em.remove(entity)`).
5. **Query Execution:** Execute JPQL or native SQL queries (`em.createQuery(...)`).
6. **Transaction Management:** Provide access to `EntityTransaction` for ACID compliance.
7. **Cache Management:** Maintain the L1 (first-level) persistence context cache.

**Example:**

```java
EntityManagerFactory emf = Persistence.createEntityManagerFactory("my-pu");
EntityManager em = emf.createEntityManager();
em.getTransaction().begin();
Student s = new Student();
s.setName("Kiran");
em.persist(s);
em.getTransaction().commit();
em.close();
emf.close();
```

---

### Model Solution for Q. B (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | JOINED Strategy | TABLE_PER_CLASS Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Table Count** | One table per class (including abstract) | One table per **concrete** class only |
| **NULL Columns** | None | None |
| **JOIN Required** | Yes (for polymorphic queries) | No (UNION used) |
| **Schema** | Normalized | Denormalized |
| **Performance** | Slower (joins) | Slower (UNION for polymorphism) |
| **Use Case** | When normalization is critical | Rarely used, similar to concrete table inheritance |

**JOINED Strategy Example:**

```java
@Entity
@Table(name = "animal_joined")
@Inheritance(strategy = InheritanceType.JOINED)
public abstract class Animal {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;
    private String name;
}

@Entity
@Table(name = "dog_joined")
public class Dog extends Animal {
    private String breed;
}

@Entity
@Table(name = "cat_joined")
public class Cat extends Animal {
    private boolean indoor;
}
```

**Generated SQL (JOINED):**

```sql
SELECT a.id, a.name, d.breed 
FROM animal_joined a 
LEFT JOIN dog_joined d ON a.id = d.id;
```

**TABLE_PER_CLASS Strategy Example:**

```java
@Entity
@Inheritance(strategy = InheritanceType.TABLE_PER_CLASS)
public abstract class Animal {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;
    private String name;
}

@Entity
@Table(name = "dog_tpc")
public class Dog extends Animal {
    private String breed;
}

@Entity
@Table(name = "cat_tpc")
public class Cat extends Animal {
    private boolean indoor;
}
```

**Generated SQL (TABLE_PER_CLASS):**

```sql
SELECT id, name, breed, NULL AS indoor 
FROM dog_tpc
UNION
SELECT id, name, NULL AS breed, indoor 
FROM cat_tpc;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing cardinalities:** Students frequently mix up `@OneToMany` and `@ManyToOne`. Remember: The side that holds the foreign key column in the database is `@ManyToOne`. The inverse side is `@OneToMany` with `mappedBy`.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting `mappedBy`:** When establishing bidirectional relationships, failing to use `mappedBy` on the inverse side creates **two foreign key columns** in the database, violating the design. Always use `mappedBy` to avoid redundancy.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring FetchType:** Default fetch type for `@ManyToOne` and `@OneToOne` is EAGER, while for `@OneToMany` and `@ManyToMany` it is LAZY. Not specifying `FetchType.LAZY` can cause severe performance issues (N+1 query problem).
> 
> 4. **Forgetting `@JoinColumn`:** Without `@JoinColumn`, Hibernate uses a default naming convention. While not always wrong, it is **best practice to specify** the column name explicitly for clarity.
> 
> 5. **Discriminator column confusion:** Many students write `@DiscriminatorValue` without `@DiscriminatorColumn` on the parent class. Both annotations are **mandatory** for the SINGLE_TABLE strategy to function correctly.
> 
> 6. **HashMap null key rule:** Students lose marks in Map-based questions by claiming `Hashtable` allows null keys/values — it does **NOT**. Only `HashMap`, `LinkedHashMap`, and `TreeMap` (value only) allow nulls.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mapping Concepts**

- **ORM** bridges Java's object model and the relational database model, solving the **impedance mismatch** problem.
- The **four cardinalities** of association mapping are `@OneToOne`, `@OneToMany`, `@ManyToOne`, `@ManyToMany`.
- **Bidirectional relationships** must use `mappedBy` on the inverse (non-owning) side to avoid duplicate foreign keys.
- **Many-to-Many** relationships always require a **join table** (associative entity) with composite primary key.
- **SINGLE_TABLE** inheritance uses a **discriminator column**; fastest but denormalized.
- **JOINED** inheritance uses **one table per class** with foreign key joins; normalized but slower.
- **TABLE_PER_CLASS** inheritance uses **one table per concrete class**; rarely recommended.
- **`HashMap`** offers $O(1)$ average complexity, allows one null key, is not thread-safe.
- **`Hashtable`** is synchronized (thread-safe), does NOT allow null keys or values.
- **`TreeMap`** maintains sorted keys using a Red-Black tree, offers $O(\log n)$ complexity.
- **`LinkedHashMap`** preserves insertion order while maintaining $O(1)$ access.
- The **load factor** of HashMap defaults to **0.75**; table is rehashed (doubled) when exceeded.
- **HashMap bucket index** = `(h ^ h >>> 16) & (n - 1)` where $n$ is always a power of 2.
- **Cascade types**: `PERSIST`, `MERGE`, `REMOVE`, `REFRESH`, `DETACH`, `ALL` — used in parent-child relationships.
- **Fetch types**: `EAGER` (immediate loading) vs `LAZY` (on-demand loading) — choose LAZY for collections.
- The **EntityManager** is the primary JPA interface for persisting, finding, merging, and removing entities.
- **JPQL** (Java Persistence Query Language) operates on entities, not database tables.
- **L1 cache** (persistence context) is mandatory and transaction-scoped; **L2 cache** is optional and JVM-scoped.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mapping Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping Activities (Object-Oriented Design)** is the systematic implementation phase in the **Object Modeling Technique (OMT)** where the logical, problem-domain **Design Model** (consisting of class diagrams, state diagrams, and interaction diagrams) is **transformed into a physically deployable software artifact** using a chosen object-oriented programming language such as **Java**.

The mapping is a **refinement process** — not a direct mechanical translation — because the design model is implementation-independent, while the target language (Java) imposes its own rules for **classes, inheritance, type-safety, persistence, and concurrency**.

### 1.1.1 The Four-Stage Transformation Pipeline

| Stage | From | To |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Refinement | Conceptual Class Diagram | Implementation-ready Class Diagram |
| 2. Translation | Classes & Attributes | Java source files (`.java`) |
| 3. Implementation | Methods (algorithm design) | Method bodies (statements) |
| 4. Optimization | Forward/Reverse Engineering | Performance-tuned code |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "Architect's Blueprint to House"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the design model as an **architect's blueprint** and Java as the **construction team with bricks, cement, and tools**.
> 
> The blueprint shows walls, doors, windows, and water pipelines (analogous to **classes, methods, and associations**), but it does not tell the mason *exactly* how to mix the cement or which brand of bricks to buy. The mason (Java developer) must interpret the blueprint and use available tools (language features) to build a real house (executable program).
> 
> **Mapping Activities = the "Construction Process"** of turning abstract design into a runnable system.

---

## 1.3 Why Mapping is Necessary (KTU 2024 Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEITT411 Module 4)**, mapping activities are crucial because:
> - Design diagrams are **language-neutral** (UML).
> - Java enforces **strict type rules** (no multiple inheritance of classes).
> - Many UML constructs (e.g., **n-ary associations, association classes**) have **no direct Java equivalent**.
> - The result of poor mapping is a **design-implementation gap** that leads to buggy, unmaintainable software.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Mapping Tasks (Rumbaugh OMT)

The Rumbaugh Object Modeling Technique (the reference methodology in KTU OOAD modules) defines **three mapping activities**:

1. **Class Mapping** — Translate each class into a Java `class` or `interface`.
2. **Association Mapping** — Translate relationships (`association`, `aggregation`, `composition`, `inheritance`) into Java references/fields.
3. **Constraint & Rule Mapping** — Translate invariants, multiplicities, and ordering into Java code (constructors, exceptions, generics).

---

## 2.2 Mapping Multiplicities (the "How Many?" Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplicity** specifies how many objects participate in an association. Java has **only ONE** built-in mechanism for multiplicity — the **object reference (field)**. Therefore, the multiplicity dictates *how* the reference is declared.

| Multiplicity (UML) | Java Realization | Memory Cost |
|---|---|---|
| `0..1` (zero or one) | `Type field;` (uninitialized reference) | $O(1)$ |
| `1` (exactly one) | `final Type field;` (set in constructor) | $O(1)$ |
| `1..*` (one or more) | `ArrayList<Type> field = new ArrayList<>();` (no removal allowed) | $O(n)$ |
| `0..*` (zero or more) | `ArrayList<Type> field = new ArrayList<>();` | $O(n)$ |
| `n` (fixed number) | `Type[] field = new Type[n];` (Java array) | $O(n)$ |
| `*` ordered | `LinkedList<Type> field = new LinkedList<>();` | $O(n)$ |
| `*` sorted | `TreeSet<Type> field = new TreeSet<>();` | $O(n \log n)$ |

> The choice of container **directly affects time complexity** — this is a frequent KTU 2-mark and 7-mark exam question.

---

## 2.3 Mapping Inheritance & Generalization

> [!WARNING]
> Java **does not support multiple inheritance of classes** (no `extends A, B`). This forces designers to use **interfaces** to recover the lost UML semantic.

| UML Construct | Java Realization |
|---|---|
| `extends` (single inheritance) | `class Sub extends Super { }` |
| Multiple inheritance of classes | **NOT ALLOWED** — use **multiple interfaces** |
| Abstract class (`{abstract}`) | `abstract class X { abstract void m(); }` |
| Interface (pure abstract) | `interface I { void m(); }` (Java 8+ allows `default` and `static` methods) |
| Overriding | `@Override` annotation (compile-time safety) |
| Polymorphism | `Super ref = new Sub(); ref.method();` → runtime dispatch |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Mapping Rules Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table for any 14-mark mapping question. It is the **most-tested** KTU content from Module 4.

| UML Design Element | Java Implementation Pattern |
|---|---|
| Class | `public class ClassName { }` |
| Attribute (private) | `private DataType attributeName;` |
| Attribute (public/read-only) | `public final DataType NAME;` |
| Operation | `public ReturnType opName(Params) { }` |
| Class Attribute (static/underlined) | `private static DataType name;` |
| Class Operation (static) | `public static ReturnType op() { }` |
| Abstract Operation (italic) | `abstract ReturnType op(Params);` |
| One-way Association (`-->`) | Field reference in the **source** class only |
| Two-way Association (`<-->`) | Field references in **both** classes |
| Aggregation (hollow diamond ◇) | Weak reference + no lifecycle coupling |
| Composition (filled diamond ◆) | Strong reference + object created **inside** constructor |
| Association Class (box on link) | Inner / nested class holding link + extras |
| Constraint `{ordered}` | `List<Type>` (preserves insertion order) |
| Constraint `{unique}` | `Set<Type>` (rejects duplicates) |
| n-ary Association | Auxiliary **link class** with fields to all n participants |
| `<<interface>>` | `interface InterfaceName { }` |
| `<<enumeration>>` | `enum EnumName { VAL1, VAL2, }` |
| `<<utility>>` (no instances) | Class with **only `static` methods** + **private constructor** |
| Realization (dashed triangle) | `class C implements Interface { }` |
| Dependency (dashed arrow) | Local variable / parameter / static call (no field) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mapping activities are the **backbone of Model-Driven Development (MDD)** used in:
> - **Enterprise Java (Spring/Hibernate frameworks)** — JPA entities map directly from UML class diagrams.
> - **Android Development** — Room database entities are mapped from domain classes.
> - **Microservices & APIs** — UML → OpenAPI specifications → Java/Kotlin stubs.
> 
> A study by the **Object Management Group (OMG)** shows that teams with disciplined mapping reduce post-deployment defects by **~30%**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Mapping Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Canonical 8-Step Mapping Algorithm (Rumbaugh)

This is the **complete procedural derivation** for converting a UML Class Diagram to Java source. Every KTU 14-mark mapping question expects you to **list and apply these steps**.

### Step 1 — Refine the Class Diagram

> Strip implementation-irrelevant details (e.g., remove notes, simplify unused classes). Add **internal classes** and **helper methods** that the implementation will need.

### Step 2 — Map Each Class to a Java Source File

**Rule:** One public class per `.java` file, file name = class name.

```java
public class Employee {              // ← maps UML class «Employee»
    // fields, methods to be filled below
}
```

### Step 3 — Map Attributes (Member Variables)

**Visibility translation:**

$$
\text{UML Symbol} \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \text{Java Access Modifier}
$$

| UML Visibility | Java Keyword |
|---|---|
| `+` (public) | `public` |
| `-` (private) | `private` |
| `#` (protected) | `protected` |
| `~` (package) | *(no keyword)* |

**Derivation of attribute declaration:**

$$
\text{Java Field} = (\text{visibility}) \; (\text{static?}) \; (\text{final?}) \; \text{Datatype} \; \text{name} \; (\text{= init})
$$

**Example mapping:**

| UML Attribute | Java Field |
|---|---|
| `- name : String` | `private String name;` |
| `- empId : int` | `private int empId;` |
| `+ MAX_SAL : double = 100000` | `public static final double MAX_SAL = 100000.0;` |

### Step 4 — Map Operations (Methods)

$$
\text{Java Method} = (\text{visibility}) \; (\text{static?}) \; (\text{abs?}) \; \text{ReturnType} \; \text{name} \; (\text{Params}) \; \{ \text{body} \}
$$

**Operations** like `+ calculateSalary() : double` map to:

```java
public double calculateSalary() {
    // implementation from interaction diagrams
    return baseSalary + bonus;
}
```

### Step 5 — Map Inheritance (extends / implements)

**Derivation rules:**

$$
\text{UML Generalization} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Java} \begin{cases} \text{extends} & \text{if super is a class} \\ \text{implements} & \text{if super is an interface} \end{cases}
$$

For multiple inheritance in UML (`Faculty` and `Researcher` both parents):

```java
public class Professor implements Faculty, Researcher {
    // must implement methods of BOTH interfaces
    @Override public void teach()   { /* ... */ }
    @Override public void publish() { /* ... */ }
}
```

### Step 6 — Map Associations (1-to-1, 1-to-many, many-to-many)

**For a one-to-one unidirectional association `Person ──> Passport` (1:1):**

```java
public class Person {
    private Passport passport;   // 0..1 multiplicity → uninitialized allowed
}
```

**For a one-to-many `Department ──○ Employee` (1 to 0..*):**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class Department {
    private List<Employee> employees = new ArrayList<>();
    public void addEmployee(Employee e) { employees.add(e); }
}
```

**For a two-way association**, place a field reference in **both** classes:

```java
public class Student {
    private Course[] courses;     // reverse reference
}
public class Course {
    private Student[] enrolled;   // forward reference
}
```

### Step 7 — Map Aggregation (◇) and Composition (◆)

**Aggregation (whole-part, but parts can outlive the whole):**

```java
public class Team {                 // Whole
    private List<Player> players;   // weak reference — Player exists independently
}
```

**Composition (whole-part, parts DIE with the whole):**

```java
public class House {                // Whole
    private final List<Room> rooms; // strong reference
    public House() {
        rooms = new ArrayList<>();
        rooms.add(new Room("Living"));
        rooms.add(new Room("Bedroom"));
    }
}
```

> The `final` keyword + construction **inside** the constructor = **composition** in Java. The KTU examiner's key looks for exactly this.

### Step 8 — Implement Methods, Constraints, and Optimizations

Use **interaction diagrams (sequence/collaboration)** to derive method bodies. Apply design optimizations:

- Replace O(n²) searches with `HashMap` (O(1) average).
- Cache frequently accessed objects.
- Use `final` for immutability.
- Apply `enum` for fixed sets.
- Implement `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()` when storing in collections.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Java Construct?"

| UML Scenario | Best Java Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `*` with duplicates allowed | `ArrayList<T>` | Preserves order, allows duplicates |
| `*` with no duplicates | `HashSet<T>` | O(1) `add`, rejects duplicates |
| `*` sorted | `TreeSet<T>` | O(log n) insert, sorted iteration |
| Fixed-size `n` | `T[]` array | Constant size, fastest access |
| Key-Value lookup | `HashMap<K, V>` | O(1) average `get`/`put` |
| Two-way 1-1 | Two fields (one `final`) | Mutual reference, no cycle issues |
| 0..1 with possible null | Plain reference | Java allows `null` by default |
| Inheritance diamond | Two interfaces | Java forbids multiple class inheritance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Mapping Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UML Design Model] --> B[Refined Class Diagram]
    B --> C[Class Mapping]
    B --> D[Association Mapping]
    B --> E[Inheritance Mapping]
    B --> F[Constraint Mapping]
    C --> G[Java Source Files]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compiled .class Files]
    H --> I[Optimized System]

    styleA fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    styleG fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    styleI fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 UML-to-Java Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UML Element] --> B{Is it a Class?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Abstract?}
    B -- No --> D{Is it an Interface?}
    C -- Yes --> E[abstract class X]
    C -- No --> F[class X]
    D -- Yes --> G[interface I]
    D -- No --> H{Is it an Association?}
    H -- Yes --> I{Multiplicity?}
    I -- 0..1 --> J[Reference field]
    I -- 1 --> K[final field in constructor]
    I -- 0..* --> L[List or Set field]
    I -- n --> M[Fixed array T]
    H -- No --> N{Is it Inheritance?}
    N -- Yes --> O{Single?}
    O -- Yes --> P[extends]
    O -- No --> Q[implements multiple interfaces]

    styleE fill:#bbdefb
    styleF fill:#bbdefb
    styleG fill:#c5e1a5
    styleP fill:#ffcc80
    styleQ fill:#ffcc80
```

## 4.3 Composition vs Aggregation — Java Realization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AGG[Aggregation - Weak Whole-Part]
        A1[Team] -.->|has-a weak| A2[Player]
        note1[Player can exist without Team]
    end

    subgraph COMP[Composition - Strong Whole-Part]
        C1[House] ==>|has-a strong| C2[Room]
        note2[Room dies with House - final field, created in constructor]
    end

    styleAGG fill:#e3f2fd
    styleCOMP fill:#fff3e0
```

## 4.4 n-ary Association Mapping Pattern

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Triangle {
        -vertexA : Point
        -vertexB : Point
        -vertexC : Point
    }
    Point --> Triangle : used in
    class Point {
        +x : int
        +y : int
    }
```

> **Pattern:** For an n-ary association, create a **link class** that holds a reference to **each** of the n participants. The above `Triangle` class is the link class for a 3-ary association between three `Point` objects.

## 4.5 Mapping Workflow Sequence (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Designer
    participant U as UML Diagram
    participant J as Java Developer
    participant C as Compiler

    D->>U: 1. Create Class Diagram
    U->>J: 2. Hand off for mapping
    J->>J: 3. Map classes to .java files
    J->>J: 4. Map attributes to fields
    J->>J: 5. Map operations to methods
    J->>J: 6. Map relationships to references
    J->>C: 7. Compile
    C-->>J: 8. Feedback on type errors
    J->>J: 9. Optimize
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]`

**Q: Define the term "Mapping Activities" in the context of object-oriented design. List any two UML design constructs and their corresponding Java realizations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Mapping Activities** is the design-phase process of transforming the implementation-independent UML design model (class diagrams, state diagrams, interaction diagrams) into source code in a specific object-oriented programming language such as Java.
>
> **Two UML-to-Java mappings:**
>
> 1. **UML Class `Employee`** → `public class Employee { }` *(1 mark)*
> 2. **UML Attribute `-name : String`** → `private String name;` *(1 mark)*
> 3. **UML Operation `+ calc() : double`** → `public double calc() { }` *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sessional Exam Sample]`

**Q: How is UML multiple inheritance realized in Java? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Java **does not support multiple inheritance of classes** to avoid the *Diamond Problem*. Instead, UML multiple inheritance is realized using **multiple interface implementation** (1.5 marks):
>
> ```java
> public class Professor implements Faculty, Researcher {
>     @Override public void teach() { }
>     @Override public void publish() { }
> }
> ```
>
> Here `Professor` inherits behavior from both `Faculty` and `Researcher` interfaces, recovering the UML semantic (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Main Paper]`

**Consider the following UML Class Diagram for a University Course Management System:**
- `Department` has a **composition** relationship with `Course` (1 to 1..*).
- `Course` has a **two-way association** with `Student` (1 to 0..*).
- `Student` is an **abstract class**; `GraduateStudent` and `UndergraduateStudent` are concrete subclasses.
- `Student` has a private attribute `studentId : int` and a public abstract method `computeGrade() : char`.

**(a)** Map the above design to a complete Java implementation with all classes. Show **field declarations, constructors, and method signatures**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **composition** and **two-way association** differ in their Java realization. Justify your choice of collection class for the `Student-Course` association. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

// ===== Abstract class Student =====
public abstract class Student {
    private int studentId;
    private Course course;     // reverse pointer for two-way assoc

    public Student(int id) {
        this.studentId = id;
    }
    public int getStudentId() { return studentId; }
    public void setCourse(Course c) { this.course = c; }
    public abstract char computeGrade();
}

// ===== Concrete Subclasses =====
public class GraduateStudent extends Student {
    public GraduateStudent(int id) { super(id); }
    @Override
    public char computeGrade() {
        return 'A';   // algorithm from interaction diagram
    }
}

public class UndergraduateStudent extends Student {
    public UndergraduateStudent(int id) { super(id); }
    @Override
    public char computeGrade() {
        return 'B';
    }
}

// ===== Course =====
public class Course {
    private String title;
    private List<Student> students = new ArrayList<>();
    private Department department;     // composition reverse pointer

    public Course(String t) { this.title = t; }
    public void addStudent(Student s) {
        students.add(s);
        s.setCourse(this);     // maintain two-way link
    }
}

// ===== Department (Composition Owner) =====
public class Department {
    private final List<Course> courses;
    private String name;

    public Department(String n) {
        this.name = n;
        this.courses = new ArrayList<>();
        // Courses created INSIDE constructor → composition
        courses.add(new Course("OOAD"));
        courses.add(new Course("DBMS"));
    }
}
```

**Mark split:** Class declarations (1) + attributes (1) + constructors (1.5) + inheritance & abstract (1.5) + two-way maintenance (1) + composition marker (1) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Composition (◆) vs Two-Way Association (—):**

| Aspect | Composition (◆) | Two-Way Association (—) |
|---|---|---|
| Lifecycle coupling | Strong: parts die with whole | Weak: parts exist independently |
| Java marker | `final` field + object created in constructor | Plain field; can be set later |
| Memory ownership | Whole owns the part | Both classes reference each other |
| Example in code | `Department → Course` | `Course ↔ Student` |

**Collection choice for `Student-Course`:**
- Multiplicity is **0..*** (variable number of students per course).
- **Duplicates allowed** (same student can retake a course) → `List`, not `Set`.
- Order is **insertion order** (semester roster) → `ArrayList` over `HashSet`.
- Therefore: `private List<Student> students = new ArrayList<>();` (3 marks for justification).

**Mark split:** Difference table (3) + Composition Java marker (2) + Collection justification (2) = **7 marks**.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Supplementary]`

**A Library Management System has the following UML design:**
- `Library` has a **composition** of `Book` (1 to 1..*).
- `Book` has a **one-way association** with `Author` (1 to 1).
- `LibraryMember` has a private attribute `memberId : int` and operation `+ borrowBook(b : Book) : void`.
- An `<<interface>> Searchable` declares `+ search(query : String) : List<Book>`.
- `Book` **realizes** `Searchable`.

**(a)** Write the complete Java code for all the above classes and the interface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the mapping rules for: **(i)** `<<interface>>` and **realization** in UML, **(ii)** composition vs aggregation, and **(iii)** one-way association with multiplicity 1. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

// ===== Interface =====
public interface Searchable {
    List<Book> search(String query);
}

// ===== Author =====
public class Author {
    private String name;
    public Author(String n) { this.name = n; }
    public String getName() { return name; }
}

// ===== Book (realizes Searchable) =====
public class Book implements Searchable {
    private String title;
    private Author author;     // one-way 1-to-1
    public Book(String t, Author a) { this.title = t; this.author = a; }

    @Override
    public List<Book> search(String query) {
        List<Book> result = new ArrayList<>();
        if (this.title.contains(query)) result.add(this);
        return result;
    }
}

// ===== Library (Composition owner) =====
public class Library {
    private final List<Book> books;
    private String libraryName;

    public Library(String n) {
        this.libraryName = n;
        this.books = new ArrayList<>();
        Author a1 = new Author("Joshua Bloch");
        books.add(new Book("Effective Java", a1));
    }
}

// ===== LibraryMember =====
public class LibraryMember {
    private int memberId;
    public LibraryMember(int id) { this.memberId = id; }
    public void borrowBook(Book b) {
        System.out.println("Member " + memberId + " borrowed " + b);
    }
}
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**(i) `<<interface>>` and Realization (3 marks):**
- The UML `<<interface>>` stereotype maps to Java's `interface` keyword. All interface methods are implicitly `public abstract` (pre-Java 8) or may be `default`/`static` from Java 8+.
- A dashed hollow-triangle realization arrow maps to the `implements` keyword.
- A class that realizes an interface must provide implementations for **all** its abstract methods, or be declared `abstract`.

**(ii) Composition vs Aggregation (2 marks):**
- **Composition (◆):** Strong whole-part ownership. Java realization uses a `final` reference field initialized **inside the constructor** of the whole. Example: `Library → Book` — books cease to exist when the library is destroyed.
- **Aggregation (◇):** Weak whole-part relationship. Java uses a regular (non-`final`) reference field, settable at any time. Example: `Team → Player` — players can transfer to other teams.

**(iii) One-way association, multiplicity 1 (2 marks):**
- A one-way association with multiplicity `1` means the **source** class holds exactly one reference to the target.
- Java realization: `private final TargetType target;` declared in the source class and assigned in the constructor.
- No reverse field exists in the target class. The target object is unaware of the association.
- Example: `Book → Author` — the Book knows its Author, but the Author does not know about specific Books.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in KTU mapping questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `final` on composition fields** (−1 mark). The KTU key explicitly checks for the `final` keyword + construction inside the constructor.
> 2. **Using `extends` for multiple inheritance** (−2 marks). Always use `implements` for multiple interface inheritance. Java will not compile otherwise.
> 3. **Omitting `@Override` annotation** (−0.5 mark). Although not strictly required, KTU examiners reward it as a best practice.
> 4. **Using `ArrayList` when the UML says `{unique}`** (−1 mark). The KTU key expects `Set` (or `HashSet`) for uniqueness constraints.
> 5. **Forgetting to maintain two-way links in `add` methods** (−1 mark). When adding `Student` to `Course`, you must also call `s.setCourse(this)`.
> 6. **Not initializing the collection in the field declaration** (−0.5 mark). KTU expects `= new ArrayList<>();` to avoid `NullPointerException`.
> 7. **Forgetting to add `public` to interface method implementations** (−1 mark). Java interface methods are public by default, and implementations must match.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Mapping Activities" (PEITT411 Module 4):**
> 
> - **Definition:** Mapping is the transformation of the UML design model into Java source code.
> - **Three core tasks:** Class mapping, association mapping, constraint/multiplicity mapping.
> - **8-Step Procedure:** Refine → Classes → Attributes → Operations → Inheritance → Associations → Aggregation/Composition → Optimize.
> - **Multiplicity → Container rule:**
>   - `0..1` → plain reference
>   - `1` → `final` reference in constructor
>   - `0..*` / `1..*` → `List` / `Set` / `Map` based on order/duplicates
>   - Fixed `n` → array
> - **Aggregation (◇)** → weak reference; object exists independently.
> - **Composition (◆)** → `final` field + creation in constructor.
> - **Multiple inheritance** in UML → `implements` multiple interfaces in Java.
> - **Realization (dashed triangle)** → `implements` keyword.
> - **Generalization (solid triangle)** → `extends` keyword (single class only).
> - **Two-way associations** require fields in **both** classes + setter logic to keep links consistent.
> - **n-ary associations** are realized using a **link class** with one field per participant.
> - **Association classes** in UML map to **inner/nested classes** in Java.
> - **`<<interface>>`** → Java `interface`.
> - **`<<enumeration>>`** → Java `enum`.
> - **`<<utility>>`** → class with `static` methods + `private` constructor.
> - **Optimization tip:** Use `HashMap<K,V>` for O(1) lookups, `final` for immutability, and `enum` for fixed type sets.
> - **Always include:** `@Override`, package imports (`java.util.*`), and proper access modifiers in the mapped Java code.
> - **Examiner's mantra:** *“If the UML says composition, the Java code must say `final` + constructor creation.”*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT411 - OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Managing Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Managing Implementation — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Managing Implementation** in Object-Oriented Design refers to the systematic organization, reuse, control, and extension of class implementations through Java's mechanism set — namely **inheritance**, **abstract classes**, **interfaces**, **final classes/methods**, **packages**, **access modifiers**, **inner classes**, and **enumerations** — to build maintainable, extensible, and loosely-coupled software systems.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Managing Implementation* is the phase where the designer decides **how an object's behavior is actually realized in code** and **how code is shared, restricted, overridden, or extended** across a class hierarchy.

The topic encompasses the following pillars:

| # | Pillar | KTU 2024 Learning Goal |
|---|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | Inheritance & Code Reuse | Reuse parent class implementation in subclasses |
| 2 | Abstract Classes & Methods | Force subclasses to provide specific behavior |
| 3 | Interfaces | Define contracts independent of class hierarchy |
| 4 | `final` keyword | Prevent unwanted modification of implementation |
| 5 | Access Modifiers | Control visibility of implementation details |
| 6 | Packages | Group related classes and manage namespace |
| 7 | Inner/Nested Classes | Logically group helper implementations |
| 8 | Enumerations | Manage fixed sets of implementation variants |
| 9 | Object Class methods | Standardize how every object reports itself |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Restaurant Franchise" Model 🍔**

Imagine you are the **CEO of a fast-food chain** (the *base implementation*). You have:

- A **Master Recipe Book** (the *parent class*) that defines the core cooking process for a burger.
- **Branch franchises** (the *subclasses*) that inherit the master recipe but may tweak sauces or toppings.
- **Non-Negotiable Rules** (the *final methods*) — you *forbid* franchises from changing the grill temperature.
- **Mandatory Standards** (the *abstract methods*) — every franchise **must** invent its own signature drink.
- **Quality Certifications** (the *interfaces*) — franchises can earn "Vegan Certified" or "Halal Certified" — different branches can earn the *same* certification from *different* parent companies.
- **Kitchen Sections** (the *packages*) — beverages, desserts, grills — separated for hygiene.
- **Secret Recipes** (the *private* methods) — only the head chef knows.
- **Daily Specials** (the *inner classes*) — temporary menu items cooked inside a branch.
- **Limited-Edition Menus** (the *enums*) — only Summer, Winter, Monsoon.

Just as a CEO **manages** how the burger is implemented across thousands of branches, a Java developer **manages** how class behavior is implemented across the class hierarchy. That's **Managing Implementation**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> In Java, every class implicitly extends `java.lang.Object` if no other parent is declared. The Object class is the **root of all implementation hierarchies**.

Standard visibility levels recognized by KTU 2024:

| Modifier | Visibility Scope | Package Context |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| `private` | Same class only | **0%** external access |
| (default) | Same package | **Package-private** |
| `protected` | Same package + subclasses | **Inheritance-aware** |
| `public` | Everywhere | **100%** global access |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Inheritance for Implementation Reuse

**Inheritance** is the mechanism by which one class (subclass/child) acquires the fields and methods of another class (superclass/parent).

### Types of Inheritance in Java

| Type | Diagram Shape | Java Support | Example |
|------|--------------|--------------|---------|
| Single | A → B | ✅ Yes | `class B extends A` |
| Multilevel | A → B → C | ✅ Yes | `class C extends B` |
| Hierarchical | A → B, A → C | ✅ Yes | `class B extends A` & `class C extends A` |
| Multiple | A, B → C | ❌ No (use interfaces) | Not allowed via classes |
| Hybrid | Mix of above | ❌ No (use interfaces) | Achieved via interfaces |

### Key Implementation Rules

- A subclass **inherits** all accessible members of its superclass.
- A subclass can **override** instance methods to provide specialized behavior.
- The `super` keyword refers to the immediate parent class.
- Constructors are **not inherited**, but the parent's constructor is invoked via `super(...)`.
- Java enforces **single inheritance** for classes (one direct parent only).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method Overriding Rules (KTU 2024 High-Yield)**:
> 1. Method signature (name + parameter list) must be **identical**.
> 2. Return type must be the **same or a covariant subtype**.
> 3. Access modifier cannot be **more restrictive** than the parent's.
> 4. Cannot override `static`, `final`, or `private` methods.
> 5. Use `@Override` annotation (best practice, not mandatory).

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Abstract Classes & Methods

An **abstract class** is a class that **cannot be instantiated** and may contain **abstract methods** (methods without a body).

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> An **abstract method** is a *contract declaration without implementation* — it specifies **what** a method must do, but defers **how** to subclasses.

### Declaration Syntax

```java
abstract class Shape {
    abstract double area();   // No body
    void display() {          // Concrete method allowed
        System.out.println("Shape");
    }
}
```

### Why Abstract Classes Exist

- Provide a **common base** for related classes.
- Enforce a **mandatory contract** (abstract methods).
- Allow **partial implementation** (mix of abstract + concrete methods).
- Support **shared state** (instance variables).

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Interfaces

An **interface** is a pure abstract type containing abstract methods, default methods, static methods, and constants.

### Java 8+ Interface Capabilities

| Member Type | Allowed | Notes |
|-------------|---------|-------|
| Abstract method | ✅ | Implicitly `public abstract` |
| Default method | ✅ | Has body, marked `default` |
| Static method | ✅ | Has body, called via interface name |
| Constant | ✅ | Implicitly `public static final` |
| Private method | ✅ (Java 9+) | Helper inside interface |
| Instance variable | ❌ | Only constants allowed |

### Class vs Interface — The Core Distinction

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Instantiation | ❌ | ❌ |
| `extends` / `implements` | `extends` (single) | `implements` (multiple) |
| State (instance vars) | ✅ | ❌ (only constants) |
| Constructor | ✅ | ❌ |
| Method bodies | ✅ (partial) | ✅ (default/static) |
| Access modifiers | Any | Implicitly `public` |
| When to use | "is-a" with shared code | "can-do" capability |

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — The `final` Keyword

The `final` keyword has three distinct implementations in Java:

| Applied To | Effect | KTU Use Case |
|------------|--------|--------------|
| Variable | Value cannot change | Constants |
| Method | Cannot be overridden | Lock down behavior |
| Class | Cannot be subclassed | Immutable types (e.g., `String`) |

> [!WARNING]
> A `final` class **cannot be extended**. A `final` method **cannot be overridden**. A `final` variable **cannot be reassigned** (must be initialized).

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Access Modifiers (Implementation Visibility)

### Visibility Matrix

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff pkg) | World |
|----------|:---------:|:-----------:|:------------------:|:-----:|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| (default) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encapsulation Principle (KTU Pillar Concept)**: Hide implementation details using `private`, and expose controlled access via `public` getters/setters. This is the **core mechanism for managing implementation** in OOP.

## 2.6 Pillar 6 — Packages

A **package** is a namespace that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces.

### Package Implementation Benefits

- **Namespace management** — avoid class name conflicts.
- **Access control** — package-private visibility.
- **Modularization** — group related implementation.
- **Distribution** — JAR files contain packages.

### Package Access Rules

- A class can be `public` (accessible everywhere) or package-private (default — accessible only within the same package).
- To use a class from another package, either **import** it or use the **fully qualified name**.

## 2.7 Pillar 7 — Inner / Nested Classes

A class defined **within another class** is called a **nested class**. There are four types:

| Type | Defined In | Access to Outer Members | Use Case |
|------|-----------|------------------------|----------|
| Member Inner Class | Inside class, outside method | All (including private) | Helper tightly coupled to outer |
| Static Nested Class | Inside class, marked `static` | Static only | Grouping helper |
| Local Inner Class | Inside a method | Effectively final locals | One-off helper |
| Anonymous Inner Class | Inside expression, no name | All (final/effectively final) | Inline interface implementation |

## 2.8 Pillar 8 — Enumerations (`enum`)

An `enum` is a special class that represents a **fixed set of constants**.

```java
enum Season { SPRING, SUMMER, AUTUMN, WINTER }
```

Enums in Java are **more powerful** than in C/C++:

- They can have **constructors**, **fields**, and **methods**.
- They can **implement interfaces**.
- They are **implicitly `final`** — cannot be extended.
- They are **type-safe** — cannot assign arbitrary values.

## 2.9 Pillar 9 — The `Object` Class & Standard Methods

Every Java class implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`. The three most-overridden methods for managing implementation are:

| Method | Purpose | KTU Override Rule |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| `toString()` | Human-readable representation | Always override for debugging |
| `equals(Object o)` | Logical equality | Override when "value equality" matters |
| `hashCode()` | Hash for `HashMap`/`HashSet` | **Must** override alongside `equals` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`equals` & `hashCode` Contract (KTU 2024 Critical)**:
> - If two objects are equal via `equals()`, they **must** have the same `hashCode()`.
> - If `hashCode()` is equal, objects **may or may not** be equal.
> - Violating this contract breaks `HashMap`, `HashSet`, and `Hashtable`.

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Rule | Memory Aid |
|---|---------|------|------------|
| 1 | Inheritance depth | Unbounded (until StackOverflow) | "Chain of command" |
| 2 | Abstract class instantiation | ❌ Forbidden | "Blueprint, not building" |
| 3 | Interface multiple inheritance | ✅ Allowed | "Many capabilities" |
| 4 | `final` class extension | ❌ Forbidden | "Sealed forever" |
| 5 | `final` method override | ❌ Forbidden | "Locked behavior" |
| 6 | Static method override | ❌ (can hide) | "Class-level, not instance" |
| 7 | `private` method override | ❌ (invisible to subclass) | "Invisible contract" |
| 8 | Default access modifier | Package-private | "No keyword = package" |
| 9 | `protected` access | Package + subclasses | "Family secret" |
| 10 | `equals` ↔ `hashCode` | Equal ⇒ same hash | "Equal implies same bucket" |
| 11 | Polymorphic call | Resolved at runtime (dynamic dispatch) | "Late binding" |
| 12 | Enum inheritance | ❌ Cannot extend (implicitly `final`) | "Closed set" |
| 13 | Inner class access | Can access all outer members | "Inside the family" |
| 14 | Static nested class | Accesses only static outer members | "Detached cousin" |
| 15 | Package-private top class | No `public` keyword allowed outside the file's named class | "One public per file" |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Designing a Class Hierarchy to Manage Implementation

### Problem Statement

Design a class hierarchy for a payroll system where `Employee` is the abstract base, and `FullTimeEmployee` and `PartTimeEmployee` are concrete subclasses. Calculate salary polymorphically.

### Step-by-Step Build

**Step 1:** Identify the abstract base.

```java
abstract class Employee {
    protected String name;
    protected int id;
    
    public Employee(String name, int id) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
    }
    
    public abstract double calculateSalary();
    
    public void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name);
    }
}
```
*Logic:* `name` and `id` are common to all employees. `calculateSalary()` is abstract because it differs by type.

**Step 2:** Derive `FullTimeEmployee` (fixed monthly salary).

```java
class FullTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    private double monthlySalary;
    
    public FullTimeEmployee(String name, int id, double monthlySalary) {
        super(name, id);  // Call parent constructor
        this.monthlySalary = monthlySalary;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double calculateSalary() {
        return monthlySalary;
    }
}
```
*Logic:* Inheritance acquires `name`, `id`, and `displayInfo()`. We override `calculateSalary()`.

**Step 3:** Derive `PartTimeEmployee` (hourly wage).

```java
class PartTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    private double hourlyRate;
    private int hoursWorked;
    
    public PartTimeEmployee(String name, int id, double hourlyRate, int hours) {
        super(name, id);
        this.hourlyRate = hourlyRate;
        this.hoursWorked = hours;
    }
    
    @Override
    public double calculateSalary() {
        return hourlyRate * hoursWorked;
    }
}
```
*Logic:* Salary formula: $\text{Salary} = \text{hourlyRate} \times \text{hoursWorked}$.

**Step 4:** Use polymorphism (dynamic dispatch).

```java
public class PayrollTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee[] staff = new Employee[2];
        staff[0] = new FullTimeEmployee("Alice", 101, 50000);
        staff[1] = new PartTimeEmployee("Bob", 102, 500, 40);
        
        double total = 0;
        for (Employee e : staff) {
            e.displayInfo();
            double sal = e.calculateSalary();   // Polymorphic call
            System.out.println("Salary: " + sal);
            total = total + sal;                // Accumulate
        }
        System.out.println("Total Payroll: " + total);
    }
}
```

### Mathematical Trace

$$
\text{Total Payroll} = \text{Salary}_{\text{FullTime}} + \text{Salary}_{\text{PartTime}}
$$

$$
\text{Total Payroll} = 50000 + (500 \times 40) = 50000 + 20000 = 70000
$$

**Output Trace:**

$$
\text{ID: 101, Name: Alice} \\
\text{Salary: 50000.0} \\
\text{ID: 102, Name: Bob} \\
\text{Salary: 20000.0} \\
\text{Total Payroll: 70000.0}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Interface-Based Multiple Implementation Management

### Problem

A class `SmartPhone` must be chargeable, callable, and browsable. Show how **interfaces** manage multiple implementation contracts.

### Step-by-Step Build

**Step 1:** Define the contracts.

```java
interface Chargeable {
    void charge();
}

interface Callable {
    void makeCall(String number);
}

interface Browsable {
    void browse(String url);
}
```

**Step 2:** Implement all three in one class.

```java
class SmartPhone implements Chargeable, Callable, Browsable {
    @Override
    public void charge() {
        System.out.println("Charging via USB-C");
    }
    
    @Override
    public void makeCall(String number) {
        System.out.println("Calling " + number);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void browse(String url) {
        System.out.println("Browsing " + url);
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> A class can `implements` **multiple interfaces**, achieving Java's version of multiple inheritance. This is **the** KTU-mandated technique for managing multi-dimensional implementation contracts.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — `equals` and `hashCode` Contract

### Problem

Create a `Student` class where two students are "equal" if their `rollNo` is the same.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Override `equals`.

```java
@Override
public boolean equals(Object o) {
    if (this == o) return true;                       // Same reference
    if (o == null || getClass() != o.getClass()) return false;
    Student s = (Student) o;
    return rollNo == s.rollNo;
}
```

**Step 2:** Override `hashCode` to honor the contract.

```java
@Override
public int hashCode() {
    return Integer.hashCode(rollNo);
}
```

### Mathematical Proof of Contract

Let $e(s)$ be `equals(s)` and $h(s)$ be `hashCode(s)`.

$$
\forall s_1, s_2 \in \text{Student}: \quad e(s_1, s_2) = \text{true} \;\Rightarrow\; h(s_1) = h(s_2)
$$

**Proof by construction:** Since equality is determined solely by `rollNo`, and `hashCode` returns a deterministic function of `rollNo`:

$$
s_1.\text{rollNo} = s_2.\text{rollNo} \;\Rightarrow\; h(s_1) = h(s_2) \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Forgetting to override `hashCode` after overriding `equals`. This breaks `HashSet` membership — a `Student` you added may not be findable!

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Enumerations with Behavior

```java
enum Direction {
    NORTH(0, 1), SOUTH(0, -1), EAST(1, 0), WEST(-1, 0);
    
    private final int dx, dy;
    
    Direction(int dx, int dy) {
        this.dx = dx;
        this.dy = dy;
    }
    
    public int getDx() { return dx; }
    public int getDy() { return dy; }
}
```

### Vector Movement Equation

$$
\text{New Position} = \text{Current Position} + \begin{bmatrix} dx \\ dy \end{bmatrix}
$$

For moving NORTH by 5 units:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + 5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y + 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Implementation Tool

| Requirement | Recommended Tool | Why |
|-------------|-----------------|-----|
| Share common code among related classes | Abstract Class | Provides state + partial impl |
| Define a pure contract, no shared code | Interface | Lightweight, multiple inheritance |
| Lock a method from being overridden | `final` method | Prevents subclass modification |
| Create constants group | `enum` | Type-safe, supports behavior |
| Group helper classes | Static nested class | Namespace + no outer reference |
| Quick one-off implementation | Anonymous inner class | Inline, no separate file |
| Hide implementation details | `private` fields/methods | Encapsulation |
| Organize large codebases | Packages | Namespace + access control |

```mermaid

    D -- FullTimeEmployee --> E[Invoke FullTimeEmployee.calculateSalary]
    D -- PartTimeEmployee --> F[Invoke PartTimeEmployee.calculateSalary]
    E --> G[Return monthly salary]
    F --> H[Return hourly rate times hours]
    G --> I[Caller receives polymorphic result]
    H --> I
```
