# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**  
Differentiate between an **abstract class** and an **interface** in Java. When would you prefer one over the other?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> An **abstract class** is a partially implemented class that can hold state (instance variables), constructors, and a mix of abstract and concrete methods. It supports **single inheritance** via the `extends` keyword.
>
> An **interface** is a pure contract that (in Java 8+) can contain abstract, default, and static methods, but **no instance state** — only `public static final` constants. A class can **implement multiple interfaces**, achieving multiple inheritance of type.
>
> **Preference Guidelines:**
> - Use an **abstract class** when classes share *common code* and *state* (an "is-a" relationship with shared implementation).
> - Use an **interface** when unrelated classes must share a *capability* (a "can-do" relationship), or when multiple inheritance of type is needed.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
What is the significance of the `final` keyword when applied to a **class**, a **method**, and a **variable**? Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The `final` keyword restricts further modification at three levels:
>
> 1. **`final` class** — Cannot be subclassed. Example:
>    `public final class String { ... }`  → No one can extend `String`.
> 2. **`final` method** — Cannot be overridden by subclasses. Example:
>    `public final void display() { ... }`  → Lock the behavior in subclasses.
> 3. **`final` variable** — Can be assigned **only once**. Used to declare **constants**:
>    `public static final double PI = 3.14159;`
>
> The keyword is essential for **security**, **immutability**, and **design contracts** in managing implementation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Inheritance, Abstract Class & Polymorphism

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Design a Java class hierarchy for a banking system with an abstract class `Account` and two subclasses `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount`. Include relevant fields, constructors, and an abstract method `calculateInterest()`. Implement `calculateInterest()` in both subclasses using:
- SavingsAccount: 4% per annum on balance
- CurrentAccount: 2% per annum on balance

**(b)** [7 Marks] Write a `BankTest` class that demonstrates **runtime polymorphism** by storing accounts in an array of `Account` references and computing total interest. Show the output for the given test data.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Class Hierarchy Implementation:**

```java
abstract class Account {
    protected String accountHolder;
    protected double balance;
    
    public Account(String accountHolder, double balance) {
        this.accountHolder = accountHolder;
        this.balance = balance;
    }
    
    public abstract double calculateInterest();
    
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Holder: " + accountHolder + ", Balance: " + balance);
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    public SavingsAccount(String holder, double balance) {
        super(holder, balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    public double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.04;   // 4% per annum
    }
}

class CurrentAccount extends Account {
    public CurrentAccount(String holder, double balance) {
        super(holder, balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    public double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.02;   // 2% per annum
    }
}
```

**(b) Polymorphic Driver with Output Trace:**

```java
public class BankTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account[] accounts = new Account[3];
        accounts[0] = new SavingsAccount("Anu", 100000);
        accounts[1] = new CurrentAccount("Balu", 200000);
        accounts[2] = new SavingsAccount("Chitra", 50000);
        
        double totalInterest = 0;
        for (Account a : accounts) {
            a.display();
            double interest = a.calculateInterest();  // Polymorphic dispatch
            System.out.println("Interest: " + interest);
            totalInterest = totalInterest + interest;
        }
        System.out.println("Total Interest Payout: " + totalInterest);
    }
}
```

**Output Trace Computation:**

$$
I_{\text{Anu}} = 100000 \times 0.04 = 4000
$$

$$
I_{\text{Balu}} = 200000 \times 0.02 = 4000
$$

$$
I_{\text{Chitra}} = 50000 \times 0.04 = 2000
$$

$$
\text{Total Interest} = 4000 + 4000 + 2000 = 10000
$$

**Output:**

```
Holder: Anu, Balance: 100000.0
Interest: 4000.0
Holder: Balu, Balance: 200000.0
Interest: 4000.0
Holder: Chitra, Balance: 50000.0
Interest: 2000.0
Total Interest Payout: 10000.0
```

### Question B — Interfaces, Multiple Inheritance & Access Control

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define three interfaces — `Printable`, `Scannable`, and `Faxable` — each with one abstract method. Create a class `MultiFunctionPrinter` that implements all three interfaces. Demonstrate how Java achieves multiple inheritance of type through interfaces.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the four access modifiers in Java with a **visibility matrix** and write a program demonstrating the use of `private`, `protected`, and `public` modifiers across a parent class `Vehicle` and a subclass `Car` in a different package scenario (use package declarations as comments if needed).

#### Model Solution

**(a) Interface Implementation:**

```java
interface Printable {
    void print(String document);
}

interface Scannable {
    void scan(String document);
}

interface Faxable {
    void fax(String document, String number);
}

class MultiFunctionPrinter implements Printable, Scannable, Faxable {
    @Override
    public void print(String document) {
        System.out.println("Printing: " + document);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void scan(String document) {
        System.out.println("Scanning: " + document);
    }
    
    @Override
    public void fax(String document, String number) {
        System.out.println("Faxing " + document + " to " + number);
    }
}

public class MFPTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MultiFunctionPrinter mfp = new MultiFunctionPrinter();
        mfp.print("Report.pdf");
        mfp.scan("Invoice.jpg");
        mfp.fax("Contract.docx", "9876543210");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Printing: Report.pdf
Scanning: Invoice.jpg
Faxing Contract.docx to 9876543210
```

**(b) Visibility Matrix & Cross-Package Demo:**

**Visibility Matrix:**

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff pkg) | Other Packages |
|----------|:---------:|:-----------:|:------------------:|:--------------:|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| (default) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

```java
// File: com/ktu/vehicles/Vehicle.java
package com.ktu.vehicles;

public class Vehicle {
    private String engineNumber = "ENG-1234";
    protected int wheels = 4;
    public String brand = "Generic";
    
    private void showEngine() {
        System.out.println("Engine: " + engineNumber);  // Accessible inside same class
    }
}

// File: com/ktu/cars/Car.java
package com.ktu.cars;
import com.ktu.vehicles.Vehicle;

public class Car extends Vehicle {
    public void display() {
        // engineNumber = "X";    // ❌ Compile error - private
        // showEngine();           // ❌ Compile error - private
        System.out.println("Wheels: " + wheels);        // ✅ protected - inherited
        System.out.println("Brand: " + brand);          // ✅ public
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `protected` member `wheels` is **inaccessible** to non-subclasses in `com.ktu.cars` but **accessible** inside `Car` because `Car` extends `Vehicle`. This is the cornerstone of *inheritance-aware access control* in Java.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Deduction Traps in "Managing Implementation" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `super()` call** in subclass constructors → Compile error or default values. **Always** call `super(args)` as the **first line** of the subclass constructor.
>
> 2. **Inverted access modifier** in overriding → Cannot reduce visibility (e.g., `public → protected` in subclass is a compile error).
>
> 3. **Missing `hashCode` when overriding `equals`** → Breaks `HashSet`/`HashMap`. KTU explicitly tests the contract.
>
> 4. **Confusing `implements` with `extends` for interfaces** → `class A extends B` works for class, not interface. Use `class A implements I` or `class A extends B implements I, J`.
>
> 5. **Trying to instantiate abstract class or interface** → `new Shape()` or `new Runnable()` is a **compile-time error**. KTU examiners love this trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Managing Implementation**

- ✅ **Inheritance** enables code reuse via `extends` (single inheritance only for classes).
- ✅ **Abstract classes** can have state, constructors, and partial implementation; cannot be instantiated.
- ✅ **Interfaces** (Java 8+) can have `default`, `static`, and `private` (Java 9+) methods, plus constants; support **multiple inheritance of type**.
- ✅ **`final` keyword** locks classes (no extension), methods (no override), and variables (no reassignment).
- ✅ **Access modifiers** in increasing visibility: `private < default < protected < public`.
- ✅ **`protected`** = package access + subclass access (even in different package).
- ✅ **Packages** group related classes; package-private is the default access for top-level classes.
- ✅ **Inner classes** come in 4 flavors: member, static nested, local, anonymous — each with different scope rules.
- ✅ **`enum`** is implicitly `final`, type-safe, and can have fields, methods, and constructors.
- ✅ **`Object` class methods** (`toString`, `equals`, `hashCode`) should be overridden for proper debugging and collection behavior.
- ✅ **`equals`–`hashCode` contract**: Equal objects must have equal hash codes (the reverse is NOT required).
- ✅ **Polymorphism** uses **dynamic method dispatch** — the JVM picks the overridden method at runtime based on the actual object type.
- ✅ **Method overriding** requires identical signature, covariant return type, and equal-or-wider access.
- ✅ **Static, final, and private methods** cannot be overridden (they can be hidden in the case of static).
- ✅ **`@Override` annotation** is optional but strongly recommended — it triggers a compile error if the method doesn't actually override anything.
- ✅ **Constructor chaining** uses `super(...)` to invoke the parent constructor and `this(...)` to invoke another constructor in the same class.
- ✅ **Abstract method signatures** in a subclass must match the parent's exactly, with compatible return types.
- ✅ **Default methods** in interfaces allow backward-compatible evolution of APIs without breaking implementers.
- ✅ **Anonymous inner classes** are useful for one-off implementations, but **lambda expressions** (Java 8+) are preferred for functional interfaces.
